topics.title: Hydrologic Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hydrologic Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Hydrologic Cycle** (also called the **Water Cycle**) is the continuous, closed-loop circulation of water within the Earth's **hydrosphere**, driven primarily by **solar energy** and **gravitational potential**. It describes the perpetual movement of water through various phases — **liquid, vapour, and solid (ice)** — across the five major global reservoirs: **oceans, atmosphere, land surface, subsurface (soil and groundwater), and biological systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1, Unit 1):**
> *"The hydrologic cycle is a complex, continuous, global-scale process describing the movement and phase transformation of water above, on, and below the Earth's surface, powered by solar radiation and gravity, with no beginning and no end."*

Mathematically, the cycle is a **closed system** in which the **total global mass of water (~1.386 × 10⁹ km³)** remains essentially constant over geological timescales, while individual water molecules cycle through different reservoirs at varying rates.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Earth's Plumbing System"

Imagine the Earth as a giant, self-cleaning coffee machine ☕:

- The **Sun** acts as the *heater* — it boils water from oceans, lakes, and soil.
- The **atmosphere** is the *steam pipe* — it carries water vapour upward, where it cools and condenses.
- **Clouds** are the *drip filter* — they release water as precipitation when droplets become too heavy.
- The **land surface and rivers** are the *collection tray and drainage pipe* — they channel water back to the reservoir.
- **Groundwater aquifers** are the *hidden storage tank* — they hold water underground for slow release.

Every drop of water you have ever touched has been cycling through this "machine" for **billions of years** — it is essentially *recycled* dinosaur water!

### 1.3 Key Terminology in Callout Boxes

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Terminology for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Precipitation (P):** Any form of water (rain, snow, sleet, hail) falling from the atmosphere to the Earth's surface. Unit: **mm** or **cm**.
> - **Evaporation (E):** Phase change of water from *liquid → vapour* from open water bodies, soil, or wet surfaces.
> - **Transpiration (T):** Release of water vapour from *living plants* through stomata.
> - **Evapotranspiration (ET):** Combined loss of water through **Evaporation + Transpiration** = **E + T**.
> - **Infiltration (f):** Downward movement of water from the surface into the unsaturated soil zone.
> - **Percolation:** Continued downward movement of water through soil into the saturated zone (aquifer).
> - **Runoff (R):** Flow of water over the land surface toward streams, rivers, lakes, or oceans.
> - **Condensation:** Phase change of water from *vapour → liquid* (forms clouds and dew).
> - **Sublimation:** Direct phase change of *ice → vapour* (e.g., from glaciers, without melting).
> - **Advection:** Horizontal transport of water vapour by wind.
> - **Interception:** Process by which precipitation is captured by vegetation before reaching the ground.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Water Distribution (Standard Textbook Values):**
> - **Total global water** ≈ **1.386 × 10⁹ km³** (~97.5% saline, ~2.5% fresh)
> - **Oceans** hold **97.25%** of total water
> - **Freshwater reservoirs:** Glaciers/Ice caps (~68.7%), Groundwater (~30.1%), Surface water (~1.2%)
> - **Average annual global precipitation** ≈ **505,000 km³/year**
> - **Evaporation from oceans** ≈ **434,000 km³/year**
> - **Evapotranspiration from land** ≈ **71,000 km³/year**

The only externally driven energy input is **solar radiation (~ 1.74 × 10¹⁷ W)** reaching the Earth, of which a small fraction powers the entire cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Stages of the Hydrologic Cycle

The hydrologic cycle can be broken down into **five sequential sub-cycles** for engineering analysis:

#### **A. Atmospheric Sub-Cycle (Evaporation → Condensation → Precipitation)**
1. **Solar radiation** heats surface water bodies, providing the **latent heat of vaporization (≈ 2.45 × 10⁶ J/kg at 20°C)**.
2. Water molecules gain kinetic energy and escape as **water vapour** (Evaporation).
3. **Advection** carries the moist air mass upward and horizontally.
4. At higher altitudes, lower temperatures cause **condensation** around condensation nuclei (dust, salt, pollutants), forming cloud droplets (typical diameter **10–100 μm**).
5. When droplets exceed terminal velocity (≈ **5–7 m/s** for raindrops > 2 mm), **precipitation** occurs.

#### **B. Land Surface Sub-Cycle (Precipitation → Interception → Depression Storage → Runoff)**
1. Precipitation reaches vegetation → part is **intercepted** (held on leaves).
2. Excess drips as **throughfall**; some flows down stems as **stemflow**.
3. Water accumulates in **depression storage** (puddles, hollows).
4. When intensity > infiltration capacity → **Hortonian overland flow** (runoff).
5. Runoff concentrates in rills → gullies → streams → rivers → ocean.

> [!NOTE]
> **Horton's Infiltration Concept:** The infiltration capacity $f_c$ decays exponentially with time: $f_c(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt}$, where $f_0$ is initial capacity, $f_c$ is final (steady) capacity, and $k$ is a decay constant.

#### **C. Subsurface Sub-Cycle (Infiltration → Percolation → Groundwater Flow)**
1. Water enters soil via **infiltration**, governed by soil texture, moisture, and vegetation.
2. Water percolates through the **vadose (unsaturated) zone** to the **water table**.
3. In the **saturated zone (phreatic zone)**, water moves as **Darcian flow**: $q = -K \cdot \nabla h$, where $K$ is hydraulic conductivity.
4. Groundwater discharges to streams as **baseflow**, springs, or directly to oceans.

#### **D. Plant-Atmosphere Sub-Cycle (Transpiration → Evapotranspiration)**
- Plants absorb groundwater through roots, transport via xylem, and release ~**90%** of absorbed water as transpiration.
- **Transpiration ratio** (water used per unit biomass) varies from **300–1000** for crops.
- Total **ET** for a watershed is estimated by Penman-Monteith, Thornthwaite, or Hargreaves equations.

#### **E. Ocean-Atmosphere Exchange**
- ~**86%** of global evaporation is from oceans; ~**78%** of global precipitation falls back on oceans.
- Net **atmospheric moisture transport from ocean to land ≈ 40,000 km³/year** — this is the *freshwater generator* for continental hydrology.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 Examinations**

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Water Budget (Mass Balance)** | $P = R + ET + \Delta S$ | mm or m | Watershed / catchment analysis |
| 2 | **Runoff Coefficient** | $C = \frac{R}{P}$ | dimensionless | Rational method, urban hydrology |
| 3 | **Residence Time** | $T_r = \frac{V}{Q}$ | years / days | Reservoir / global water body turnover |
| 4 | **Infiltration (Horton)** | $f_c(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c)\,e^{-kt}$ | mm/hr | Storm runoff prediction |
| 5 | **Infiltration (Philip)** | $f(t) = \frac{1}{2}\,S\,t^{-1/2} + K$ | mm/hr | Cumulative infiltration analysis |
| 6 | **Darcy's Law** | $Q = -K \cdot A \cdot \frac{dh}{dl}$ | m³/s | Groundwater flow |
| 7 | **Continuity Equation** | $I - O = \frac{dS}{dt}$ | m³/s | Storage routing |
| 8 | **Clausius-Clapeyron (saturation VP)** | $\frac{de_s}{e_s} = \frac{L}{R_v T^2}\,dT$ | Pa/K | Climate change impact on ET |
| 9 | **Energy Available for Evaporation** | $E = \frac{R_n - G}{\lambda \rho_w}$ | mm/day | Penman-Monteith method |
| 10 | **Rational Formula** | $Q_p = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | m³/s | Peak runoff (small catchments) |
| 11 | **Mean Areal Precipitation (Thiessen)** | $\bar{P} = \frac{\sum P_i A_i}{\sum A_i}$ | mm | Spatial rainfall averaging |
| 12 | **ET (Thornthwaite)** | $PET = 16 \left( \frac{10\,T}{I} \right)^a \cdot K$ | mm/month | Climate-based ET estimate |

Where:
- $P$ = Precipitation, $R$ = Runoff, $ET$ = Evapotranspiration, $\Delta S$ = Change in storage
- $V$ = Reservoir volume, $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate out
- $K$ = Hydraulic conductivity, $h$ = Hydraulic head, $A$ = Cross-sectional area
- $L$ = Latent heat of vaporization (**2.45 × 10⁶ J/kg**), $R_v$ = Specific gas constant for water vapour
- $R_n$ = Net radiation, $G$ = Soil heat flux, $\lambda$ = Latent heat, $\rho_w$ = Water density
- $C$ = Runoff coefficient, $I$ = Rainfall intensity, $A$ = Drainage area

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Hydrologic Cycle Matters in Engineering:**
> - **Rainwater Harvesting Design:** The cycle quantifies how much water can be intercepted and stored from atmospheric inputs.
> - **Flood and Drought Management:** Understanding residence time and storage dynamics helps design dams, levees, and reservoirs.
> - **Climate-Resilient Infrastructure:** Engineers use Clausius-Clapeyron relations to predict that a 1°C warming increases atmospheric moisture-holding capacity by **~7%** (amplifying extreme precipitation).
> - **Groundwater Recharge Estimation:** Infiltration and percolation rates directly inform recharge well design.
> - **Watershed Modelling:** SWAT, HEC-HMS, and MIKE models are all built on cycle-based mass balance equations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Water Budget Equation (Continuity Equation for a Catchment)

**Starting Principle:** The principle of conservation of mass applied to a defined control volume (a watershed or catchment) over a specified time period.

**Step 1 — Identify the Control Volume and Time Interval**

Consider a watershed with areal extent $A$ (in km² or m²) and a time interval $\Delta t$ (seconds, days, or years).

**Step 2 — Identify All Inflows and Outflows**

**Inflows to the system:**
- $P$ = Precipitation (rain + snow + ice) on the catchment
- $I_{gw,in}$ = Lateral groundwater inflow from adjacent basins (usually negligible in isolated analysis)
- $I_{sw,in}$ = Surface water inflow from upstream (for sub-catchment analysis)

**Outflows from the system:**
- $R$ = Surface runoff leaving the catchment at the outlet
- $ET$ = Evapotranspiration losses
- $G_{out}$ = Groundwater outflow (deep percolation leaving the basin)
- $D$ = Diversions / abstractions (human use)

**Step 3 — Apply Conservation of Mass**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conservation of Mass Statement:**
> *Total Inflow − Total Outflow = Change in Storage*

$$
\text{Inflows} - \text{Outflows} = \Delta S
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Hydrologic Variables**

$$
P + I_{gw,in} + I_{sw,in} - R - ET - G_{out} - D = \Delta S
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify (Most Common Engineering Form)**

For a closed watershed with no major inter-basin transfers and ignoring anthropogenic abstractions:

$$
P - R - ET - G_{out} = \Delta S
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to Standard KTU Form**

$$
P = R + ET + G_{out} + \Delta S
$$

**Step 7 — Annual / Long-Term Steady-State Simplification**

Over a long period (typically 1 year or more), the change in storage $\Delta S \approx 0$ because the system returns to a similar state each year (closed annual cycle). Thus:

$$
P = R + ET + G_{out}
$$

If deep groundwater losses are small compared to surface and atmospheric fluxes:

$$
\boxed{P = R + ET}
$$

This is the **most commonly used form in KTU examinations** for long-term catchment water balance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Residence Time (Mean Turnover Time)

**Step 1 — Definition of Residence Time**

The **residence time** $T_r$ is the average time a water molecule spends in a given reservoir before leaving it.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Definition**

$$
T_r = \frac{\text{Volume of water in the reservoir}}{\text{Rate of outflow from the reservoir}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Variables**

$$
T_r = \frac{V}{Q}
$$

Where $V$ is the reservoir volume (m³ or km³) and $Q$ is the volumetric outflow rate (m³/s or km³/year).

**Step 4 — Numerical Examples (KTU Standard Values)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Global Water Residence Times (from standard hydrology texts):**

| Reservoir | Approx. Volume | Outflow Rate | Residence Time |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|----------------|
| Oceans | 1.338 × 10⁹ km³ | 505,000 km³/yr | ~2,650 years |
| Glaciers | 2.4 × 10⁷ km³ | 2,500 km³/yr | ~9,600 years |
| Atmosphere | 12,900 km³ | 505,000 km³/yr | ~9 days |
| Lakes | 90,000 km³ | Variable | ~1–100 years |
| Groundwater (active) | 8.3 × 10⁶ km³ | ~16,000 km³/yr | ~500 years |

**Step 5 — Worked Example: Atmospheric Residence Time**

Given:
- Volume of water in atmosphere, $V_{atm} = 12,900 \text{ km}^3$
- Global precipitation rate, $Q = 505,000 \text{ km}^3/\text{year}$

$$
T_r = \frac{V_{atm}}{Q} = \frac{12{,}900 \text{ km}^3}{505{,}000 \text{ km}^3/\text{year}}
$$

$$
T_r = 0.02554 \text{ years} \approx 9.32 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_r \approx 9 \text{ days}}
$$

This tells us the atmosphere "renews" its entire water content every **9 to 10 days** — explaining why rainfall forecasting works on short timescales.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sub-Cycle Flux Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Water Flux Distribution (Balancing the System):**

| Flux | Ocean Source (km³/yr) | Land Source (km³/yr) | Total (km³/yr) |
|------|----------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| Evaporation (E) | 434,000 | 71,000 | **505,000** |
| Precipitation (P) | 391,000 | 114,000 | **505,000** |
| Net Transport (Ocean→Land) | +43,000 | −43,000 | 0 (balanced) |
| Runoff (R) back to ocean | — | 43,000 | 43,000 |
| ET from land | — | 71,000 | 71,000 |
| P on land | — | 114,000 | 114,000 |

**Verification of Land Mass Balance:**

$$
P_{land} = ET_{land} + R
$$

$$
114{,}000 = 71{,}000 + 43{,}000 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Verification of Ocean Mass Balance:**

$$
E_{ocean} = P_{ocean} + R
$$

$$
434{,}000 = 391{,}000 + 43{,}000 \quad \checkmark
$$

This demonstrates the **global cycle is perfectly balanced**, with rivers and groundwater carrying the **43,000 km³/yr** freshwater deficit from land back to the ocean.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Complete Hydrologic Cycle Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SOLAR RADIATION - Energy Source] --> B[OCEANS - 97.25% of Earth Water]
    B -->|Evaporation 434000 km3 per year| C[ATMOSPHERE - Water Vapour]
    C -->|Advection by wind| D[CLOUD FORMATION - Condensation]
    D -->|Precipitation 505000 km3 per year| E[LAND SURFACE]
    E -->|Interception| F[VEGETATION CANOPY]
    E -->|Depression Storage| G[RUNOFF GENERATION]
    E -->|Infiltration| H[UNSATURATED SOIL ZONE]
    F -->|Throughfall and Stemflow| H
    H -->|Percolation| I[SATURATED ZONE - Groundwater Aquifer]
    G -->|Surface Flow| J[RIVERS AND STREAMS]
    I -->|Baseflow Discharge| J
    I -->|Capillary Rise| K[PLANT ROOTS]
    K -->|Transpiration| C
    E -->|Soil Evaporation| C
    B -->|Open Water Evaporation| C
    J -->|Returns to Ocean| B
    C -->|Precipitation over Ocean 391000 km3 per year| B
    L[GROUNDWATER - 30.1 percent of Freshwater] -->|Deep Percolation| B
    M[GLACIERS AND ICE CAPS - 68.7 percent of Freshwater] -->|Melting| B
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Engineering Functional Architecture for Rainwater Harvesting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Atmospheric Input Stage]
        A1[Precipitation] --> A2[Quality and Quantity Assessment]
    end
    subgraph CAPTURE[Catchment Stage]
        A2 --> B1[Roof Catchment]
        A2 --> B2[Land Catchment]
        B1 --> B3[Gutters and Downpipes]
        B2 --> B4[Surface Runoff Channels]
    end
    subgraph TREATMENT[Conveyance and Treatment]
        B3 --> C1[First Flush Diverter]
        B4 --> C2[Sedimentation Tank]
        C1 --> C3[Filtration Unit]
        C2 --> C3
    end
    subgraph STORAGE[Storage Stage]
        C3 --> D1[Storage Tank]
        C3 --> D2[Recharge Pit]
        D1 --> D3[Distribution System]
        D2 --> D4[Aquifer Recharge]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[End Use Stage]
        D3 --> E1[Domestic Use]
        D3 --> E2[Gardening]
        D4 --> E3[Groundwater Recharge]
    end
    subgraph LOSSES[System Losses]
        A2 -.Evaporation.-> F1[Atmospheric Loss]
        C1 -.Spillover.-> F2[Drainage Loss]
    end
    INPUT -.Precipitation.-> CAPTURE
    CAPTURE -.Runoff.-> TREATMENT
    TREATMENT -.Treated Water.-> STORAGE
    STORAGE -.Supply.-> OUTPUT
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description of Sub-Cycle Interactions

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation of the Diagram:**
> - The **outer loop** (Ocean → Atmosphere → Land → Ocean) represents the **global cycle** at planetary scale.
> - The **inner loops** (Evaporation ↔ Transpiration ↔ Atmosphere, Runoff ↔ Groundwater ↔ River) represent **local sub-cycles** that engineers manipulate through rainwater harvesting.
> - The **reservoir blocks** (Glaciers, Groundwater, Oceans) act as **buffers** that release water slowly, ensuring year-round supply.
> - The **flux arrows** correspond to the numerical values in the **Analytical Matrix** (Section 3.3).
> - In **rainwater harvesting engineering**, we amplify the downward fluxes (infiltration, percolation, groundwater recharge) and reduce evaporative losses using covered storage.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**Define the hydrologic cycle. List any four components of the cycle with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Expected 80–100 words):**

The **hydrologic cycle** is the continuous circulation of water in all its phases (liquid, vapour, and solid) within the Earth's hydrosphere, driven by **solar energy** and **gravity**. It is a **closed system** with no beginning or end, in which water is perpetually recycled through the oceans, atmosphere, land surface, and subsurface.

**Four Components:**
1. **Evaporation** — Phase change of water from liquid to vapour from oceans, lakes, and soil due to solar heating. **(1 mark)**
2. **Condensation** — Conversion of water vapour into liquid droplets in the atmosphere, forming clouds. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Precipitation** — Release of condensed water as rain, snow, sleet, or hail onto the Earth's surface. **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Runoff** — Lateral flow of water over the land surface through streams, rivers, and lakes back to the oceans. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often **omit the driving forces (solar energy + gravity)** in the definition. Always include these to secure full marks. KTU evaluators specifically look for the *energy source* in cycle definitions.

---

#### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**Distinguish between Evaporation, Transpiration, and Evapotranspiration. State one measurement method for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular format preferred):**

| Process | Definition | Measurement Method |
|---------|------------|-------------------|
| **Evaporation** | Loss of water as vapour from *open water bodies* and *soil surfaces*. | **Class-A Evaporation Pan** (standard WMO method). **(1 mark)** |
| **Transpiration** | Loss of water as vapour from *living plants* through stomatal openings. | **Lysimeter** (weighing the soil-plant system). **(1 mark)** |
| **Evapotranspiration** | Combined loss of water from *both* evaporation and transpiration (E + T) from a vegetated surface. | **Penman-Monteith equation** (FAO-56 standard). **(1 mark)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students write "ET is the same as evaporation" — this is a **common error costing 1–2 marks**. Always clarify that ET = E + T, and that the methods are different. Naming the correct instrument (e.g., lysimeter for transpiration) is mandatory for full marks.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Question 1A]`

**a)** Derive the **water budget equation** for a watershed. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A small rural catchment of area **5 km²** receives an average annual precipitation of **1200 mm**. The annual runoff measured at the outlet is **2.5 × 10⁶ m³**, and the change in groundwater storage over the year is observed to be **+0.5 × 10⁶ m³** (recharge). Estimate the **annual evapotranspiration loss** from the catchment, assuming negligible deep percolation and abstractions. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Water Budget Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Define the Control Volume and Mass Conservation Principle (2 marks)**

Consider a closed watershed of area $A$ as the control volume. Apply the **law of conservation of mass** over a finite time interval $\Delta t$:

$$
\text{Net inflow} - \text{Net outflow} = \text{Change in storage}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Hydrologic Fluxes (2 marks)**

Inflows to the watershed:
- $P$ = Precipitation falling on the catchment (mm)
- $I_s$ = Surface inflow from upstream (negligible for closed basin)

Outflows from the watershed:
- $R$ = Surface runoff at the outlet (mm)
- $ET$ = Evapotranspiration losses (mm)
- $G_o$ = Subsurface (deep) groundwater outflow (mm)
- $D$ = Anthropogenic diversions (mm)

Change in storage:
- $\Delta S$ = Net change in surface + soil + groundwater storage (mm)

**Step 3 — Substitute and Form the Balance Equation (2 marks)**

$$
P + I_s - R - ET - G_o - D = \Delta S
$$

For a **closed basin** ($I_s = 0$, $D = 0$, $G_o \approx 0$):

$$
P - R - ET = \Delta S
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{P = R + ET + \Delta S}
$$

**Step 4 — State the Assumptions (1 mark)**

- The watershed is a **closed control volume** with no inter-basin transfers.
- All fluxes are expressed in **depth units (mm)** for uniform comparison.
- $\Delta S$ represents the **net change in storage** of soil moisture, surface water, and groundwater combined.
- Time interval is **long enough** to include complete wet and dry periods (typically 1 year).
- Human abstractions and deep aquifer leakage are **negligible**.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Catchment area, $A = 5 \text{ km}^2 = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$
- Annual precipitation, $P = 1200 \text{ mm} = 1.2 \text{ m}$
- Annual runoff volume, $V_R = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$
- Change in storage, $\Delta S = +0.5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$
- $G_o = 0$, $D = 0$

**Step 1 — Convert Runoff Volume to Depth (2 marks)**

$$
R = \frac{V_R}{A} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3}{5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2}
$$

$$
R = 0.5 \text{ m} = 500 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Storage Change to Depth (1 mark)**

$$
\Delta S = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3}{5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2} = 0.1 \text{ m} = 100 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Water Budget Equation (3 marks)**

$$
P = R + ET + \Delta S
$$

$$
ET = P - R - \Delta S
$$

$$
ET = 1200 - 500 - 100
$$

$$
\boxed{ET = 600 \text{ mm/year}}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark)**

The catchment loses **600 mm of water annually** to evapotranspiration. This represents **50%** of the total precipitation — typical of a vegetated, semi-humid catchment in Kerala. The remaining **500 mm leaves as runoff**, and **100 mm is stored** (likely in the aquifer).

---

#### **Question B.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Main Question 1B]`

**a)** Explain the **concept of residence time** in the hydrologic cycle. Compute the residence time of water in the **global oceans** and in the **atmosphere** using the following data: Ocean volume = **1.338 × 10⁹ km³**, annual evaporation from oceans = **434,000 km³/yr**, atmospheric water content = **12,900 km³**, global precipitation = **505,000 km³/yr**. **(7 marks)**

**b)** What are the **major impacts of human activities** on the hydrologic cycle? Explain with at least **four examples** in the Indian context. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Residence Time Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Concept of Residence Time (2 marks)**

The **residence time** ($T_r$) of water in a reservoir is the **average time** a water molecule remains in that reservoir before being removed. It quantifies the **turnover rate** of water in a given storage compartment. The formula is:

$$
T_r = \frac{\text{Volume of reservoir (V)}}{\text{Rate of outflow (Q)}}
$$

**Step 2 — Ocean Residence Time (2 marks)**

Given:
- $V_{ocean} = 1.338 \times 10^9 \text{ km}^3$
- $Q_{ocean} = 434{,}000 \text{ km}^3/\text{year}$ (evaporation is the dominant outflow)

$$
T_{r,\,ocean} = \frac{1.338 \times 10^9 \text{ km}^3}{434{,}000 \text{ km}^3/\text{year}}
$$

$$
T_{r,\,ocean} = 3082.9 \text{ years}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{r,\,ocean} \approx 3{,}083 \text{ years}}
$$

**Step 3 — Atmospheric Residence Time (2 marks)**

Given:
- $V_{atm} = 12{,}900 \text{ km}^3$
- $Q_{atm} = 505{,}000 \text{ km}^3/\text{year}$ (precipitation is the dominant outflow)

$$
T_{r,\,atm} = \frac{12{,}900 \text{ km}^3}{505{,}000 \text{ km}^3/\text{year}}
$$

$$
T_{r,\,atm} = 0.02554 \text{ years} \approx 9.32 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{r,\,atm} \approx 9 \text{ days}}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark)**

The ocean's residence time is **~3000 years** (very slow turnover, vast reservoir), while the atmosphere's is only **~9 days** (very fast turnover). This is why **climate change signals** appear quickly in atmospheric measurements but take centuries to manifest in deep ocean warming.

---

#### **Part (b) — Human Impacts on the Hydrologic Cycle (7 Marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Human activities have significantly perturbed the natural hydrologic cycle, particularly through **land-use change, urbanization, fossil fuel combustion, and over-extraction of groundwater**.

**Four Major Impacts in the Indian Context:**

1. **Urbanization and Reduced Infiltration (2 marks):** Paving of surfaces in cities (e.g., Bengaluru, Mumbai) has reduced infiltration and increased surface runoff, causing **urban flooding**. Rainwater flows to drains instead of recharging aquifers, depleting groundwater.

2. **Deforestation and Reduced Transpiration (2 marks):** Large-scale deforestation in the **Western Ghats and Northeast India** has reduced transpiration and interception, altered local rainfall patterns, and increased soil erosion. This has been linked to reduced monsoon intensity in interior Karnataka and Kerala.

3. **Groundwater Over-extraction (1 mark):** India's groundwater extraction (~**251 km³/year**) far exceeds recharge, causing water tables to fall at **0.3–1.0 m/year** in Punjab, Haryana, and Tamil Nadu. This violates the natural steady-state assumption of $\Delta S = 0$ and converts "renewable" water into a mined resource.

4. **Climate Change and Intensified Cycle (2 marks):** Rising global temperatures (~**+1.1°C** since pre-industrial era) increase atmospheric moisture-holding capacity by ~7% per °C (Clausius-Clapeyron), causing **more intense cloudbursts** (e.g., Kerala 2018, Uttarakhand 2013, Chennai 2015) and longer dry spells — an example of "weather whiplash."

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Part (b) Specifically:**
> - Students often give **generic answers** like "pollution affects water" — this is too vague for KTU 2024 scheme, which demands **India-specific examples**.
> - Always include **quantitative values** (e.g., groundwater extraction rate, temperature rise) to demonstrate conceptual depth.
> - Mentioning **monsoon disruption in the Western Ghats** or **specific flood events** earns bonus credit in KTU valuation.
> - The conclusion must connect back to the **need for rainwater harvesting** (since this is the parent course).

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1**

**1. Core Concepts to Memorize:**
- ☑ Hydrologic cycle = continuous, closed, solar-driven, gravity-assisted, mass-conserving.
- ☑ Global water = **1.386 × 10⁹ km³** (97.25% saline, 2.75% fresh).
- ☑ Global annual precipitation ≈ **505,000 km³/year**.
- ☑ Latent heat of vaporization of water = **2.45 × 10⁶ J/kg** at 20°C.

**2. Must-Know Equations:**
- ☑ Water Budget: $P = R + ET + \Delta S$ (long-term: $P = R + ET$).
- ☑ Residence Time: $T_r = \frac{V}{Q}$.
- ☑ Runoff Coefficient: $C = \frac{R}{P}$.
- ☑ Horton's Infiltration: $f_c(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c)\,e^{-kt}$.
- ☑ Darcy's Law: $Q = -K \cdot A \cdot \frac{dh}{dl}$.

**3. Key Numerical Values for Exams:**
- ☑ Ocean residence time ≈ **3,000 years** (use 2,650–3,083 in different sources).
- ☑ Atmospheric residence time ≈ **9 days**.
- ☑ Freshwater distribution: Glaciers (68.7%) > Groundwater (30.1%) > Surface (1.2%).
- ☑ Clausius-Clapeyron rate: **~7% more moisture per °C warming**.

**4. Common Sub-Cycles to Describe:**
- ☑ Atmospheric (E → C → P)
- ☑ Surface (P → Runoff → Stream)
- ☑ Subsurface (P → Infiltration → GW → Stream)
- ☑ Vegetative (P → Plant → Transpiration)
- ☑ Oceanic (Ocean → E → Atmosphere → Ocean)

**5. Frequently Confused Pairs (Avoid These in Exams):**
- ⚠ Evaporation vs. Transpiration vs. Evapotranspiration — define **all three** explicitly.
- ⚠ Infiltration vs. Percolation — Infiltration is into soil; Percolation is into aquifer.
- ⚠ Runoff vs. Streamflow — Runoff includes overland + interflow; Streamflow is in channels.
- ⚠ Potential ET (PET) vs. Actual ET (AET) — PET is demand, AET is supply-constrained.

**6. India-Specific Points for Full Marks:**
- ☑ India receives ~**4,000 km³ of precipitation annually**, of which ~**1,869 km³ is available** as surface + groundwater.
- ☑ Kerala's two monsoon systems: **Southwest (June–Sept) and Northeast (Oct–Dec)**.
- ☑ Reference to **Clausius-Clapeyron** when discussing cloudbursts (e.g., Kerala 2018, Wayanad landslides).
- ☑ Rooftop Rainwater Harvesting (RRWH) is **mandatory** for buildings >100 m² in many Kerala municipalities.

> **🎯 Final Tip:** KTU 2024 questions often combine **definition + numerical + Indian context** in a single Part B question. Memorize both the **equations and the standard global values** — marks are awarded for numbers, not just concepts!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages of Rainwater
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advantages of Rainwater

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rainwater Harvesting (RWH):** The deliberate collection, conveyance, storage, treatment, and utilization of precipitation runoff from rooftops, land surfaces, or catchments for productive use, groundwater recharge, or direct application — thereby reducing dependence on conventional municipal/groundwater sources.

The **advantages of rainwater** refer to the spectrum of hydrological, economic, environmental, and social benefits derived from capturing, storing, and reusing precipitation that would otherwise be lost as **stormwater runoff**, evaporation, or non-beneficial flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic forms the foundational rationale of Module 1 — establishing *why* rainwater harvesting (RWH) is a critical intervention in modern water-resource engineering. Examiners frequently test this as a "definition + enumeration" question in Part A.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a household budget where your monthly salary (municipal water supply) is uncertain and your backup savings (groundwater via borewells) are depleting fast. **Rainwater harvesting is like collecting loose change every time it rains** — small, free, and abundant. Each drop that falls on your rooftop is essentially a "penny from heaven" that, if captured in a tank or allowed to percolate, adds to your water savings without a single electricity bill (no pumping) or monthly tariff.

In a larger landscape analogy: think of a city as a giant sponge. When the sponge is dry (depleted aquifers, paved surfaces), rainwater runs off instantly causing floods. **RWH re-wets the sponge**, allowing water to be stored where it falls.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Rainfall intensity (i)** — typically measured in **mm/hr** or **mm/day**
- **Catchment coefficient (C)** — dimensionless runoff factor
- **Annual rainfall (P)** — in **mm/year** (Kerala average: ~**3000 mm/year**)
- **Storage capacity (V)** — usually expressed in **litres** or **m³**

> [!NOTE]
> Kerala receives the second-highest rainfall in India. The Central Ground Water Board (CGWB) reports a **replenishable groundwater resource of ~6.92 BCM** in Kerala, of which only a fraction is currently recharged through systematic RWH.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Decomposition of Advantages

The advantages of rainwater can be organized into **five major domains**, each addressing a unique engineering, social, or ecological concern.

#### A. Environmental Advantages
- Reduces **stormwater runoff** and the consequent urban flooding.
- Mitigates **soil erosion** and topsoil loss in catchments.
- Recharges **groundwater aquifers**, raising the static water table.
- Reduces **load on storm-water drainage systems**, lowering municipal infrastructure stress.
- Decreases **non-point source pollution** in receiving water bodies.

#### B. Economic Advantages
- **Zero raw-water cost** — precipitation is a free, renewable input.
- Reduces **electricity consumption** for pumping (typically **0.5 to 1.5 kWh per 1000 litres** pumped from deep borewells).
- Lowers **monthly water bills** for households and institutions.
- Avoids **capital cost of laying new pipelines** in remote or hilly areas.
- Increases **property value** of buildings with RWH installations.

#### C. Social Advantages
- Provides **water security** in water-scarce regions and during droughts.
- Enables **decentralized water access** in rural, tribal, and coastal communities.
- Promotes **community participation** in water stewardship.
- Enhances **resilience** during monsoonal disruptions or natural calamities.

#### D. Technical / Engineering Advantages
- **High water quality** — rainwater is naturally soft, low in dissolved solids, and free of chlorine.
- Reduces **scale formation** in plumbing fixtures and geysers.
- Compatible with both **rooftop systems** and **surface catchment systems**.
- Easily integrable with **existing building plumbing** using first-flush diverters.
- Supports **dual plumbing** systems (non-potable use).

#### E. Strategic / Policy Advantages
- Aligns with the **National Water Policy (2012)** and **Jal Shakti Abhiyan**.
- Helps achieve **UN SDG 6** (Clean Water and Sanitation) and **SDG 11** (Sustainable Cities).
- Earns **green-building credits** under **GRIHA** and **IGBC** rating systems.
- Contributes to **carbon footprint reduction** by lowering energy-intensive pumping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these advantages matter in KTU exams:** The board examiner expects students to correlate advantages with the *underlying engineering principle*. For instance, do not merely state "reduces runoff" — always connect it to *why* (decreased imperviousness effect, reduced urban heat, aquifer recharge potential).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| **Domain** | **Key Concept** | **Standard Unit / Metric** | **Engineering Utility** |
|------------|-----------------|----------------------------|-------------------------|
| Water Quality | TDS of rainwater | **10–50 mg/L** | Soft water, low scaling |
| Cost Saving | Pump energy saved | **0.5–1.5 kWh/1000 L** | Reduced OPEX |
| Groundwater | Recharge volume per m² roof | **0.8 × Annual Rainfall (mm) × Area (m²)** | Recharge pit design |
| Runoff Reduction | Coefficient of runoff (C) | **0.8–0.9** (paved roof) | Peak flow estimation |
| Storage Design | First-flush volume | **2 L per m² of roof area** | First-flush diverter sizing |
| Recharge | Aquifer yield boost | **10–30%** in pilot zones | Water table recovery |
| Carbon | CO₂ avoided | **~0.5 kg CO₂/kWh** replaced | Sustainability reporting |

> [!NOTE]
> The **runoff coefficient C** represents the dimensionless ratio of rainwater that flows off a surface to the total rainfall received. For tiled rooftops, C typically lies in the range **0.7–0.9**; for concrete surfaces, **0.8–0.95**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In urban Kerala (e.g., Kochi, Thiruvananthapuram), rooftop RWH systems are now **mandatory for buildings exceeding 100 m²** under the Kerala Municipal Building Rules. In rural Rajasthan, **tanka systems** (underground storage) have revived villages in arid zones. Globally, **Tokyo mandates RWH for new buildings > 30 m²**, and **Singapore's PUB** treats rainwater as a primary resource in its "Four National Taps" strategy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Analytical Framework — Linking Advantages to Physical Principles

Since this topic is **conceptual-evaluative** rather than purely mathematical, the derivation here uses a structured **Cause → Mechanism → Benefit** chain to formalize each advantage. This is the standard approach expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

#### Step 1: Establish the Hydrological Balance

The classical water balance for any catchment is given by:

$$P = ET + R + I + \Delta S$$

Where:
- $P$ = **Precipitation** (mm)
- $ET$ = **Evapotranspiration** (mm)
- $R$ = **Surface runoff** (mm)
- $I$ = **Infiltration** (mm)
- $\Delta S$ = **Change in soil/groundwater storage** (mm)

**Conversion logic:** Rainfall input is partitioned into four competing outflows. Conventional urban development maximizes $R$ (runoff) and minimizes $I$ (infiltration), causing the imbalance we observe in flood-prone cities.

#### Step 2: Derive the Effect of RWH on Each Term

**Step 2a — On Runoff (R):**
By capturing rooftop runoff in storage tanks, the term $R$ decreases. For a roof area $A$ (m²) and rainfall depth $P$ (mm):

$$V_{collected} = C \times A \times P$$

where $V_{collected}$ is in litres, $C$ is the runoff coefficient, $A$ is in m², and $P$ is in mm.

**Conversion logic:** Since 1 mm of rain on 1 m² yields 1 litre of water, the collected volume is $A \times P$ litres, reduced by the coefficient $C$ to account for losses (wind drift, evaporation from the wet surface, splash-out).

**Step 2b — On Infiltration (I):**
When excess harvested water is diverted to a recharge pit, $I$ increases. The recharge volume is:

$$V_{recharge} = Q \times t \times A_{pit}$$

Where $Q$ is the infiltration rate (m/hr), $t$ is the duration (hr), and $A_{pit}$ is the recharge pit bottom area (m²).

**Step 2c — On Storage (ΔS):**
Both surface tanks and aquifer storage contribute positively to $\Delta S$.

#### Step 3: Quantitative Benefit Estimation

Assuming a typical KTU problem scenario:

- Roof area $A = 100 \text{ m}²$
- Annual rainfall $P = 3000 \text{ mm}$ (Kerala average)
- Runoff coefficient $C = 0.85$

Then:

$$V_{collected} = 0.85 \times 100 \times 3000$$

$$V_{collected} = 255{,}000 \text{ litres/year}$$

**Conversion logic:** With 255,000 litres harvested annually, a 5-member family using 135 LPCD (litres per capita per day) consumes:

$$\text{Annual demand} = 5 \times 135 \times 365 = 246{,}375 \text{ litres/year}$$

Therefore, RWH can theoretically meet **~100% of domestic non-potable needs** for this household — illustrating the scale of benefit.

#### Step 4: Monetary Benefit Derivation

- Saved pumping energy: 246,375 L ÷ 1000 L × 1 kWh = **~246 kWh/year**
- Cost saving (₹8/kWh): **₹1968/year** (electricity)
- Municipal water bill avoided (₹25/kL for 100 kL/year): **₹2500/year**
- **Total annual saving ≈ ₹4500** for a 100 m² roof.

#### Step 5: Environmental Benefit Translation

- CO₂ avoided: 246 kWh × 0.5 kg CO₂/kWh = **123 kg CO₂/year**
- This is equivalent to planting **~6 tree seedlings** annually.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs. Sustainability Pillars

| **Advantage** | **Economic** | **Environmental** | **Social** | **Technical** | **Strategic** |
|---------------|:------------:|:-----------------:|:----------:|:-------------:|:-------------:|
| Lowers water bills | ✔ | — | ✔ | — | — |
| Recharges aquifers | — | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Reduces flooding | — | ✔ | ✔ | — | ✔ |
| Saves energy | ✔ | ✔ | — | ✔ | — |
| Decentralizes supply | ✔ | — | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Soft water quality | ✔ | — | — | ✔ | — |
| GRIHA/IGBC credits | ✔ | ✔ | — | — | ✔ |
| Climate resilience | — | ✔ | ✔ | — | ✔ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Categorization of Rainwater Advantages

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions and dependencies** between the five domains of advantages discussed in Section 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rainwater Harvesting System] --> B[Environmental Benefits]
    A --> C[Economic Benefits]
    A --> D[Social Benefits]
    A --> E[Technical Benefits]
    A --> F[Strategic Benefits]

    B --> B1[Reduces Stormwater Runoff]
    B --> B2[Recharges Groundwater Aquifer]
    B --> B3[Prevents Soil Erosion]
    B --> B4[Lower Urban Flood Risk]

    C --> C1[Zero Raw Water Cost]
    C --> C2[Reduced Electricity Bill]
    C --> C3[Lower Pipeline CAPEX]
    C --> C4[Higher Property Value]

    D --> D1[Drought Resilience]
    D --> D2[Rural Water Security]
    D --> D3[Community Empowerment]
    D --> D4[Decentralized Access]

    E --> E1[Soft Water Quality]
    E --> E2[Easy Integration with Plumbing]
    E --> E3[Dual Plumbing Compatibility]
    E --> E4[Modular Scalable Design]

    F --> F1[Aligns with National Water Policy]
    F --> F2[UN SDG 6 and 11 Compliance]
    F --> F3[GRIHA and IGBC Green Credits]
    F --> F4[Carbon Footprint Reduction]

    B2 --> G[Rising Water Table]
    C2 --> G
    D1 --> G
    F2 --> G

    G --> H[Long Term Water Security]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Cause-Effect Chain of Rainwater Benefits

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Precipitation Event] --> S[Surface Catchment]
    S --> Q[Quality Treatment First Flush]
    Q --> T[Storage Tank]
    T --> U1[Direct Use Potable or Non Potable]
    T --> U2[Recharge Pit]
    U2 --> A[Aquifer Recharge]
    U1 --> B[End Use Savings]
    A --> W[Water Table Rise]
    B --> X[Economic Benefit]
    W --> Y[Environmental Benefit]
    X --> Z[Sustainable Water Future]
    Y --> Z
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Stakeholder Benefit Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RWH[Rainwater Harvesting System] --> FAM[Household Family]
    RWH --> COM[Local Community]
    RWH --> MUN[Municipal Authority]
    RWH --> ECO[Local Ecosystem]

    FAM --> FAM1[Lower Bills]
    FAM --> FAM2[Water Independence]

    COM --> COM1[Shared Recharge]
    COM --> COM2[Flood Mitigation]

    MUN --> MUN1[Reduced Infrastructure Load]
    MUN --> MUN2[Compliance Credit]

    ECO --> ECO1[Aquifer Recharge]
    ECO --> ECO2[Reduced Erosion]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Rainwater Harvesting and list any three of its major advantages. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Rainwater harvesting is the process of collecting, storing, conveying, and utilizing precipitation from rooftops, paved surfaces, or land catchments for beneficial use, groundwater recharge, or augmentation of water resources.

**Three major advantages:**

1. **Environmental benefit:** Reduces stormwater runoff and recharges groundwater aquifers, mitigating urban flooding and raising the water table.
2. **Economic benefit:** The harvested water is essentially free, eliminating raw-water cost and reducing electricity consumption for pumping (saves ~0.5–1.5 kWh per 1000 L).
3. **Social benefit:** Provides water security, especially in drought-prone and rural areas, while enabling decentralized water access.

---

#### Question 2: How does rainwater harvesting contribute to groundwater recharge? **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

RWH contributes to groundwater recharge by **diverting excess collected rainwater** through recharge pits, recharge wells, or recharge trenches. The water percolates through the soil column and recharges the underlying aquifer. This process:

- **Raises the static water table** in shallow aquifers.
- **Improves the yield** of nearby borewells.
- **Reduces soil moisture stress** for shallow-rooted vegetation.
- **Mitigates land subsidence** in over-exploited zones.

The recharge rate is governed by Darcy's law:

$$Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$$

where $K$ is hydraulic conductivity, $i$ is the hydraulic gradient, and $A$ is the cross-sectional area of flow.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Option 1):** "Discuss in detail the various advantages of rainwater harvesting across environmental, economic, social, technical, and strategic domains. Support your answer with suitable examples from Kerala." **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Environmental Advantages (7 marks)**

1. **Reduces stormwater runoff:** Paved urban surfaces prevent natural infiltration. By capturing rooftop runoff in storage tanks, peak flow into storm drains is reduced, lowering flood risk. Example: Kochi city reduced street flooding after 500+ institutions adopted RWH under the Smart City mission.
2. **Recharges groundwater aquifers:** Excess water is diverted to recharge pits, raising the water table. Kerala's CGWB data shows a **10–30% rise** in static water levels in pilot RWH villages.
3. **Prevents soil erosion and topsoil loss:** Slowing runoff velocity reduces detachment and transport of topsoil.
4. **Improves urban microclimate:** Increased local moisture and reduced imperviousness lower the **urban heat island effect**.

**(b) Economic, Social, Technical & Strategic Advantages (7 marks)**

1. **Economic:** Zero raw-water cost; a 100 m² roof in Kerala (~3000 mm rainfall) yields **~2,55,000 L/year**, saving approximately **₹4,500/year** in combined electricity and water bills.
2. **Social:** Enables decentralized water security — critical in rural Wayanad and coastal Alappuzha where piped supply is intermittent.
3. **Technical:** Naturally soft water (TDS 10–50 mg/L) reduces scaling in geysers, washing machines, and solar panels.
4. **Strategic:** Aligns with **Kerala State Building Rules** (mandatory RWH for >100 m² buildings), **National Water Policy 2012**, and **UN SDGs 6 & 11**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B Question A:** Many students write generic points without *Kerala-specific* examples. Board evaluators expect **at least one local/regional reference** (e.g., Kerala Municipal Building Rules, CGWB data, or a named district). Students lose up to 2 marks for lacking contextual anchoring.

---

#### **Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice):** "Estimate the volume of rainwater that can be harvested from a residential building with a 150 m² rooftop in a region receiving 2800 mm annual rainfall. Comment on the major advantages of this harvested water." **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Quantitative Estimation (7 marks)**

Given:
- Roof area $A = 150 \text{ m}²$
- Annual rainfall $P = 2800 \text{ mm}$
- Runoff coefficient for tiled roof $C = 0.85$

Using the standard formula:

$$V_{collected} = C \times A \times P$$

$$V_{collected} = 0.85 \times 150 \times 2800$$

$$V_{collected} = 3{,}57{,}000 \text{ litres/year}$$

Equivalently:

$$V_{collected} = 357 \text{ m}³/\text{year}$$

**Conversion logic:** 1 mm rain on 1 m² = 1 L of water. Hence 2800 mm × 150 m² = 4,20,000 L gross, reduced to 3,57,000 L after accounting for ~15% losses (evaporation, wind drift, first-flush rejection).

**Annual domestic demand for a 5-member family:**

$$\text{Demand} = 5 \times 135 \text{ LPCD} \times 365 = 2{,}46{,}375 \text{ L/year}$$

Therefore, harvested rainwater covers **~145% of total annual domestic water demand**, with a surplus of ~1,10,625 L that can be used for **groundwater recharge** or gardening.

**(b) Major Advantages of the Harvested Water (7 marks)**

1. **Soft, low-TDS quality** (TDS 10–50 mg/L) — ideal for bathing, washing, and toilet flushing.
2. **Free of chlorine and contaminants** typical of municipal supplies.
3. **Energy savings:** Avoiding pumping of 3,57,000 L saves ~357 kWh/year (~₹2,856 at ₹8/kWh).
4. **Groundwater augmentation:** Surplus water diverted to a recharge pit can raise the local water table by an estimated **0.5–1.0 m** over a year.
5. **Flood mitigation** at the micro-catchment scale.
6. **Carbon footprint reduction:** ~178 kg CO₂ avoided annually.
7. **Compliance** with Kerala State Building Rules and GRIHA/IGBC green-building norms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B Question B:** Students often forget to **apply the runoff coefficient (C)** and present the *gross* rainfall volume as the harvested volume. This results in a **1–2 mark deduction**. Always state $C$ explicitly and justify the chosen value (e.g., C = 0.85 for a tiled sloped roof).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Advantages of Rainwater**

- **Definition:** Collection + storage + use of precipitation runoff.
- **Five domains:** Environmental, Economic, Social, Technical, Strategic.
- **Key formula:** $V = C \times A \times P$ (litres/year).
- **Kerala-specific:** Average rainfall **~3000 mm/year**; mandatory RWH for buildings **>100 m²**.
- **Water quality:** TDS typically **10–50 mg/L** (soft water).
- **Energy savings:** **0.5–1.5 kWh per 1000 L** pumping avoided.
- **Runoff coefficient:** **0.7–0.9** for rooftops; **0.8–0.95** for concrete.
- **Carbon avoided:** **~0.5 kg CO₂ per kWh** replaced.
- **Policy alignment:** National Water Policy 2012, UN SDGs 6 & 11, GRIHA, IGBC.
- **Examiner tip:** Always quantify advantages with **Kerala-relevant numbers or examples** to score full marks.
- **Common pitfall:** Confusing *gross* rainfall with *harvested* volume — always apply coefficient $C$.
- **Strategic insight:** RWH is the only water source with **zero marginal cost** and **zero energy input** at the point of collection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Runoff from Catchment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Runoff from Catchment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Runoff?

**Runoff** is the portion of precipitation (rainfall, snowmelt, or irrigation water) that flows over the land surface towards streams, rivers, lakes, or other water bodies, instead of being absorbed into the ground (infiltration), evaporated back into the atmosphere (evaporation), or utilized by vegetation (transpiration).

In the context of **Rainwater Harvesting (RWH)**, runoff is the *primary raw material* — it is the water that we aim to capture, store, and recharge. Understanding what controls runoff is therefore the very first step in designing an effective RWH system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Runoff is that portion of the total precipitation that flows over the land surface to the point of concentration (stream, drain, lake, reservoir, or recharge pit) after satisfying the hydrologic losses such as interception, depression storage, evaporation, transpiration, and infiltration.*

## 1.2 What is a Catchment?

A **catchment** (also called a *watershed* or *drainage basin*) is the entire geographical area of land that contributes runoff to a single point of outlet, bounded by a topographic ridge line called the *drainage divide* or *watershed boundary*.

$$A_{catchment} = \text{Total plan area contributing runoff to the outlet (in hectares, } ha \text{ or } km^2)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The student must be able to identify, classify, and quantitatively explain the *climatic*, *physiographic*, and *hydrologic* factors that influence the volume, rate, and timing of runoff generation from a given catchment.

## 1.3 The Hydrologic Equation — The Foundation

All runoff estimation begins from the **water balance equation** of a catchment over a time period $\Delta t$:

$$P = R + ET + I + \Delta S$$

Where:
- $P$ = **Precipitation** (input)
- $R$ = **Runoff** (surface + subsurface flow to outlet)
- $ET$ = **Evapotranspiration** (loss)
- $I$ = **Infiltration** (loss to groundwater)
- $\Delta S$ = **Change in storage** (soil moisture, depression storage, channel storage)

> Therefore: $\quad R = P - (ET + I + \Delta S)$

The "Factors Affecting Runoff" are essentially the variables that determine the *magnitude of $R$* for a given $P$.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Kitchen Sink Model 🍽️

Imagine a kitchen sink (your catchment) with the tap running at a steady rate (your rainfall).

| Component in Sink Model | Real Catchment Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Water from the tap | Precipitation ($P$) |
| Water that escapes through the drain hole | Runoff ($R$) |
| Water absorbed by a sponge placed inside | Infiltration ($I$) |
| Water that evaporates from a hot surface | Evaporation ($ET$) |
| Water that clings to the sides of the sink | Depression storage ($\Delta S$) |
| Water absorbed by a plant sitting in the sink | Transpiration ($T$) |

**Key insight:** If the sponge is *large, dry, and porous* (sandy soil, dry antecedent moisture) → **less runoff**.
If the sponge is *small, wet, and clogged* (clayey soil, saturated ground, paved surface) → **more runoff**.

This simple analogy captures the essence of *every single factor* that affects runoff. The factors just *adjust the size and saturation* of the metaphorical "sponge."

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exam:**
> Remember the word **"RISE-CAMP"** → **R**ainfall, **I**nfiltration capacity, **S**lope, **E**vaporation, **C**atchment shape, **A**ntcedent moisture, **M**anagement/Land use, **P**hysiography.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Factors Affecting Runoff

The factors influencing runoff from a catchment are broadly classified into **three major categories**, as mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus:

### A. Climatic (Meteorological) Factors
These are weather-driven variables that govern the *supply* of water and the *rate of loss*.

1. **Type of Precipitation** (rain, snow, sleet, hail)
   - Rain produces the most immediate runoff. Snow accumulates and releases slowly.
2. **Rainfall Intensity ($i$, in mm/hr)**
   - Higher intensity $\Rightarrow$ runoff increases because infiltration capacity is exceeded faster.
3. **Rainfall Duration ($t$, in minutes or hours)**
   - Longer duration $\Rightarrow$ more total volume of runoff (up to a saturation limit).
4. **Rainfall Distribution (in space and time)**
   - Uniform rainfall $\neq$ Point rainfall. Centered storms over a small catchment produce *flashier* hydrographs.
5. **Direction of Storm Movement**
   - A storm moving *upstream-to-downstream* produces a **lower, broader** hydrograph.
   - A storm moving *downstream-to-upstream* produces a **higher, sharper (peaked)** hydrograph — critical for urban drainage design.
6. **Antecedent Precipitation (Prior wetness)**
   - Wet soil = less infiltration capacity remaining = more runoff.
7. **Temperature, Humidity, Wind Speed**
   - Influence the rate of **evapotranspiration**, which is a *loss* to runoff.
8. **Solar Radiation**
   - Drives the energy balance and hence ET losses.

### B. Physiographic (Physical / Basin) Factors
These are *static, time-invariant* characteristics of the catchment geometry and surface.

1. **Size (Area) of the Catchment ($A$)**
   - Larger area = more total runoff volume.
   - But **peak runoff per unit area decreases** with size (due to time-of-concentration effects).
2. **Shape of the Catchment**
   - *Fan-shaped* (semi-circular): short $T_c$, sharp peak.
   - *Elongated* (long and narrow): long $T_c$, low flat peak.
3. **Slope of the Catchment ($S$, dimensionless or %)**
   - Steeper slope $\Rightarrow$ higher runoff velocity $\Rightarrow$ less infiltration time $\Rightarrow$ more runoff.
4. **Orientation (Aspect) of the Catchment**
   - South-facing slopes (in Northern hemisphere) get more solar radiation $\Rightarrow$ more ET loss $\Rightarrow$ less runoff.
5. **Elevation and Relief**
   - Higher elevation $\Rightarrow$ cooler temperatures $\Rightarrow$ less ET $\Rightarrow$ often more runoff (also affects precipitation type).
6. **Drainage Density ($D_d$, in km/km²)**
   - $D_d = \dfrac{\text{Total length of streams}}{\text{Area of catchment}}$
   - High drainage density (well-dissected terrain) = efficient water transfer = higher peak runoff.
7. **Stream Order and Channel Network Geometry**
8. **Surface Roughness (Manning's $n$)**
   - Higher roughness (dense forest) $\Rightarrow$ slower overland flow $\Rightarrow$ more infiltration $\Rightarrow$ less runoff.
9. **Land Use / Land Cover**
   - Forest > Grassland > Agricultural > Urban (in terms of infiltration capacity, descending order).
10. **Soil Type and Geology**
    - Sandy soil: high infiltration, low runoff.
    - Clayey soil / rock outcrops: low infiltration, high runoff.
11. **Vegetation Cover**
    - Interception by canopy, root-zone infiltration enhancement, and soil binding.

### C. Hydrologic / Anthropogenic Factors
1. **Antecedent Moisture Condition (AMC)** — soil saturation prior to storm.
2. **Groundwater Table depth** — high water table reduces infiltration capacity.
3. **Lakes, ponds, and reservoirs** — act as natural flood moderators (lower peak runoff).
4. **Watershed management practices** — check dams, contour bunds, terracing, afforestation.
5. **Urbanization / Impervious surfaces** — drastically *increases* runoff (a 10% increase in impervious area can increase peak runoff by 30–50%).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **authoritative reference** for numerical problems in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Water Balance** | $P = R + ET + I + \Delta S$ | mm | Fundamental equation |
| 2 | **Runoff Depth** | $R = P - L$ where $L = ET + I + \Delta S$ | mm | $L$ = total losses |
| 3 | **Runoff Coefficient** $C$ | $C = \dfrac{R}{P} = \dfrac{\text{Runoff depth}}{\text{Rainfall depth}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le C \le 1$ |
| 4 | **Rational Method (Peak Runoff)** | $Q_p = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | m³/s | Urban / small catchment (< 50 km²) |
| 5 | **Drainage Density** | $D_d = \dfrac{\sum L_i}{A}$ | km/km² | Higher = flashier catchment |
| 6 | **Form Factor** | $F_f = \dfrac{A}{L^2}$ | dimensionless | $L$ = axial length; $F_f \to 1$ = fan-shaped |
| 7 | **Compactness Coefficient** | $C_c = \dfrac{0.2821 \cdot P_b}{\sqrt{A}}$ | dimensionless | $P_b$ = basin perimeter |
| 8 | **Time of Concentration (Kirpich)** | $T_c = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} \cdot S^{-0.385}$ | minutes | For small rural catchments |
| 9 | **Manning's Equation** | $V = \dfrac{1}{n} R_h^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | m/s | $R_h$ = hydraulic radius; $S$ = slope |
| 10 | **SCS-CN Runoff** | $R = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S}$ where $S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254$ | mm | $CN$ = Curve Number (0–100) |
| 11 | **Infiltration (Horton's)** | $f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt}$ | mm/hr | $f_c$ = final; $f_0$ = initial; $k$ = decay constant |
| 12 | **Rainfall Intensity-Duration-Frequency** | $i = \dfrac{a}{t + b}$ (rational IDF) | mm/hr | Used to find design storm intensity |

> **Note on symbols used in KTU problems:**
> - $C$ — Runoff coefficient (NOT to be confused with $C_c$, the compactness coefficient).
> - $I$ — Rainfall intensity in rational method (NOT to be confused with infiltration).
> - Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation for absolute value to avoid breaking tables.

---

## 2.3 Why These Factors Matter in Real Engineering

| Engineering Application | Most Critical Factor |
|---|---|
| Urban storm drain design | Imperviousness / land use |
| Spillway design of a dam | Catchment area + 100-yr storm intensity |
| Roof-top rainwater harvesting | Runoff coefficient of roof material (tiles, RCC) |
| Recharge pit design | Antecedent moisture + soil infiltration capacity |
| Check dam sizing | Drainage density + slope |
| Flood forecasting | Direction of storm movement + shape of basin |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rational Method (Peak Runoff Formula)

The **Rational Method** is the most frequently asked formula in KTU Module 1 numerical problems. Let us derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 — The Governing Assumption

The Rational Method assumes that the **peak rate of runoff** $Q_p$ from a small catchment (typically $A < 50$ km²) occurs when the **entire catchment is contributing runoff simultaneously**, which happens at time $t = T_c$ (time of concentration).

### Step 2 — Rainfall Volume Over the Catchment

Consider a uniform rainfall of intensity $I$ (mm/hr) falling for a duration $t \ge T_c$ over a catchment of area $A$ (m² or ha).

Volume of rainfall in time $\Delta t$:

$$V_{rain} = I \cdot A \cdot \Delta t$$

Where:
- $I$ in m/hr (after unit conversion: $1 \text{ mm/hr} = 0.001 \text{ m/hr}$)
- $A$ in m²
- $\Delta t$ in hours

### Step 3 — Introducing the Runoff Coefficient $C$

Only a *fraction $C$* of this rainfall becomes surface runoff. The rest is lost to infiltration, evaporation, interception, and depression storage.

$$V_{runoff} = C \cdot V_{rain} = C \cdot I \cdot A \cdot \Delta t$$

### Step 4 — Converting Volume to Discharge

Discharge is volume per unit time. Taking $\Delta t$ as the time interval:

$$Q = \dfrac{V_{runoff}}{\Delta t} = C \cdot I \cdot A$$

### Step 5 — Unit Conversion (Critical for KTU Numerical Problems)

For $A$ in **hectares (ha)** and $I$ in **mm/hr**:

$$1 \text{ mm/hr over 1 ha} = \dfrac{0.001 \text{ m} \times 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2}{3600 \text{ s}} = \dfrac{10}{3.6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 2.778 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Therefore the Rational Formula in SI units becomes:

$$Q_p = \dfrac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{3.6}$$

Where:
- $Q_p$ = peak runoff in **m³/s**
- $C$ = dimensionless runoff coefficient
- $I$ = rainfall intensity in **mm/hr**
- $A$ = catchment area in **hectares (ha)**
- $3.6$ = unit conversion constant

### Step 6 — If $A$ is given in km²

$$Q_p = \dfrac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{3.6} \quad \text{with } A \text{ in ha}$$

$$Q_p = \dfrac{C \cdot I \cdot A \times 100}{3.6} = 0.278 \cdot C \cdot I \cdot A \quad \text{with } A \text{ in km}^2$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Rational Method (KTU Style)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam – June 2024 style):**
> A small urban catchment of area **12 ha** has a runoff coefficient of **0.65**. A design storm produces a uniform rainfall intensity of **40 mm/hr** for a duration exceeding the time of concentration. Calculate the **peak runoff** using the Rational Method.

### Step 1 — Identify Given Data
$$A = 12 \text{ ha}, \quad C = 0.65, \quad I = 40 \text{ mm/hr}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Rational Formula
$$Q_p = \dfrac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{3.6}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Values
$$Q_p = \dfrac{0.65 \times 40 \times 12}{3.6}$$

### Step 4 — Numerator Calculation
$$0.65 \times 40 = 26.0$$
$$26.0 \times 12 = 312$$

### Step 5 — Final Division
$$Q_p = \dfrac{312}{3.6} = 86.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 6 — Final Answer with Units
$$\boxed{Q_p \approx 86.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Check:** Many students forget the $3.6$ factor and write $Q_p = 312$ (incorrect units assumed). Always write the *units* explicitly to earn the method mark.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Effect of Slope and Land Use on Runoff Coefficient

> **Problem (Part A, 3 marks style):**
> A catchment of area **5 km²** is partially forested (60%) and partially paved (40%). The runoff coefficients are: $C_{forest} = 0.20$ and $C_{paved} = 0.90$. A storm produces a uniform rainfall of **30 mm** over 24 hours. Compute the **total runoff volume** in hectare-metres (ha-m).

### Step 1 — Compute the Composite (Weighted) Runoff Coefficient
$$C_{composite} = f_1 C_1 + f_2 C_2$$

$$C_{composite} = (0.60 \times 0.20) + (0.40 \times 0.90)$$

$$C_{composite} = 0.12 + 0.36 = 0.48$$

### Step 2 — Compute Runoff Depth
$$R = C \cdot P = 0.48 \times 30 \text{ mm} = 14.4 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Area to Hectares
$$A = 5 \text{ km}^2 = 5 \times 100 = 500 \text{ ha}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Runoff Volume

The fundamental conversion: $1 \text{ mm over 1 ha} = 10 \text{ m}^3$

$$\text{Volume} = R_{mm} \times A_{ha} \times 10 \quad \text{(in m}^3\text{)}$$

$$V = 14.4 \times 500 \times 10 = 72{,}000 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 5 — Express in Hectare-Metres (ha-m)
$$V = 14.4 \text{ mm} \times 500 \text{ ha} = 7200 \text{ ha-mm} = 7.2 \text{ ha-m}$$

$$\boxed{V = 7.2 \text{ ha-m} = 72{,}000 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Runoff to Each Factor

The following matrix is critical for KTU descriptive answers and viva voce.

| Factor | Direction of Effect on Runoff ($R$) | Typical Quantitative Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Increase in rainfall intensity | $\uparrow R$ (strongly) | Doubling intensity can increase peak $R$ by ~80% |
| Steeper slope | $\uparrow R$ | 5% slope gives ~25% higher $Q_p$ than 1% slope |
| Increase in area | $\uparrow R$ (volume) but $\downarrow R$ (per unit area) | Total volume scales as $A^{0.9}$ approx. |
| Fan-shaped catchment | $\uparrow$ peak $R$, $\downarrow$ time to peak | $T_c$ shorter by up to 40% |
| Dense vegetation | $\downarrow R$ | Forest cover can reduce $R$ by 30–60% |
| Clay soil (vs sand) | $\uparrow R$ | $C$ for clay = 0.70; for sand = 0.20 |
| Urbanization (+10% impervious) | $\uparrow R$ by 30–50% | Highly non-linear response |
| High antecedent moisture | $\uparrow R$ | $C$ may double (AMC II → AMC III) |
| Higher temperature / ET | $\downarrow R$ | Tropical dry season: 25–40% loss to ET |
| Check dams / terraces | $\downarrow$ peak $R$ | Sediment storage + detention effect |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any six factors affecting runoff from a catchment.**

**Model Answer (6 factors, each ≈ 0.5 mark for clarity):**

The runoff from a catchment is influenced by the following factors:

1. **Rainfall intensity** — Higher intensity produces more runoff as infiltration capacity is exceeded.
2. **Rainfall duration** — Longer duration saturates the soil and increases total runoff volume.
3. **Catchment slope** — Steeper slopes accelerate overland flow, reducing time available for infiltration.
4. **Catchment area and shape** — Fan-shaped basins produce higher peaks than elongated ones.
5. **Soil type** — Sandy soils allow more infiltration (less runoff); clayey soils produce more runoff.
6. **Land use / vegetation cover** — Forests and grasslands reduce runoff; urbanized areas increase it.

*(Any six with one-line explanation = 3 marks. KTU expects neat classification.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Define the term "Runoff Coefficient" and state its typical range for (a) paved surface and (b) forest area.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Runoff Coefficient ($C$)** is the ratio of peak runoff rate to the rainfall intensity, assuming the rainfall intensity is uniform over the catchment for a duration equal to or greater than the time of concentration.

$$C = \dfrac{\text{Peak Runoff Rate}}{\text{Rainfall Intensity} \times \text{Area}} \quad \text{(dimensionless, range 0 to 1)}$$

Typical values (KTU-recommended):
- **Paved / Concrete / Bituminous surface:** $C = 0.85$ to $0.95$
- **Forested area (dense vegetation):** $C = 0.10$ to $0.20$

> *(Definition 1.5 marks + range with justification 1.5 marks = 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Provision (KTU 2024):** Answer **ONE** of the following.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]
**(a)** Explain in detail the **climatic factors** that affect runoff from a catchment. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A small urban catchment of area **8 hectares** has the following land use distribution:

| Land Use | Area (ha) | Runoff Coefficient $C$ |
|---|---|---|
| Roof (RCC) | 3.0 | 0.90 |
| Paved road | 2.0 | 0.85 |
| Open ground (bare) | 2.0 | 0.40 |
| Garden/lawn | 1.0 | 0.20 |

A design storm of **50 mm/hr** intensity and duration exceeding the time of concentration is expected. Calculate the **peak runoff** by the Rational Method. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The **climatic factors** that govern the rate and volume of runoff from a catchment are:

1. **Type of Precipitation** *(1 mark)*
   - Rain is the most effective producer of immediate runoff. Snow accumulates and melts gradually, producing a delayed runoff response. Hail and sleet are intermediate.

2. **Rainfall Intensity** *(1 mark)*
   - Intensity is the most influential climatic factor. If $i > f$ (infiltration capacity), Hortonian overland flow begins. Higher intensity storms disproportionately increase peak runoff.

3. **Rainfall Duration** *(1 mark)*
   - For runoff to occur at the outlet, the storm must last at least for a duration equal to the time of concentration $T_c$. Beyond $T_c$, runoff continues but the *peak* remains constant.

4. **Rainfall Distribution in Space and Time** *(1 mark)*
   - Uniform rainfall over a small area is ideal. Centered, intense storms produce flashy hydrographs. Temporal variability (peaked vs. flat hyetograph) changes the shape of the runoff hydrograph.

5. **Direction of Storm Movement** *(1 mark)*
   - A storm moving downstream-to-upstream causes flow contributions to *stack up* → higher peak.
   - A storm moving upstream-to-downstream spreads the flow → lower, broader peak.

6. **Antecedent Precipitation (Prior wetness)** *(1 mark)*
   - A wet catchment has lower remaining infiltration capacity → higher runoff. The SCS-CN method uses three AMC classes: I (dry), II (average), III (wet).

7. **Other Climatic Variables — ET, Wind, Temperature, Humidity** *(1 mark)*
   - All affect evaporative losses, which directly reduce the rainfall excess available for runoff.

> *Conclusion:* Climatic factors are the *time-varying drivers* of runoff and must be characterized using historical IDF curves and storm data for proper design.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Compute the Weighted (Composite) Runoff Coefficient** *(3 marks)*

$$C = \dfrac{\sum C_i A_i}{\sum A_i}$$

Numerator:
$$(0.90 \times 3.0) + (0.85 \times 2.0) + (0.40 \times 2.0) + (0.20 \times 1.0)$$
$$= 2.70 + 1.70 + 0.80 + 0.20 = 5.40$$

Denominator:
$$\sum A_i = 3.0 + 2.0 + 2.0 + 1.0 = 8.0 \text{ ha}$$

Therefore:
$$C = \dfrac{5.40}{8.0} = 0.675$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Rational Method** *(3 marks)*

$$Q_p = \dfrac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{3.6}$$

Substituting:
$$Q_p = \dfrac{0.675 \times 50 \times 8.0}{3.6}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator Calculation** *(0.5 mark)*

$$0.675 \times 50 = 33.75$$
$$33.75 \times 8.0 = 270$$

**Step 4 — Final Computation** *(0.5 mark)*

$$Q_p = \dfrac{270}{3.6} = 75.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_p = 75.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

> *Exam tip: Always retain 2–3 decimal places in $C$ to avoid rounding error in the final answer.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1 — Alternative]
**(a)** Describe the **physiographic factors** affecting runoff. Explain how the **shape and slope** of a catchment influence the runoff hydrograph. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A catchment has the following characteristics:
- Area $A = 25 \text{ km}^2$
- Length of longest stream $L = 10 \text{ km}$
- Basin perimeter $P_b = 32 \text{ km}$
- Axial length of basin = 8 km
- Average slope $S = 0.005$

Compute the **(i) Form Factor**, **(ii) Compactness Coefficient**, and **(iii) Kirpich Time of Concentration**. State whether the basin is *fan-shaped* or *elongated*. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Physiographic factors** are the *static physical characteristics* of the catchment that control runoff:

1. **Catchment Area** *(1 mark)* — Larger area contributes more total runoff volume but lower per-unit-area peak.
2. **Shape** *(2 marks)* — Quantified by **Form Factor** $F_f = A / L^2$ and **Compactness Coefficient** $C_c$.
   - *Fan-shaped* basin ($F_f$ close to 1, $C_c$ close to 1) → shorter $T_c$, higher peak.
   - *Elongated* basin ($F_f \ll 1$, $C_c > 1.5$) → longer $T_c$, lower broader peak.
3. **Slope** *(2 marks)* — Steeper slope increases the velocity of overland flow by Manning's equation $V \propto S^{1/2}$, reducing the time water spends on the surface and hence reducing infiltration → more runoff, sharper hydrograph.
4. **Drainage Density** *(1 mark)* — High $D_d$ (well-dissected terrain) means quick channelized transfer → higher peak.
5. **Soil, Geology, Vegetation, Elevation, Orientation** *(1 mark)* — These influence infiltration, ET, and interception.

> *Conclusion:* The shape and slope together control the **timing and magnitude of the peak** of the runoff hydrograph.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**(i) Form Factor** *(2.5 marks)*

$$F_f = \dfrac{A}{L_{axial}^2}$$

Given: $A = 25$ km², $L_{axial} = 8$ km

$$F_f = \dfrac{25}{(8)^2} = \dfrac{25}{64} = 0.3906$$

$$\boxed{F_f \approx 0.39}$$

**(ii) Compactness Coefficient** *(2.5 marks)*

$$C_c = \dfrac{0.2821 \cdot P_b}{\sqrt{A}}$$

Given: $P_b = 32$ km, $A = 25$ km²

$$C_c = \dfrac{0.2821 \times 32}{\sqrt{25}} = \dfrac{9.0272}{5} = 1.8054$$

$$\boxed{C_c \approx 1.81}$$

**(iii) Kirpich Time of Concentration** *(2 marks)*

$$T_c = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} \cdot S^{-0.385}$$

Given: $L = 10$ km, $S = 0.005$

$$L^{0.77} = 10^{0.77} = 5.888$$
$$S^{-0.385} = (0.005)^{-0.385} = (5 \times 10^{-3})^{-0.385} = 4.978$$

$$T_c = 0.01947 \times 5.888 \times 4.978$$

$$T_c = 0.01947 \times 29.31 \approx 0.5706 \text{ minutes}$$

*(Note: For $L$ in km, Kirpich's constant is sometimes quoted as $0.01947 \times (1000 L)^{0.77} \times S^{-0.385}$ to yield $T_c$ in seconds. Many KTU papers accept either form if the unit is stated.)*

> *Alternate (commonly accepted KTU form):*
> $$T_c \, (\text{min}) = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} \cdot S^{-0.385} \quad \text{with } L \text{ in meters, } S \text{ in m/m}$$
> Here $L = 10{,}000$ m, so $L^{0.77} = 10{,}000^{0.77} \approx 1096.5$
> $S^{-0.385} = 4.978$
> $T_c = 0.01947 \times 1096.5 \times 4.978 \approx 106.3$ min $\approx 1.77$ hr.

**Inference on Shape**:
Since $F_f = 0.39$ (much less than 1) and $C_c = 1.81$ (greater than 1), the catchment is **elongated**, not fan-shaped.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Rational Method** — Forgetting the $3.6$ divisor when $A$ is in ha and $I$ in mm/hr. This alone costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Kirpich Formula Units** — Many students write $L$ in km and use the mm-form constant, leading to $T_c$ in seconds when minutes were expected. **Always specify the unit convention.**
> 3. **Composite $C$** — Using simple arithmetic mean instead of **area-weighted mean**. The examiner strictly deducts for this.
> 4. **Confusing $C$ (runoff coefficient) with $C_c$ (compactness coefficient)** — they are entirely different.
> 5. **Omitting the "explanation" in descriptive parts** — Listing factors without a 1-line explanation loses at least 1 mark per factor.
> 6. **Forgetting antecedent moisture in $C$** — The runoff coefficient is NOT a constant; it changes with AMC, season, and storm pattern.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### ✅ Quick-Recall Checklist (for last-minute revision)

- [ ] Runoff is governed by the **water balance equation**: $P = R + ET + I + \Delta S$.
- [ ] The **Rational Method** $Q_p = C \cdot I \cdot A / 3.6$ is valid for $A < 50$ km² and is the most-tested formula in Module 1.
- [ ] **Climatic factors** are dynamic; **physiographic factors** are static; **hydrologic factors** are quasi-static (change with season).
- [ ] **High intensity + steep slope + clay soil + urban cover + high AMC = maximum runoff.**
- [ ] **Dense forest + gentle slope + sandy soil + dry AMC = minimum runoff.**
- [ ] The **runoff coefficient $C$** depends on land use, soil, and slope — KTU provides a table (memorize: roof 0.9, road 0.85, lawn 0.2).
- [ ] **Form Factor** $F_f = A/L^2$: closer to 1 = fan-shaped.
- [ ] **Compactness Coefficient** $C_c = 0.2821 \cdot P_b / \sqrt{A}$: minimum 1 for circular basin.
- [ ] **Drainage Density** $D_d = \sum L_i / A$ — high = flashier flood response.
- [ ] **Kirpich Time of Concentration** — required for selecting design storm duration.
- [ ] **SCS-CN Method** — for ungauged rural catchments; remember $S = 25400/CN - 254$ (CN in 0–100).
- [ ] Direction of storm movement affects **peak shape**, not peak magnitude (for uniform rainfall).
- [ ] **Roof-top RWH design** = function of (roof area, rainfall, runoff coefficient of roof).
- [ ] **Recharge pit design** = function of (catchment area, post-monsoon soil infiltration, groundwater depth).
- [ ] Always use **area-weighted $C$** for composite catchments.
- [ ] The **mnemonic "RISE-CAMP"** (Rainfall, Infiltration, Slope, Evaporation, Catchment shape, AMC, Management, Physiography) covers all 8 essential runoff factors.

### 🎯 Common KTU Mark Distribution for this Topic
| Question Type | Marks | Expected Length |
|---|---|---|
| Define + list factors | 3 | 5–6 lines + formula |
| Climatic / Physiographic factors descriptive | 7 | 1 full page with diagram |
| Rational Method numerical | 7 | 4–5 steps with unit conversion |
| Composite C + Runoff Volume | 7 | 3–4 steps + box answer |
| Form Factor / Compactness | 7 | 3 separate calculations + interpretation |

> *Master this topic, and you master ~30% of Module 1 marks in KTU RWH (OECET832).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Important Points Relating to Water Storage and Recharging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Important Points Relating to Water Storage and Recharging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Water Storage** in the context of rainwater harvesting refers to the deliberate impounding, holding, and conservation of collected runoff in engineered reservoirs for later use during dry periods. **Recharging** refers to the artificial or natural process of directing surface water into subsurface aquifers to replenish groundwater reserves, thereby sustaining well yields and baseflow in streams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
