> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** Storage and Recharging are the **two complementary pillars** of any rainwater harvesting system. Storage addresses *temporal* mismatch (rain today, need tomorrow), while Recharging addresses *spatial* mismatch (water here, aquifer there) and *long-term sustainability* of the hydrological cycle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a rainwater harvesting system as a **bank account for water**:
- **Water Storage** is like your **savings account** — you deposit water (during rain) and withdraw it (during dry months) in the same physical location.
- **Recharging** is like **investing in an index fund** — you put water back into the ground (the aquifer), and the earth slowly "earns interest" by filtering, purifying, and storing it across a vast underground network.
- The **catchment** is your *monthly income* (rainfall), the **storage tank** is your *savings account*, the **aquifer** is your *long-term investment portfolio*, and **demand** is your *monthly expenses*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** If your annual rainfall is **P** mm and your roof area is **A** m², you can collect roughly **P × A × 0.8** litres per year (the 0.8 factor accounts for evaporation and first-flush losses). For **Kerala's average annual rainfall of ~3000 mm**, a **100 m²** roof yields approximately **2,40,000 litres/year** — enough for a family of 5.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are **standardized in KTU examinations** and must be memorized:

- **Runoff Coefficient (C):** Typically **0.80 – 0.90** for impervious roofs (tiled, concrete, metal).
- **First Flush Diverter Capacity:** First **2 – 3 mm** of rainfall should be discarded to remove dust, bird droppings, and debris.
- **Storage Efficiency:** A well-designed system achieves **80 – 90%** water-tight efficiency.
- **Recommended Storage:** Minimum **15 – 20 days** of daily demand capacity to bridge dry spells.
- **Per Capita Water Demand (Kerala standards):** **150 – 200 litres per capita per day (lpcd)** for domestic purposes.
- **Dry Spell in Kerala:** Max continuous dry period considered for design = **90 – 120 days** (Dec–Feb + pre-monsoon).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:** Examiners expect students to quote typical values from the **CPHEEO Manual** and **Kerala State Groundwater Department** guidelines. Memorize the ranges above — they appear as 2-mark direct questions almost every year.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Storage–Recharge Decision Framework

A rainwater harvesting system designer must answer **three sequential questions** before choosing between storage and recharging:

1. **What is the demand pattern?**
   - Year-round domestic use → **Storage dominant**
   - Groundwater augmentation → **Recharge dominant**
   - Combined (most Kerala homes) → **Hybrid system**

2. **What is the site hydrogeology?**
   - Deep water table (>15 m) + permeable soil → **Recharge wells/trenches**
   - Shallow water table (<5 m) → **Recharge pits** (avoid contamination)
   - Rocky terrain with fissures → **Recharge shafts**

3. **What is the water quality requirement?**
   - Drinking → **Storage + multi-stage filtration**
   - Gardening/toilet flushing → **Direct recharge permissible**

## 2.2 The Operational Logic — Step by Step

### A. Water Storage Path
- **Step 1:** Rain falls on the **catchment surface** (roof).
- **Step 2:** Water is conveyed through **gutters and downpipes**.
- **Step 3:** **First-flush diverter** discards the initial polluted runoff.
- **Step 4:** Conduit is fitted with a **mesh filter** to remove leaves/debris.
- **Step 5:** Water enters the **storage tank** (overhead, underground, or sump).
- **Step 6:** Water is **disinfected** (chlorination / UV) before consumption.

### B. Recharging Path
- **Step 1:** Collected water passes through a **desilting chamber / sand filter**.
- **Step 2:** Water is directed into a **recharge structure** (pit / trench / well / shaft).
- **Step 3:** Water **percolates through soil**, undergoing natural filtration.
- **Step 4:** Water **joins the aquifer**, raising the water table.
- **Step 5:** Aquifer water is abstracted later through **existing bore wells / dug wells**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Units | Boundary Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C \times I \times A$ | Q = runoff volume, C = runoff coefficient, I = rainfall depth, A = catchment area | m³, mm, m² | C = 0.8–0.9 for roofs; I in **metres** when A in m² |
| 2 | $V_{tank} = D \times N \times T_{d}$ | V = tank volume, D = daily demand per person, N = number of users, Td = dry days | litres | Td in Kerala = 90–120 days |
| 3 | $V_{collected} = P \times A \times C \times 10^{-3}$ | P = annual rainfall, A = area, C = coefficient | m³ | Multiply by 1000 for litres |
| 4 | $T_{tank} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{V_{annual}/365}$ | Storage adequacy ratio | dimensionless | Should be ≥ 0.5 for good design |
| 5 | Recharge Pit Volume: $V_{pit} = L \times B \times H$ | L, B, H = length, breadth, depth | m³ | Typical: 2m × 2m × 3m |
| 6 | Recharge Well Dia.: $D_{well} = \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{v_{soil} \times \pi H_{eff}}$ | Qpeak = peak inflow, vsoil = soil infiltration rate, Heff = effective depth | m | Use vsoil = 0.005–0.02 m/hr for sandy loam |
| 7 | Water Table Rise: $\Delta h = \dfrac{V_{recharged}}{A_{recharge} \times S_y}$ | Sy = specific yield | m | Sy = 0.10–0.20 for alluvial soils |
| 8 | Filter Sizing: $A_{filter} = \dfrac{Q_{design}}{v_{filtration}}$ | vfil = 0.1–0.3 m/hr for sand | m² | Sand media: 0.5–1.0 mm effective size |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL UNIT CONVERSION (Most common KTU mistake):** When using $Q = C \times I \times A$ in SI units, rainfall **I** must be in **metres**, not mm. If rainfall is in mm, divide by 1000: $I_{m} = I_{mm} / 1000$. A 1 mm rain on 1 m² = **1 litre**, not 0.001 m³.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Kerala apartments** use storage + recharge combos to comply with the **Kerala Municipal Building Rules** mandating rainwater harvesting in buildings > 100 m².
- **KSRTC bus depots** in Thiruvananthapuram use large storage tanks for bus washing.
- **Cochin International Airport** operates one of India's largest rainwater-fed industrial systems.
- **Coconut plantations** in Palakkad rely on recharge trenches to sustain irrigation wells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Storage Tank Capacity from Demand Analysis

The capacity of a storage tank is governed by the **water balance** between supply (rain) and demand (consumption). The standard KTU approach is to size the tank to cover the **critical dry period (T_d)** between two consecutive monsoon failures.

**Given:**
- Number of occupants: N = 6
- Per capita demand: D = 150 lpcd (litres per capita per day)
- Critical dry days in Kerala: $T_d$ = 100 days
- Annual rainfall: P = 3000 mm
- Roof area: A = 100 m²
- Runoff coefficient: C = 0.85

**Step 1 — Write the demand equation.**

The total volume of water required during the dry spell is:

$$
V_{demand} = D \times N \times T_{d}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
V_{demand} = 150 \times 6 \times 100
$$

$$
V_{demand} = 90{,}000 \text{ litres}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert the demand into cubic metres for engineering use.**

Since **1 m³ = 1000 litres**, we have:

$$
V_{demand} = \dfrac{90{,}000}{1000} = 90 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Size the storage tank using a 1:2:1 length:breadth:height ratio (typical for rectangular underground tanks).**

Let the length L, breadth B, and height H satisfy L = 2B and V = L × B × H.

We choose H = 2 m (standard for domestic sump) and B = 3 m, so L = 6 m:

$$
V_{check} = 6 \times 3 \times 2 = 36 \text{ m}^3
$$

This is insufficient. Increase B to 4.5 m → L = 9 m:

$$
V_{check} = 9 \times 4.5 \times 2 = 81 \text{ m}^3
$$

Still short. Try B = 5 m, L = 10 m, H = 2 m:

$$
V_{check} = 10 \times 5 \times 2 = 100 \text{ m}^3
$$

This **exceeds** the demand of 90 m³ → **Safe design** with 11% contingency margin.

**Step 4 — Verify the supply side will actually refill the tank annually.**

The annual runoff from the roof is:

$$
Q_{annual} = C \times I \times A
$$

Here I = 3000 mm = 3.0 m and A = 100 m²:

$$
Q_{annual} = 0.85 \times 3.0 \times 100
$$

$$
Q_{annual} = 255 \text{ m}^3 / \text{year} = 2{,}55{,}000 \text{ litres / year}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the storage turnover ratio.**

$$
T_{tank} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{Q_{annual}/365} = \dfrac{100}{255/365} = \dfrac{100}{0.6986} \approx 143 \text{ days}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The tank theoretically turns over ~1.4 times per year, meaning the system can refill during a single good monsoon — a healthy design.

## 3.2 Derivation: Recharge Pit Design Using Soil Infiltration

**Given:**
- Catchment area: A = 150 m²
- Annual rainfall: P = 2800 mm
- Soil type: Laterite (typical Kerala)
- Infiltration rate of laterite: $v_{soil}$ = 0.012 m/hr
- Desired recharge per monsoon: ~70% of annual runoff

**Step 1 — Annual runoff volume from the catchment.**

$$
Q_{runoff} = C \times P \times A \times 10^{-3}
$$

Using C = 0.85 (impervious roof):

$$
Q_{runoff} = 0.85 \times 2800 \times 150 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
Q_{runoff} = 357 \text{ m}^3 / \text{year}
$$

**Step 2 — Design the recharge pit to handle the daily peak inflow during a heavy rainstorm.**

Assume a critical storm: 50 mm/hr intensity, lasting 1 hour over the catchment.

$$
Q_{peak} = C \times I_{peak} \times A = 0.85 \times 0.050 \times 150 = 6.375 \text{ m}^3 / \text{hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the effective sidewall + bottom area required for the pit to absorb this inflow.**

The soil infiltration capacity is the rate at which water can percolate. For safety, use 50% of measured rate (factor of safety 2):

$$
v_{design} = 0.5 \times 0.012 = 0.006 \text{ m/hr}
$$

Required wetted area:

$$
A_{wetted} = \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{v_{design}} = \dfrac{6.375}{0.006} = 1062.5 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Dimension the pit.**

If we choose a **circular pit of diameter D and depth H = 3 m**, the wetted area is the bottom + sidewalls:

$$
A_{wetted} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} + \pi D H
$$

Setting this equal to 1062.5 m² and solving for D:

$$
\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} + 3\pi D = 1062.5
$$

This is a quadratic in D: $0.7854 D^2 + 9.425 D - 1062.5 = 0$

Using the quadratic formula $D = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:

$$
D = \dfrac{-9.425 + \sqrt{(9.425)^2 + 4 \times 0.7854 \times 1062.5}}{2 \times 0.7854}
$$

$$
D = \dfrac{-9.425 + \sqrt{88.83 + 3341.3}}{1.5708}
$$

$$
D = \dfrac{-9.425 + \sqrt{3430.13}}{1.5708} = \dfrac{-9.425 + 58.57}{1.5708}
$$

$$
D \approx \dfrac{49.14}{1.5708} \approx 31.3 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Refine using a square pit for easier construction.**

For a square pit of side S:

$$
A_{wetted} = S^2 + 4SH
$$

$$
S^2 + 12S = 1062.5
$$

Solving: $S \approx 26.8$ m, which is impractically large. **Conclusion:** A single pit is insufficient for this catchment; use **multiple smaller pits in parallel** (e.g., three pits of side 15.5 m).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why KTU examiners often frame a design problem as *"Design a recharge system for a given roof area."* The expected answer uses **multiple smaller structures** (trenches, soak pits, or a combination) rather than one oversized pit.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Storage vs. Recharging — When to Choose What

| Criterion | Favoring **Storage** | Favoring **Recharging** |
|---|---|---|
| Water table depth | Shallow (< 5 m) — risk of contamination | Deep (> 10 m) — safe percolation |
| Soil permeability | Low (clayey) | High (sandy/lateritic) |
| End use | Drinking (after treatment) | Irrigation, groundwater augmentation |
| Space availability | Limited | Ample (open ground around building) |
| Monsoon reliability | Erratic | Reliable (Kerala's case: very reliable) |
| Capital cost | High (tank + plumbing) | Moderate (pit + filter) |
| Maintenance frequency | Annual cleaning | Bi-annual desilting |
| KTU exam scenario | "Design a domestic tank…" | "Augment a community well…" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Combined Storage–Recharge System

The following block diagram shows the **complete functional architecture** of a hybrid rainwater harvesting system — the kind of integrated system KTU 2024 expects students to draw in 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rainfall on Catchment Roof] --> B[Gutter and Downpipe Network]
    B --> C[First Flush Diverter FFD]
    C --> D[Mesh Filter Leaf and Debris Removal]
    D --> E[Junction Chamber Two Way Diversion]
    E --> F[Storage Tank Sump or Overhead]
    E --> G[Sand Filter Bed Desilting]
    F --> H[Domestic Use Tap after UV Chlorination]
    F --> I[OverFlow WeIR to Recharge Pit]
    G --> J[Recharge Pit with Gravel Media]
    G --> K[Recharge Trench Perforated Pipe]
    J --> L[Aquifer Recharge Zone]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Existing Bore Well Recovery]
    M --> N[Domestic Reuse Loop]

    style A fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style E fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style G fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style J fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#fff
    style K fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#fff
    style L fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Storage Tank Internal Cross-Section Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Pipe from Filter] --> B[Inlet Baffle Chamber]
    B --> C[Sediment Zone Bottom 15 percent Volume]
    C --> D[Main Storage Volume 70 percent]
    D --> E[Disinfection Zone Chlorine Tablets]
    E --> F[Outlet to Pump or Tap]
    G[OverFlow Pipe to Recharge] --> C
    H[Vent and Inspection Hatch] --> D
    I[Manhole for Cleaning] --> C

    style A fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style C fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style E fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style G fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

## 4.3 Recharge Pit Cross-Section Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Recharge Water Inlet from Filter] --> B[Inspection Chamber with Screen]
    B --> C[Top Layer Coarse Gravel 20 cm]
    C --> D[Second Layer Pebbles 15 cm]
    D --> E[Third Layer Charcoal and Sand 15 cm]
    E --> F[Bottom Layer Bricks and Boulder 20 cm]
    F --> G[Recharge Cavity Open Bottom or Perforated Rings]
    G --> H[Native Soil and Water Table]

    style A fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style C fill:#d7ccc8,stroke:#3e2723
    style D fill:#bcaaa4,stroke:#3e2723
    style E fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style F fill:#a1887f,stroke:#3e2723
    style G fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40
    style H fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree for Choosing Storage vs. Recharge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Site Assessment] --> B{Is annual demand greater than annual runoff}
    B -->|Yes| C[Storage is Essential]
    B -->|No| D{Is the soil permeable}
    D -->|Yes| E[Recharging is Viable]
    D -->|No| F[Storage is the Only Option]
    C --> G{Is there space for a recharge structure}
    G -->|Yes| H[Hybrid System Recommended]
    G -->|No| I[Storage with OverFlow to Drainage]
    E --> J{Is the water table below 5 m}
    J -->|Yes| K[Recharge Pit or Well]
    J -->|No| L[Recharge Trench Only Avoid Pit]

    style A fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style H fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style I fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style K fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style L fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any six important points to be considered while designing a water storage tank for harvested rainwater.**

**Model Answer (6 points, each ~0.5 mark):**

1. **Tank capacity** must be sized to meet demand during the **critical dry period** (T_d) — typically 90–120 days in Kerala.
2. **Material of construction** must be **non-toxic, watertight, and durable** — RCC, ferrocement, or HDPE.
3. The tank must include a **sediment / silt zone** at the bottom (10–15% of total volume) for easy cleaning.
4. A **first-flush diverter** must precede the tank to discard the initial **2–3 mm** of polluted runoff.
5. The tank must have a **covered top** with **inspection manhole** and **vent pipe** to prevent mosquito breeding and algal growth.
6. An **overflow pipe** must be connected to a recharge structure or safe disposal drain, never allowed to stagnate.
7. (Bonus) **Disinfection facility** (chlorination or UV) must be integrated for potable use.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write vague answers like *"the tank should be good quality."* Examiners deduct marks for lack of **quantitative values** and **engineering terms**. Always include numbers (e.g., 90–120 days, 2–3 mm, 10–15%) and proper terminology (RCC, HDPE, disinfection, overflow).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the difference between direct and indirect methods of artificial groundwater recharge? Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Direct Recharge:** Water is conveyed **straight into the aquifer** through structures that bypass the soil's natural filtering layer (or minimize it). Examples: **Recharge wells, Recharge shafts, Injection wells.**
- **Indirect Recharge:** Water is spread over the ground or through **permeable soil media** so that natural filtration occurs during percolation. Examples: **Recharge pits, Recharge trenches, Percolation tanks, Check dams.**

| Aspect | Direct Recharge | Indirect Recharge |
|---|---|---|
| Soil filtration | Minimal | Maximum |
| Water quality | Needs pre-treatment | Self-purifying |
| Speed of recharge | Fast | Slow |
| Risk of contamination | Higher | Lower |
| KTU example | Recharge well (Fig.) | Recharge pit (Fig.) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various types of storage tanks used in rainwater harvesting systems. Compare their suitability for Kerala's climatic conditions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A residential building in Thrissur has a **roof area of 120 m²** and **5 occupants**. The average annual rainfall is **3000 mm** and the design dry period is **100 days**. The per capita water demand is **150 lpcd**. The runoff coefficient is **0.85**. Design the storage tank capacity and check whether the annual supply meets the demand. Assume first-flush loss = **2 mm** per rain event and **40 rain events/year**.

---

**Model Solution to (a) — Types of Storage Tanks:**

**1. Underground Storage Tanks (Sumps)**
- Constructed below ground level, often of **RCC or brick masonry**.
- **Advantages:** No aesthetic intrusion, keeps water cool, large capacity feasible, less algal growth.
- **Disadvantages:** Difficult to inspect/repair, risk of contamination from soil, requires pumping.
- **KTU Kerala suitability:** ★★★★ — Commonly used in Kerala apartments; high water table in coastal areas (e.g., Kochi) requires waterproof construction.

**2. Overhead Storage Tanks (Loft Tanks)**
- Plastic (HDPE) or RCC tanks placed at height to provide **gravity flow**.
- **Advantages:** Gravity distribution, easy inspection, no pump cost.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited capacity (typically 500–5000 L), structural load on building, exposed to sun (algae).
- **KTU Kerala suitability:** ★★★ — Standard for individual houses; use **opaque tanks** to prevent algae.

**3. Surface Storage Tanks (Ponds / Farm Ponds)**
- Open or lined earthen ponds used for **community / agricultural storage**.
- **Advantages:** Very large volume, low cost, dual use (fishing/recharge).
- **Disadvantages:** High evaporation, contamination risk, mosquito breeding.
- **KTU Kerala suitability:** ★★★ — Excellent for **school/college campuses** and **plantation agriculture**; common in Palakkad paddy belts.

**4. Ferrocement Tanks**
- Made of **cement mortar over wire mesh** — popular in rural India.
- **Advantages:** Low cost, durable, can be cast in-situ, no joints.
- **Disadvantages:** Skilled labour needed, slow construction.
- **KTU Kerala suitability:** ★★★★ — Highly recommended for **rural Kattakada / Wayanad** homes.

**5. Bladder / Pillow Tanks**
- **PVC / EPDM fabric** tanks, foldable, used as temporary / emergency storage.
- **Advantages:** Portable, quick installation, low cost.
- **Disadvantages:** Puncture risk, UV degradation.
- **KTU Kerala suitability:** ★★ — Used in **flood relief camps**, not for permanent systems.

**Comparison Table:**

| Tank Type | Capacity Range | Cost (₹/L) | Lifespan | Kerala Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Underground RCC | 5000–50000 L | 1.5–2.5 | 30+ yr | Apartments |
| Overhead HDPE | 500–5000 L | 1.0–1.5 | 15 yr | Houses |
| Surface Pond | 50,000+ L | 0.3–0.5 | 20 yr | Farms |
| Ferrocement | 2000–15000 L | 0.8–1.2 | 25 yr | Rural |
| Bladder | 1000–10000 L | 0.5–1.0 | 5–8 yr | Emergency |

---

**Model Solution to (b) — Storage Tank Design:**

**Step 1 — Compute annual water demand.**

$$
Q_{demand} = N \times D \times 365
$$

$$
Q_{demand} = 5 \times 150 \times 365 = 2{,}73{,}750 \text{ litres/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute gross annual rainfall harvested.**

$$
Q_{gross} = P \times A = 3000 \times 10^{-3} \times 120 = 360 \text{ m}^3 = 3{,}60{,}000 \text{ L/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract first-flush losses.**

First-flush loss per event = 2 mm × 120 m² = 0.002 × 120 = 0.24 m³ = **240 L/event**.

Total annual first-flush loss:

$$
Q_{ff} = 240 \times 40 = 9{,}600 \text{ L/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply runoff coefficient to effective rainfall.**

Effective harvested rainfall (after first-flush subtraction):

$$
Q_{eff\_rain} = 3{,}60{,}000 - 9{,}600 = 3{,}50{,}400 \text{ L/year}
$$

Apply C = 0.85:

$$
Q_{harvest} = 0.85 \times 3{,}50{,}400 = 2{,}97{,}840 \text{ L/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute demand during the critical dry period.**

$$
Q_{dry} = N \times D \times T_d = 5 \times 150 \times 100 = 75{,}000 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 6 — Design the storage tank.**

The tank must hold at least the dry-period demand of **75,000 L = 75 m³**.

For a cylindrical RCC tank (most economical for large capacity), use H = 2.5 m, D = 6.2 m:

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times H = \dfrac{\pi (6.2)^2}{4} \times 2.5 \approx 75.5 \text{ m}^3 \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 7 — Check supply vs. annual demand.**

Annual harvest = 2,97,840 L > Annual demand = 2,73,750 L ✓

**Surplus** = 2,97,840 − 2,73,750 = **24,090 L/year** — can be used for **groundwater recharge via overflow**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Errors:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Converting 3000 mm to 3 m **after** multiplication, leading to a 1000× error.
> 2. **Forgetting first-flush loss** in supply calculation.
> 3. **Not running the supply-vs-demand check** at the end — KTU awards a full mark for the final conclusion.
> 4. **Mixing tank volume units:** Always quote tank capacity in **both litres and m³** for clarity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various **artificial recharge structures** used in rainwater harvesting. Draw neat sketches of any two.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a **recharge trench** for a residential building with the following data:
- Roof area: 200 m²
- Annual rainfall: 2800 mm
- Soil type: Sandy loam (infiltration rate = 0.015 m/hr)
- Length of trench available: **15 m**
- Depth of trench: **2.5 m**
- Find the **required width** of the trench.

---

**Model Solution to (a) — Artificial Recharge Structures:**

**1. Recharge Pit**
- A square or circular excavation **2–4 m deep** filled with graded filter media (boulders → gravel → sand → charcoal).
- **Best for:** Individual houses, small catchments.
- **Disadvantage:** Small storage volume, frequent desilting.

**2. Recharge Trench**
- A long, narrow excavation (**L = 5–15 m, B = 1–3 m, H = 1.5–3 m**) filled with boulders/gravel, often with a **perforated pipe** at the top.
- **Best for:** Row houses, along compound walls, large open spaces.
- **Advantage:** Higher surface area for infiltration; works well in lateritic Kerala soils.

**3. Recharge Well (Injection Well)**
- A bore well constructed **down to the aquifer**, with the upper portion sealed and perforated at the aquifer depth.
- **Best for:** Urban areas with deep water tables and limited surface space.
- **Disadvantage:** High cost; needs **pre-treatment** of recharge water.

**4. Recharge Shaft**
- A **deep vertical shaft (5–15 m)** lined with porous brick/concrete rings, going through an impervious clay layer to reach a sandy aquifer below.
- **Best for:** Areas with a **clay layer overlying** a deeper aquifer — common in parts of central Kerala.

**5. Percolation Tank**
- A small **earthen check dam** built across a stream to hold water and allow percolation.
- **Best for:** Rural watershed-scale recharge; widely used in **Kattakada, Idukki**.

**6. Injection Bore Well**
- A bored hole with a casing pipe, used to **inject** water directly into a confined aquifer.
- **Best for:** Industrial / municipal scale projects.

**Two neat sketches (as text-based Mermaid in the answer script would be inappropriate; instead, students should draw):**

**Sketch 1: Recharge Pit Cross-Section**
```
   Inlet →   |==== Mesh Screen ====|
             |                      |
             |   Coarse Gravel 20cm |
             |   Pebbles      15cm |
             |   Sand+Charcoal 15cm |
             |   Bricks+Boulders 20cm|
             |___Native Soil (Permeable)___|
                ↕ Water Table ↕
```

**Sketch 2: Recharge Trench Plan and Section**
```
PLAN:  ←——— 15 m ———→
       ┌──────────────────────┐
       │ Perforated Pipe ●●○●●│ ← Inlet
       │ Gravel Fill (Boulder)│
       │ Sand Filter Layer    │
       └──────────────────────┘
SECTION: 
       Surface ═══════════
       Perforated Pipe (PVC) ●
       | Gravel Media        |
       | Sand Filter         |
       | Native Soil         |
```

---

**Model Solution to (b) — Recharge Trench Width:**

**Step 1 — Compute the peak hourly runoff from the roof.**

Assume a design storm intensity of 50 mm/hr (typical Kerala peak):

$$
Q_{peak} = C \times I_{peak} \times A = 0.85 \times 0.050 \times 200 = 8.5 \text{ m}^3 / \text{hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective infiltration rate of the trench with factor of safety.**

$$
v_{design} = 0.5 \times 0.015 = 0.0075 \text{ m/hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the wetted area required.**

$$
A_{wetted} = \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{v_{design}} = \dfrac{8.5}{0.0075} = 1133.3 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Set up the area equation for the trench.**

Wetted area of a trench = bottom + sidewalls:

$$
A_{wetted} = (L \times B) + (2 \times H \times L)
$$

Substituting L = 15 m and H = 2.5 m:

$$
1133.3 = (15 \times B) + (2 \times 2.5 \times 15)
$$

$$
1133.3 = 15B + 75
$$

$$
15B = 1058.3
$$

$$
B = \dfrac{1058.3}{15} \approx 70.55 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Practical engineering conclusion.**

A **70.55 m wide trench is impractical** for a residential plot. The correct KTU conclusion is:

> **"The required trench width of 70.55 m exceeds the available site footprint. The recharge system must be redesigned using multiple trenches, a recharge pit, or a recharge well to handle the catchment."**

This shows the examiner the student understands **both calculation and engineering judgment** — a hallmark of full-mark answers in KTU 2024.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Many students compute the answer and simply write the value (e.g., "B = 70.55 m") without commenting on its **practical feasibility**. Examiners specifically look for the **engineering conclusion** and deduct up to 2 marks if missing. Always end with a feasibility statement and a redesign suggestion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist — Important Points Relating to Water Storage and Recharging**

- [ ] **Two pillars:** Storage (saves water for later) and Recharge (replenishes aquifer).
- [ ] **Three critical Kerala design numbers:** 3000 mm annual rainfall, 100–120 day dry spell, 150 lpcd demand.
- [ ] **Runoff coefficient C = 0.85** for tiled/metal/concrete roofs (use 0.80 for asbestos, 0.70 for sloped clay tile).
- [ ] **First-flush:** Discard first 2–3 mm of every rain event to remove bird droppings, dust, leaves.
- [ ] **Storage tank sizing formula:** $V_{tank} = N \times D \times T_d$ — multiply occupants × per capita demand × dry days.
- [ ] **Standard tank ratios:** Rectangular underground → L:B:H = 2:1:1; Cylindrical → D:H = 2.5:1.
- [ ] **Recharge pit layers (top to bottom):** Coarse gravel → pebbles → sand+charcoal → bricks+boulders → native soil.
- [ ] **Recharge trench advantages:** Long, narrow, high wetted-area-to-volume ratio — best for lateritic Kerala.
- [ ] **Direct vs. indirect recharge:** Direct = bypasses soil (wells/shafts); Indirect = through soil (pits/trenches).
- [ ] **Always include a factor of safety of 2** on soil infiltration rates to account for siltation over time.
- [ ] **Overflow from storage tanks** should always be routed to a recharge structure — never wasted to a drain.
- [ ] **Design storm for recharge sizing:** 50 mm/hr peak intensity over 1 hour is the standard KTU assumption.
- [ ] **End every numerical answer** with: (i) the final dimension/capacity, (ii) feasibility statement, (iii) redesign suggestion if impractical.
- [ ] **CPHEEO Manual + Kerala Groundwater Department guidelines** are the recommended references; do not fabricate textbook names.
- [ ] **Kerala-specific hazards:** High water table in coastal districts (Kochi, Alappuzha) → avoid unlined pits; laterite soil → account for clay content in infiltration estimates.
- [ ] **Maintenance schedule:** Tanks — annual cleaning; Pits/Trenches — biannual desilting (pre- and post-monsoon).
- [ ] **Water quality:** Storage water for drinking MUST be disinfected (chlorination @ 1–2 mg/L or UV); recharge water needs only desilting + sand filtration.
- [ ] **Hybrid systems** (storage + recharge) are the **most KTU-favoured** answer for full 14-mark design questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rainwater Harvesting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rainwater Harvesting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rainwater Harvesting (RWH)** is the deliberate collection, conveyance, storage, treatment, and utilization of rainwater that falls on a defined catchment surface (such as rooftops, paved courtyards, or landscaped land) for the purpose of augmenting water supply, recharging groundwater aquifers, or directly meeting non-potable and (with appropriate treatment) potable end-use demands.

Mathematically, the harvested quantity is governed by the water balance identity:

$$Q_{harvested} = P \times A \times C_{r} \times \eta_{system}$$

where every term has a precise engineering meaning that will be derived in Section 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:**
> The course OECET832 explicitly covers the principles, components, design criteria, types, and implementation strategies of Rainwater Harvesting. Module 1 lays the foundation by introducing the basic terminology, the water scarcity context, the broad classifications, and the design philosophy. The student is expected to understand the *why* and the *what* before proceeding to hydrological design in later modules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Definition (Board Exam Ready):**
> "Rainwater Harvesting is a sustainable water management technique that intercepts precipitation on a catchment surface, channels it through a conveyance system, removes debris and contaminants, stores the clarified water in surface or subsurface reservoirs, and either uses it directly or recharges the groundwater table to combat water scarcity."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Account of Drops" Intuition

Imagine your rooftop is a giant **savings account** and every raindrop is a *currency note* that falls freely from the sky. In conventional urban systems, we let all this "money" slide straight off the roof, down the drain, and into the storm-water network — effectively **throwing it away**. Rainwater Harvesting is simply the act of **installing a collection tray (gutter) and a piggy bank (tank) under the roof** so that the "money" can be saved for a dry day.

| Common Analogy Element | Engineering Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Savings account | Catchment surface (roof, pavement) |
| Falling money | Precipitation (rainfall) |
| Piggy bank | Storage tank / recharge well |
| Cleaning the coins before saving | First-flush diverter & filtration |
| Withdrawing on a dry day | End-use distribution / aquifer recovery |

The intuition is powerful: *don't waste the rain that falls on your own land — capture it locally.*

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants Used in RWH

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics recur throughout KTU questions and must be memorized:
> - **Annual average rainfall in Kerala:** ≈ **3000 mm** (state average, highly variable from **≈ 1800 mm** in low-rainfall zones to **≈ 4000 mm** in highland districts).
> - **Density of water:** $\rho_w = \mathbf{1000 \; kg/m^3} = \mathbf{1 \; kg/L}$.
> - **1 cubic metre (1 m³) = 1000 litres = 10⁶ mL = 10³ L**.
> - **1 hectare = 10,000 m²**.
> - **1 mm of rainfall over 1 m² = 1 litre of water** (the single most useful rule-of-thumb in RWH).
> - **Standard runoff coefficient (Cᵣ) for sloped tiled/concrete roof** ≈ **0.8 to 0.9**.
> - **Standard runoff coefficient (Cᵣ) for paved courtyard** ≈ **0.6 to 0.7**.
> - **Standard runoff coefficient (Cᵣ) for unpaved land** ≈ **0.2 to 0.3**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why 1 mm × 1 m² = 1 Litre?**
> Volume = Area × Depth = 1 m² × (1 mm = 0.001 m) = 0.001 m³ = 1 L. This identity is the *backbone* of every RWH numerical and is guaranteed to appear in KTU calculations.

### 1.4 Classification — Broad Taxonomy of RWH Systems

```
Rainwater Harvesting
├── By End-Use
│   ├── Potable (drinking, cooking)
│   └── Non-Potable (washing, flushing, gardening)
├── By Storage Medium
│   ├── Surface Storage Tanks
│   ├── Underground Sump
│   └── Subsurface Aquifer Recharge
├── By Catchment Type
│   ├── Rooftop RWH
│   ├── Land / Surface Runoff RWH
│   └── Storm-water Harvesting
└── By Recharge Method
    ├── Recharge Pits
    ├── Recharge Trenches
    ├── Recharge Wells (Bore / Open)
    └── Percolation Tanks
```

This classification is the standard answer-grid for any "explain the types" question in KTU Part A.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Six Functional Subsystems

A complete RWH system is **not merely a tank**. It is a sequence of six engineered subsystems, each with a clear purpose. The student must remember the *logical chain* of water as it flows from sky to consumer (or to aquifer).

1. **Catchment Surface** — the first interception plane.
   - *Why?* Provides the impermeable or semi-permeable surface on which rain lands and from which it can be efficiently collected.
   - *How?* Material (RCC, GI sheet, tiled, asbestos, thatched) and slope govern the runoff coefficient $C_r$.

2. **Gutters and Down-pipes (Conveyance)** — the transport network.
   - *Why?* Move water from catchment to storage without spillage or contamination ingress.
   - *How?* Sized using hydrological intensity-duration-frequency (IDF) relations; slope ≥ 1 in 120 for self-cleaning.

3. **First-Flush Diverter** — the quality gatekeeper.
   - *Why?* The first 2–5 mm of rainfall washes accumulated dust, bird droppings, pollen, and leaf litter from the catchment, producing highly turbid, microbiologically contaminated water.
   - *How?* A simple manual or automatic valve that *diverts and discards* the initial dirty runoff before routing cleaner water to the tank.

4. **Filter Unit (Sand / Charcoal / Mesh)** — the polishing stage.
   - *Why?* Removes suspended solids, colour, and odour, prolonging storage life.
   - *How?* Layered bed: coarse gravel (bottom) → fine sand → activated charcoal (top), enclosed in a chamber.

5. **Storage Tank / Recharge Structure** — the buffer reservoir.
   - *Why?* Decouples the *intermittent supply* (rainfall is seasonal) from the *continuous demand* (water is needed year-round).
   - *How?* Surface RCC tank, underground sump, or recharge well / pit / trench transferring water to the aquifer.

6. **Draw-off / Distribution System** — the delivery interface.
   - *Why?* Connects the stored water to the point of use (tap, pump, irrigation line).
   - *How?* Gravity-fed from elevated tanks; pump-fed from underground sumps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "CGD-FSD" (Catchment, Gutter, Divert, Filter, Store, Draw):**
> When asked to "list the components of a rooftop RWH system", reproduce these six in order. Marks are awarded for *sequence* and *functional justification*, not just naming.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield, Exam-Ready)

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = P \times A \times C_r$ | Annual rainwater potential (litres) if $P$ in mm, $A$ in m² | Base potential estimation |
| 2 | $Q_{net} = Q \times \eta_{system}$ | Net harvestable water after losses | Accounting for first-flush + filter losses |
| 3 | $\text{Volume} = A \times d$ | Rainfall depth to volume | $1 \text{ mm} \times 1 \text{ m}^2 = 1 \text{ L}$ |
| 4 | $Q = A \times C \times I$ | Rational method for peak runoff | Peak gutter / pipe sizing |
| 5 | $\text{Capacity} = D_d \times N \times q$ | Storage tank sizing (days × persons × per-capita demand) | Sizing domestic tanks |
| 6 | $T = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{Q_{daily\ demand}}$ | Residence / payback time | Feasibility check |
| 7 | $C_r = \dfrac{\text{Runoff depth}}{\text{Rainfall depth}}$ | Definition of runoff coefficient | Direct numerical problems |
| 8 | $V_{recharge\ pit} = A_p \times h \times n$ | Volume of recharge pit (porosity $n$ for soil storage) | Design of recharge structures |
| 9 | $Q_{well} = \dfrac{2\pi k b s}{\ln(R/r_w)}$ | Darcy-Thiem well equation (for yield verification) | Aquifer-recharge feasibility |
| 10 | $\eta_{system} = 0.80 \text{ to } 0.90$ | Typical system efficiency after filtration | Net available water |

> [!NOTE]
> **Variable Definitions for the Cheat Sheet:**
> - $P$ = annual rainfall depth (mm) ; $A$ = horizontal projection of catchment area (m²)
> - $C_r$ = runoff coefficient (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
> - $\eta_{system}$ = overall system efficiency (accounts for first-flush, evaporation, leakage)
> - $C$ = runoff coefficient (rational form) ; $I$ = rainfall intensity (mm/hr or m/s)
> - $D_d$ = number of dry days to be sustained ; $N$ = number of users
> - $q$ = per-capita daily demand (lpcd, typically 135 lpcd in Kerala)
> - $k$ = hydraulic conductivity ; $b$ = aquifer thickness ; $s$ = drawdown

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

RWH is not a theoretical exercise. It is a **regulatory requirement** in many Indian states and a **practical lifeline** in Kerala's water-stressed midland and highland belts. The Indian Standard **IS 15797:2008** (and its revisions) provides the official design code for rooftop RWH; **CPWD** and **PWD Kerala** mandate RWH in all new government buildings; the **Ministry of Jal Shakti** has launched the *Jal Sanchay Jan Bhagidari* program explicitly leveraging RWH for groundwater replenishment.

For a Kerala house with a 100 m² tiled roof, an annual rainfall of 3000 mm, and $C_r = 0.85$:

$$Q = 3000 \times 100 \times 0.85 = 255{,}000 \text{ L/year} = 255 \text{ kL/year}$$

That single roof harvests enough water to meet the *entire non-potable demand* of a 4-member family for a year — a powerful illustration of why RWH is now mainstream engineering practice.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — The Annual Rainwater Harvest Potential (ARHP) Equation

**Statement (What we want to prove / derive):**
The annual volume of rainwater that can be captured from a horizontal catchment is given by:

$$Q_{annual} = P \times A \times C_r$$

**Given:**
- Annual rainfall depth at the location: $P$ (in mm)
- Horizontal projected area of the catchment: $A$ (in m²)
- Runoff coefficient of the catchment material: $C_r$ (dimensionless)

**Assumptions (to be stated explicitly in any KTU answer):**
1. The catchment is horizontal or its horizontal projection is taken (not the sloped surface area).
2. Rainfall is uniformly distributed over the catchment.
3. $C_r$ is a time-averaged, event-averaged constant for the catchment material.
4. Evaporation and leakage losses during the brief overland-flow period are subsumed in $C_r$.

**Step 1 — Convert rainfall depth from mm to metres:**

Because $1 \text{ mm} = \dfrac{1}{1000} \text{ m}$, the rainfall depth in metres is:

$$d = \dfrac{P}{1000} \text{ (metres)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total volume of rain that fell on the catchment:**

Using the geometric definition of volume (area × depth):

$$V_{rain} = A \times d = A \times \dfrac{P}{1000} \text{ (in m}^3\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the runoff coefficient:**

Only a fraction $C_r$ of the incident rainfall appears as surface runoff (the rest is lost to evaporation, depression storage on the rough surface, infiltration through porous tiles, and wind drift). Therefore:

$$Q_{annual} = C_r \times V_{rain} = C_r \times A \times \dfrac{P}{1000} \text{ (m}^3\text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to litres (the unit expected in KTU answers):**

Since $1 \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ L}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{annual} &= C_r \times A \times \dfrac{P}{1000} \times 1000 \\
&= C_r \times A \times P \text{ (in Litres)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\;Q_{annual} \text{ (Litres)} = P \text{ (mm)} \times A \text{ (m}^2\text{)} \times C_r\;}$$

**Verification by dimensional check:**
- $[mm] \times [m^2]$: since $1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, this becomes $10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$ per m² = **1 L per m² per mm of rain**. ✓
- Multiplied by dimensionless $C_r$, the result is in **Litres**. ✓

This identity is the single most-tested formula in the OECET832 paper.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Net Harvestable Water After System Losses

**Statement:**
The water actually delivered to the storage tank is less than the gross potential $Q_{annual}$ because of the first-flush diversion, filter head-loss, and pipe-network leakage. The net harvestable water is:

$$Q_{net} = Q_{annual} \times \eta_{system}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the three principal loss mechanisms:**

- First-flush diversion loss: typically **2 to 5 mm × A** of rain is discarded, i.e., a fraction ≈ 0.5% to 1% of annual rainfall.
- Filtration loss: 5% to 10% of the filtered water is retained as sludge / absorbed in the sand bed.
- Conveyance & evaporation loss: 5% to 8% combined (depending on pipe length, joints, ambient temperature).

**Step 2 — Combine the loss fractions:**

Let $f_1, f_2, f_3$ be the three loss fractions. The overall system efficiency is:

$$\eta_{system} = (1 - f_1)(1 - f_2)(1 - f_3)$$

**Step 3 — Apply standard KTU-assumed value:**

For a well-designed system: $f_1 = 0.01$, $f_2 = 0.07$, $f_3 = 0.06$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{system} &= (1 - 0.01)(1 - 0.07)(1 - 0.06) \\
&= 0.99 \times 0.93 \times 0.94 \\
&= 0.865 \approx 0.85
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Write the final expression:**

$$\boxed{\;Q_{net} = P \times A \times C_r \times \eta_{system}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The student must explicitly mention *what each loss fraction accounts for* in the KTU answer. Simply writing $Q_{net} = Q \times 0.85$ with no justification will lose marks.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Sizing the Storage Tank

**Statement:**
The required capacity of a domestic RWH storage tank is:

$$V_{tank} = D_d \times N \times q$$

**Step 1 — Define the parameters:**

- $D_d$ = design dry period (number of consecutive days with negligible rainfall that the tank must bridge). For Kerala, $D_d$ is typically **30 to 90 days** (longest dry spell during the South-West monsoon break).
- $N$ = number of beneficiaries (family members, students in a hostel, etc.)
- $q$ = per-capita daily demand in litres (lpcd). For non-potable use, Kerala PWD recommends **40 to 70 lpcd**; for total domestic use, **135 lpcd**.

**Step 2 — Compute total daily demand:**

$$Q_{day} = N \times q \quad (\text{L/day})$$

**Step 3 — Compute total demand over the dry period:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tank} &= Q_{day} \times D_d \\
&= N \times q \times D_d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply an economic and safety cap:**

In practice, $V_{tank}$ is capped between **5 kL and 25 kL** for residential buildings (terrace loadings) and sized to **not exceed the terrace's structural capacity**, generally ≤ 0.4 kL per m² of free terrace area.

> [!TIP]
> **Worked numerical check (typical KTU Part B sub-question):**
> For a 5-member family in Thrissur, $N = 5$, $q = 70$ lpcd, $D_d = 60$ days:
> $V_{tank} = 5 \times 70 \times 60 = 21{,}000 \text{ L} = 21 \text{ kL}$.
> This is feasible as an RCC sump (3 m × 3 m × 2.33 m) or a cylindrical HDPE tank of 3.7 m diameter × 2 m height.

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Verification Using the "1 mm × 1 m² = 1 L" Identity

**Statement:**
Show that the identity $1 \text{ mm} \times 1 \text{ m}^2 = 1 \text{ L}$ is dimensionally and numerically correct.

**Step 1 — Unit conversion of rainfall depth:**

$$1 \text{ mm} = 0.001 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the volume over 1 m²:**

$$V = A \times d = 1 \text{ m}^2 \times 0.001 \text{ m} = 0.001 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Convert m³ to litres:**

$$0.001 \text{ m}^3 \times 1000 \text{ L/m}^3 = 1 \text{ L} \quad \blacksquare$$

This identity is the *shortcut* used in every KTU numerical. Whenever a problem gives area in m² and rainfall in mm and asks for volume in litres, *multiply directly* — do not go through m³ unless required by the answer format.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — "Given a Parameter, Find the Variable"

| If the KTU question gives... | And asks for... | Use formula... | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P$, $A$, $C_r$ | Annual harvest $Q$ | $Q = P A C_r$ | Output in L if P in mm, A in m² |
| $P$, $A$, $C_r$, $\eta$ | Net harvest | $Q_{net} = P A C_r \eta$ | Apply loss factors |
| $D_d$, $N$, $q$ | Tank capacity | $V = D_d N q$ | Output in L |
| Annual $Q$, $q$, $N$ | Days of self-sufficiency | $T = Q / (N q)$ | Output in days |
| Roof slope, material | $C_r$ | Standard table | Memorize |
| Peak rainfall intensity, $A$, $C$ | Gutter peak discharge | $Q = C I A$ | Rational method |

This matrix is the *decision tree* a student should internalize before walking into the exam hall.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Rooftop Rainwater Harvesting — System Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end functional flow** of a typical residential rooftop RWH system, from rain-bearing cloud to storage / aquifer to end-use. It explicitly shows every component introduced in Section 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CLOUD Precipitation Source] -->|Rainfall P| B[ROOF CATCHMENT Surface]
    B -->|Runoff C_r × A| C[GUTTER Conveyance Channel]
    C -->|Piped flow| D[FIRST FLUSH DIVERTER Quality Gate]
    D -->|Discards initial 2 to 5 mm dirty water| D1[WASTE TO DRAIN]
    D -->|Clean water| E[FILTER UNIT Sand Charcoal Gravel]
    E -->|Polished water| F{TANK OR RECHARGE Decision}
    F -->|Storage path| G[SURFACE STORAGE TANK RCC or HDPE]
    F -->|Recharge path| H[RECHARGE PIT or BORE WELL Subsurface]
    G -->|Gravity or pump| I[END USE Domestic Non Potable]
    H -->|Infiltration| J[AQUIFER Groundwater Recharge]
    I --> K[USER Taps Wash Toilet Garden]
    J --> L[RECOVERY Hand Pump Bore Well Later]
    L --> K
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU Answer Grid):**

- Node **B → C** is where the runoff coefficient $C_r$ operates.
- Node **D** is the first-flush diverter; the branch **D → D1** is a *loss* pathway and is *intentionally* drawn to the drain to highlight the discards.
- Node **F** is the engineering decision diamond: *Do I store in a tank (immediate use) or recharge the aquifer (long-term storage)*? Both pathways are valid and both are usually implemented in parallel.
- Node **H → J → L** illustrates the *delayed-recovery* pathway unique to aquifer recharge.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — The Six Subsystem Pipeline

The following block architecture illustrates the *role* of each subsystem and the *information / quality / flow transformation* it performs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[SUBSYSTEM 1 CATCHMENT Collects rain] --> A2[SUBSYSTEM 2 CONVEYANCE Transports rain]
    A2 --> A3[SUBSYSTEM 3 DIVERSION Discards first flush]
    A3 --> A4[SUBSYSTEM 4 FILTRATION Polishes water]
    A4 --> A5[SUBSYSTEM 5 STORAGE Buffers supply]
    A5 --> A6[SUBSYSTEM 6 DISTRIBUTION Delivers to user]
    A6 --> A7[END USE Potable Non Potable Recharge]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark-Recovery Tip:** In any diagram question, label the *function* of each subsystem, not just the *name*. The marker is required to award the function-related 1 mark per box; merely drawing boxes with "Catchment", "Conveyance", "Filter", "Tank" will fetch only partial credit.

---

### 4.3 Decision Tree — "Which RWH System Do I Choose?"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Goal of project} -->|Augment domestic supply| Q2{Catchment type}
    Q1 -->|Improve groundwater| Q3{Soil type}
    Q1 -->|Stormwater management| Q4[STORM WATER DRAINAGE RWH]
    Q2 -->|Building roof| R1[ROOFTOP RWH TANK]
    Q2 -->|Open land paved| R2[LAND RWH POND]
    Q3 -->|Sandy permeable| R5[RECHARGE PIT TRENCH]
    Q3 -->|Clayey low k| R6[RECHARGE WELL BORE]
    R1 --> OUT1[Surface tank plus recharge pit hybrid]
    R2 --> OUT2[Percolation pond or check dam]
    R5 --> OUT3[Shallow pit 1 to 2 m deep]
    R6 --> OUT4[Deep bore well up to aquifer]
```

This decision tree is the recommended **flowchart to reproduce** in any KTU 14-marker that asks *"Design a rainwater harvesting system for a given site"*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> *Define rainwater harvesting. List any four components of a rooftop rainwater harvesting system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — board-style):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Rainwater harvesting is the technique of collecting and storing rainwater that falls on rooftops, land surfaces, or rock catchments, by means of engineered conveyance and storage systems, for direct use or for groundwater recharge.

**Four Components (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Catchment** — the roof or paved surface that intercepts rainfall.
2. **Gutters and down-pipes** — channels that transport water from the catchment to the storage point.
3. **First-flush diverter** — a device that discards the initial dirty runoff containing dust, leaves, and bird droppings.
4. **Filter unit** — a sand-charcoal-gravel chamber that removes suspended impurities before storage.

> [!NOTE]
> A student may also list *storage tank*, *recharge well*, *pump*, *distribution network*; any four correct, functionally-distinct items earn full marks.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series A]**
> *State the formula for the annual rainwater harvest potential. Explain the term "runoff coefficient" with two typical values.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$Q = P \times A \times C_r$$
where $Q$ = annual harvest volume (litres), $P$ = annual rainfall (mm), $A$ = catchment area (m²), $C_r$ = runoff coefficient.

**Runoff Coefficient Definition (1 mark):**
The runoff coefficient $C_r$ is the dimensionless ratio of the depth of rainwater that actually runs off the catchment to the depth of rainfall received on it. It depends on the surface material, slope, texture, and age.

**Two Typical Values (1 mark):**

- Sloped tiled or concrete roof: $C_r = 0.8$ to $0.9$
- Unpaved open ground: $C_r = 0.2$ to $0.3$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *(a)* Explain the various **components of a rooftop rainwater harvesting system** with the help of a neat sketch. Describe the function of each component. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* A residential building has a **roof area of 120 m²** located in a region receiving an **annual rainfall of 2800 mm**. The **runoff coefficient is 0.85** and the **system efficiency is 80%**. Compute:
> (i) the **annual rainwater harvest potential**,
> (ii) the **net water available** for use, and
> (iii) the **number of days** a 4-member family can be sustained if the per-capita demand is **70 lpcd**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The rooftop RWH system comprises six functional components, arranged in series along the flow path:

| # | Component | Function (Marks split 1 + 0.5) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Catchment** (RCC / tiled / GI roof) | Intercepts rainfall; provides the collecting surface. |
| 2 | **Gutters & Down-pipes** | Convey water from roof edge to the first-flush diverter; self-cleaning slope ≥ 1:120. |
| 3 | **First-Flush Diverter** | Diverts the initial 2–5 mm of contaminated runoff (wash-off of dust, droppings, pollen) to waste. |
| 4 | **Filter Chamber** | Layered bed (gravel → sand → activated charcoal) removes turbidity, colour, and odour. |
| 5 | **Storage Tank** (surface / sump) | Buffers seasonal supply against continuous demand; sized using $V = N \cdot q \cdot D_d$. |
| 6 | **Recharge Structure** (optional) | Transfers surplus water to subsurface aquifer through pits, trenches, or bore wells. |

**Neat sketch (2 marks):** Must show — roof → gutter → first-flush → filter → tank → use. Add arrows indicating flow direction; label the discards from the first-flush to the drain.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks (1 + 1 + 5 split; final numerical: 4 marks for arithmetic, 1 mark for units + interpretation):**

**Given:**
- $A = 120$ m²
- $P = 2800$ mm/year
- $C_r = 0.85$
- $\eta_{system} = 0.80$
- $N = 4$ persons
- $q = 70$ lpcd (litres per capita per day)

**Step 1 — Annual Harvest Potential (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{annual} &= P \times A \times C_r \\
&= 2800 \times 120 \times 0.85 \\
&= 2800 \times 102 \\
&= 285{,}600 \text{ Litres/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Net Water Available (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{net} &= Q_{annual} \times \eta_{system} \\
&= 285{,}600 \times 0.80 \\
&= 228{,}480 \text{ Litres/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Annual Domestic Demand (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{demand} &= N \times q \times 365 \\
&= 4 \times 70 \times 365 \\
&= 102{,}200 \text{ Litres/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of Days of Self-Sufficiency (2 marks):**

Since the family uses $4 \times 70 = 280$ L/day:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{Q_{net}}{N \times q} = \dfrac{228{,}480}{4 \times 70} \\
&= \dfrac{228{,}480}{280} \\
&= 816 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark for interpretation):** The harvested water is sufficient to meet the *entire non-potable demand* of the 4-member family for **816 days (≈ 2.2 years)**, demonstrating that rooftop RWH can supply the household independently even in a single average rainfall year.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> *(a)* Differentiate between **rooftop rainwater harvesting** and **surface runoff harvesting**. Mention the typical catchment area, storage structure, and end-use for each. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* A rural panchayat proposes a **percolation tank** on a 2-hectare catchment receiving **1500 mm annual rainfall**. The runoff coefficient is **0.35**. Compute the **annual runoff volume reaching the tank** in m³ and in litres. If the percolation tank recharges the aquifer at an average rate of **50 m³/day**, calculate the **number of days** the tank can sustain the recharge. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

| Parameter | Rooftop RWH | Surface Runoff RWH |
|---|---|---|
| **Catchment** | Building roof (RCC, tile, GI sheet) | Open land, paved courtyard, hilly terrain |
| **Typical area** | 50 – 500 m² (per household) | 0.5 – 50 hectares (community-scale) |
| **Storage structure** | Surface RCC tank or HDPE sump (1–25 kL) | Percolation pond / check dam / recharge pit |
| **First-flush needed?** | Yes (mandatory) | No / minimal (large area dilutes contaminants) |
| **Water quality** | Relatively clean → domestic non-potable use | Sediment-laden → recharges aquifer; rarely used directly |
| **End-use** | Washing, flushing, gardening, drinking (with treatment) | Groundwater recharge; irrigation via open wells |
| **Cost** | Low (household scale) | Moderate to high (community project) |

(Marks: 4 for tabular differentiation + 1 each for *typical catchment area* and *end-use* specified in the question + 1 for any one extra engineering distinction like cost or treatment.)

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $A = 2$ hectares $= 2 \times 10{,}000 = 20{,}000$ m²
- $P = 1500$ mm/year
- $C_r = 0.35$
- Recharge rate: $50$ m³/day

**Step 1 — Annual Runoff Volume in m³ (3 marks):**

First, convert rainfall depth to metres: $P = 1500 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \text{ m}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{m^3} &= A \times P \times C_r \\
&= 20{,}000 \times 1.5 \times 0.35 \\
&= 20{,}000 \times 0.525 \\
&= 10{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Litres (1 mark):**

$$
Q_{L} = 10{,}500 \times 1000 = 10{,}500{,}000 \text{ L/year} = 10.5 \text{ ML/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Days of Sustained Recharge (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{Q_{m^3}}{50 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}} \\
&= \dfrac{10{,}500}{50} \\
&= 210 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The single percolation tank can sustain aquifer recharge for **210 days per year (≈ 7 months)** at the design rate, effectively bridging Kerala's mid-monsoon dry spells and post-monsoon recovery.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Rainwater Harvesting Questions:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students frequently plug rainfall in *metres* but forget to multiply by 1000 to convert to litres, or use area in hectares without converting to m². Always state the units at every step.
> 2. **Confusing gross vs. net harvest:** Writing $Q = PAC_r$ and *then* $Q_{net} = Q \times \eta$ as a single line loses 1 mark. State *what* $\eta$ accounts for (first-flush + filter + leakage).
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply by 365:** When asked "number of days of self-sufficiency", the denominator is *daily demand* ($N \times q$), not annual demand. Dividing by annual demand gives a *dimensionless ratio* in years, not days.
> 4. **Runoff coefficient misread:** A flat concrete terrace is *not* the same as a sloped tiled roof. $C_r$ for a flat terrace with depression storage is ≈ 0.7; for a steeply-pitched tiled roof, ≈ 0.9.
> 5. **Sketch without labels:** A diagram with no flow arrows, no functional labels, and no first-flush branch is awarded *at most* 1 mark out of 2 for the drawing portion.
> 6. **Ignoring the first-flush in the design:** Examiners expect an explicit *numerical* (e.g., "2 mm first-flush over 120 m² = 240 L discarded per event") in design problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Rainwater Harvesting" (Module 1):**

- ☑ **Definition:** RWH = collection + conveyance + storage + utilization of rainwater from a catchment.
- ☑ **Master Formula:** $Q = P \times A \times C_r$ (P in mm, A in m² → Q in Litres).
- ☑ **Net formula:** $Q_{net} = P \times A \times C_r \times \eta_{system}$.
- ☑ **Quick rule:** 1 mm × 1 m² = 1 L of water.
- ☑ **Kerala annual rainfall range:** ≈ 1800 – 4000 mm; use **3000 mm** as a state-average default.
- ☑ **Runoff coefficient values (memorize):**
  - Tiled / concrete sloped roof → 0.80 to 0.90
  - Flat terrace → 0.70
  - Paved courtyard → 0.60 to 0.70
  - Unpaved ground → 0.20 to 0.30
- ☑ **Six components (sequence CGD-FSD):** Catchment → Gutter → Diverter (first flush) → Filter → Store → Draw-off.
- ☑ **First-flush volume:** 2 to 5 mm of rainfall × catchment area = litres to be discarded.
- ☑ **Filter bed layers (bottom → top):** Gravel → Sand → Activated Charcoal.
- ☑ **Tank sizing:** $V = N \times q \times D_d$ (with $q \approx 70$ lpcd for non-potable Kerala use).
- ☑ **System efficiency** $\eta_{system} \approx 0.80$ to $0.90$ for a well-designed installation.
- ☑ **Types of RWH:** Rooftop, Land/Surface runoff, Stormwater; Storage-tank vs. Aquifer-recharge.
- ☑ **Recharge structures:** Recharge pit, recharge trench, recharge well, percolation tank.
- ☑ **Indian Standard:** IS 15797:2008 (Roof Top Rainwater Harvesting — Code of Practice).
- ☑ **Kerala context:** RWH is *mandatory* in buildings above a threshold plinth area (as per KMBR / PWD notification) and is a key element of the state **Jal Jeevan Mission** strategy.
- ☑ **Conversion identities to memorize:**
  - 1 ha = 10,000 m²
  - 1 m³ = 1000 L
  - 1 mm = 10⁻³ m
  - $\rho_{water}$ = 1000 kg/m³
- ☑ **Valuation-safe habit:** Always state assumptions (e.g., "assuming horizontal projection of the roof", "assuming average losses of 15%") *before* writing the formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of Rainwater Harvesting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components of Rainwater Harvesting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rainwater Harvesting (RWH)** is the technique of collecting, conveying, storing, filtering, and utilizing rainwater that falls on a catchment surface (typically a roof or paved area) for various purposes such as domestic use, groundwater recharge, and landscape irrigation. The *components* of a rainwater harvesting system refer to the engineered structural and hydraulic elements that work in series to transform a precipitation event into a usable, treated, and consumable water resource.

As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, the components are broadly classified into:

1. **Catchment / Collection Surface**
2. **Conveyance System (Gutters, Downpipes, Drains)**
3. **First-Flush Diverter**
4. **Filtration Units (Mesh, Sand, Charcoal, Cartridge)**
5. **Storage Structures (Sumps, Tanks, Cisterns)**
6. **Recharge Structures (Pits, Trenches, Shafts, Recharge Wells)**
7. **Distribution and Treatment System (Pumps, Plumbing, Disinfection)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Students are expected to identify each component, describe its hydraulic function, sketch its schematic position in the system, and apply design formulas for sizing key elements such as gutters, first-flush volume, filter area, and storage tank capacity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a **rainwater harvesting system as a "personal bank account" for a building**:

- The **roof** is the *salary* that nature deposits during the monsoon.
- The **gutters and downpipes** are the *salary slip* and the *cash counter* that pass the deposit on.
- The **first-flush diverter** is the *deduction* for the dusty start of the month — you discard the first soiled coins.
- The **filters** are the *currency-checking machine* that rejects damaged notes (debris, leaves, dust).
- The **storage tank** is the *fixed deposit account* where the money is locked away for the dry summer.
- The **pump and plumbing** is the *ATM network* that lets you withdraw clean water whenever needed.
- The **recharge structure** is *reinvesting* surplus back into the groundwater table — earning "interest" for the entire neighbourhood.

This way, every component has a specific financial role, and omitting one breaks the chain of value.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

- **First-Flush Diversion Depth (D):** typically **2 mm** of rainfall (IS 14961:2001 / CPHEEO manual).
- **Roof Runoff Coefficient (C):** **0.7 to 0.9** (for impervious concrete, tile, metal roofs).
- **Design Rainfall Intensity (I):** **50 mm/hr to 100 mm/hr** for short-duration events in Kerala.
- **Recommended Storage Factor:** storage of **1 month** of dry-season demand as a working rule.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term to Memorize for KTU Exam:**
> *Catchment Efficiency (η) = (Runoff Volume / Rainfall Volume) × 100 %.*
> This single ratio is the cornerstone of every RWH design question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Component-by-Component Engineering Analysis

**A. Catchment / Collection Surface**
- The **first interface** between precipitation and the engineered system.
- Material options: RCC roof, asbestos sheet, galvanized iron (GI) sheet, tiled roof, paved courtyard.
- Governing parameter: **Runoff Coefficient (C)** which represents the fraction of rainfall that becomes runoff.
- Logic chain: *Rain hits surface → some evaporates (loss) → some infiltrates into porous material (loss) → remainder flows as runoff to gutter.*
- Why it matters: C directly scales the harvestable volume; under-estimating C leads to **undersized** tanks, over-estimating leads to **cost overrun**.

**B. Conveyance System (Gutters & Downpipes)**
- **Gutters** are horizontal channels fixed at the roof edge (eaves).
- **Downpipes** are vertical pipes carrying water from gutter to ground level.
- Sizing logic: they must handle the *peak rainfall intensity* for the design storm (typically 15-min peak for Kerala).
- Material options: PVC, GI, uPVC, asbestos cement (legacy).
- Hydraulic principle: **Manning's equation** for open channel flow in gutters, **Hagen-Poiseuille / Darcy-Weisbach** for full-bore pipe flow.

**C. First-Flush Diverter**
- A simple chamber or pipe segment that *discards the initial dirty water* containing dust, bird droppings, leaves, and accumulated pollutants.
- Diversion volume is calculated as a function of roof area.
- Two types: **Manual (ball-valve)** and **Automatic (floating ball with tipping mechanism)**.
- Engineering "why": the first 2 mm of rain washes ~80 % of the dry-deposited pollutants from a roof.

**D. Filtration Units**
- Removes suspended particles, fine debris, microbial load (partial), and turbidity.
- Types in series: **Mesh Filter (40–60 mesh)** → **Sand Filter (slow sand)** → **Activated Charcoal** → **UV / Chlorination**.
- The filter is sized on **filtration velocity (V_f)**, typically 0.1 to 0.3 m³/(m²·hr) for slow sand filters.

**E. Storage Structures**
- Surface sumps, underground masonry tanks, RCC overhead tanks, HDPE / PVC modular tanks, ferro-cement tanks.
- Sized on **demand-side mass balance** (Mays's graphical method or analytical mass curve).
- Critical design parameter: **dead storage volume** to prevent pump running dry.

**F. Recharge Structures**
- Used when objective is **groundwater augmentation** rather than direct storage.
- Common in Kerala because of laterite soil and fractured aquifers.
- Types: Recharge Pit, Recharge Trench, Recharge Shaft, Injection Well, Percolation Tank.

**G. Distribution & Treatment**
- Submersible / monoblock pump, riser pipe, water meter, and a small disinfection unit.
- For drinking water: **chlorination (1–2 mg/L residual Cl₂)** or **UV exposure (≥ 40 mJ/cm²)** is mandatory.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | Typical KTU Value |
|---|-----------|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Peak Runoff Rate (Rational Method) | $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $I$ in m/s, $A$ in m² | — |
| 2 | Annual Harvestable Volume | $V = P \cdot A \cdot C_r$ | $V$ in m³, $P$ in m, $A$ in m² | — |
| 3 | First-Flush Diversion Volume | $V_{ff} = D \cdot A_{roof}$ | $V_{ff}$ in L, $D$ in mm, $A_{roof}$ in m² | $D = 2$ mm |
| 4 | Gutter Flow Capacity (Manning) | $Q = \tfrac{1}{n} A_c R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | $A_c$ cross-section, $R$ hydraulic radius, $S$ slope | $n = 0.012$ (PVC) |
| 5 | Downpipe Diameter (DARCY) | $Q = \tfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} \cdot v$ | $v \le 1.5$ m/s (recommended) | — |
| 6 | Sand Filter Area | $A_f = Q / V_f$ | $V_f$ = 0.1–0.3 m/hr | $V_f = 0.2$ m/hr |
| 7 | Storage Tank Volume (Demand) | $V_t = N \cdot q \cdot T_d$ | $N$ users, $q$ L/capita·d, $T_d$ days | $q = 135$ LPCD |
| 8 | Recharge Pit Volume | $V_{pit} = A_{pit} \cdot d \cdot n$ | $A_{pit}$ area, $d$ depth, $n$ porosity | $n = 0.30$ (gravel) |
| 9 | Draw-off Time | $T = V_t / (N \cdot q)$ | Used for storage reliability check | — |
| 10 | Catchment Efficiency | $\eta = (V_{runoff} / V_{rain}) \cdot 100$ | Expressed in % | 70–90 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Conversion Tip for KTU Numericals:**
> $1 \text{ mm rainfall over } 1 \text{ m}^2 = 1 \text{ Litre of water.}$
> This single conversion shortens 70 % of RWH design problems in the exam.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic RWH** in Kerala homes reduces municipal water bill by 30–50 %.
- **Industrial RWH** at factories (e.g., Cochin Shipyard, Kerala Minerals) reduces raw-water drawal from public supply.
- **Urban RWH** in apartments supports fire reserves and landscape irrigation.
- **Recharge RWH** in schools (under Jal Shakti Abhiyan) helps replenish village wells.
- **Rural RWH** integrated with *MGNREGA* ponds recharges open wells in Kasaragod, Wayanad, and Palakkad.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Harvestable Annual Volume from a Roof

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the volume of water that can be harvested annually from a given roof catchment.

**Given:**
- Catchment (roof) area = $A$ (m²)
- Annual rainfall depth = $P$ (mm)
- Runoff coefficient = $C_r$ (dimensionless, $0 \le C_r \le 1$)

**Step 1 — Express rainfall depth in metres:**
$$P_{m} = \frac{P}{1000} \quad \text{(metres)}$$

**Step 2 — Total rainfall volume falling on the roof:**

The volume of rain intercepted by the roof is area × depth.

$$V_{rain} = A \cdot P_{m} = \frac{A \cdot P}{1000} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the runoff coefficient:**

Only a fraction $C_r$ of the incident rainfall appears as runoff; the rest is lost to evaporation, interception by leaves, and roof absorption.

$$V_{runoff} = C_r \cdot V_{rain}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3:**

$$V_{runoff} = C_r \cdot \frac{A \cdot P}{1000}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to litres for engineering convenience:**

Since $1 \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ L}$,

$$V_{runoff} = C_r \cdot A \cdot P \quad \text{(Litres)}$$

This compact form is the form students should memorize.

$$\boxed{V_{runoff} \; (\text{L}) = C_r \cdot A \; (\text{m}^2) \cdot P \; (\text{mm})}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — First-Flush Volume to be Diverted

**Statement:** Derive the diversion volume required to discard the first polluting wash from a roof.

**Given:**
- Roof area $A$ (m²)
- Diversion depth $D$ (mm) = first 2 mm of rainfall to be discarded

**Step 1 — Express diversion depth in metres:**

$$D_m = \frac{D}{1000}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of water in the first $D$ mm over area $A$:**

$$V_{ff} = A \cdot D_m = \frac{A \cdot D}{1000} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to litres:**

$$V_{ff} = A \cdot D \quad \text{(Litres)}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical form for $D = 2$ mm:**

$$\boxed{V_{ff} = 2 A \quad \text{(Litres, when } A \text{ is in m}^2\text{)}}$$

This is the most-asked expression in KTU Module 1 numericals.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sizing a Slow Sand Filter

**Statement:** Derive the filter surface area required to treat a given peak inflow.

**Given:**
- Design inflow rate $Q$ (m³/hr)
- Filtration velocity $V_f$ (m/hr), typically 0.1 to 0.3 m/hr for slow sand

**Step 1 — Continuity principle:**

The volume entering the filter per hour must equal volume passing through the sand bed.

$$Q = A_f \cdot V_f$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the filter area:**

$$A_f = \frac{Q}{V_f}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $V_f = 0.2$ m/hr (typical design value):**

$$\boxed{A_f = \frac{Q}{0.2} = 5Q \quad \text{(m}^2\text{)}}$$

**Step 4 — Example numerical trace:**

If $Q = 2$ m³/hr, then $A_f = 5 \times 2 = 10$ m². A square filter would be $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.16$ m on each side. This is the kind of one-line derivation a KTU 7-mark sub-part expects.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Downpipe Diameter from Continuity

**Statement:** Derive the minimum downpipe diameter for a given peak runoff.

**Given:**
- Peak runoff $Q$ (m³/s)
- Recommended flow velocity $v = 1.5$ m/s (to avoid noise and erosion)

**Step 1 — Continuity equation for full-bore pipe flow:**

$$Q = A_{pipe} \cdot v = \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} \cdot v$$

**Step 2 — Solve for diameter $d$:**

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{4 Q}{\pi v}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $v = 1.5$ m/s:**

$$\boxed{d = \sqrt{\frac{4 Q}{1.5 \pi}} = 0.92 \sqrt{Q} \quad \text{(m, with } Q \text{ in m}^3\text{/s)}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical check:**

If $Q = 0.001$ m³/s (= 1 L/s), then $d = 0.92 \times \sqrt{0.001} = 0.029$ m = 29 mm. Choose next standard size, **40 mm PVC**.

### 3.5 Worked Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Recharge Structures

| Structure | Best Soil Type | Typical Size | Suitability in Kerala | Maintenance |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|------------------------|-------------|
| Recharge Pit | Loam / Sandy Loam | 1–3 m deep, 1–2 m dia | Moderate (laterite caps the pit) | Low — annual desilting |
| Recharge Trench | Sandy / Gravelly | 0.5–1.5 m deep, 1–5 m long | High (works in laterite) | Moderate |
| Recharge Shaft | Deep aquifers needed | 5–15 m deep, 300 mm dia | Excellent (fractured rock below 3 m) | Low |
| Injection Well | Confined aquifer | 100–300 mm dia, ≥ 10 m | Excellent in coastal sandy zones | Moderate |
| Percolation Tank | Large catchment (>1 ha) | 5–10 m deep pond | Good for schools, panchayats | High (desilting) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram — RWH System Architecture

The following **Mermaid block diagram** shows how the components interlink in a typical urban rooftop RWH system serving both storage and groundwater recharge.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[RAINFALL EVENT] --> B1[ROOF CATCHMENT SURFACE]
    B1 --> B2[GUTTER NETWORK AT EAVES]
    B2 --> B3[DOWNPIPE CONVEYANCE]
    B3 --> C1[FIRST FLUSH DIVERTER UNIT]
    C1 --> D1[LEAF AND MESH SCREEN FILTER]
    D1 --> E1[SAND AND CHARCOAL FILTER UNIT]
    E1 --> F1[STORAGE TANK OR SUMP]
    F1 --> G1[SUBMERSIBLE PUMP]
    G1 --> H1[DISINFECTION UNIT CHLORINE OR UV]
    H1 --> I1[DOMESTIC DISTRIBUTION NETWORK]
    F1 --> J1[OVERFLOW PIPE TO REJECT EXCESS]
    J1 --> K1[RECHARGE PIT WITH GRAVEL MEDIA]
    K1 --> L1[RECHARGE TRENCH PERFORATED PIPE]
    L1 --> M1[RECHARGE SHAFT INTO DEEP AQUIFER]
    M1 --> N1[GROUNDWATER TABLE RECHARGE]
    F1 --> O1[OVERHEAD TANK FOR GRAVITY SUPPLY]
    O1 --> I1
    I1 --> P1[END USER POINTS TAPS TOILETS GARDEN]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram — Water Path in RWH

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[INPUT PRECIPITATION] --> stepB[INTERCEPTION ON CATCHMENT]
    stepB --> stepC[RUNOFF GENERATION USING C FACTOR]
    stepC --> stepD[COLLECTION IN GUTTERS]
    stepD --> stepE[VERTICAL CONVEYANCE DOWNPIPE]
    stepE --> stepF[POLLUTION REMOVAL FIRST FLUSH]
    stepF --> stepG[PARTICULATE REMOVAL FILTRATION]
    stepG --> stepH[STORAGE ALLOCATION TANK OR RECHARGE]
    stepH --> stepI[DISINFECTION BEFORE USE]
    stepI --> stepJ[RELIABLE WATER SUPPLY END USE]
    stepH --> stepK[GROUNDWATER RECHARGE PATH]
    stepK --> stepL[AQUIFER RECOVERY INCREASED YIELD]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Cross-Section of a Recharge Pit (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layerA[SURFACE GRADE WITH SLOPE TOWARDS PIT] --> layerB[INLET PIPE FROM OVERFLOW]
    layerB --> layerC[GEOTEXTILE OR MESH LAYER]
    layerC --> layerD[COARSE GRAVEL 20 TO 40 MM]
    layerD --> layerE[MEDIUM GRAVEL 10 TO 20 MM]
    layerE --> layerF[FINE GRAVEL 5 TO 10 MM]
    layerF --> layerG[COARSE SAND LAYER]
    layerG --> layerH[CHARCOAL LAYER OPTIONAL]
    layerH --> layerI[NATURAL SUBGRADE LATERITE OR SOIL]
    layerI --> layerJ[VADOSE ZONE UNSATURATED]
    layerJ --> layerK[GROUNDWATER TABLE SATURATED ZONE]
    layerD --> layerV[VENT PIPE FOR AIR RELEASE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exam:**
> The examiner often asks: *"Sketch and label the components of a rooftop RWH system."* A clean Mermaid-style block diagram with all 7–9 components labelled, plus arrow directions, fetches full 7 marks even if drawn freehand.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List and briefly explain the three essential components of any rooftop rainwater harvesting system.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The three essential components are:
1. **Catchment Surface** — the roof, which intercepts rainfall and generates runoff governed by the runoff coefficient $C_r$.
2. **Conveyance System** — gutters and downpipes that transport runoff from the catchment to the storage or recharge point.
3. **Storage / Recharge Structure** — the tank, sump, pit, trench, or shaft where the harvested water is held or infiltrated.

*(1 mark per component with functional explanation. Total 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a first-flush diverter? Why is it necessary in a rainwater harvesting system?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A first-flush diverter is a device that **diverts the initial 2 mm of rainfall** away from the storage tank, since the first wash carries the highest concentration of dust, leaves, bird droppings, and atmospheric pollutants accumulated on the roof during the dry period. It is necessary to (i) **reduce suspended solids and turbidity** entering the tank, (ii) **lower microbial load**, and (iii) **extend the life of downstream filters**. The volume discarded is $V_{ff} = D \cdot A$ litres, where $D$ is the diversion depth and $A$ is the roof area.

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks for reasons. Total 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic diagram, describe the **components of a domestic rooftop rainwater harvesting system** with both storage and groundwater recharge options. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Catchment (1 Mark):**
The sloped roof acts as the catchment. Tiled / metal roofs have $C_r = 0.8$ to $0.9$. The roof is graded towards the gutter line.

**Step 2 — Gutter and Downpipe (1 Mark):**
A semi-circular PVC gutter of 100–150 mm width is fixed at the eaves. Downpipes of 75–100 mm dia carry water vertically to the first-flush unit.

**Step 3 — First-Flush Diverter (1 Mark):**
