A 100–150 mm dia vertical chamber, sized using $V_{ff} = 2A$ litres, discards the first wash. A manual ball-valve or auto-tipping mechanism resets the diverter.

**Step 4 — Filters (1 Mark):**
In series: a 40-mesh screen (debris) → slow sand filter (turbidity) → activated charcoal (odour / organics). Filter area $A_f = Q / V_f$ with $V_f = 0.2$ m/hr.

**Step 5 — Storage Tank (1 Mark):**
Underground RCC sump of capacity $V_t = N \cdot q \cdot T_d$. For a 4-member family, $T_d = 30$ days, $q = 135$ LPCD gives $V_t = 4 \times 135 \times 30 = 16\,200$ L.

**Step 6 — Recharge Structure (1 Mark):**
Overflow pipe leads to a recharge pit 1.5 m dia × 2 m deep, filled with graded gravel ($n = 0.30$).

**Step 7 — Pump and Disinfection (1 Mark):**
A 0.5 HP submersible pump lifts water to an overhead tank. A 1 mg/L chlorine dose or 40 mJ/cm² UV ensures potability.

*Neat labelled diagram = 2 additional marks awarded under presentation.*

---

**(b)** A residential building in Kottayam has a **roof area of 120 m²** and receives an **annual rainfall of 2700 mm**. The **runoff coefficient is 0.85**. The household has **5 members** with a daily demand of **135 LPCD**.
Calculate: (i) the **annual harvestable volume**, (ii) the **first-flush volume** to be diverted, and (iii) the **storage tank capacity** for a 30-day storage. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Annual Harvestable Volume (3 Marks):**

$$V_{runoff} = C_r \cdot A \cdot P = 0.85 \times 120 \times 2700$$

$$V_{runoff} = 275\,400 \text{ L} = 275.4 \text{ m}^3$$

*(1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark final answer with unit.)*

**(ii) First-Flush Volume (2 Marks):**

$$V_{ff} = 2 \cdot A = 2 \times 120 = 240 \text{ L}$$

*(1 mark formula, 1 mark answer.)*

**(iii) Storage Tank Capacity (2 Marks):**

$$V_t = N \cdot q \cdot T_d = 5 \times 135 \times 30 = 20\,250 \text{ L} \approx 20.25 \text{ m}^3$$

*(1 mark formula, 1 mark answer.)*

**Conclusion:** A 21 m³ underground RCC sump with a 240 L first-flush diverter is sufficient to meet 30 days of full demand and harvest 275 m³ annually.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **types of recharge structures** used in rainwater harvesting. State the conditions under which each is preferred. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Recharge Pit** (2 Marks): A square or circular excavation 1–3 m deep, filled with graded gravel-sand. Suitable where the water table is moderately deep (>5 m) and surface soil has medium permeability. Common in loamy soils of central Kerala.

2. **Recharge Trench** (2 Marks): A long shallow trench (0.5–1.5 m deep, 1–5 m wide, 5–20 m long) filled with boulders and gravel, often with a perforated pipe. Best for low-permeability zones like laterite caps; widely used in Wayanad and Idukki.

3. **Recharge Shaft** (1 Mark): A borehole 100–300 mm dia, drilled 5–15 m deep through impervious strata to reach fractured rock. Suitable where deep permeable aquifers exist, such as in Changanassery and Kanjirappally.

4. **Injection Well** (1 Mark): A tube well with a delivery pipe and gravel pack; used to recharge confined aquifers. Common in coastal sandy zones of Alappuzha and Ernakulam.

5. **Percolation Tank** (1 Mark): A large earthen embankment pond that holds water for natural infiltration. Suitable for community / school buildings with catchment >1 ha.

---

**(b)** For an institutional building in Thrissur with a **roof catchment of 800 m²** and a **design rainfall intensity of 90 mm/hr**, determine: (i) the **peak runoff rate** using the rational method (assume $C = 0.85$), (ii) the **downpipe diameter** for a flow velocity of 1.5 m/s, and (iii) the **slow sand filter area** if the daily demand is 10 m³. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Peak Runoff Rate — Rational Method (3 Marks):**

Convert units: $I = 90$ mm/hr = $90 / 3600$ m/s = $0.000\,025$ m/s.
$$A = 800 \text{ m}^2, \quad C = 0.85$$

$$Q = C \cdot I \cdot A = 0.85 \times 0.000\,025 \times 800$$

$$Q = 0.017 \text{ m}^3\text{/s} = 17 \text{ L/s}$$

*(1 mark formula, 1 mark conversion, 1 mark final answer.)*

**(ii) Downpipe Diameter (2 Marks):**

$$d = 0.92 \sqrt{Q} = 0.92 \sqrt{0.017} = 0.92 \times 0.1304 = 0.120 \text{ m}$$

Choose next standard size = **125 mm dia PVC downpipe**.

*(1 mark formula, 1 mark answer with standard-size comment.)*

**(iii) Slow Sand Filter Area (2 Marks):**

$$Q_{daily} = 10 \text{ m}^3\text{/day} = 10 / 24 = 0.417 \text{ m}^3\text{/hr}$$

$$A_f = \frac{Q}{V_f} = \frac{0.417}{0.2} = 2.08 \text{ m}^2 \approx 2.1 \text{ m}^2$$

A square filter of side $\sqrt{2.08} = 1.44$ m is sufficient.

*(1 mark formula, 1 mark final area.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion mistakes** cost 2–3 marks per numerical — always state $1 \text{ mm over } 1 \text{ m}^2 = 1 \text{ L}$.
> 2. **Omitting the runoff coefficient** in annual-harvest problems is the single most common error; examiners deduct 1 mark explicitly for "missing $C_r$".
> 3. **Not choosing a standard pipe size** in downpipe questions is marked down. If you calculate 120 mm, write *“choose 125 mm standard PVC”* to grab the full 2 marks.
> 4. **Label all 7 components** in a freehand diagram question — examiners use a tick-list. A missing arrow direction or label = 0.5 mark cut.
> 5. **Don't confuse** first-flush *depth* (mm) with first-flush *volume* (L). The depth is on the roof, the volume is the diverted water.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Catchment** → roof, paved area; governed by $C_r = 0.7$–$0.9$ for roofs.
- **Gutters** → Manning's equation, PVC/uPVC, half-round preferred.
- **Downpipes** → $d = 0.92 \sqrt{Q}$, design velocity $1.5$ m/s, **choose next standard size**.
- **First-Flush** → $V_{ff} = 2A$ litres, divert first 2 mm.
- **Filters** → Mesh → Sand → Charcoal → UV/Cl₂; filter area $A_f = Q / V_f$, $V_f = 0.2$ m/hr.
- **Storage** → $V_t = N \cdot q \cdot T_d$; standard demand 135 LPCD (India) / 150 LPCD (Kerala design).
- **Recharge** → Pit, Trench, Shaft, Injection Well, Percolation Tank; porosity of gravel = 0.30.
- **Conversion:** $1 \text{ mm on } 1 \text{ m}^2 = 1 \text{ L}$ — **always remember**.
- **Rational Method** $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ is the *anchor formula* for any peak-flow question.
- **Catchment Efficiency** $\eta = (V_{runoff}/V_{rain}) \times 100$ is a 2-mark direct question waiting to happen.
- For KTU, the answer must always close with a **practical recommendation** (e.g., “*a 21 m³ sump with 125 mm downpipe is recommended*”) — this fetches the conclusion mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Catchment Area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Catchment Area

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Catchment Area** (also termed as **Watershed**, **Drainage Basin**, or **Catchment Basin**) is the topographical surface — comprising roofs, pavements, open land, or natural terrain — from which precipitation flows across the ground surface (as **surface runoff**) or beneath (as **sub-surface flow**) toward a common collection point, such as a storage tank, recharge pit, reservoir, or stream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> *The catchment area is the geographic area, bounded by a divide (watershed ridge), that contributes all of its precipitation, surface runoff, and sub-surface flow to a single outlet or drainage system. In the context of Rainwater Harvesting (RWH), it is the receiving surface from which rainfall is intercepted, collected, and channeled to the storage/recharge structure.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine an **inverted umbrella** held out in the rain:
- The **fabric of the umbrella** = the **catchment area** (catches the rain).
- The **central shaft / handle** = the **conveyance system** (gutters, pipes).
- The **bucket placed at the bottom** = the **storage tank / recharge pit**.

> [!NOTE]
> A *larger* catchment area means *more rain is intercepted*, just like a *larger umbrella catches more drops*. However, a *slippery (impervious) surface* catches more water than a *sandy (pervious) surface*, because much of the water soaks into the ground on rough terrain — similar to water slipping off a waxed raincoat versus being absorbed by a sponge.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus-Aligned)

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Catchment Area (A)** | The horizontal projected area (in m² or hectares) that receives rainfall. |
| **Watershed** | Synonym for catchment area; often used for larger natural basins. |
| **Runoff Coefficient (C or η)** | The dimensionless ratio of runoff depth to rainfall depth. |
| **Topographical Divide** | The ridge line that separates two adjoining catchments. |
| **Sub-catchment** | A smaller, nested catchment within a larger basin. |
| **Effective Catchment Area (A_eff)** | Area after deducting portions that are non-contributing (e.g., flat zones, pervious losses). |
| **Impervious Surface** | A surface that resists water infiltration (concrete, metal, tile roofs). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Units (KTU 2024):**
> Catchment area is universally measured in **square meters (m²)**, **hectares (ha)** where **1 ha = 10,000 m²**, or **km²** for large basins.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic: How a Catchment Area Functions

The catchment area operates on a sequential hydrological logic. Each step is critical for RWH design:

1. **Precipitation Input** → Rain (or snowmelt) falls uniformly on the projected horizontal area.
2. **Interception** → A small fraction is lost to evaporation from the wet surface (typically **0.5 mm – 2 mm** for roofs).
3. **Depression Storage** → Water fills micro-pockets and depressions (significant on flat roofs and unpaved surfaces, often **2 mm – 5 mm**).
4. **Infiltration** → On pervious surfaces, water seeps into the soil (zero on impervious roofs).
5. **Surface Runoff Generation** → Excess water flows over the surface as **runoff (Q)**, governed by the *Manning's equation* overland or by *sheet flow* dynamics.
6. **Conveyance** → Runoff is channeled through gutters, downpipes, and drains.
7. **Delivery to Collection Point** → The water enters the storage tank or recharge structure.
8. **First-Flush Diversion** → The initial dirty runoff is discarded (typically first **2 mm – 5 mm** of rainfall).

## 2.2 Classification of Catchment Areas (KTU Board-Relevant)

### A. Based on Surface Type
- **Rooftop Catchments** → Sloped roofs (tile, RCC, metal, asbestos) — most common in urban RWH.
- **Pavement / Ground-Level Catchments** → Courtyards, driveways, parks.
- **Natural Catchments** → Hill slopes, forested terrain — used in rural/macro RWH.

### B. Based on Permeability
- **Impervious Catchment** (η = 0.7 – 0.9) → Roofs, concrete.
- **Semi-pervious Catchment** (η = 0.4 – 0.6) → Brick, stone-packed surfaces.
- **Pervious Catchment** (η = 0.1 – 0.3) → Lawns, agricultural land.

### C. Based on Scale
- **Micro Catchment** (1 m² – 500 m²) → Single house/building.
- **Mini Catchment** (500 m² – 10 ha) → Small community/society.
- **Macro Catchment** (>10 ha) → Village, watershed, municipality.

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Catchment Sizing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The catchment area is the **engineer's "input dial"** in RWH design. Yield scales **linearly with area** but **sub-linearly with efficiency**. Doubling the roof does not double usable water if conveyance losses are high.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Standard Values |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V = A \times P \times C$ | **Annual Water Harvesting Potential** (volume) | $V$ in litres, $A$ in m², $P$ in mm, $C$ = runoff coefficient (dimensionless) |
| 2 | $V = A \times P \times C \times 10^{-3}$ | Same as above with $V$ in m³ | $V$ in m³, $A$ in m², $P$ in mm |
| 3 | $Q = C \cdot i \cdot A$ | **Rational Method** (peak runoff rate) | $Q$ in m³/s, $i$ = rainfall intensity (m/s or mm/hr), $A$ in hectares |
| 4 | $A_{eff} = A_{total} \times \eta_{surface}$ | **Effective Catchment Area** | Both in m²; $\eta_{surface}$ = surface efficiency factor |
| 5 | $Q = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | **Manning's Equation** (overland/open channel flow) | $Q$ in m³/s, $n$ = Manning's roughness, $R$ = hydraulic radius (m), $S$ = slope |
| 6 | $C_{composite} = \frac{\sum (C_i \times A_i)}{\sum A_i}$ | **Composite Runoff Coefficient** (mixed surfaces) | $C_i, A_i$ = coefficient and area of the *i*th sub-surface |
| 7 | $t_c = \frac{0.143 (L)^{0.643}}{(S)^{0.143} (C)^{0.643}}$ | **Time of Concentration (Kirpich Formula)** | $t_c$ in minutes, $L$ = max flow length (m), $S$ = slope (m/m) |
| 8 | $P_{eff} = P - I - D - E$ | **Effective Precipitation** | All depths in mm; $I$ = interception, $D$ = depression storage, $E$ = evaporation |

## 2.5 Standard Runoff Coefficient Values (IS 15797:2008 / KTU Reference)

| Surface Type | Runoff Coefficient ($C$) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Concrete / Asphalt Pavement** | **0.70 – 0.85** |
| **RCC Roof (Flat)** | **0.70 – 0.80** |
| **Sloped Tile / Metal Roof** | **0.75 – 0.90** |
| **Brick Pavement** | **0.50 – 0.60** |
| **Unpaved / Compacted Earth** | **0.20 – 0.30** |
| **Lawns / Gardens (Flat)** | **0.10 – 0.25** |
| **Lawns / Gardens (Sloped > 10%)** | **0.25 – 0.35** |
| **Forest / Dense Vegetation** | **0.10 – 0.20** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This formula set is the *backbone* of RWH feasibility reports. Municipal corporations, builders (for IGBC/GRIHA ratings), and farm irrigation designers all rely on the **Rational Method** and the **Annual Yield Equation** to size tanks, recharge pits, and recharge trenches.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annual Water Harvesting Potential from Catchment Area

This is the **most important derivation** in RWH (frequently asked in KTU ESE, 14-mark questions).

### Statement
The annual volume of rainwater that can be harvested from a catchment is equal to the product of the catchment area, the annual rainfall depth, the runoff coefficient, and a unit-conversion factor.

### Step 1 — Define the Physical Variables
Let:
- $A$ = Horizontal projected catchment area (m²)
- $P$ = Annual precipitation depth (mm)
- $C$ = Runoff coefficient (dimensionless, $0 \le C \le 1$)
- $V$ = Harvestable volume of water

### Step 2 — Express Precipitation as a Volume
Rainfall depth $P$ is given in **millimetres (mm)**, which is a depth, not a volume. To convert to volume over area $A$:

$$ V_{rain} = A \times P $$

The dimensions: m² × mm = m² × (mm / 1) — note that **1 mm of rain on 1 m² = 1 litre of water**.

> **Step 2 Logic:** This is because 1 mm of depth spread over 1 m² is exactly 0.001 m × 1 m² = 0.001 m³ = 1 litre. Hence the convenience: m² × mm = litres.

### Step 3 — Apply the Runoff Coefficient
Not all rain becomes runoff. The fraction that *does* become harvestable runoff is $C$:

$$ V_{runoff} = V_{rain} \times C = A \times P \times C $$

### Step 4 — Final Working Formula
The harvestable volume (in **litres**) is:

$$ V = A \times P \times C $$

If the answer is required in **m³**, divide by 1000:

$$ V = A \times P \times C \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{(m³)} $$

### Step 5 — Sanity Check
Let $A = 100$ m², $P = 2000$ mm, $C = 0.8$:
- $V = 100 \times 2000 \times 0.8 = 160{,}000$ litres per year = **160 m³/year** ✓
- (Equivalent to ~440 litres/day, sufficient for a 4-member family.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Rational Method for Peak Runoff

### Step 1 — Origin
The Rational Method assumes that for a small catchment, **peak runoff rate** $Q$ occurs when the **entire catchment is contributing**, and the rainfall intensity $i$ is uniform over a duration equal to the **time of concentration** $t_c$.

### Step 2 — Start with the Continuity Equation

$$ Q = \frac{\text{Volume of runoff}}{\text{Unit time}} $$

### Step 3 — Volume of Runoff in Time $t_c$
The runoff volume is the rainfall volume multiplied by the runoff coefficient:

$$ V = C \times A \times i \times t_c $$

where $i$ is rainfall intensity in **depth per unit time** (e.g., mm/hr or m/s).

### Step 4 — Divide by Time to Get Rate

$$ Q = \frac{C \times A \times i \times t_c}{t_c} $$

$$ Q = C \cdot i \cdot A $$

### Step 5 — Result

$$ \boxed{\,Q = C \cdot i \cdot A\,} $$

> **Step 5 Logic:** This elegant cancellation ($t_c$ cancels) shows that peak runoff depends only on **intensity**, not on storm duration. This is the foundational equation for designing roof gutters, downpipes, and recharge-pipe diameters.

### Step 6 — Unit Conversion (Important for KTU)
If $A$ is in **hectares** and $i$ is in **mm/hr**, then:

$$ Q \, (\text{m}^3/\text{s}) = \frac{C \cdot i \, (\text{mm/hr}) \cdot A \, (\text{ha})}{360} $$

> **Conversion Logic:** $1$ ha = $10{,}000$ m². Multiplying $10{,}000$ m² by $i$ mm/hr (=$i \times 10^{-3}$ m/hr) gives $10 i$ m³/hr. Dividing by 3600 s/hr gives $10i / 3600$ m³/s. Per hectare, that is $i / 360$. Hence the $1/360$ factor.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Composite Runoff Coefficient (Mixed Catchment)

When a catchment has *multiple surfaces* (e.g., part roof, part lawn, part pavement), a **weighted-average** $C$ is computed.

### Step 1 — Define Sub-Areas
Let the catchment consist of $n$ sub-areas $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$ with runoff coefficients $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$.

### Step 2 — Total Runoff Volume
Total runoff = sum of runoff from each sub-area:

$$ V_{total} = C_1 A_1 P + C_2 A_2 P + \cdots + C_n A_n P $$

### Step 3 — Factor Out $P$

$$ V_{total} = P \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i A_i $$

### Step 4 — Equate to a Single-Equivalent Catchment
A single equivalent catchment of total area $A_{total} = \sum A_i$ and composite coefficient $C_{comp}$ would yield:

$$ V_{total} = P \cdot C_{comp} \cdot A_{total} $$

### Step 5 — Solve for $C_{comp}$

$$ C_{comp} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i A_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i} $$

### Step 6 — Engineering Insight
This is the **area-weighted average**. It is *not* a simple arithmetic mean — a large concrete roof with $C=0.8$ will dominate the result over a small lawn patch with $C=0.2$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Catchment-to-Storage Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete flow of rainwater from the catchment surface to the storage/recharge point, including all losses and diversions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Precipitation Input Rain P mm] --> A2[Catchment Area A m2]
    A2 --> B1[Interception Loss I mm]
    A2 --> B2[Depression Storage D mm]
    A2 --> B3[Infiltration Loss f mm]
    B1 --> C1[Effective Rainfall P eff]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Surface Runoff Q C x P eff x A]
    D1 --> E1[Gutters and Downpipes]
    E1 --> F1[First Flush Diverter 2 to 5 mm Discarded]
    F1 --> G1[Filter Unit Sand Gravel Charcoal]
    G1 --> H1[Storage Tank or Recharge Pit]
    H1 --> H2[Domestic Use or Groundwater Recharge]
    H1 --> H3[Overflow to Storm Drain]
    C1 --> C2[Evaporation Loss E mm]
    C2 --> D2[Sub Surface Flow Lost]
```

> **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU Exam):** When asked to "draw the schematic of an RWH system," always label the **catchment area**, the **conveyance system**, the **first-flush diverter**, the **filter**, and the **storage/recharge structure**. Marks are awarded for these specific labels.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Catchment Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Catchment Area Classification] --> P2[By Surface Type]
    P1 --> P3[By Permeability]
    P1 --> P4[By Scale]
    P2 --> P2a[Rooftop]
    P2 --> P2b[Pavement]
    P2 --> P2c[Natural Slope]
    P3 --> P3a[Impervious C 0.7 to 0.9]
    P3 --> P3b[Semi Pervious C 0.4 to 0.6]
    P3 --> P3c[Pervious C 0.1 to 0.3]
    P4 --> P4a[Micro less than 500 m2]
    P4 --> P4b[Mini 500 m2 to 10 ha]
    P4 --> P4c[Macro more than 10 ha]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Factors Affecting Catchment Yield

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Factors Influencing Catchment Yield] --> F2[Surface Properties]
    F1 --> F3[Meteorological Inputs]
    F1 --> F4[Geometric Layout]
    F2 --> F2a[Material Type]
    F2 --> F2b[Slope Angle]
    F2 --> F2c[Surface Roughness Manning n]
    F2 --> F2d[Age and Cleanliness]
    F3 --> F3a[Annual Rainfall mm]
    F3 --> F3b[Rainfall Intensity mm/hr]
    F3 --> F3c[Rainfall Distribution]
    F3 --> F3d[Evaporation Rate]
    F4 --> F4a[Horizontal Projected Area]
    F4 --> F4b[Aspect Direction]
    F4 --> F4c[Length to Width Ratio]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Note (KTU):** For 14-mark questions on catchment design, examiners expect the student to *enumerate* these factors with **one-line explanations each**. Memorize at least 4 factors in each of the three categories above.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Model QP 2024 Scheme]`
**Define catchment area. List any four factors that affect the runoff from a catchment.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

A *catchment area* is the topographical surface from which precipitation flows as runoff to a common collection point. It is also called a *watershed* or *drainage basin* and is bounded by a *topographical divide*.

**Four factors affecting runoff:**

1. **Surface type** — Impervious surfaces (concrete, metal roofs) yield more runoff than pervious ones (soil, lawns).
2. **Slope of the catchment** — Steeper slopes generate faster, higher-volume runoff.
3. **Rainfall intensity** — Higher intensity exceeds infiltration capacity, increasing runoff.
4. **Antecedent moisture condition** — A wet, saturated soil surface produces more runoff than a dry one.

*(Anticipated marks: Definition 1.5 + Factors 1.5 = 3.0)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Model QP 2024 Scheme]`
**What is the runoff coefficient? State its typical values for (i) concrete pavement, (ii) sloped tile roof, and (iii) lawn.**

**Model Answer (8 lines):**

The *runoff coefficient* ($C$ or $\eta$) is the dimensionless ratio of the depth of runoff to the depth of rainfall on a given surface. It represents the fraction of rainfall that becomes surface flow.

**Typical values:**

1. **Concrete pavement:** $C = 0.70$ to $0.85$
2. **Sloped tile roof:** $C = 0.75$ to $0.90$
3. **Lawn (flat):** $C = 0.10$ to $0.25$

Higher values indicate lower infiltration and higher runoff potential.

*(Anticipated marks: Definition 1.5 + Three values 1.5 = 3.0)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model QP 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the formula for the **annual rainwater harvesting potential** of a rooftop catchment. Define each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A residential building in Kerala has a **rooftop catchment area of 150 m²** (sloped tile roof, $C = 0.85$). The average annual rainfall at the location is **2900 mm**, with **20%** of the rainfall lost to first-flush diversion and filter backwash. Calculate:
- (i) The **gross annual water harvesting potential** in m³. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The **net usable volume** in litres per day for a 4-member family. **(4 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Derivation

Let $A$ = horizontal projected catchment area (m²), $P$ = annual rainfall depth (mm), and $C$ = runoff coefficient.

Rainfall depth in mm over area $A$ gives a rainfall volume:

$$ V_{rain} = A \times P \quad \text{(in litres, since 1 mm × 1 m² = 1 litre)} $$

Multiplying by the runoff coefficient to account for losses (infiltration, evaporation, depression storage):

$$ V_{harvest} = A \times P \times C \quad \text{(litres/year)} $$

Converting to m³:

$$ V_{harvest} = A \times P \times C \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{(m³/year)} $$

> **Each term:** $A$ = rooftop area projected on the horizontal plane (NOT the sloped area); $P$ = total annual precipitation measured by rain gauge; $C$ = empirically determined runoff coefficient depending on roof material and slope.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $A = 150$ m²
- $C = 0.85$
- $P = 2900$ mm
- Diversion loss = 20% of gross harvest

**Step 1 — Gross Annual Harvesting Potential:**

$$ V_{gross} = A \times P \times C \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ V_{gross} = 150 \times 2900 \times 0.85 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ V_{gross} = 369{,}750 \times 10^{-3} = 369.75 \text{ m³/year} $$

**Step 2 — Net Usable Volume (after 20% loss):**

$$ V_{net} = V_{gross} \times (1 - 0.20) = 369.75 \times 0.80 = 295.80 \text{ m³/year} $$

Converting to litres: $V_{net} = 295{,}800$ litres/year

**Step 3 — Daily Available Volume:**

$$ V_{daily} = \frac{295{,}800}{365} = 810.4 \text{ litres/day} $$

**Step 4 — Per-Capita Availability:**

$$ V_{per\_capita} = \frac{810.4}{4} \approx 202.6 \text{ litres/person/day} $$

> **Conclusion:** The system can supply ~**810 litres/day** to a 4-member family (≈ **200 Lpcd**), comfortably meeting the WHO basic water-need threshold of **50 Lpcd** and the Indian urban benchmark of **135 Lpcd**.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Model QP 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **Rational Method** for estimating peak runoff from a catchment. State its assumptions and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small residential colony has a mixed catchment: **800 m² of concrete pavement** ($C = 0.8$) and **400 m² of lawn** ($C = 0.2$). A 30-minute storm has an intensity of **60 mm/hr**. Calculate the **composite runoff coefficient** and the **peak runoff rate** using the Rational Method. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Rational Method Explanation

The **Rational Method** estimates the **peak rate of surface runoff** $Q$ from a small catchment during a uniform rainfall event:

$$ Q = C \cdot i \cdot A $$

where $Q$ = peak runoff (m³/s), $C$ = runoff coefficient, $i$ = rainfall intensity (m/s or mm/hr), $A$ = area (m² or ha).

**Key Assumptions:**

1. Rainfall is **uniform in space and time** over the catchment.
2. The **storm duration** equals the **time of concentration** $t_c$ (worst-case scenario).
3. The runoff coefficient $C$ is **constant** during the storm.
4. The catchment is **small** (typically $< 50$ ha) so that the above assumptions are valid.

**Limitations:**

1. Does not account for **storm variability** in time or space.
2. **Single-valued** $C$ oversimplifies complex land uses.
3. Not suitable for **large watersheds** (>50 ha) where overland flow dynamics dominate.
4. Does not predict **runoff hydrograph shape** — only the peak.
5. Cannot be used for **flood routing** or **detention pond design**.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- Concrete pavement: $A_1 = 800$ m², $C_1 = 0.8$
- Lawn: $A_2 = 400$ m², $C_2 = 0.2$
- Rainfall intensity $i = 60$ mm/hr
- Duration: 30 min (assumed $\ge t_c$)

**Step 1 — Composite Runoff Coefficient:**

$$ C_{comp} = \frac{C_1 A_1 + C_2 A_2}{A_1 + A_2} $$

$$ C_{comp} = \frac{0.8 \times 800 + 0.2 \times 400}{800 + 400} $$

$$ C_{comp} = \frac{640 + 80}{1200} = \frac{720}{1200} = 0.60 $$

**Step 2 — Convert Area to Hectares:**
$A_{total} = 1200 \text{ m}^2 = 0.12 \text{ ha}$

**Step 3 — Apply Rational Method (with hectare-mm/hr convention):**

$$ Q = \frac{C_{comp} \cdot i \cdot A_{total}}{360} $$

$$ Q = \frac{0.60 \times 60 \times 0.12}{360} $$

$$ Q = \frac{4.32}{360} = 0.012 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$

**Step 4 — Convert to Litres per Second:**

$$ Q = 0.012 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 12 \text{ litres/second (L/s)} $$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The drain/pipe network serving this colony must be sized to carry at least **12 L/s** peak flow. A standard 150 mm diameter pipe at 1% slope can carry ~12 L/s — hence the design is **just adequate** for a 30-min, 60 mm/hr storm.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Using the SLOPED roof area** instead of the **horizontal projected area** in the formula $V = A \cdot P \cdot C$. The catchment area is the *plan projection* (m²) — never the inclined surface area.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in the Rational Method** — students often forget the $1/360$ factor when $A$ is in hectares and $i$ is in mm/hr. Always check units *before* writing the final answer.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply the first-flush loss / filter loss** when computing *usable* volume. The question may explicitly say "after losses" — read carefully.
> 4. **Confusing $C$ (runoff coefficient) with $C_{c}$ (collection efficiency)**. $C$ is a surface property; collection efficiency refers to the *post-catchment* system (gutter, filter, pipe losses). Some texts combine them into a single "overall efficiency."
> 5. **Arithmetic errors in composite $C$** — never use a *simple arithmetic mean*; always use the **area-weighted** formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Catchment Area**
> - **Definition:** Topographical surface contributing runoff to a common point.
> - **Synonyms:** Watershed, drainage basin, catchment basin.
> - **Units:** m² (residential), ha / km² (large-scale).
> - **Master formula:** $V = A \cdot P \cdot C$ (litres/year).
> - **Rational Method:** $Q = C \cdot i \cdot A$ (m³/s).
> - **Composite $C$:** Area-weighted average — never simple mean.
> - **Always use horizontal projected area**, not sloped surface.
> - **Unit conversion:** m² × mm × 10⁻³ = m³.
> - **Hectare-mm/hr in Rational Method:** divide by 360.
> - **Standard $C$ values to memorize:** Roofs ≈ 0.8, Concrete ≈ 0.8, Lawn ≈ 0.2, Forest ≈ 0.15.
> - **Factors affecting yield:** Surface type, slope, rainfall intensity, antecedent moisture, evaporation, interception, depression storage, infiltration.
> - **Effective Precipitation:** $P_{eff} = P - I - D - E - f$ (deduct all losses).
> - **First-flush diversion:** Typically discards first **2–5 mm** of every storm.
> - **Manning's equation** is for *flow overland* (in channels/gutters), not for catchment yield itself.
> - **Kirpich formula** gives $t_c$, used to find the *critical* rainfall intensity.
> - **Catchment efficiency can drop** over time due to dust, moss, and roof degradation — assume 5% degradation/year in long-term designs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harvesting Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Harvesting Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Harvesting Structures — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Harvesting Structures* are the **engineered civil components** that intercept, convey, store, and/or artificially recharge the rainwater collected from a defined catchment surface. They form the **physical interface** between the **catchment system** (roof, ground, paved area), the **conveyance system** (gutters, pipes, filters) and the **end-use / aquifer**, and are classified by their **hydraulic function** into two broad families — **Storage Structures** and **Artificial Recharge Structures**.

In the KTU 2024 OECET832 syllabus, Module 1 treats *Harvesting Structures* as the **delivery end of the RWH chain**, after the student has understood rainfall, runoff, and catchment hydrology.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — The "Water Bank" idea:**
> Imagine rainwater is your **monthly salary** that lands in your bank account (the **sky → catchment**). The *gutters and pipes* are the **withdrawal slips** that move money to the locker. The **Harvesting Structure is the locker itself**. There are **two types of lockers** in the bank:
> 1. **A personal locker at home** — you keep the cash and use it whenever you need (this is a **Storage Tank**).
> 2. **A locker in the bank's central vault (underground)** — the money is deposited there to strengthen the entire banking system for the community (this is a **Recharge Structure**).
>
> Choosing the right locker depends on whether you want *immediate, controlled use* (storage) or *long-term, indirect benefit through groundwater* (recharge). The same logic applies to harvesting structures in RWH.

## 1.3 Functional Classification (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two functional families of harvesting structures:**
> - **Storage Structures** — retain water for **direct, controlled use** (drinking, washing, irrigation). Example: surface tanks, underground sumps, ferro-cement cisterns, ponds.
> - **Recharge Structures** — transfer harvested water **into the subsurface** to **augment groundwater**. Example: recharge pits, recharge trenches, recharge wells, percolation tanks, check dams.

The choice between them is dictated by:

| Decision Driver | Favours Storage | Favours Recharge |
|---|---|---|
| **Water table depth** | Deep (> 15 m) | Shallow to moderate (< 15 m) |
| **Soil permeability** | Any (lined) | High (sand, gravel, fractured rock) |
| **End use** | Potable / domestic / rooftop | Irrigation / community aquifer |
| **Space available** | Plenty of ground area | Constrained urban plots |
| **Geology** | Impervious/rocky | Porous alluvium / weathered rock |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metrics used in design (highlighted for KTU board exams):**
> - **Unit weight of water, γw = 9.81 kN/m³** (often taken as **9.81 kN/m³** or **9810 N/m³**).
> - **1 m³ of stored water ≈ 1000 litres** ≈ **1 tonne** of mass.
> - **1 hectare = 10 000 m²**.
> - **Annual runoff yield** is conventionally expressed in **litres / m² of catchment per year** or in **cubic metres (m³)**.
> - Standard recharge pit depths range from **2 m to 6 m**; standard recharge well depths from **10 m to 30 m**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Logic of Harvesting Structures — Step-by-Step

Every harvesting structure executes **four sequential hydraulic operations**, regardless of type:

1. **Interception** — the structure accepts water at the **point of delivery** from the conveyance (gutter, pipe, channel). The structure must have an **inlet** sized to the design peak flow.
2. **Settling / Pre-treatment** — coarse silt, leaves, and debris are removed (often by an **inspection chamber, desilting pit, or first-flush diverter**) so that the storage / recharge media is not choked.
3. **Storage or Transfer** — water is held (storage) or **infiltrated into the subsurface** (recharge) over a designed residence time.
4. **Recovery or Overflow** — water is drawn through a **pump, tap, borewell, or overflow weir** for use or safe disposal to a downstream drain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** A common board question asks *"Differentiate between a storage and a recharge structure."* The cleanest answer is to **walk through these 4 steps** and show where the two families diverge — divergence happens at **Step 3 (Storage vs. Transfer)** and **Step 4 (Tap vs. Overflow / Pump-back)**.

## 2.2 Detailed Description of Each Structure Type

### 2.2.1 Storage Structures

**a) Surface Storage Tanks (Cisterns)**
- Constructed of **RCC, ferro-cement, PVC, or HDPE** above ground beside the building.
- Capacity typically **5 000 L to 50 000 L** for domestic dwellings; up to several lakh litres for institutional buildings.
- **Must be opaque** to prevent algal growth; **must have a mosquito-proof lid** (public-health requirement).

**b) Underground Sumps**
- Constructed below the plinth or in the front yard, depth commonly **3 m to 6 m**.
- Preferred where **floor area is scarce**; constructed of **RCC rings or brick masonry**.
- Equipped with **submersible pump** for delivery.

**c) Elevated / Overhead Tanks**
- Placed on staging at height ≥ 15 m to provide **gravity head** for distribution.
- Commonly used as the *secondary* storage after an underground sump.

**d) Ponds / Surface Reservoirs**
- Excavated or embanked basins for **community / farm-level** storage.
- Lined with **HDPE / LDPE / brick pitching / clay** to control seepage losses.

**e) Barrels / Drums (Domestic Micro-storage)**
- 200 L to 1 000 L drums connected to a single down-pipe.
- Useful as **first-stage storage** to capture *first flush*.

### 2.2.2 Artificial Recharge Structures

**a) Recharge Pit**
- A **vertical rectangular or circular excavation**, typically **1.5 m to 3 m wide and 2 m to 5 m deep**, filled with graded gravel / boulders (top) and coarse sand (top layer) as a **filter media**.
- Backfilled around a **central PVC pipe** that admits water from the conveyance.
- Suitable for **rooftop RWH in urban plots** where the water table is moderately deep (5–15 m).

**b) Recharge Trench**
- A **horizontal trench**, length **5 m to 10 m**, width **0.5 m to 1.5 m**, depth **1 m to 3 m**, filled with boulders/cobbles.
- Surface area for infiltration is **high** because of its length; preferred for **linear plots** and for **recharging along a property boundary**.

**c) Recharge Well (Injection Well)**
- A **bored well of 100 mm to 300 mm diameter**, depth **10 m to 30 m**, cased through the impervious layer and **left open / screened in the aquifer**.
- Water is delivered via a **delivery pipe** and infiltrates **vertically through the aquifer column**.
- Best suited for **deep water tables (> 15 m)** or **multi-aquifer systems**.

**d) Recharge Shaft / Bore**
- A **vertical shaft of 300 mm to 1 000 mm diameter**, depth **5 m to 15 m**, with the side walls **lined with porous rings or brick-bat** to allow lateral seepage.
- Used where a **clay layer** overlies the aquifer; the shaft pierces the clay.

**e) Percolation Pond / Tank**
- An **earthen embankment** built across a natural drainage to impound runoff **over a large surface area**.
- Designed to **lose water by infiltration** rather than by evaporation; depth usually **2 m to 4 m** with a large surface plan.
- Best suited for **rural catchments** and for **sandy / lateritic** formations.

**f) Soak Pit**
- A small pit (often **1 m × 1 m × 1 m**) filled with brickbats, used to dispose of *first-flush* or as a **secondary filter** downstream of a recharge pit.

**g) Check Dam / Gabion Structure**
- A small **masonry or gabion weir** built across a **stream or nala** to retain runoff temporarily and force infiltration.
- Typically **1 m to 2 m high** across the full width of the stream.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **master formula set** is the **board-exam essentials** for the topic "Harvesting Structures". All KTU problems in this module reduce to one or more of these.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | $Q$ = annual runoff volume, $C$ = runoff coefficient, $I$ = annual rainfall, $A$ = catchment area | $Q$ in $\mathrm{m^{3}}$, $I$ in $\mathrm{m}$, $A$ in $\mathrm{m^{2}}$ | Annual yield from catchment |
| 2 | $Q_{\text{daily}} = \dfrac{C \cdot I_{\text{day}} \cdot A}{1000}$ | $Q_{\text{daily}}$ in $\mathrm{m^{3}}$; $I_{\text{day}}$ in $\mathrm{mm}$ | $\mathrm{m^{3}}$/day | Daily storage design |
| 3 | $V_{\text{storage}} = \dfrac{C \cdot I_{\text{annual}} \cdot A \cdot \eta}{1000}$ | $\eta$ = storage efficiency (0.6–0.8) | $\mathrm{litres/year}$ | Tank sizing |
| 4 | $A_{\text{infiltration}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{recharge}}}{k \cdot (h/L)}$ | Darcy's law for recharge pit / trench | $\mathrm{m^{2}}$ | Recharge pit / trench area |
| 5 | $t_{\text{drain}} = \dfrac{V}{A_{\text{base}} \cdot k_{\text{sat}}}$ | Time to drain recharge pit | $\mathrm{seconds/hours}$ | Soakage time check |
| 6 | $D_{\text{design}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{peak}}}{v \cdot A_{\text{pipe}}}$ | Manning / continuity for inlet pipe | $\mathrm{m}$ | Pipe sizing |
| 7 | $h_{\text{f}} = \dfrac{4.66 \cdot f \cdot L \cdot Q^{2}}{d^{5}}$ | Hazen-Williams head loss | $\mathrm{m}$ | Pipe hydraulics |
| 8 | $V_{\text{rect}} = L \cdot B \cdot H$ | Volume of rectangular tank | $\mathrm{m^{3}}$ | Storage tank volume |
| 9 | $V_{\text{cyl}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot H$ | Volume of circular / cylindrical tank or shaft | $\mathrm{m^{3}}$ | Cylindrical storage / shaft |
| 10 | $A_{\text{trench}} = L_{\text{trench}} \cdot W_{\text{trench}}$ | Recharge trench surface area | $\mathrm{m^{2}}$ | Trench sizing |
| 11 | $\text{Number of pits} = \dfrac{V_{\text{season}}}{V_{\text{each pit}}}$ | Iterative pit sizing | dimensionless | Multiple-pit design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit trap in KTU valuation:** $I$ (rainfall) is given in **mm** by the India Meteorological Department (IMD), but the formula $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ requires $I$ in **metres**. Always convert — divide mm by **1000** before substitution. Many KTU students lose 1 mark here.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban plots (Kerala-style row-houses):** A **recharge pit + first-flush diverter** is the *de facto* standard for compliance with the Kerala Building Rules and the **State Groundwater Act**.
- **Apartment complexes:** A combination of **underground RCC sump (storage) + recharge well to deep aquifer** is the most common KTU-cited design.
- **Watershed programmes (Mahatma Gandhi NREGA & Jal Shakti Abhiyan):** **Percolation tanks and check dams** dominate rural harvesting structures.
- **Industrial campuses:** Large **HDPE-lined ponds (storage)** supplemented by **recharge trenches along the boundary** for storm-water management under **Smart Cities Mission**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering approach (for KTU board paper):** Every harvesting structure sizing problem decomposes into **three sub-problems** — (i) determine the *available runoff volume* (demand), (ii) choose the *structure type* that can handle the volume, and (iii) dimension the *structure geometry*. The three derivations below cover these.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annual Water Harvesting Potential

The most fundamental equation in RWH is the **rational runoff equation** applied over an annual rainfall depth.

### Step 1 — Define the basic runoff yield from a unit catchment

The **hydrologic balance** over a catchment in a given time interval gives:

$$
\text{Runoff depth, } R_d = C \times I
$$

where $C$ is the *runoff coefficient* (dimensionless) and $I$ is the *rainfall depth* over the same interval.

### Step 2 — Convert runoff depth to volume per unit area

By definition, *1 mm of water depth over 1 m² of area equals 1 litre*.

$$
R_d \; (\text{mm}) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_v = R_d \times A \;(\text{litres})
$$

When $R_d$ is expressed in metres:

$$
R_v = R_d \times A = (C \cdot I) \times A
$$

with $R_v$ in $\mathrm{m^{3}}$ and $A$ in $\mathrm{m^{2}}$.

### Step 3 — Substitute to obtain the final form

$$
Q_{\text{annual}} = C \cdot I_{\text{annual}} \cdot A
$$

If $I_{\text{annual}}$ is in mm, divide by 1000:

$$
Q_{\text{annual}} = \dfrac{C \cdot I_{\text{annual,mm}} \cdot A}{1000} \quad [\mathrm{m^{3}/year}]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most-tested equation** in KTU OECET832. Always quote both forms — with $I$ in metres and with $I$ in mm — to be safe.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sizing a Rectangular Recharge Pit

Given the annual recharge volume $Q$, the design must yield pit dimensions $L \times B \times H$ such that the pit can accept the volume within an acceptable drain time.

### Step 1 — Storage volume required per recharge event

The pit must accommodate the **delivered volume from a single storm** (or a **monsoon month**, depending on design philosophy). For a single storm of depth $P$ (mm) on catchment $A$ ($\mathrm{m^{2}}$):

$$
V_{\text{delivered}} = \dfrac{C \cdot P \cdot A}{1000} \quad [\mathrm{m^{3}}]
$$

### Step 2 — Geometric constraint (volume of the pit)

The pit is a rectangular prism:

$$
V_{\text{pit}} = L \times B \times H
$$

A standard practice is to fix the **depth** $H$ at 2 m to 4 m (limitations of manual excavation, structural stability of vertical walls).

### Step 3 — Surface area required for pit base

Setting $V_{\text{pit}} = V_{\text{delivered}}$:

$$
L \times B = \dfrac{V_{\text{delivered}}}{H} = \dfrac{C \cdot P \cdot A}{1000 \cdot H}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the aspect-ratio constraint (L:B commonly 1.5 : 1)

If $L = 1.5 \, B$:

$$
(1.5 B) \times B = \dfrac{C \cdot P \cdot A}{1000 \cdot H}
$$

$$
B^{2} = \dfrac{C \cdot P \cdot A}{1500 \cdot H}
$$

$$
B = \sqrt{\dfrac{C \cdot P \cdot A}{1500 \cdot H}} \quad ; \quad L = 1.5 \, B
$$

### Step 5 — Verification using Darcy's law

The **infiltration rate through the pit base** must be greater than the inflow rate. Using Darcy:

$$
q_{\text{in}} = k_{\text{sat}} \cdot A_{\text{base}} = k_{\text{sat}} \cdot L \cdot B
$$

If $q_{\text{in}} \geq Q_{\text{peak storm}}$, the design is safe; otherwise, increase the surface area or use a *recharge trench* instead.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sizing a Recharge Trench (length-dominated)

A recharge trench is a **horizontal** structure whose *plan area* is dominated by its *length*.

### Step 1 — Define the design inflow

Same as recharge pit:

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{T \cdot 3600 \times 24} \quad [\mathrm{m^{3}/s}]
$$

where $T$ is the **duration of the design storm in days**.

### Step 2 — Infiltration through the bottom and sides of the trench

For a trench of length $L_t$, width $W_t$, depth $H_t$:

$$
Q_{\text{out}} = k_{\text{sat}} \cdot (W_t \cdot L_t + 2 \cdot H_t \cdot L_t)
$$

(The factor 2 is for the **two long side walls**.)

### Step 3 — Equate inflow to infiltration (steady-state design)

$$
\dfrac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{T \cdot 86400} = k_{\text{sat}} \cdot L_t \cdot (W_t + 2 H_t)
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for trench length

$$
L_t = \dfrac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{T \cdot 86400 \cdot k_{\text{sat}} \cdot (W_t + 2 H_t)}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU board style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical worked example (predictable KTU question):**
>
> A rooftop catchment of area $A = 150 \; \mathrm{m^{2}}$ with runoff coefficient $C = 0.85$ receives an annual rainfall of $I = 2400 \; \text{mm}$. A **recharge pit** of depth $H = 3 \; \text{m}$ with $L:B = 1.5:1$ is proposed. Find the pit dimensions. Also verify if 60 % of the annual runoff is recharged by 4 such pits.

**Step A — Annual runoff volume**

$$
Q = \dfrac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{1000} = \dfrac{0.85 \times 2400 \times 150}{1000} = 306 \;\mathrm{m^{3}/year}
$$

**Step B — Target recharge volume (60 % of annual)**

$$
Q_{\text{target}} = 0.60 \times 306 = 183.6 \;\mathrm{m^{3}}
$$

**Step C — Volume per pit (4 pits equally share the load)**

$$
V_{\text{each pit}} = \dfrac{183.6}{4} = 45.9 \;\mathrm{m^{3}}
$$

**Step D — Solve for $L$ and $B$**

Using $V = 1.5 B^{2} \cdot H$:

$$
45.9 = 1.5 \cdot B^{2} \cdot 3
$$

$$
B^{2} = \dfrac{45.9}{4.5} = 10.2
$$

$$
B = \sqrt{10.2} = 3.194 \;\mathrm{m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; L = 1.5 \times 3.194 = 4.79 \;\mathrm{m}
$$

**Step E — Adopt practical dimensions**

$$
\boxed{L = 4.8 \; \mathrm{m}, \quad B = 3.2 \; \mathrm{m}, \quad H = 3.0 \; \mathrm{m}}
$$

The design recharges **183.6 m³/year** ≈ **1.84 lakh litres/year** into the aquifer.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Table of Harvesting Structures** — the most common **2-mark or 7-mark** KTU question is a direct or tabular comparison. This matrix is your ready answer.

| Structure | Primary Function | Typical Size | Best Suited Soil | Water-Table Depth | Maintenance | Cost (Relative) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Storage Tank (RCC)** | Store water for use | 5–50 m³ | Any (lined) | Any | Low | Medium |
| **Underground Sump** | Store water for use | 5–30 m³ | Any (lined) | Any | Low | High |
| **Recharge Pit** | Recharge aquifer | 5–30 m³ | Sandy, laterite | 5–15 m | Medium (de-silt yearly) | Low |
| **Recharge Trench** | Recharge aquifer | 10–50 m³ | Sandy, loam | 3–10 m | Medium | Low |
| **Recharge Well** | Recharge deep aquifer | 1–5 m³ | Any (with casing) | > 15 m | High (de-clog) | Medium |
| **Recharge Shaft** | Pierce clay layer | 2–10 m³ | Clay over sand | 8–20 m | Medium | Medium |
| **Percolation Tank** | Recharge via surface | 1000–100 000 m³ | Sandy / laterite | 5–20 m | Low | High |
| **Check Dam** | Recharge stream bed | 50–500 m³ | Stream beds | Variable | High (de-silt after flood) | Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Functional Classification of Harvesting Structures

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[RWH Catchment] --> B1[Conveyance System Gutters Pipes Filters]
    B1 --> C1{End Use Decision}
    C1 --> D1[Storage Structure Family]
    C1 --> D2[Recharge Structure Family]
    D1 --> E1[Surface Tank RCC HDPE]
    D1 --> E2[Underground Sump]
    D1 --> E3[Overhead Tank]
    D1 --> E4[Surface Pond Lined]
    D1 --> E5[Ferrocement Cistern]
    D2 --> F1[Recharge Pit 2m to 5m deep]
    D2 --> F2[Recharge Trench 5m to 10m long]
    D2 --> F3[Recharge Well 10m to 30m deep]
    D2 --> F4[Recharge Shaft Clay piercer]
    D2 --> F5[Percolation Tank Rural]
    D2 --> F6[Check Dam Gabion]
    D2 --> F7[Soak Pit First Flush]
    E1 --> G1[Tap Pump for end use]
    E2 --> G1
    F1 --> H1[Aquifer via Infiltration]
    F2 --> H1
    F3 --> H1
    F4 --> H1
    F5 --> H1
    F6 --> H1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **catchment** feeds the **conveyance system**, which then bifurcates at the *End-Use Decision* node. The **storage family** (E1–E5) terminates in a *direct-use tap/pump*, while the **recharge family** (F1–F7) terminates in the *aquifer via infiltration*.

## 4.2 Mermaid — Schematic Cross-Section of a Recharge Pit

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Downpipe from rooftop] --> P2[First Flush Diverter]
    P2 --> P3[Inspection Chamber with Desilting Bucket]
    P3 --> P4[Recharge Pit Top Gravel Layer 100 mm]
    P4 --> P5[Recharge Pit Middle Coarse Sand 200 mm]
    P5 --> P6[Recharge Pit Bottom Boulders 1m to 3m]
    P6 --> P7[Native Soil Permeable Stratum]
    P7 --> P8[Groundwater Aquifer 5m to 15m below]
    P3 -.Overflow.-> P9[Percolation to Storm Drain]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Water travels **down → down → down** through *first-flush → inspection → filter media → native soil → aquifer*. The dotted arrow is the **overflow path** during intense storms when the pit is full.

## 4.3 Mermaid — Decision Tree for Selecting a Harvesting Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Start Site Condition Survey] --> S1{Water Table Depth}
    S1 -->|Less than 5 m| S2[Use Recharge Pit or Trench]
    S1 -->|5 m to 15 m| S3{Soil Type}
    S1 -->|More than 15 m| S4[Use Recharge Well or Shaft]
    S3 -->|Sandy Loam| S5[Recharge Pit Preferred]
    S3 -->|Clay Layer Present| S6[Recharge Shaft to pierce clay]
    S3 -->|Rocky with fractures| S7[Recharge Well with screen]
    S2 --> S8{Catchment Size}
    S5 --> S8
    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S8
    S4 --> S8
    S8 -->|Less than 200 sqm| S9[Single Recharge Pit]
    S8 -->|200 to 1000 sqm| S10[Recharge Trench or Multiple Pits]
    S8 -->|More than 1000 sqm| S11[Percolation Tank or Check Dam]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This is the **decision logic** a KTU answer should reproduce when asked *"How do you select a harvesting structure for a given site?"*. Walk **left → right** through the decision boxes.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Define a "harvesting structure" and list any four types with one-line functions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 mark):** A harvesting structure is a **civil engineering component that intercepts, conveys, stores, and/or artificially recharges the rainwater** collected from a defined catchment surface.
>
> **Types with functions (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
> 1. **Storage tank** — stores water in an RCC / HDPE / ferro-cement vessel for direct domestic use.
> 2. **Recharge pit** — infiltrates water into the ground through a vertical, gravel-filled excavation.
> 3. **Recharge trench** — infiltrates water into the ground through a long, shallow gravel-filled channel.
> 4. **Recharge well** — admits water into a deep aquifer through a bored, cased well with screen.
> 5. **Percolation tank** — impounds runoff in an earthen embankment to promote large-area infiltration.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> *Differentiate between a "storage structure" and a "recharge structure" in RWH.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Aspect | Storage Structure | Recharge Structure |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Hydraulic function** | Holds water for *direct use* | Transfers water to *groundwater aquifer* |
> | **Water exit path** | Through a *tap, pump, or outlet pipe* | Through *infiltration into the soil* |
> | **Lining** | Usually *lined/impervious* | Always *unlined / porous* |
> | **Typical depth** | Variable (surface / underground) | Mostly 2 m to 30 m into the subsurface |
> | **Examples** | RCC tank, underground sump, pond | Recharge pit, trench, well, shaft, percolation tank |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
> *(a)* Classify the various types of **harvesting structures** used in rainwater harvesting with neat sketches. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* A rooftop of area **200 m²** with runoff coefficient **0.8** receives an annual rainfall of **2200 mm**. Design a **rectangular recharge pit** of depth **3 m** with **L : B = 1.5 : 1** such that it can accommodate **50 % of the annual runoff**. Find the **length and breadth** of the pit. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Classification (7 marks):**

Refer to the classification table in **Section 3.4** of these notes. The examiner expects:
- **Two families** (Storage + Recharge) — 1 mark
- **At least 4 storage types** with one-line function — 2 marks
- **At least 4 recharge types** with one-line function — 2 marks
- **Neat schematic** of one recharge pit OR one storage tank — 2 marks

**Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Annual runoff volume (1 mark)**

$$
Q = \dfrac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{1000} = \dfrac{0.8 \times 2200 \times 200}{1000} = 352 \;\mathrm{m^{3}/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Target volume (50 %) (1 mark)**

$$
V_{\text{target}} = 0.50 \times 352 = 176 \;\mathrm{m^{3}}
$$

**Step 3 — Volume per pit (single pit) (1 mark)**

$$
V_{\text{pit}} = L \times B \times H = 1.5 B^{2} \times 3 = 4.5 \, B^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for $B$ (2 marks)**

$$
4.5 \, B^{2} = 176 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B^{2} = 39.11 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B = 6.25 \;\mathrm{m}
$$

$$
L = 1.5 \times 6.25 = 9.38 \;\mathrm{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Adopt and present (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{L = 9.4 \;\mathrm{m},\quad B = 6.25 \;\mathrm{m},\quad H = 3.0 \;\mathrm{m}}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification (1 mark)** — volume check: $9.4 \times 6.25 \times 3 = 176.25 \;\mathrm{m^{3}}$ ✓ (matches the target).

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
> *(a)* Explain the **design considerations** for selecting a harvesting structure for a residential plot in Kerala. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Compare a **recharge pit**, a **recharge trench**, and a **recharge well** in terms of geometry, soil suitability, depth, and cost. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Design Considerations (7 marks):**

1. **Catchment characteristics** (1 mark) — roof area, roofing material (C value for RCC = 0.8–0.9, GI sheet = 0.7–0.8, thatched = 0.5).
2. **Rainfall regime** (1 mark) — annual rainfall, monsoon concentration, intensity-duration-frequency (IDF) for peak design.
3. **Hydrogeology** (1 mark) — depth of water table, presence of clay/confining layers, aquifer transmissivity.
4. **Soil infiltration capacity** (1 mark) — sandy > laterite > loam > clay; use *percolation test* (IS 8408) to confirm.
5. **Plot geometry & setbacks** (1 mark) — minimum 2 m from any foundation, away from septic tank (≥ 5 m per NBC).
6. **End-use demand** (1 mark) — storage if rooftop is for direct use; recharge if groundwater augmentation.
7. **Regulatory & cost factors** (1 mark) — local panchayat / municipality by-laws, budget, O&M.

**Part (b) — Comparison (7 marks):**

Refer to the comparison table in **Section 3.4**. The examiner expects a **tabular answer** with these headers:

- **Geometry** (1.5 marks): Pit — square/rectangular, 2–5 m deep; Trench — long shallow, 5–10 m long, 1–3 m deep; Well — vertical bore, 10–30 m deep, 100–300 mm diameter.
- **Soil suitability** (1.5 marks): Pit — sandy/laterite; Trench — sandy loam; Well — any (cased through impervious layers).
- **Water-table depth** (1.5 marks): Pit — 5–15 m; Trench — 3–10 m; Well — > 15 m.
- **Cost & maintenance** (1.5 marks): Pit — low / medium; Trench — low / medium; Well — medium / high.
- **Conclusion** (1 mark) — pick the structure based on site-specific water table and soil.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion (1 mark lost commonly):** Rainfall in **mm must be divided by 1000** when used with area in $\mathrm{m^{2}}$ to get volume in $\mathrm{m^{3}}$. Students often forget and write 2200 instead of 2.2 — examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Aspect ratio misuse (1 mark lost):** In $L = 1.5 \, B$, students sometimes *add* instead of *multiply* — check your arithmetic.
> 3. **Function confusion (1–2 marks lost):** Writing *"recharge pit is used for storing water"* is a **factual error** that costs 1 mark. Memorise the **function first, then the type**.
> 4. **Skipping the schematic (2 marks lost):** In a 14-mark question with a *"neat sketch"* clause, omitting the diagram forfeits up to **2 marks**. Even a simple rectangle with arrows earns partial credit.
> 5. **Volume verification step (1 mark lost):** Always substitute the *final $L, B, H$* back into the volume formula and check the result matches the target — KTU examiners award 1 mark for the **consistency check**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 15-bullet rapid-revision list — read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- **Two families** of harvesting structures: **Storage** (use water) and **Recharge** (augment aquifer).
- **Master equation** (the most tested): $Q = \dfrac{C \cdot I_{\text{mm}} \cdot A}{1000}$ — units: $\mathrm{m^{3}}$.
- **Conversion memory hook:** $1 \;\mathrm{mm}$ rainfall on $1 \;\mathrm{m^{2}}$ = **1 litre** of water.
- **Storage structures** are **lined**; **recharge structures** are **unlined / porous**.
- **Recharge pit** depth = 2–5 m, square or rectangular, filled with graded gravel/sand/boulder.
- **Recharge trench** is *long and shallow* — preferred when the plot is long and narrow.
- **Recharge well** is for *deep aquifers* (water table > 15 m) and pierces confining clay layers.
- **Recharge shaft** pierces a clay layer *without* going all the way to the aquifer.
- **Percolation tank / check dam** is a *community-scale* rural structure for large catchments.
- **First-flush diverter** is a **mandatory** pre-treatment component for *all* rooftop RWH systems.
- **Rectangular pit** uses $V = 1.5 B^{2} H$ when $L:B = 1.5:1$.
- **Cylindrical tank** uses $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} H$.
- **Setback rule** — recharge pit ≥ **2 m** from building, ≥ **5 m** from septic tank.
- **O&M** — de-silt the pit/trench **at least once a year** before monsoon.
- **Real-world application** — Kerala rooftop RWH in row-houses almost always uses a **recharge pit + first-flush diverter**; apartments use a **sump + recharge well** combination.

---

> **End of Notes — Harvesting Structures (Module 1) | OECET832 — KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soil Moisture Conservation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Soil Moisture Conservation ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Soil Moisture Conservation** refers to the systematic application of engineering, agronomic, and watershed management practices aimed at retaining and utilizing the maximum possible proportion of precipitation water within the root zone of the soil profile for productive use by vegetation, while simultaneously minimizing losses due to **runoff**, **evaporation**, and **deep percolation** beyond the root zone. In the context of **KTU OECET832 – Rainwater Harvesting**, soil moisture conservation is treated as the *first and most critical in-situ* step of the rainwater harvesting chain. It bridges the hydrological cycle and the soil-plant-atmosphere continuum (SPAC), ensuring that harvested rainwater is not just collected in storage structures, but is *productively retained in the soil mantle* for crop growth, groundwater recharge, and ecological sustenance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 1 Highlight):** Soil moisture conservation is the foundational concept that links rainfall input → infiltration → storage → plant uptake. Without effective soil moisture conservation, structural rainwater harvesting (tanks, recharge pits) becomes a low-efficiency endeavour. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge Bucket Model" Imagine a **dry sponge** placed on a slanted kitchen counter, with a tap pouring water at a slow rate: - If the sponge has **large, open pores** (sandy soil), water rushes through quickly and drips off the counter → most water is lost as runoff or deep drainage. - If the sponge has **fine, closed pores** (clayey soil), water enters slowly, may pool on the surface and evaporate, or run off before infiltrating. - A **loamy soil with good structure** (sandy loam, silt loam) acts like a *well-designed sponge*: water enters steadily, is held firmly against gravity, and is slowly released for plant use. **Soil moisture conservation is the art of "tuning" the sponge** — through land treatments, mulches, bunds, vegetation cover, and tillage — so that the soil acts like a perfectly balanced sponge for every raindrop that falls. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following standard hydrological and soil-physics metrics are universally used in KTU examinations: - **Bulk Density of Soil (ρ_b):** Typical range **1.0 – 1.7 g/cm³** - **Particle Density of Soil (ρ_s):** Standard value **2.65 g/cm³** - **Standard Gravitational Acceleration (g):** **9.81 m/s²** - **Density of Water (ρ_w):** **1000 kg/m³** (or 1 g/cm³) - **Permanent Wilting Point (PWP):** Soil suction of **−15 bar (≈ −1500 kPa)** - **Field Capacity (FC):** Soil suction of **−1/3 bar (≈ −33 kPa)** for medium-textured soils - **Hygroscopic Coefficient:** Soil suction of **−31 bar (≈ −3100 kPa)** > [!NOTE] > **Definition — Available Water Capacity (AWC):** The volume of water held between Field Capacity and Permanent Wilting Point that is actually available for plant uptake. It is the single most important metric in irrigation scheduling and soil moisture conservation engineering. ### 1.4 Why Soil Moisture Conservation Matters in Rainwater Harvesting Rainwater harvesting is often misunderstood as *only* the construction of storage tanks, percolation pits, or recharge wells. KTU 2024 scheme explicitly emphasizes the **hierarchy of interventions**: 1. **In-situ conservation** (soil-based) — *highest priority, lowest cost* 2. **Micro-catchment harvesting** (farm ponds, check dams) 3. **Storage structures** (tanks, percolation tanks) 4. **Groundwater recharge** (recharge wells, injection wells) If step 1 fails, the efficiency of steps 2–4 collapses dramatically. Therefore, **soil moisture conservation is the silent workhorse of every successful rainwater harvesting system**. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Soil Moisture Constants — The "Staircase" of Water Availability Soil water exists at varying energy states, controlled by matric and osmotic suction. From saturation to oven-dry, the water passes through four critical thresholds that every KTU student must memorize: | Stage | Suction (approx.) | Description | Plant Availability | |:------|:------------------|:------------|:-------------------| | **Saturation** | 0 kPa | All pores water-filled; occurs immediately after heavy rain | Gravitational water drains rapidly; not held | | **Field Capacity (FC)** | −33 kPa | Upper limit of plant-available water; drainage practically ceases | **Plant-available — upper limit** | | **Permanent Wilting Point (PWP)** | −1500 kPa | Lower limit at which plants can no longer extract water | **Plant-available — lower limit** | | **Hygroscopic Coefficient** | −3100 kPa | Water held as thin films on soil particles; unavailable to plants | Not available | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Available Water Capacity (AWC)** is mathematically defined as the difference between Field Capacity and Permanent Wilting Point, expressed either in **% by weight** (gravimetric) or **mm of water per metre depth of soil** (volumetric — preferred for engineering design). ### 2.2 Soil Physical Properties Governing Moisture The capacity of a soil to absorb, transmit, and store water depends on three interlinked properties: **A. Soil Texture** — Relative proportion of sand, silt, and clay particles. - **Sandy soils:** High infiltration, low retention (poor for moisture conservation). - **Clayey soils:** Low infiltration, high retention at high suction (often waterlogged). - **Loamy soils:** Balanced infiltration and retention (ideal for most crops). **B. Soil Structure** — Arrangement of soil particles into aggregates (peds). - Granular / crumb structure → high macroporosity → good aeration, moderate infiltration. - Platy structure → restricts vertical flow → increases runoff. - Blocky / prismatic structure → moderate behaviour. **C. Soil Depth and Profile** — Deeper soils offer a larger reservoir for root-zone storage. ### 2.3 Soil Moisture Content — Two Ways to Express It **i. Gravimetric Moisture Content (θ_g):** $$\theta_g = \frac{M_{wet} - M_{dry}}{M_{dry}} \times 100 \;\; (\%)$$ where $M_{wet}$ = mass of wet soil sample, $M_{dry}$ = mass of oven-dry soil. **ii. Volumetric Moisture Content (θ_v):** $$\theta_v = \frac{V_{water}}{V_{soil}} = \theta_g \times \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_w}$$ where $\rho_b$ = bulk density of soil, $\rho_w$ = density of water. **iii. Depth Equivalent of Soil Moisture (d):** $$d = \theta_v \times D_{root}$$ where $D_{root}$ = effective root zone depth (typically 0.5 – 1.5 m for most crops). > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Insight:** The depth-equivalent form is the *engineer's favourite* because it directly converts soil moisture into a "depth of water" (mm), which is consistent with rainfall, irrigation, and evapotranspiration units. A volumetric moisture of 30% in the top 1 m of soil = **300 mm of water stored** — a sizeable reservoir. ### 2.4 Infiltration — The Gateway Process Infiltration is the downward entry of water from the surface into the soil. It is the **single most important process** in soil moisture conservation because *no infiltration = no conservation*. **Horton's Infiltration Equation (1933)** — Empirical model for decreasing infiltration capacity with time: $$f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) \, e^{-kt}$$ where: - $f(t)$ = infiltration capacity at time $t$ (mm/hr) - $f_0$ = initial infiltration capacity (mm/hr) - $f_c$ = final (steady-state) infiltration capacity (mm/hr) - $k$ = Horton's decay constant (hr⁻¹) **Cumulative infiltration F(t):** $$F(t) = f_c \, t + \frac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k} \left[ 1 - e^{-kt} \right]$$ ### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equations | # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Description | Engineering Utility | |:--|:------------------|:-------------------|:--------------------| | 1 | $\theta_g = \dfrac{M_{wet} - M_{dry}}{M_{dry}} \times 100$ | Gravimetric moisture | Lab/field measurement | | 2 | $\theta_v = \theta_g \cdot \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_w}$ | Volumetric moisture | Engineering design | | 3 | $AWC = FC - PWP$ | Available water capacity | Irrigation scheduling | | 4 | $d = \theta_v \cdot D_{root}$ | Depth-equivalent moisture | Water balance | | 5 | $f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c)\,e^{-kt}$ | Horton's infiltration | Storm runoff estimation | | 6 | $F(t) = f_c t + \dfrac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k}[1 - e^{-kt}]$ | Cumulative infiltration | Total water entering soil | | 7 | $ET_c = ET_0 \cdot K_c$ | Crop evapotranspiration | Moisture loss estimation | | 8 | $\eta_{conservation} = \dfrac{\text{Storage}}{\text{Input}} \times 100$ | Conservation efficiency | System performance | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall Alert:** In question papers, students often confuse **gravimetric** and **volumetric** moisture. Always read carefully — if the question gives *mass* values, use $\theta_g$; if it gives *volume* or *depth of water*, use $\theta_v$. ### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields Soil moisture conservation principles are deployed in: - **Agricultural engineering** — designing irrigation schedules, conservation terraces, and farm ponds. - **Watershed management** — RWH planning at micro, mini, and macro catchment levels. - **Civil engineering** — slope stabilization, earth dam design, and foundation drainage. - **Environmental engineering** — landfill cover design, phytoremediation, and carbon sequestration studies. - **Climate resilience** — drought-proofing, flood moderation, and carbon farming. ### 2.7 Operational Steps in Soil Moisture Conservation Engineering 1. **Site characterization** — soil texture, structure, depth, slope, and rainfall pattern. 2. **Estimate water balance** — rainfall (P) = runoff (R) + infiltration (I) + evapotranspiration (ET) + storage change (ΔS). 3. **Identify dominant loss pathway** — runoff-dominant, ET-dominant, or drainage-dominant. 4. **Select conservation measures** — agronomic, vegetative, or mechanical. 5. **Design and implement** — dimensions of bunds, terraces, ponds, etc. 6. **Monitor and adapt** — tensiometers, neutron probes, TDR sensors, and remote sensing. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion Between Gravimetric and Volumetric Moisture **Starting Point:** A soil sample is collected, weighed, oven-dried, and reweighed. **Given:** - $M_{wet} = 250$ g (mass of wet soil) - $M_{dry} = 200$ g (mass of oven-dry soil) - $\rho_b = 1.40$ g/cm³ (bulk density) - $\rho_w = 1.00$ g/cm³ (density of water) **Step 1:** Calculate mass of water present in the sample. $$M_{water} = M_{wet} - M_{dry} = 250 - 200 = 50 \text{ g}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the gravimetric moisture content (θ_g). $$\theta_g = \frac{M_{water}}{M_{dry}} \times 100 = \frac{50}{200} \times 100 = 25\%$$ **Step 3:** Convert gravimetric to volumetric using the bulk-density ratio. $$\theta_v = \theta_g \times \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_w} = 25 \times \frac{1.40}{1.00} = 35\%$$ **Step 4:** Interpret the result in terms of *depth of water* per unit depth of soil. $$\text{Equivalent water depth} = \theta_v \times D_{root}$$ If $D_{root} = 0.80$ m, then: $$\text{Equivalent water depth} = 0.35 \times 0.80 \text{ m} = 0.28 \text{ m} = 280 \text{ mm}$$ **Conclusion:** A 25% gravimetric reading corresponds to a 35% volumetric reading, which represents **280 mm of plant-available water** in the top 0.8 m — enough to sustain a medium-demand crop (e.g., maize) for about 2–3 weeks without rain. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Available Water Capacity (AWC) for a Loamy Soil **Given data (textbook standard values):** - Field Capacity, $FC = 28\%$ (volumetric) - Permanent Wilting Point, $PWP = 14\%$ (volumetric) - Effective root zone depth, $D_{root} = 1.0$ m **Step 1:** Compute AWC as a percentage (volumetric). $$AWC_{\%} = FC - PWP = 28\% - 14\% = 14\%$$ **Step 2:** Convert to depth equivalent. $$AWC_{depth} = AWC_{\%} \times D_{root} = 0.14 \times 1000 \text{ mm} = 140 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3:** Practical interpretation. A wheat crop in this soil with an effective root zone of 1.0 m can draw on **140 mm of stored water** between rainfall events. With a typical daily evapotranspiration of 4–5 mm/day during the heading stage, this gives approximately: $$\text{Days of moisture buffer} = \frac{140 \text{ mm}}{5 \text{ mm/day}} \approx 28 \text{ days}$$ This buffer allows the crop to survive a 4-week dry spell — a critical resilience parameter for rainfed agriculture. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Horton's Infiltration — Worked Numerical **Problem:** A bare loamy soil has the following Horton parameters: - $f_0 = 75$ mm/hr - $f_c = 12$ mm/hr - $k = 0.4$ hr⁻¹ **Find:** (a) Infiltration capacity at $t = 2$ hours, (b) Cumulative infiltration in the first 3 hours. **Part (a) — Infiltration at t = 2 hr:** $$f(2) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c)\,e^{-kt}$$ $$f(2) = 12 + (75 - 12)\,e^{-0.4 \times 2}$$ $$f(2) = 12 + 63 \times e^{-0.8}$$ Using $e^{-0.8} \approx 0.4493$: $$f(2) = 12 + 63 \times 0.4493 = 12 + 28.31 = 40.31 \text{ mm/hr}$$ **Part (b) — Cumulative infiltration in 3 hours:** $$F(3) = f_c \, t + \frac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k} \left[ 1 - e^{-kt} \right]$$ $$F(3) = 12 \times 3 + \frac{63}{0.4} \left[ 1 - e^{-1.2} \right]$$ $$F(3) = 36 + 157.5 \times (1 - 0.3012)$$ $$F(3) = 36 + 157.5 \times 0.6988 = 36 + 110.06 = 146.06 \text{ mm}$$ **Conclusion:** In the first 3 hours, the soil can absorb ~**146 mm** of rainfall — a very high number indicating excellent infiltration. By comparison, a clay soil might absorb only 25–30 mm in the same period, with the rest lost as runoff. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Conservation Measures | Dominant Loss Pathway | Recommended Primary Measure | Secondary Measure | Engineering Limitation | |:----------------------|:----------------------------|:------------------|:-----------------------| | **High runoff** (steep slope, low infiltration) | Contour bunds, terraces | Mulching, cover crops | Land shaping cost | | **High evaporation** (hot, dry, low humidity) | Mulching, wind breaks, shade nets | Conservation tillage | Mulch availability | | **High deep drainage** (sandy soil) | Add organic matter, clay amendments | Sub-surface barriers | Long-term soil management | | **Low storage** (shallow soil) | Deep tillage, ripping | Farm pond construction | Pond seepage control | | **Compacted surface** (crusting) | Conservation tillage, organic mulches | Gypsum application | Gypsum cost on sodic soils | ### 3.5 KTU-Style Numerical — Water Balance in a Cropped Field **Problem:** A cropped field in Kottayam district, Kerala, receives a storm of **80 mm** in 4 hours. Horton's parameters: $f_0 = 50$ mm/hr, $f_c = 8$ mm/hr, $k = 0.5$ hr⁻¹. The crop ET during this period is **3 mm**. Compute the runoff, total infiltration, and soil moisture storage change, assuming negligible deep percolation and initial dry soil. **Step 1:** Cumulative infiltration (using Horton): $$F(4) = 8 \times 4 + \frac{50 - 8}{0.5} \left[ 1 - e^{-0.5 \times 4} \right]$$ $$F(4) = 32 + 84 \times (1 - e^{-2})$$ $$F(4) = 32 + 84 \times (1 - 0.1353) = 32 + 84 \times 0.8647$$ $$F(4) = 32 + 72.63 = 104.63 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2:** But rainfall was only 80 mm, so the soil cannot infiltrate more than 80 mm (rainfall is the limiting input). Thus: $$F_{actual} = 80 \text{ mm (ponded condition assumed)}$$ **Step 3:** Water balance: $$P = R + I + ET + \Delta S$$ $$80 = R + 80 + 3 + \Delta S$$ $$R = -3 - \Delta S \text{ (negative, so no runoff)}$$ **Step 4:** Compute storage change: $$\Delta S = P - I - ET = 80 - 80 - 3 = -3 \text{ mm}$$ **Conclusion:** All 80 mm infiltrates. ET consumes 3 mm, so the net storage *decreases* by 3 mm — i.e., the soil was initially slightly dry and absorbed the ET deficit. **No runoff occurs** — an ideal soil moisture conservation outcome. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Soil Moisture Conservation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Rainfall Input P] --> B[Infiltration Zone] B --> C[Soil Profile Reservoir] C --> D[Root Zone Uptake] C --> E[Deep Percolation] C --> F[Evaporation Loss] B --> G[Surface Runoff Loss] D --> H[Crop Biomass] H --> I[Transpiration Loss T] I --> J[Atmospheric Return] F --> J E --> K[Groundwater Recharge] K --> L[Aquifer Storage] C --> M[Conservation Measures] M --> N[Agronomic: Mulching, Cover Crops] M --> O[Mechanical: Terraces, Bunds] M --> P[Agroforestry: Windbreaks, Hedgerows] M --> Q[Engineering: Farm Ponds, Recharge Pits] style A fill:#FFE08A style C fill:#7FC8A9 style H fill:#F4A261 style M fill:#90BE6D style L fill:#43AA8B ``` ### 4.2 Process Flow — Soil Moisture Dynamics ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Oven Dry Soil] --> B[Hygroscopic Water

5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]** Define the term **Field Capacity** of soil. Mention its typical suction value and explain its significance in soil moisture conservation. **Model Answer:** Field Capacity (FC) is the amount of water held in the soil after gravitational drainage has practically ceased, typically 2–3 days after a saturating rainfall or irrigation event. It represents the **upper limit of plant-available water** in the root zone. **Typical suction value:** Soil water suction of **−1/3 bar (≈ −33 kPa)** for medium-textured soils. **Significance in soil moisture conservation:** - It is the **upper benchmark** for irrigation scheduling — over-irrigation beyond FC wastes water as deep percolation. - It defines the **maximum reservoir** that soil moisture conservation measures aim to fill. - The difference between FC and Permanent Wilting Point gives the **Available Water Capacity (AWC)**, the key design parameter for soil moisture conservation systems. **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark definition + 1 mark suction value + 1 mark significance]** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]** List any **four mechanical methods** of soil moisture conservation and state one benefit of each. **Model Answer:** | # | Method | Benefit | |:-|:-------|:--------| | 1 | **Contour Bunding** | Intercepts runoff along the contour, increasing time of concentration and infiltration. | | 2 | **Terracing** (bench, broad-bed) | Reduces effective slope length, controls erosion, retains moisture in terraces. | | 3 | **Check Dams / Gabion Structures** | Slows gully flow, induces sedimentation, and enhances percolation. | | 4 | **Farm Ponds** | Provides surface storage of runoff for reuse and groundwater recharge. | **[3 Marks: 0.5 per method + 0.5 per benefit — keep within word limit]** --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]* **(a)** Explain the **soil moisture characteristics curve** with a neat sketch. Discuss how texture and structure of soil influence the shape of this curve. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A loamy soil in Palakkad has Field Capacity = 30% (vol.) and Permanent Wilting Point = 15% (vol.). The effective root zone depth is **1.2 m**. Calculate: (i) Available Water Capacity in mm of water. (ii) Number of days a maize crop can survive without rainfall, if daily ET = 5 mm/day.
