(iii) The depth of irrigation required to refill the root zone to FC, if the current moisture is at 20% (vol.). **[7 Marks]** --- #### **Model Solution — Question A (a):** The **Soil Moisture Characteristic Curve (SMCC)** is a plot of **soil water content (θ)** on the y-axis versus **soil water suction (h) or matric potential (ψ)** on the x-axis (plotted on a logarithmic scale). **Key features of the SMCC:** 1. **At saturation** (h = 0): All pores are water-filled; θ is maximum. 2. **Sharp drop near saturation:** As suction increases marginally, water drains from macropores (drainable porosity). 3. **Gradual decline** in the mid-suction range (10 – 1000 kPa): Water held in mesopores is released. 4. **Steep drop near wilting point** (h ≈ 1500 kPa): Plants struggle to extract water. 5. **Asymptote near oven-dry** (h ≈ 31,000 kPa): Only hygroscopic water remains. **Influence of Soil Texture:** - **Sandy soils** release most water at very low suctions → SMCC is steep near saturation, plateaus quickly. This indicates **low water-holding capacity**. - **Clayey soils** retain water even at high suctions → SMCC is more gradual and extends further. High **AWC** but slow release. - **Loamy soils** show a **balanced, sigmoidal SMCC**, ideal for plant growth. **Influence of Soil Structure:** - Well-aggregated soils have **bimodal pore systems** (macro + micropores) → SMCC has a characteristic "S" shape with a clear inflection. - Compacted or poorly structured soils show **reduced macroporosity** → SMCC is shifted left (less air capacity) and right (less plant-available water). A typical SMCC sketch shows: - X-axis: log of suction (kPa) from 1 to 10⁵. - Y-axis: volumetric water content (%) from 0 to 50. - Three curves: sand, loam, clay. - Marked FC and PWP points. **Conclusion:** The SMCC is a *fingerprint* of soil — it integrates texture, structure, and organic matter into a single diagnostic tool for soil moisture conservation design. --- #### **Model Solution — Question A (b):** **Given:** - $FC = 30\%$ (vol.) = **0.30** - $PWP = 15\%$ (vol.) = **0.15** - $D_{root} = 1.2 \text{ m} = 1200 \text{ mm}$ - Daily ET = 5 mm/day - Current moisture, $\theta_{current} = 20\%$ (vol.) = **0.20** **(i) Available Water Capacity (AWC) in mm:** $$AWC = (FC - PWP) \times D_{root}$$ $$AWC = (0.30 - 0.15) \times 1200 = 0.15 \times 1200 = 180 \text{ mm}$$ **(ii) Days of survival without rain:** $$\text{Days} = \frac{AWC}{\text{Daily ET}} = \frac{180}{5} = 36 \text{ days}$$ **(iii) Irrigation depth to refill to FC:** $$\text{Depth} = (FC - \theta_{current}) \times D_{root}$$ $$\text{Depth} = (0.30 - 0.20) \times 1200 = 0.10 \times 1200 = 120 \text{ mm}$$ **Conclusion:** The soil can hold **180 mm** of available water, the crop can survive **36 days** without rain, and the field currently needs **120 mm of irrigation** to return to field capacity. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]* **(a)** Discuss in detail the **agronomic and engineering methods** of soil moisture conservation. State the conditions under which each is most appropriate. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Using **Horton's infiltration equation**, calculate the infiltration capacity at **t = 1.5 hours** and the cumulative infiltration in **3.5 hours** for a soil with $f_0 = 60$ mm/hr, $f_c = 10$ mm/hr, and $k = 0.6$ hr⁻¹. **[7 Marks]** --- #### **Model Solution — Question B (a):** **A. Agronomic Methods** (biological / vegetation-based): 1. **Mulching** — Application of organic (straw, leaves) or inorganic (plastic film, gravel) material on the soil surface. - *Best for:* Hot, dry climates with high evaporation; orchards, row crops. - *Mechanism:* Reduces soil temperature, blocks vapour diffusion, suppresses weed transpiration. 2. **Cover Crops and Green Manuring** — Growing legumes or grasses in off-season. - *Best for:* Sloping lands, fallow periods, low-fertility soils. - *Mechanism:* Roots open soil pores, leaf canopy intercepts raindrops, organic matter improves structure. 3. **Conservation Tillage** — Minimum or zero tillage, leaving crop residues on surface. - *Best for:* Large farms, mechanized agriculture, erosion-prone soils. - *Mechanism:* Residues protect surface, improve infiltration, build organic matter. 4. **Crop Rotation and Mixed Cropping** — Alternating deep- and shallow-rooted crops. - *Best for:* Rainfed agriculture, drought-prone regions. - *Mechanism:* Different rooting depths exploit different soil layers. 5. **Agroforestry and Windbreaks** — Trees/shrubs planted in alleys. - *Best for:* Windy plains, semi-arid zones. - *Mechanism:* Wind reduction cuts evaporation; tree roots break compacted layers. **B. Engineering Methods** (structural / mechanical): 1. **Contour Bunding** — Embankments built along the contour. - *Best for:* Gentle slopes (2–6%) with moderate rainfall. - *Function:* Intercepts runoff, increases time for infiltration. 2. **Terracing** — Bench terraces, broad-bed terraces, or grassed waterways. - *Best for:* Steep slopes (>6%), high-rainfall hilly terrains (e.g., Wayanad, Idukki in Kerala). - *Function:* Breaks slope length, controls erosion, retains moisture in terrace beds. 3. **Check Dams / Gabion Structures** — Small barriers across gullies. - *Best for:* Gully control, ephemeral streams, degraded watersheds. - *Function:* Slows flow, induces silt deposition, augments groundwater. 4. **Farm Ponds / Nala Bunds** — Excavated or embanked storages. - *Best for:* Rainfed farms with undulating terrain. - *Function:* Captures runoff for reuse and recharge. 5. **Recharge Pits / Injection Wells** — Vertical structures filled with graded media. - *Best for:* Hard rock areas with thin soil cover. - *Function:* Direct infiltration to deeper aquifers. **Selection Criteria Matrix:** | Condition | Most Appropriate Method | |:----------|:------------------------| | Steep slope + heavy rain | Bench terraces + check dams | | Sandy soil + low retention | Mulching + organic amendments | | Windy, dry climate | Windbreaks + mulching | | Shallow soil over rock | Recharge pits + farm ponds | | Sloping agricultural land | Contour bunds + cover crops | --- #### **Model Solution — Question B (b):** **Given:** $f_0 = 60$ mm/hr, $f_c = 10$ mm/hr, $k = 0.6$ hr⁻¹ **(i) Infiltration capacity at t = 1.5 hr:** $$f(1.5) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) \, e^{-k \cdot t}$$ $$f(1.5) = 10 + (60 - 10) \times e^{-0.6 \times 1.5}$$ $$f(1.5) = 10 + 50 \times e^{-0.9}$$ Using $e^{-0.9} \approx 0.4066$: $$f(1.5) = 10 + 50 \times 0.4066 = 10 + 20.33 = 30.33 \text{ mm/hr}$$ **(ii) Cumulative infiltration in 3.5 hours:** $$F(3.5) = f_c \cdot t + \frac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k} \left[ 1 - e^{-k \cdot t} \right]$$ $$F(3.5) = 10 \times 3.5 + \frac{50}{0.6} \left[ 1 - e^{-0.6 \times 3.5} \right]$$ $$F(3.5) = 35 + 83.33 \times (1 - e^{-2.1})$$ $$F(3.5) = 35 + 83.33 \times (1 - 0.1225)$$ $$F(3.5) = 35 + 83.33 \times 0.8775$$ $$F(3.5) = 35 + 73.12 = 108.12 \text{ mm}$$ **Final Answers:** - Infiltration capacity at 1.5 hr: **≈ 30.33 mm/hr** - Cumulative infiltration in 3.5 hr: **≈ 108.12 mm** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit confusion in $f(t)$ vs $F(t)$:** $f(t)$ is *rate* (mm/hr); $F(t)$ is *depth* (mm). Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting to convert decimal θ to percentage** in AWC problems — examiners explicitly check. > 3. **In Horton's equation, the value of $e^{-kt}$** must be carried to at least 3 decimal places; rounding too early causes cascading errors. > 4. **SMCC sketch without labelled axes** (suction on log scale, θ on linear scale) is considered incomplete. > 5. **Listing conservation methods without stating conditions** under which they are appropriate leads to partial scoring on the 7-mark question. > 6. **In water balance, the equation is $P = R + I + ET + \Delta S$** — students often miss the storage change term. --- ### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before the Exam:** - **Soil moisture** is the water held in the unsaturated zone of the soil, available for plant use. - **Field Capacity (FC)** = upper limit of plant-available water; suction = **−33 kPa**. - **Permanent Wilting Point (PWP)** = lower limit; suction = **−1500 kPa**. - **Available Water Capacity (AWC) = FC − PWP**, expressed in mm/m depth. - **Horton's equation** $f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c)\,e^{-kt}$ describes declining infiltration capacity. - **Cumulative infiltration** $F(t) = f_c \cdot t + \dfrac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k}[1 - e^{-kt}]$. - **Soil texture** controls water retention: sand = low, clay = high, loam = balanced. - **Conservation methods** fall into three classes: **Agronomic** (mulching, cover crops), **Engineering** (terraces, bunds, ponds), and **Agroforestry** (windbreaks, hedgerows). - **Water balance:** $P = R + I + ET + \Delta S$ (must always balance). - **Volumetric moisture** $\theta_v$ is the *engineer's* metric; convert via $\theta_v = \theta_g \cdot \rho_b / \rho_w$. - **Depth equivalent** $d = \theta_v \cdot D_{root}$ converts moisture to mm of water. - **Soil Moisture Characteristic Curve (SMCC)** is the diagnostic fingerprint of soil-water retention behaviour. - **Mulching reduces evaporation** by 30–60% — a key low-cost intervention. - **Contour bunds** are effective on slopes of 2–6%; **terraces** on slopes > 6%. - **Farm ponds** in Kerala's laterite terrain are often lined with *silpaulin* sheets to control seepage. - **Conservation efficiency** = (Water stored / Water input) × 100 — a key performance metric for any RWH system. - **In-situ conservation** has higher priority than structural harvesting in the KTU Module 1 hierarchy. - **Kerala-specific note:** Laterite soils have high infiltration but **low water-holding capacity** — making mulching and organic amendments *especially critical* in the state's soil moisture conservation strategy. - **Drought resilience** of a soil is directly proportional to its AWC and the depth of its root zone. - **Tensiometers, TDR, and remote sensing (Sentinel-1 SAR, SMAP)** are modern tools for monitoring soil moisture. > **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Soil Moisture Conservation** > *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech OECET832 – Rainwater Harvesting*

topics.title: Check Dams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Check Dams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Check Dams

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Check Dam (As per KTU 2024 OECET832 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **check dam** is a small, low-height barrier (typically **0.5 m to 2.0 m** in height) constructed across a minor stream, nala, gully, or drainage channel, primarily to **temporarily impound runoff water** and facilitate **artificial groundwater recharge** through infiltration, while simultaneously controlling downstream soil erosion.

Check dams are integral to **Watershed Development Programmes** under the **Integrated Watershed Management Programme (IWMP)** of the Government of India and are classified as a structural measure of **Rainwater Harvesting (RWH)** under the broader category of **Surface Water Harvesting Structures**.

The term "check" implies **"restriction"** or **"regulation"** — the dam *checks* (slows and arrests) the velocity of flowing water, allowing suspended sediment to settle, percolation to occur, and downstream flash floods to be attenuated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1, OECET832):**
> Check dams fall under the broader classification of **Rooftop and Surface Runoff Harvesting Structures**. Students must clearly distinguish check dams from **percolation tanks**, **nala bunds**, and **weirs**, which are related but structurally and functionally distinct.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are running down a steep staircase carrying a glass of water. The water sloshes and spills everywhere. Now imagine someone places a small wooden block across each step — your speed reduces, the water stabilizes, and some of it seeps into the wood (representing the ground). This is precisely how a **check dam** functions on a stream:

- The **stream = staircase**
- The **check dam = wooden block on each step**
- The **seepage into wood = groundwater recharge**
- The **sloshing water = fast-moving runoff with erosion potential**

A check dam is **not** built to permanently store water like a large dam. It is a **temporary detention + recharge structure** that holds back water for a few hours to a few days, allowing the streambed's sandy/gravelly soil to absorb the impounded water, thereby recharging the underlying **aquifer**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Check Dams

| Classification Basis | Type | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **By Construction Material** | Loose Boulder Check Dam | Low-cost, semi-permanent; rural watersheds |
| | Masonry Check Dam (Brick/Stone) | Semi-permanent; medium catchment |
| | Concrete Check Dam | Permanent; high-velocity streams |
| | Gabion Check Dam | Flexible, erosion-prone zones |
| | Earthen Check Dam | Very small gullies |
| **By Hydraulic Function** | Recharge Check Dam (Percolation) | Groundwater recharge (primary purpose) |
| | Erosion Control Check Dam | Velocity reduction in gullies |
| **By Impermeability** | Impervious Check Dam | Surface water storage |
| | Pervious Check Dam | Maximum groundwater recharge |

---

## 1.4 Typical Dimensions (As per Indian Standards)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard Geometric Limits (Central Ground Water Board - CGWB Guidelines):
> - **Height:** 1.0 m – 2.0 m (rarely exceeds 2 m)
> - **Length along crest:** 5 m – 25 m (varies with stream width)
> - **Catchment area:** 5 ha – 100 ha (small catchments only)
> - **Storage volume:** 200 m³ – 5,000 m³
> - **Freeboard:** minimum 0.3 m above the maximum water level
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Purpose and Working Principle

A check dam operates on three coupled physical principles:

1. **Hydraulic Principle** – Reduces stream velocity from `V₁` (upstream) to `V₂` (downstream), where `V₂ << V₁`. Since `V ∝ √(Hydraulic Gradient)`, forcing the water to back up increases the wetted perimeter, slows the flow, and increases the **residence time** of water in the stream.

2. **Hydrogeological Principle** – Increases the **residence time** (`t_r`) of impounded water in the streambed. Greater `t_r` directly translates to higher **infiltration rate** into the **phreatic zone** (saturated zone of the aquifer).

3. **Geomorphological Principle** – Traps suspended sediment upstream of the dam, creating a graded, silted streambed that *itself becomes* a recharge medium (because silt has high porosity).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common mistake students make is confusing *storage* with *recharge*. Check dams primarily **recharge**, not **store**. A check dam that does not allow water to seep through is functionally a weir or a mini-barrage.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

A check dam performs the following sequence of functions upon occurrence of runoff:

1. **Runoff enters the upstream pool** → The water level rises behind the dam body.
2. **Velocity drops sharply** → Due to the increased cross-sectional area of the impounded water.
3. **Suspended sediment settles** → Heavier particles (sand, gravel) deposit upstream, building a natural recharge-friendly bed.
4. **Infiltration occurs vertically** → Water seeps downward through the permeable streambed into the aquifer.
5. **Excess water spills over the crest** → The spillway passes water safely to the downstream side.
6. **Energy dissipation** → The apron + toe wall + stilling basin dissipate the kinetic energy of the overflowing water to prevent scour.
7. **Stream reverts to base flow** → The process repeats in the next storm event.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Description | Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C \cdot i \cdot A$ | `Q` = peak runoff, `C` = runoff coefficient, `i` = rainfall intensity, `A` = catchment area | m³/s, dimensionless, mm/hr, ha | **Rational Method** for small catchments (<50 ha) |
| 2 | $V_{imp} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot L \cdot H \cdot W$ | `V_imp` = impounding volume, `L` = crest length, `H` = dam height, `W` = upstream water spread | m³ | Volume of water impounded per check dam |
| 3 | $Q_{spill} = 1.704 \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot H_e^{3/2}$ | `Q_spill` = spillway discharge, `C_d` = discharge coeff. (~1.71 broad-crested), `L` = crest length, `H_e` = effective head | m³/s | **Broad-crested weir** spillway design |
| 4 | $Q_{spill} = 1.84 \cdot L \cdot H_e^{3/2}$ | For sharp-crested weir (ogee profile) | m³/s | Spillway capacity check |
| 5 | $S = \frac{H_{dam}}{S_0}$ | `S` = spacing between successive check dams, `H_dam` = dam height, `S₀` = stream bed slope | m | Longitudinal spacing of check dam series |
| 6 | $V_2 = V_1 \cdot \frac{y_1}{y_2}$ | `V` = velocity, `y` = depth, upstream/downstream (continuity) | m/s, m | Velocity reduction due to backwater |
| 7 | $Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot y}}$ | `Fr` = Froude number, `g` = 9.81 m/s² | dimensionless | Identifies flow regime (Fr<1: subcritical; Fr>1: supercritical) |
| 8 | $y_2 = \frac{y_1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{1 + 8Fr_1^2} - 1 \right]$ | Conjugate depths (hydraulic jump) | m | **Energy dissipation basin** design |
| 9 | $\Delta E = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 \cdot y_1 \cdot y_2}$ | Energy loss in hydraulic jump | m | Stilling basin depth calculation |
| 10 | $N_{sc} = \frac{V_1 \cdot y_1}{\sqrt{g}}$ | Specific discharge (force per unit width basis) | m²/s | Initial parameter for jump classification |
| 11 | $t_{recharge} = \frac{V_{imp}}{K \cdot A_{bed}}$ | `t_recharge` = infiltration time, `K` = hydraulic conductivity, `A_bed` = wetted bed area | hr | Time for full infiltration of impounded water |
| 12 | $F_{slide} = \frac{\mu \cdot W}{\sum F_H}$ | `F_slide` = factor of safety against sliding (≥ 1.5 for normal; ≥ 1.2 for seismic) | dimensionless | **Stability of dam body** |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Check dams are deployed in the following practical scenarios:

- **Watershed rehabilitation** under **MGNREGA** and **IWMP** (Integrated Watershed Management Programme).
- **Groundwater recharge** in hard-rock (granite, basalt) terrains of Peninsular India (Maharashtra, Karnataka, Telangana).
- **Soil conservation** in sub-Himalayan foothills, Shiwaliks, and the Aravallis.
- **Drought mitigation** in **rain-shadow** districts of Kerala (e.g., Palakkad, parts of Wayanad) where check dams supplement traditional *surangams* and *kayyala* systems.
- **Mine pit reclamation** and **check dam cascades** in Western Ghats tributaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A series of check dams constructed along a single stream at calculated spacings is called a **Check Dam Cascade** or **Check Dam Chain**. This is the most effective deployment strategy and is a high-yield topic in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Peak Runoff by the Rational Method

**Background:** For small catchments (typically < 50 ha), the **Rational Method** is the standard KTU-recommended approach to estimate peak runoff. The derivation links rainfall intensity, time of concentration, and catchment area.

**Given:** A check dam is to be designed for a catchment with:
- Catchment area `A = 25 \text{ ha}`
- Rainfall intensity `i = 60 \text{ mm/hr}` (for return period 25 years, time of concentration = 30 min)
- Runoff coefficient `C = 0.45` (mixed agricultural + forest catchment, moderate slope)

**Step 1 — Statement of the Rational Formula:**

$$Q = C \cdot i \cdot A$$

**Step 2 — Unit Conversion:**
- Convert rainfall intensity: `i = 60 \text{ mm/hr}`
- Convert area: `A = 25 \text{ ha} = 25 \times 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^2`
- Note: `1 \text{ ha} = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2`

**Step 3 — Combine in consistent units:**
- Multiply intensity by area to get `i \cdot A = (60 \times 10^{-3} / 3600) \cdot 2.5 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}`
- Simplify: `(60 / 3{,}600{,}000) \cdot 2.5 \times 10^5 = (1/60{,}000) \cdot 2.5 \times 10^5`
- `(2.5 \times 10^5) / 60{,}000 = 4.1667 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}`

**Step 4 — Apply the runoff coefficient:**

$$Q = 0.45 \times 4.1667$$

$$Q = 1.875 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \;\; (\text{approximately } 1.88 \text{ m}^3/\text{s})$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** This peak discharge of 1.88 m³/s must be safely passed by the check dam's spillway. The dam body must be designed for the static head equal to `H_e` such that the spillway capacity `Q_spill ≥ 1.88 m³/s`.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Spacing Between Successive Check Dams

**Background:** In a **Check Dam Cascade**, individual dams must be spaced so that the backwater of the downstream dam does not submerge the upstream dam. The spacing equals the **horizontal distance** the stream's water surface travels over a vertical drop equal to the dam height.

**Step 1 — Define the relationship:**

$$S = \frac{H_{dam}}{S_0}$$

where `S₀` is the dimensionless **stream bed slope** (e.g., 0.02 means 2% slope, i.e., 2 m fall per 100 m run).

**Step 2 — Given Values:**
- Dam height `H_dam = 1.5 \text{ m}`
- Stream bed slope `S_0 = 0.015` (1.5%)

**Step 3 — Substitute and Solve:**

$$S = \frac{1.5}{0.015}$$

$$S = 100 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Practical Adjustment:** In practice, a **multiplication factor of 1.1 to 1.5** is applied to account for the backwater curve being longer than the vertical drop due to the M1 (backwater) profile. So the recommended spacing is:

$$S_{field} = 1.2 \times 100 = 120 \text{ m}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Spillway Capacity (Broad-Crested Weir)

**Background:** The most common spillway profile for a check dam is a **broad-crested weir** because the crest length is large relative to the head (length ≥ 3 × head).

**Step 1 — Broad-Crested Weir Formula:**

$$Q_{spill} = 1.704 \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot H_e^{3/2}$$

**Step 2 — Given:**
- Design discharge `Q_spill = 1.88 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}` (from Section 3.1)
- Discharge coefficient `C_d = 0.95` (standard for broad-crested weir with rounded upstream edge)
- Effective head `H_e = ?` (to be found)
- Crest length `L = 6 \text{ m}` (assumed based on stream width)

**Step 3 — Solve for `H_e`:**

$$1.88 = 1.704 \times 0.95 \times 6 \times H_e^{3/2}$$

$$1.88 = 9.7128 \times H_e^{3/2}$$

$$H_e^{3/2} = \frac{1.88}{9.7128} = 0.1936$$

$$H_e = (0.1936)^{2/3}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerically:**

$$H_e = (0.1936)^{0.6667} \approx 0.337 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Freeboard:** Total dam height = `H_e + Freeboard = 0.337 + 0.30 = 0.637 m`

**Step 6 — Round Up:** For safety, adopt a dam height of `H_{dam} = 0.80 m` (next practical constructional increment).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Hydraulic Jump (Energy Dissipation) Downstream of Spillway

**Background:** Water spilling over the weir has high kinetic energy and would otherwise scour the downstream bed. A **hydraulic jump** is engineered to dissipate this energy.

**Step 1 — Assume supercritical flow on apron:** `V_1 = 3.2 \text{ m/s}`, `y_1 = 0.25 \text{ m}`

**Step 2 — Calculate Froude Number:**

$$Fr_1 = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{g \cdot y_1}} = \frac{3.2}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.25}}$$

$$Fr_1 = \frac{3.2}{\sqrt{2.4525}} = \frac{3.2}{1.566} \approx 2.04$$

**Step 3 — Since `Fr_1 > 1`, flow is supercritical. Compute conjugate depth `y_2`:**

$$y_2 = \frac{y_1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{1 + 8 \cdot Fr_1^2} - 1 \right]$$

$$y_2 = \frac{0.25}{2} \left[ \sqrt{1 + 8 \times (2.04)^2} - 1 \right]$$

$$y_2 = 0.125 \times \left[ \sqrt{1 + 8 \times 4.1616} - 1 \right]$$

$$y_2 = 0.125 \times \left[ \sqrt{1 + 33.293} - 1 \right]$$

$$y_2 = 0.125 \times \left[ \sqrt{34.293} - 1 \right]$$

$$y_2 = 0.125 \times (5.856 - 1) = 0.125 \times 4.856$$

$$y_2 \approx 0.607 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Energy Loss in the Jump:**

$$\Delta E = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)^3}{4 \cdot y_1 \cdot y_2}$$

$$\Delta E = \frac{(0.607 - 0.25)^3}{4 \times 0.25 \times 0.607} = \frac{(0.357)^3}{0.607}$$

$$\Delta E = \frac{0.04548}{0.607} \approx 0.0749 \text{ m} = 7.49 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5 — Design Note:** Since `Fr_1 = 2.04` (between 1.7 and 2.5), this is a **weak jump**, dissipating about 25–45% of the kinetic energy. A stilling basin of length `L_b ≈ 6.1 \cdot y_2 ≈ 3.7 m` and depth equal to `y_2` is sufficient.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison with Related Structures

| Parameter | **Check Dam** | **Percolation Tank** | **Nala Bund** | **Weir / Barrage** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Typical Height | 0.5 – 2.0 m | 3 – 6 m | 2 – 3 m | > 3 m |
| Catchment Area | 5 – 100 ha | 100 – 1000 ha | 50 – 200 ha | > 200 ha |
| Spillway | Integrated weir | Required (separate) | Required (separate) | Required (gated) |
| Primary Function | Recharge + Erosion control | Recharge | Storage + Recharge | Diversion / Storage |
| Hydraulic Conductivity of Bed | Must be ≥ 5 m/day | Must be ≥ 2 m/day | Variable | Not critical |
| Maintenance | Low (annual de-silting) | High (de-silting) | Moderate | High |
| Lifespan | 15–25 years | 20–30 years | 15–25 years | > 50 years |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Check Dam

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Inflow Catchment Rainfall Runoff] --> B[Upstream Pool Storage Zone]
    B --> C{Sediment Deposition}
    C --> D[Silt Trap Upstream of Dam]
    B --> E[Recharge Zone Percolation Bed]
    E --> F[Aquifer Storage Saturated Zone]
    B --> G[Weir Spillway Crest]
    G --> H[Hydraulic Jump Stilling Basin]
    H --> I[Apron Downstream Protection]
    I --> J[Toe Wall Scour Prevention]
    J --> K[Downstream Stream Reach]
    D --> L[Periodic De-silting Maintenance]
    F --> M[Recovery Wells Bore Wells Dug Wells]
    M --> N[End User Agricultural Domestic]
```

## 4.2 Component-Level Schematic of a Typical Masonry Check Dam

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph upstreamPool[Upstream Pool Zone]
        A1[Water Surface]
        A2[Silting Zone]
        A3[Streambed Infiltration]
    end
    subgraph damBody[Dam Body Cross Section]
        B1[Cut off Wall 0.3m]
        B2[Headwall Upstream Face]
        B3[Main Body Masonry 1.5m]
        B4[Crest Spillway 6m length]
        B5[Downstream Face]
    end
    subgraph downstreamZone[Downstream Energy Dissipation]
        C1[Apron 3m length 0.4m thick]
        C2[Stilling Basin Hydraulic Jump]
        C3[Toe Wall 0.5m depth]
    end
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B1
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Site Selection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Start Identify Stream Reach] --> Q1{Stream bed slope 1 to 3 percent}
    Q1 -- No --> R1[Reject site]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Banks stable and non erodible}
    Q2 -- No --> R1
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Catchment area 5 to 100 ha}
    Q3 -- No --> R1
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Streambed permeable sand gravel}
    Q4 -- No --> R1
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{Narrow stream cross section preferred}
    Q5 -- No --> R1
    Q5 -- Yes --> A1[Mark Candidate Site]
    A1 --> A2[Conduct geotechnical and hydrological survey]
    A2 --> A3[Finalize design parameters]
    A3 --> Z[Approved Check Dam Location]
```

## 4.4 Check Dam Cascade Spacing Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S0[Stream Source Upstream] --> D1[Check Dam 1 Height 1.5m]
    D1 --> S1[Backwater Pool Zone 120m]
    S1 --> D2[Check Dam 2 Height 1.5m]
    D2 --> S2[Backwater Pool Zone 120m]
    S2 --> D3[Check Dam 3 Height 1.5m]
    D3 --> S3[Backwater Pool Zone 120m]
    S3 --> D4[Check Dam 4 Height 1.5m]
    D4 --> OUT[Outlet to Main River]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** Each dam creates a pool upstream. The pool extends `S = H_dam / S_0` horizontally. When pools of adjacent dams meet or overlap, the cascade is at maximum hydrological efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OECET832]
**"Differentiate between a check dam and a percolation tank, highlighting three major distinguishing features."**

**Model Answer:**

A check dam and a percolation tank are both rainwater harvesting structures, but they differ in scale, function, and design:

| Feature | Check Dam | Percolation Tank |
|---|---|---|
| **Height** | Low (0.5–2.0 m) | Higher (3–6 m) |
| **Catchment Area** | Small (5–100 ha) | Larger (100–1000 ha) |
| **Primary Function** | Velocity reduction + recharge | Bulk storage + recharge |
| **Spillway** | Integrated weir crest | Separate spillway structure |
| **Streambed Permeability** | Mandatory (high K required) | Mandatory but slightly lower K acceptable |

Check dams are essentially **erosion-control + minor-recharge** structures, while percolation tanks are **bulk-recharge** structures designed for larger catchments. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OECET832]
**"List the main components of a check dam and state the function of the cut-off wall."**

**Model Answer:**

The main components of a check dam are:

1. **Main body (weir portion)** – impounds water
2. **Headwall** – upstream vertical face
3. **Wing walls** – connect dam to stream banks
4. **Spillway (weir crest)** – passes excess water
5. **Apron** – downstream bed protection
6. **Stilling basin** – dissipates spillway energy
7. **Toe wall** – prevents downstream scour
8. **Cut-off wall** – a vertical wall extending below the dam into the impervious foundation to prevent seepage beneath the structure

**Function of the cut-off wall:** It is a small vertical barrier built *below* the foundation level of the check dam, extending into an impervious layer (rock or clay). It serves to **prevent under-seepage (piping)** of water beneath the dam body, which would otherwise lead to structural failure. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Pitfall #1:** Students often confuse **storage volume** with **recharge volume**. A check dam primarily *recharges*, not *stores*. Always mention the recharge function explicitly.
> - **Common Pitfall #2:** Forgetting to add **freeboard** to the calculated spillway head. Always add a minimum of **0.3 m freeboard**.
> - **Common Pitfall #3:** Ignoring **stability checks** (sliding, overturning, uplift). The examiner expects at least a brief mention of Factor of Safety (FOS) ≥ 1.5 against sliding.
> - **Common Pitfall #4:** Writing formulas without **units** — every numerical answer MUST carry units (`m³/s`, `m`, `m³`).

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model 14-Mark Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the different types of check dams. Discuss the site selection criteria for constructing a check dam.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A check dam is proposed across a small stream with the following data:
- Catchment area = 30 ha
- Runoff coefficient C = 0.40
- Rainfall intensity (for 25-year return period, 30-min duration) = 70 mm/hr
- Stream bed slope = 1.2% (0.012)
- Assume broad-crested weir with `C_d = 0.95` and crest length `L = 8 m`

**Calculate:**
1. Peak runoff discharge
2. Effective head over the spillway
3. Spacing between successive check dams (if dam height = 1.5 m)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Types of Check Dams & Site Selection Criteria [7 Marks]:**

**Types of Check Dams:**

1. **Loose Boulder Check Dam:** Constructed by stacking boulders (200–300 mm size) across the stream, often wire-bound into a gabion box. Cost-effective, semi-permanent. Best for low-velocity streams.

2. **Masonry Check Dam:** Built using brick or stone masonry in cement mortar (1:6 or 1:4). Most common type; suitable for medium-velocity streams. Lifespan 20–30 years.

3. **Concrete Check Dam:** Cast-in-place or pre-cast concrete, often reinforced. Permanent structure; suitable for high-velocity streams and narrow gully sections.

4. **Gabion Check Dam:** Wire-mesh boxes filled with stones. Highly flexible, can absorb differential settlement. Ideal for erodible stream banks.

5. **Earthen Check Dam:** Compacted earth embankment with a masonry core wall. Used in very small gullies where material availability favors earth fill.

**Site Selection Criteria:**

1. The stream reach should have a **gradient of 1–3%** — steep enough to require erosion control, but not so steep that construction is infeasible.
2. The stream banks should be **stable, non-erodible, and of similar elevation**.
3. The streambed should be composed of **permeable sandy-gravelly soil** (hydraulic conductivity K ≥ 5 m/day) for effective recharge.
4. The catchment area should be **5–100 ha** — too small yields negligible recharge, too large exceeds design capacity.
5. The site should be **geologically stable** with a shallow **rock/impervious layer** for the cut-off wall to key into.
6. The site must have **easy accessibility** for transporting construction material and for future maintenance (de-silting).
7. The upstream pool should **not submerge agricultural land or habitation**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Design [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Peak Runoff (Rational Method):**

$$Q = C \cdot i \cdot A$$

Convert units: `i = 70 mm/hr = 70 \times 10^{-3} / 3600 m/s = 1.944 \times 10^{-5} m/s`

`A = 30 ha = 3 \times 10^5 m^2`

$$Q = 0.40 \times 1.944 \times 10^{-5} \times 3 \times 10^5$$

$$Q = 0.40 \times 5.833 = 2.333 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Answer 1:** **Peak runoff Q = 2.33 m³/s** **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Effective Spillway Head (Broad-Crested Weir):**

$$Q = 1.704 \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot H_e^{3/2}$$

$$2.333 = 1.704 \times 0.95 \times 8 \times H_e^{3/2}$$

$$2.333 = 12.9504 \times H_e^{3/2}$$

$$H_e^{3/2} = 0.1802$$

$$H_e = (0.1802)^{2/3} = 0.319 \text{ m}$$

Total dam height with 0.3 m freeboard: `H_total = 0.319 + 0.30 = 0.619 m → adopt 0.80 m`

**Answer 2:** **Effective head H_e = 0.32 m; Dam height = 0.80 m (after freeboard)** **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Spacing of Check Dams:**

$$S = \frac{H_{dam}}{S_0} = \frac{1.5}{0.012} = 125 \text{ m}$$

Apply practical multiplication factor (1.2 for backwater effect):

$$S_{field} = 1.2 \times 125 = 150 \text{ m}$$

**Answer 3:** **Field spacing ≈ 150 m** **(2 marks)**

**Final Summary Box:** **(1 mark)**

| Parameter | Computed Value |
|---|---|
| Peak Runoff (Q) | 2.33 m³/s |
| Spillway Head (H_e) | 0.32 m |
| Freeboard | 0.30 m |
| Total Dam Height (adopted) | 0.80 m |
| Check Dam Spacing | 150 m |

---

### Question B: [Alternative 14-Mark Question — Internal Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the components of a check dam with a neat cross-sectional sketch. Explain the function of the spillway, apron, and toe wall in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A watershed has a stream slope of 2%. If a series of check dams, each of height 1.2 m, is to be constructed, determine:

1. The theoretical spacing between successive check dams.
2. The actual spacing recommended in the field (with a backwater factor of 1.3).
3. The number of check dams required for a total stream length of 1.2 km within the watershed.

If the storage volume per check dam is 600 m³, estimate the **total annual groundwater recharge potential** (assuming 8 effective rainfall events per year and an infiltration efficiency of 70%).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Components of a Check Dam [7 Marks]:**

**Cross-Sectional Sketch (to be drawn in exam):**

```
         |     Water Surface
         |     ~~~~~~~~~~
   ------+-----________  <-- Crest (Spillway)
   |    |||    |
   |  MASONRY  |  <-- Dam Body (Masonry)
   |    |||    |
   |  HEADWALL |
   |__________|
   |          |  <-- Apron (downstream)
   |  Apron   |__________________
   |  3m     |  Toe Wall       |__
   |_________|__________________|  \  <-- Streambed
   | Cut-off                          \
   | Wall                             \_____
   ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ (Impervious Foundation)
```

**Function of Key Components:**

1. **Spillway (Weir Crest):** It is the elevated central (or full-width) section of the dam over which excess water flows. It is designed using the broad-crested weir formula to safely discharge the design peak runoff without overtopping the dam body. Without the spillway, water would overtop the dam body and cause catastrophic failure.

2. **Apron:** A flat, paved (stone or concrete) horizontal surface on the downstream side, immediately after the spillway. It protects the streambed from scour caused by the high-velocity spilling water. The apron length is typically 2–4 m, depending on the spillway head.

3. **Toe Wall:** A short vertical wall constructed at the downstream end of the apron, extending 0.3–0.5 m into the streambed. It anchors the apron and prevents undermining (scour pit formation) at the downstream end. Combined with the apron and a stilling basin, the toe wall forms the **energy dissipation system**.

---

**Part (b) — Spacing and Recharge Potential [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Theoretical Spacing:**

$$S_{theory} = \frac{H_{dam}}{S_0} = \frac{1.2}{0.02} = 60 \text{ m}$$

**Answer 1:** **Theoretical spacing = 60 m** **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 2 — Actual Field Spacing (with factor 1.3):**

$$S_{field} = 1.3 \times 60 = 78 \text{ m} \approx 80 \text{ m}$$

**Answer 2:** **Field spacing = 78 m (≈ 80 m)** **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — Number of Check Dams for 1.2 km:**

$$N = \frac{L_{total}}{S_{field}} = \frac{1200}{78} = 15.38 \approx 16 \text{ dams}$$

(Always round up to ensure coverage at the downstream end.)

**Answer 3:** **Number of check dams = 16** **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Total Annual Recharge Potential:**

Effective recharge volume per dam per event = `600 × 0.70 = 420 m³`

Total events per year = 8

Total recharge per dam per year = `420 × 8 = 3{,}360 m³/yr`

Total recharge for 16 dams = `16 × 3{,}360 = 53{,}760 m³/yr`

**Answer 4:** **Annual recharge potential = 53,760 m³/year (≈ 5.376 million litres/year)** **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for Check Dams (KTU OECET832):**

- ✅ Check dams are **low-height barriers (0.5–2.0 m)** built across small streams for **groundwater recharge** and **erosion control**.
- ✅ They are **NOT storage dams**; they hold water only temporarily (hours to days).
- ✅ **Catchment area limit: 5–100 ha** — beyond this, percolation tanks or nala bunds are preferred.
- ✅ **Rational Method** `Q = C · i · A` is the standard peak runoff formula for design.
- ✅ **Broad-crested weir formula** `Q = 1.704 · C_d · L · H_e^{3/2}` is used for spillway design.
- ✅ **Spacing formula** `S = H_dam / S_0` (multiplied by 1.2–1.5 in practice).
- ✅ **Freeboard ≥ 0.3 m** must be added above the maximum water level.
- ✅ **Factor of safety ≥ 1.5** against sliding and overturning is mandatory.
- ✅ **Cut-off wall** prevents under-seepage and piping failure.
- ✅ **Hydraulic jump** on the apron dissipates the energy of overflowing water.
- ✅ **Components to remember:** Headwall, Main body, Spillway, Apron, Stilling basin, Toe wall, Cut-off wall, Wing walls.
- ✅ **Types:** Loose boulder, Masonry, Concrete, Gabion, Earthen.
- ✅ **Site selection** hinges on slope (1–3%), permeable streambed, stable banks, and accessible location.
- ✅ **Check Dam Cascade** = series of check dams at calculated spacing for cumulative recharge benefit.
- ✅ **Silt trap** upstream of dam reduces maintenance and creates a natural recharge-friendly bed.
- ✅ De-silting every **3–5 years** is essential for sustained performance.

> **End of Topic: Check Dams — Module 1, OECET832 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Artificial Recharge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Artificial Recharge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Artificial Recharge (AR)** is the engineered, deliberate process of augmenting the natural replenishment of groundwater aquifers by conveying surplus surface water — typically harvested runoff, treated wastewater, or streamflow — into subsurface geological formations through specially designed structures, so that it percolates downward under the influence of gravity and is stored within the pore spaces of the aquifer matrix.

According to the **Central Ground Water Board (CGWB)** and the **Ministry of Jal Shakti** (Government of India), artificial recharge is formally defined as:

> *"The process by which the groundwater reservoir is augmented at a rate exceeding that obtainable under natural conditions, by induced infiltration from surface sources, or by direct injection through wells."*

**Standard Classification Vocabulary used in KTU answers:**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Aquifer** | Saturated geological formation storing and transmitting water |
| **Recharge** | Addition of water to an aquifer |
| **Discharge** | Removal of water from an aquifer |
| **Percolation** | Vertical movement of water through soil under gravity |
| **Infiltration** | Entry of water from the surface into the ground |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Groundwater Battery" Model

Imagine a **rechargeable battery** underground:
- **Aquifer** = the empty battery cell with porous lead plates
- **Rainfall/Monsoon** = the natural charger
- **Artificial Recharge Structures** = the external high-power charger plugged in to refill quickly
- **Bore wells / Hand pumps** = the discharge terminals draining current

During the monsoon, rainfall in Kerala is intense and short (≈ 3000 mm/year concentrated in 4–5 months). The ground cannot absorb it all — about **60–70 %** runs off into rivers and finally into the Arabian Sea within days. This is **wasted water** that could have recharged our depleting aquifers. Artificial recharge structures are essentially the engineering tools that "slow down" this runoff and "force" it into the ground.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Kerala loses nearly 70 % of its rainfall as surface runoff. The goal of artificial recharge is to convert this RUNOFF into INFILTRATION, recharging the shallow and deep aquifers for year-round use.

### 1.3 Need & Necessity (Why AR?)

- **Declining water table:** Over-extraction through bore wells causes water table to fall at **0.5 m to 2 m/year** in many Kerala districts.
- **Urban flooding:** Impervious surfaces (concrete, asphalt) prevent infiltration → flooding.
- **Saltwater intrusion in coastal Kerala** (Kochi, Alappuzha, Kozhikode) due to over-pumping.
- **Drought resilience:** Stored groundwater is more reliable than surface reservoirs in dry months.
- **Equity:** Rural water security for hand-pumps and open wells.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Memorise for KTU)

> **[!IMPORTANT] Engineering Constants**
> - Porosity of alluvial sand ≈ **30 – 35 %**
> - Specific yield of sand ≈ **20 – 25 %**
> - Specific retention of clay ≈ **45 – 55 %**
> - Permeability of laterite (Kerala typical) ≈ **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ cm/s**
> - Recommended recharge per recharge well ≈ **0.5 to 1.5 m³/hour**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Artificial Recharge Methods

KTU expects a **two-tier classification**: **Direct Methods** and **Indirect Methods**.

#### A. DIRECT METHODS (Water is added directly to the aquifer from the surface)

**A1. Surface Spreading Methods** (suitable when permeable strata occur at shallow depth, < 10 m)

| Method | Description | Best for |
|---|---|---|
| **Flooding** | Spreading water in a thin sheet over flat pervious land | Alluvial flood plains |
| **Recharge Basins** | Shallow, flat-bottomed ponds that hold water for percolation | Open spaces in institutions, parks |
| **Percolation Tanks** | Small tanks (3–6 m deep) built across seasonal streams | Rural catchments, laterite terrain |
| **Stream Channel Modification** | Cleaning, desilting, and roughening stream beds to increase infiltration | River beds in dry season |
| **Ditch and Furrow System** | Parallel ditches excavated along contour, water diverted into them | Gentle slopes |
| **Terracing (Bench Terraces)** | Step-like fields on hill slopes | Hilly terrain (Wayanad, Idukki) |

**A2. Sub-Surface / Well Methods** (suitable when impervious layer overlies the aquifer at depth)

| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| **Recharge Pit** | Square pit 1–3 m deep filled with gravel/sand, water diverted from rooftop |
| **Recharge Trench** | Long narrow trench (2–3 m deep) filled with boulders, used along drains |
| **Recharge Shaft** | Vertical shaft 5–15 m deep drilled through impervious clay layer to reach the aquifer below; packed with gravel |
| **Recharge Well (Diffusion Well)** | Ordinary bore well used in REVERSE — water poured in instead of pumped out. Slotted casing with gravel pack. |
| **Injection Well** | Tubewell with a foot valve & non-return arrangement, used to pump treated water into confined aquifers |
| **Abandoned Dug Well Recharge** | Existing open well fitted with filter, used as recharge structure |

#### B. INDIRECT METHODS (Water is induced into the aquifer by influencing natural hydraulic gradients)

- **Induced Recharge from Rivers:** Pump water from a well located near a perennial river → water table cone of depression extends to the river → river water flows laterally into the well (recharging the aquifer).
- **Sand Dams:** Sub-surface check dams built across sandy stream beds; store water within sand pores, reduce evaporation.
- **Subsurface Dykes / Underground Bandharas:** Impervious barriers constructed across subsurface flow paths to arrest groundwater flow and raise water table up-gradient.

### 2.2 Selection Criteria — Which Method to Use?

A clean tabular logic is essential for KTU answer writing:

> **[!IMPORTANT] Decision Matrix for Choosing AR Method**

| Site Condition | Recommended Method |
|---|---|
| Shallow water table, permeable sandy soil, gentle slope | Recharge Basin, Flooding |
| Impervious clay layer at surface, permeable aquifer at 5–15 m depth | Recharge Shaft / Recharge Well |
| Sloping terrain, hilly laterite area | Percolation Tank + Bench Terracing |
| Urban rooftop, limited open space | Recharge Pit / Recharge Trench |
| Confined aquifer at depth > 30 m | Injection Well |
| Seasonal stream with sandy bed | Percolation Tank / Sand Dam |
| Alluvial plain with deep water table | Recharge Well cluster |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **[!IMPORTANT] High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$ | $Q$ = discharge (m³/day); $K$ = hydraulic conductivity (m/day); $i$ = hydraulic gradient; $A$ = cross-section area (m²) | Darcy's Law — Recharge rate calculation |
| 2 | $V = K \cdot i$ | $V$ = Darcy velocity (m/day) | Seepage velocity (apparent) |
| 3 | $v_s = \dfrac{V}{n_e}$ | $v_s$ = seepage velocity (real); $n_e$ = effective porosity | Actual groundwater velocity |
| 4 | $T = K \cdot b$ | $T$ = transmissivity (m²/day); $b$ = aquifer thickness | Aquifer capacity |
| 5 | $R_{vol} = S_y \cdot A \cdot \Delta h$ | $R_{vol}$ = recharge volume; $S_y$ = specific yield; $A$ = area; $\Delta h$ = rise in water table | Volume of water stored |
| 6 | $R = P - AET \pm \Delta S - RO$ | $R$ = recharge; $P$ = precipitation; $AET$ = actual evapotranspiration; $\Delta S$ = change in storage; $RO$ = runoff | Water balance equation |
| 7 | $R_{well} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot K \cdot (H^2 - h_w^2)}{\ln(R/r_w)}$ | $H$ = static head; $h_w$ = head inside well; $R$ = radius of influence; $r_w$ = well radius | Injection well capacity (Dupuit) |
| 8 | $Q_{runoff} = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | $C$ = runoff coefficient; $I$ = rainfall intensity; $A$ = catchment area | Available water for recharge |
| 9 | $C_{RWP} = \dfrac{0.8 \cdot A_h}{A_c}$ | $C_{RWP}$ = recharge well capacity factor; $A_h$ = roof area; $A_c$ = catchment | Rooftop recharge design (GEC 2015) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** In the answer sheet, ALWAYS write the formula in symbolic form FIRST, then substitute numerical values, then state the unit. Examiners award marks for this standard order.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Kerala Suchitwa Mission** has implemented 1,00,000+ recharge structures since 2018.
- **Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme (MGNREGA)** funds AR structures.
- CGWB recommends a **per-well design capacity of 1.0 m³/hour** for rooftop recharge in Kerala.
- **GEC-2015 (Government of Kerala)** manual provides district-wise runoff coefficients: Trivandrum ≈ **0.55**, Kottayam ≈ **0.50**, Palakkad ≈ **0.45**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Recharge Well Capacity (Dupuit Equation adapted for Injection)

We start from **Darcy's Law**:

$$Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$$

For a radial flow towards a recharge well in a confined/unconfined aquifer (Dupuit assumption), consider a cylindrical surface of radius $r$ at distance from the well axis.

The cross-sectional area through which water flows is:

$$A = 2 \pi r b$$

The hydraulic gradient is the slope of water table (piezometric head drop) over the radial distance:

$$i = \dfrac{dh}{dr}$$

Substituting into Darcy's law:

$$Q = K \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dr} \cdot 2 \pi r b$$

Rearranging variables to integrate:

$$Q \cdot \dfrac{dr}{r} = 2 \pi K b \cdot dh$$

Integrating between the limits $r = r_w$ (well radius, head $h_w$) and $r = R$ (radius of influence, head $H$):

$$\begin{aligned}
Q \int_{r_w}^{R} \dfrac{dr}{r} &= 2 \pi K b \int_{h_w}^{H} dh
\end{aligned}$$

Performing the integration:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r_w}\right) &= 2 \pi K b \cdot (H - h_w)
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q = \dfrac{2 \pi K b \,(H - h_w)}{\ln(R/r_w)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For unconfined aquifer** (most Kerala case), $b$ is replaced by average saturated thickness: $b_{avg} = (H + h_w)/2$, yielding the final form:

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi K \,(H^2 - h_w^2)}{\ln(R/r_w)}$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** for recharge well design.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Specific Yield Method for Recharge Volume Estimation

If a recharge structure raises the water table by $\Delta h$ over an area $A$, the volume of water recharged is governed by the **specific yield** $S_y$ (the fraction of water that drains freely from a saturated sample):

$$R_{vol} = S_y \cdot A \cdot \Delta h$$

**Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem):**

*Given:* A recharge basin of area $A = 1000 \text{ m}^2$ in Alappuzha district. Specific yield of the sandy aquifer $S_y = 0.20$. Water table rise observed after monsoon = $\Delta h = 1.5 \text{ m}$. Compute the recharge volume.

**Solution Step-by-Step:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{vol} &= S_y \cdot A \cdot \Delta h \\
R_{vol} &= 0.20 \times 1000 \text{ m}^2 \times 1.5 \text{ m} \\
R_{vol} &= 300 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The basin has recharged **300 m³ (or 3,00,000 litres)** of water — enough to supply 50 families (at 135 lpcd × 365 days ÷ 50) for over a year if managed properly.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Catchment Runoff Yield for AR Design

For rooftop rainwater harvesting (RRH), the available runoff volume is:

$$Q_{runoff} = C \cdot P \cdot A_c$$

Where:
- $C$ = runoff coefficient (0.80 for RCC roof, 0.50 for GI sheet, 0.30 for tiled roof)
- $P$ = annual rainfall (m)
- $A_c$ = catchment (roof) area (m²)

**Worked Numerical Example:**

*Given:* A house in Kottayam with RCC roof area $A_c = 120 \text{ m}^2$, annual rainfall $P = 2.8 \text{ m}$, runoff coefficient $C = 0.80$. Recharge well capacity factor $F = 0.8 \text{ m/hr}$ (from GEC 2015). Required: a) Annual runoff harvestable b) Sizing of recharge well.

**Part (a) — Annual Runoff:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{runoff} &= C \cdot P \cdot A_c \\
&= 0.80 \times 2.8 \text{ m} \times 120 \text{ m}^2 \\
&= 268.8 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) — Recharge Well Size (Kerala Suchitwa Mission formula):**

The recommended design rule is: *recharge well diameter = 0.3 m (300 mm) for residential; depth = 6 m below water table.*

For our case, since $Q_{runoff} = 268.8 \text{ m}^3$ ≈ 2,68,800 litres/year, a **single 300 mm diameter recharge well of 6–9 m depth** is sufficient.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Table (Direct vs Indirect Methods)

| Parameter | Direct Method | Indirect Method |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Gravity percolation | Hydraulic gradient manipulation |
| Capital Cost | Low to moderate | Higher (pumps required) |
| Operation Cost | Minimal (no power) | Recurring electricity cost |
| Maintenance | Periodic desilting | Pump servicing, sand replacement |
| Suitability in Kerala | Excellent for laterite/hilly terrain | Excellent for alluvial/coastal aquifer |
| Clogging Risk | High (silt) | Moderate (biofouling) |
| Evaporation Loss | High for surface methods | Negligible |
| Recharge Efficiency | 60 – 80 % | 80 – 95 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Artificial Recharge Decision & Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Site Survey] --> B[Topography Assessment]
    A --> C[Soil Permeability Test]
    A --> D[Water Table Depth Measurement]
    A --> E[Aquifer Type Identification]

    B --> F{Decision Matrix}
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F --> G[Shallow Aquifer < 10m]
    F --> H[Deep Aquifer > 10m]
    F --> I[Confined Aquifer > 30m]

    G --> J1[Recharge Basin]
    G --> J2[Percolation Tank]
    G --> J3[Recharge Pit Trench]

    H --> K1[Recharge Shaft]
    H --> K2[Recharge Well]
    H --> K3[Abandoned Well Recharge]

    I --> L1[Injection Well]
    I --> L2[Tube Well Recharge]

    J1 --> M[Filter Media Layer]
    J2 --> M
    J3 --> M
    K1 --> N[Gravel Pack]
    K2 --> N
    K3 --> N
    L1 --> O[Foot Valve NRV]
    L2 --> O

    M --> P[Percolation into Aquifer]
    N --> P
    O --> P

    P --> Q[Groundwater Storage]
    Q --> R[Monitoring: Water Table Rise]
    Q --> S[Quality Testing: TDS, pH]

    R --> T[Annual Yield Calculation]
    S --> T
    T --> U[Sustainable Water Supply]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Rooftop Recharge System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Roof Catchment] -->|Rainwater| B1[Gutter Downpipe]
    B1 --> C1[First Flush Diverter]
    C1 --> D1[Filter Chamber Sand Gravel Charcoal]
    D1 --> E1[Inspection Chamber]
    E1 --> F1[Recharge Well 6m depth]
    F1 --> G1[Aquifer Recharge Zone]
    F1 --> H1[Overflow to Recharge Pit]
    H1 --> I1[Vegetation Recharge Basin]
    I1 --> J1[Spillway to Storm Drain]
    G1 --> K1[Groundwater Table]
    K1 --> L1[Nearby Bore Well Recharged]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Recharge Well Vertical Cross-Section (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Ground Level] --> A2[Top Soil 0 to 0.5m]
    A2 --> A3[Filter Pack: Sand Layer 0.3m]
    A3 --> A4[Boulder Layer 0.3m]
    A4 --> A5[Slotted Casing Recharge Section 3 to 6m depth]
    A5 --> A6[Gravel Pack Annulus 50mm thickness]
    A6 --> A7[Static Water Table Line]
    A7 --> A8[Aquifer Material: Laterite Sandy Clay]
    A8 --> A9[Impervious Bedrock]
```

### 4.4 Classification Tree of AR Methods

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Artificial Recharge Methods))
    Direct Methods
      Surface Spreading
        Flooding
        Recharge Basins
        Percolation Tanks
        Stream Channel Mod
        Ditch and Furrow
        Terracing
      SubSurface Methods
        Recharge Pit
        Recharge Trench
        Recharge Shaft
        Recharge Well
        Injection Well
    Indirect Methods
      Induced Recharge
      Sand Dams
      Subsurface Dykes
      Subsurface Check Dams
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define artificial recharge. Mention any two direct methods of artificial recharge.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Artificial recharge is the engineered process of augmenting the natural recharge of groundwater by conveying surface water into subsurface aquifers through specially designed structures, at a rate exceeding natural replenishment **(2 marks)**.

Two direct methods:
1. **Recharge Basin** — shallow flat-bottomed pond that holds water for vertical percolation through permeable soil **(0.5 marks)**.
2. **Recharge Shaft** — vertical shaft 5–15 m deep drilled through an impervious clay layer to allow water to reach the deeper permeable aquifer **(0.5 marks)**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
*What is the significance of specific yield in the design of artificial recharge structures?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Specific yield ($S_y$) is the ratio of the volume of water that drains from a saturated rock/soil under gravity to the total volume of the sample, expressed as a decimal **(1.5 marks)**.

Significance in AR design:
1. It directly determines the **storage capacity** of the aquifer — the higher the $S_y$, the greater the volume of water that can be recharged per unit rise of water table **(1 mark)**.
2. Used in the formula $R_{vol} = S_y \times A \times \Delta h$ to compute the **recharge volume** achievable from a structure, which in turn determines structure sizing **(0.5 marks)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** write only the definition for a 14-mark question — examiners expect **classification + diagrams + formulas + numerical** wherever possible.
> - **Always** state the Governing Equation FIRST, then substitute values, then box/underline the final numerical answer.
> - **For 7-mark sub-parts**, divide your answer into: (a) Concept (3 marks) + (b) Formula/Diagram/Numerical (4 marks).
> - **Diagrams** must be **labelled, scaled, and captioned** (e.g., "Fig. 1: Schematic of Recharge Well"). Unlabelled diagrams fetch **zero marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Classify the various methods of artificial recharge. Explain any two surface spreading methods in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A recharge basin of rectangular plan 25 m × 40 m is constructed over a sandy aquifer with specific yield 0.18. After a period of artificial recharge, the water table in the observation well rises by 1.2 m. Estimate the volume of water recharged into the aquifer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification (3 marks):**

Methods of artificial recharge are classified into:
- **Direct Methods:** Water is added directly from the surface to the aquifer.
  - *Surface spreading:* Flooding, Recharge Basins, Percolation Tanks, Stream Channel Modification, Ditch and Furrow, Terracing.
  - *Sub-surface methods:* Recharge Pit, Recharge Trench, Recharge Shaft, Recharge Well, Injection Well.
- **Indirect Methods:** Aquifer is recharged by manipulation of hydraulic gradients.
  - Induced recharge, Sand dams, Subsurface dykes.

**Surface Spreading Methods — Any Two (4 marks):**

*1. Recharge Basin:*
A shallow, flat-bottomed pond with gentle side slopes, constructed in permeable soil where the water table is at least 2 m below ground. Storm water is diverted into the basin where it stands and percolates downward. The basin is normally desilted annually. Typical design size: 100 m² to 10,000 m². Suitable for: campuses, parks, percolation over laterite.

*2. Percolation Tank:*
A small impoundment (3–6 m deep) built across a seasonal stream in laterite/hilly terrain. The bed is desilted and fractured. The tank stores water during monsoon; this water percolates through the fractured laterite bed and recharges the deep aquifer. Effective where the underlying rock is fractured or jointed. Common in Wayanad, Idukki, Palakkad.

*(Alternative: Flooding, Stream Channel Modification, Ditch & Furrow, Terracing)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given:
- Length of basin $L = 25 \text{ m}$, Breadth $B = 40 \text{ m}$
- Specific yield $S_y = 0.18$
- Rise in water table $\Delta h = 1.2 \text{ m}$

**Step 1:** Compute the basin area:

$$A = L \times B = 25 \times 40 = 1000 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Write the governing equation:

$$R_{vol} = S_y \cdot A \cdot \Delta h$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$R_{vol} = 0.18 \times 1000 \text{ m}^2 \times 1.2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Final Answer:

$$\boxed{R_{vol} = 216 \text{ m}^3 \;\;(= 2{,}16{,}000 \text{ litres})}$$

**Interpretation:** This is equivalent to the annual drinking water requirement of ~35 families @ 135 lpcd.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**(a)** What is artificial recharge? Discuss in detail the design of a recharge well. List the factors governing the selection of a recharge well site. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rooftop rainwater harvesting system is proposed for a residential building in Thrissur with a 150 m² RCC roof. Annual rainfall = 2700 mm; runoff coefficient = 0.85. Compute: (i) Annual runoff harvestable, (ii) Volume available for recharge per recharge well, assuming the standard design provides a factor of 0.8 m³/hr per 100 m² of roof area. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Artificial Recharge — Definition + Recharge Well Design + Selection Factors:**

*Definition (2 marks):* Artificial recharge is the deliberate augmentation of natural groundwater recharge by engineering methods that introduce surface water into aquifers faster than natural processes.

*Design of a Recharge Well (3 marks):* A recharge well is a bore well used in REVERSE — instead of pumping water out, treated rooftop runoff is poured in. Components:
1. **Casing:** 100–300 mm dia PVC/MS slotted pipe for the section below water table.
2. **Gravel Pack:** 50–75 mm thick annulus of graded gravel (6–20 mm) to filter suspended solids and prevent clogging.
3. **Filter Chamber at Top:** Sand + gravel + charcoal to remove debris.
4. **Slotted Section Length:** 3–6 m located in the most permeable aquifer zone.
5. **Bottom Cap:** Closed at the bottom or open in rocky strata.
6. **Depth:** 6–15 m typical in Kerala; reaches 3–6 m below static water table.

*Selection Factors (2 marks):*
- Water table should be 5–15 m below ground.
- Subsoil profile should have an impervious layer at top and permeable aquifer at depth.
- Aquifer should be unconfined or semi-confined.
- Distance from contamination sources ≥ 30 m.
- Roof catchment area ≥ 100 m² for cost-effectiveness.
- Soil permeability $K \geq 10^{-4}$ cm/s.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: Roof area $A_c = 150 \text{ m}^2$, $P = 2700 \text{ mm} = 2.7 \text{ m}$, $C = 0.85$, factor $f = 0.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr per }100 \text{ m}^2$.

**(i) Annual Runoff Harvestable:**

$$Q_{runoff} = C \cdot P \cdot A_c$$

$$Q_{runoff} = 0.85 \times 2.7 \text{ m} \times 150 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{Q_{runoff} = 344.25 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per year}}$$

**(ii) Volume Available per Recharge Well:**

Design factor $f = 0.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$ for every 100 m² of roof.

For 150 m², the equivalent flow rate is:

$$Q_{well} = f \times \dfrac{A_c}{100} = 0.8 \times \dfrac{150}{100} = 1.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

Assuming the system operates for **4 months of effective monsoon** (~ 120 days × 4 hr/day average inflow):

$$V_{well} = 1.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} \times 4 \text{ hr/day} \times 120 \text{ days}$$

$$\boxed{V_{well} = 576 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Conclusion:** A single 300 mm dia × 9 m deep recharge well will fully accommodate the rooftop runoff of the building.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **[!IMPORTANT] Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** Artificial recharge = deliberate augmentation of natural groundwater recharge through engineered structures.
- **Two main classes:** Direct (surface spreading + sub-surface) and Indirect (induced, sand dams, subsurface dykes).
- **Surface spreading methods** work when permeable soil is at top; **recharge well/shaft** is used when an impervious layer overlies the aquifer.
- **Darcy's Law** $Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$ is the foundation equation; in radial flow to a well, this transforms to the **Dupuit injection equation** $Q = \pi K (H^2 - h_w^2)/\ln(R/r_w)$.
- **Specific Yield** $S_y$ controls the recharge volume: $R_{vol} = S_y \cdot A \cdot \Delta h$.
- **Rooftop formula:** $Q_{runoff} = C \cdot P \cdot A_c$; typical $C$ for RCC = 0.8.
- **Kerala design recommendation:** 300 mm dia recharge well × 6–9 m depth for residential; 600 mm × 9–15 m for institutional.
- **Always desilt** the recharge structure once a year (May–June) to prevent clogging.
- **Monitor** the rise in water table with a permanent piezometer; minimum expected rise ≈ 0.5–1.5 m during monsoon.
- **Minimum safe distance** from soak pit, septic tank, or latrine = **30 m** (as per GEC 2015).
- **Keyword bank for answers:** Percolation, Infiltration, Hydraulic gradient, Specific yield, Transmissivity, Dupuit, Radius of influence, Runoff coefficient, Recharge well, Percolation tank, Induced recharge.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Farm Ponds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Farm Ponds # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **FARM POND (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)** > A **farm pond** is a small, artificially constructed water storage reservoir, typically excavated in agricultural land or formed by building embankments across small natural depressions or gullies, designed primarily to **capture, store, and conserve surface runoff** generated within a defined catchment area during rainfall events. The stored water is subsequently utilized for **supplemental irrigation, livestock watering, fish culture, and groundwater recharge**, thereby enhancing agricultural productivity and drought resilience. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (OECET832 – Rainwater Harvesting), farm ponds are categorized as one of the most critical **in-situ water harvesting structures** for individual farm-level implementation under the *MGNREGA*, *PMKSY*, and *Integrated Watershed Management Programme (IWMP)* frameworks adopted by the Government of India. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Family Savings Account"** > Think of a farm pond exactly like a **piggy bank** in a household. > - **The Farm (Land)** = The earning member of the family. > - **Rainfall (Income)** = Money earned during the rainy season (irregular but abundant). > - **Farm Pond (Piggy Bank)** = A small reserve where excess earnings (runoff water) are saved when the "income" (rain) is plentiful. > - **Dry Season (Spending Time)** = When natural streams dry up, the family dips into the savings (stored pond water) for daily needs (irrigation). > - **Evaporation & Seepage Losses** = Small "fees" the bank charges for keeping the money safe. > > Without a piggy bank (farm pond), surplus water (runoff) would simply *run away down the drain* and be lost forever — wasted in the same way wasted income can never be recovered. ## 1.3 Classification of Farm Ponds (KTU Reference Standard) Farm ponds are classified based on **location, method of construction, and purpose**: | Classification Basis | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | **By Location** | *Dug-out (Excavated) Pond* | Constructed entirely by digging below ground level; suitable for flat terrain with low water table. | | | *Embankment (Surface) Pond* | Built by constructing an embankment across a small stream/gully; water stored partly above ground. | | | *Sunken + Embankment (Combination)* | Part excavation + part embankment; most common KTU reference design. | | **By Lining** | *Unlined Pond* | Natural soil acts as sealant; requires clayey or silty clay soil (low permeability, k < 10⁻⁷ m/s). | | | *Lined Pond* | Uses HDPE, LDPE, PVC, or brick-cement lining to control seepage. | | **By Catchment** | *Individual / On-Farm Pond* | Serves a single farm holding (typical size: 100–1000 m³). | | | *Community Farm Pond* | Larger ponds serving a group of farmers (typical size: 1000–10,000 m³). | | **By Purpose** | *Irrigation Pond* | Primary use — supplemental irrigation to crops. | | | *Recharge Pond* | Designed to percolate water into the ground to augment groundwater. | | | *Dual Purpose Pond* | Combined irrigation + recharge. | ## 1.4 Standard KTU Reference Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Physical Constants & Design Thresholds (Bold for emphasis)** > - **Design Storm Return Period:** **25 years** (for embankment stability) and **10 years** (for spillway design) — as per *IS 8237:1985 (Reaffirmed 2016)*. > - **Minimum Storage Capacity:** Typically **100 m³** to qualify as a farm pond under GoI schemes. > - **Standard Side Slopes (Embankment):** **1.5 H : 1 V** (upstream) and **2.0 H : 1 V** (downstream) for stable earthfill. > - **Freeboard:** Minimum **0.6 m** above the maximum water level (MWL). > - **Catchment-Efficiency Ratio:** Storage capacity ≈ **1/10 to 1/15** of total annual runoff volume. --- # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Operational Logic — How a Farm Pond Functions The operating principle of a farm pond is a **four-stage hydrological cycle** integrated with engineered storage: 1. **RAINFALL STAGE (Input Generation)** - Precipitation falls on a defined **catchment area** (the contributing watershed). - Infiltration capacity of the soil is exceeded → **surface runoff (Hortonian overland flow)** is generated. - Not all rain becomes runoff; a portion is abstracted as infiltration, evaporation, interception, and depression storage. 2. **COLLECTION STAGE (Conveyance)** - Runoff is guided toward the pond via **graded channels, field bunds, or diversion drains**. - A **gully plug** or **silt trap** is typically installed upstream to prevent sediment choking of the pond. 3. **STORAGE STAGE (Retention)** - Runoff accumulates in the excavated/embankment pond. - Three losses occur simultaneously: **evaporation (E)**, **seepage (S)**, and **operational spillage (O)**. - Net usable storage = Inflow − (E + S + O). 4. **UTILIZATION STAGE (Withdrawal)** - Stored water is drawn via **gravity outlets, manually operated sluices, or pumps** for irrigation/livestock use. - In *recharge-type* ponds, water is intentionally lost to deep percolation to recharge the aquifer. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Parameter - **Why a 25-year design storm?** Because embankment failure can be catastrophic (loss of life, property, downstream flooding). The embankment is a permanent structure and is engineered for a higher safety threshold. - **Why freeboard?** To accommodate waves generated by wind and prevent overtopping during sudden cloudbursts. - **Why a silt trap?** Ponds in agricultural catchments receive high sediment loads; without desiltation, storage capacity degrades 20–30% within 5 years. - **Why a 1.5:1 side slope?** Soil mechanics — this slope lies within the safe angle of repose for most cohesive soils, ensuring embankment stability against shear failure. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates **all high-yield equations** for the Farm Ponds topic. Master these for Part A and Part B exam questions. | S.No | Parameter | Formula | Variable Definitions | Unit | Reference | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Annual Runoff Volume (Q)** | $Q = P \times A \times C_r$ | P = Annual rainfall; A = Catchment area; C_r = Runoff coefficient (0.1–0.4) | m³ | Rational Runoff | | 2 | **Runoff Depth by SCS-CN** | $Q = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^{2}}{(P + 0.8S)}$ where $S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254$ | S = Maximum retention; CN = Curve Number (0–100) | mm | USDA-SCS Method | | 3 | **Pond Storage Capacity (V)** | $V = Q - (E_{loss} + S_{loss} + O_{loss})$ | E = Evaporation loss; S = Seepage loss; O = Overflow loss | m³ | Mass Balance | | 4 | **Evaporation Loss (E)** | $E = A_s \times e_{pan} \times K_p$ | A_s = Pond surface area; e_pan = Pan evaporation; K_p = Pan coefficient (0.7–0.8) | m³ | Energy Balance | | 5 | **Seepage Loss (S)** | $S = K \times i \times A_w \times t$ | K = Hydraulic conductivity; i = Hydraulic gradient; A_w = Wetted area; t = Time | m³ | Darcy's Law | | 6 | **Embankment Volume** | $V_e = L \times [(b \times d) + (s \cdot d^{2})]$ | L = Embankment length; b = Top width; d = Height; s = Side slope factor | m³ | Trapezoidal Section | | 7 | **Top Width of Embankment (b)** | $b = 0.55H + Q^{1/2}$ (min 3 m) | H = Embankment height; Q = Design discharge | m | IS 8237 | | 8 | **Freeboard (F)** | $F = 0.6 + h_w$ (min 0.6 m) | h_w = Wave height (h_w = 0.014 × √F_1 × V_w) | m | IS 8237 | | 9 | **Pond Surface Area (A_s)** | $A_s = L_b \times W_b + 2(L_b + W_b)S \cdot d_{avg} + 4S^{2}d_{avg}^{2}$ | L_b, W_b = Bottom length & width; S = Side slope; d_avg = Average depth | m² | Trapezoidal Pond | | 10 | **Hydraulic Radius (R)** | $R = \dfrac{A_s}{P_w}$ | A_s = Cross-section area; P_w = Wetted perimeter | m | Open Channel Flow | | 11 | **Time of Concentration (t_c)** | $t_c = 0.0195 \cdot \dfrac{L^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}}$ (Kirpich) | L = Longest flow path; S = Average slope | minutes | Kirpich Formula | | 12 | **Peak Discharge (Q_p)** | $Q_p = \dfrac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{360}$ | C = Runoff coeff.; I = Rainfall intensity; A = Catchment area | m³/s | Rational Method | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU EXAM NOTE — Unit Conversions** > Always remember to convert: **1 hectare = 10,000 m²** ; **1 cusec = 0.0283 m³/s** ; **1 mm rainfall over 1 ha = 10 m³ water**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Farm ponds form the **backbone of climate-resilient agriculture** in India's drought-prone zones (e.g., Marathwada, Anantapur, Bundelkhand). They are also the building blocks of: - **Watershed development projects** (ridge-to-valley treatment). - **Micro-irrigation systems** (drip/sprinkler integration). - **Carbon sequestration programs** through afforestation of pond embankments. - **Fisheries and aquaculture** as a secondary livelihood source. --- # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Storage Capacity of a Trapezoidal Farm Pond **Problem Statement:** Derive the storage capacity equation for a farm pond with a **trapezoidal cross-section** having bottom length L_b, bottom width W_b, average depth d_avg, and uniform side slope S (H : V). **Step 1 — Visualize the Geometry** A trapezoidal pond can be visualized as a **rectangular prism (bottom portion) plus four sloping sides**. Viewed from above, the water surface forms a rectangle larger than the bottom. **Step 2 — Length and Width at the Water Surface** The water surface (top of pond) extends beyond the bottom on all four sides by an amount equal to $S \cdot d_{avg}$ (since for every 1 m rise, the slope S expands S metres horizontally). $$ L_{top} = L_b + 2 \cdot S \cdot d_{avg} $$ $$ W_{top} = W_b + 2 \cdot S \cdot d_{avg} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the Volume Formula for a Frustum (Truncated Pyramid)** The total storage volume equals the **average of top and bottom areas times the depth**. $$ A_{top} = L_{top} \times W_{top} = (L_b + 2Sd_{avg})(W_b + 2Sd_{avg}) $$ $$ A_{bottom} = L_b \times W_b $$ **Step 4 — Average Cross-Sectional Area** $$ A_{avg} = \dfrac{A_{top} + A_{bottom}}{2} = \dfrac{(L_b + 2Sd_{avg})(W_b + 2Sd_{avg}) + L_b W_b}{2} $$ **Step 5 — Final Storage Volume Formula** Multiplying the average area by the average depth yields the total storage capacity: $$ V = \dfrac{d_{avg}}{2} \cdot \left[ L_b W_b + (L_b + 2Sd_{avg})(W_b + 2Sd_{avg}) \right] $$ > **Engineering Insight:** This is the **exact KTU-recommended formula** for designing a rectangular-trapezoidal farm pond and is the most frequently tested 14-mark derivation. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Embankment Volume (Trapezoidal Cross-Section) **Problem Statement:** Compute the volume of earthfill required for an embankment of length L, top width b, height d, with side slope S (H : V) on both upstream and downstream faces. **Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area of the Embankment** The embankment cross-section is a **trapezoid** with: - Top width = b - Bottom width = $b + 2 \cdot S \cdot d$ (expanded due to side slopes on both sides) - Height = d $$ A_{cross} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (b + b + 2Sd) \times d = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (2b + 2Sd) \times d $$ $$ A_{cross} = (b + Sd) \cdot d $$ **Step 2 — Total Embankment Volume** Multiplying the cross-section area by the embankment length: $$ V_e = L \cdot (b + Sd) \cdot d $$ $$ V_e = L \cdot d \cdot b + L \cdot S \cdot d^{2} $$ **Step 3 — Include Settlement Allowance** Compaction settling reduces the embankment volume by 5–10% during construction. To compensate, the design height is increased by a **surchage factor (C_s = 1.10 to 1.15)**. $$ V_{e,design} = L \cdot d \cdot b + L \cdot S \cdot d^{2} \times C_s $$ > **Real-World Utility:** This formula is the basis for **bill of quantities (BoQ)** and cost estimation of farm pond embankments under MGNREGA/PMKSY projects. --- ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Model Answer Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **Standard 14-Mark Question Type — KTU Board Exam Pattern** **Question:** Design a farm pond for the following data and compute the storage capacity. - Bottom length: $L_b = 20$ m - Bottom width: $W_b = 15$ m - Average water depth: $d_{avg} = 2.5$ m - Side slope: 1.5 H : 1 V (S = 1.5) - Embankment length: $L = 60$ m, height: $d = 3$ m, top width: $b = 3$ m - Annual rainfall: $P = 800$ mm - Catchment area: $A = 2.0$ ha - Runoff coefficient: $C_r = 0.25$ **Solution (Step-by-Step):** **Step (a) — Compute Top Dimensions** $$ L_{top} = L_b + 2 \cdot S \cdot d_{avg} = 20 + 2(1.5)(2.5) = 27.5 \text{ m} $$ $$ W_{top} = W_b + 2 \cdot S \cdot d_{avg} = 15 + 2(1.5)(2.5) = 22.5 \text{ m} $$ **Step (b) — Compute Storage Volume** $$ A_{top} = 27.5 \times 22.5 = 618.75 \text{ m}^{2} $$ $$ A_{bottom} = 20 \times 15 = 300 \text{ m}^{2} $$ $$ V = \dfrac{2.5}{2} \times (300 + 618.75) = \dfrac{2.5 \times 918.75}{2} = 1148.44 \text{ m}^{3} $$ **Step (c) — Verify Sufficiency with Annual Runoff** $$ Q = P \times A \times C_r = 0.8 \text{ m} \times 20000 \text{ m}^{2} \times 0.25 = 4000 \text{ m}^{3} $$ Since $V = 1148.44 \text{ m}^{3} \ll Q = 4000 \text{ m}^{3}$, the pond will overflow frequently.
