> KTU 2024 demands that students can **derive the harvestable water quantity** from first principles. Below is the complete, exhaustive derivation chain.

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantifying the Harvestable Rainwater Volume

### Step 1 — Start with the Fundamental Conservation Equation

The total volume of rain falling on a catchment is the product of the rainfall depth, the catchment area, and a unit-conversion factor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{total rainfall}} &= \text{Rainfall depth} \times \text{Catchment area} \times \text{Conversion factor}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** If rainfall depth is measured in **mm**, the catchment area in **m²**, and we want the volume in **Liters**, we use the conversion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \,\text{mm} \times 1 \,\text{m}^2 &= 1 \,\text{Liter}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Runoff Coefficient

Not all rain that falls on the catchment is available for harvesting. Some is lost to evaporation, splash, absorption, and depression storage. The **Runoff Coefficient ($C$)** accounts for this.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{runoff}} &= C \times V_{\text{total rainfall}} \\
                 &= C \times I \times A \times 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A smooth concrete roof yields $C \approx 0.8$, meaning 80% of rainfall becomes available runoff.

### Step 3 — Apply the System Efficiency Factor

Even the available runoff is reduced by losses in the conveyance system, filter clogging, first-flush diverter losses, and overflow.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{harvested}} &= \eta \times V_{\text{runoff}} \\
                     &= \eta \times C \times I \times A
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Final General Expression

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{harvested}} &= C \times I \times A \times \eta \quad \text{(Liters)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **core KTU formula** for assessing the quantitative importance of RWH. It is examinable in both Part A and Part B.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem (KTU Practice Question):**
A residential building in Kochi has a rooftop area of **150 m²**. The annual rainfall is **2900 mm**. The runoff coefficient of the concrete roof is **0.85**, and the system efficiency is **0.80**. Calculate the annual harvestable rainwater volume. If the local water tariff is **₹0.05 per liter**, what is the annual monetary saving?

### Step 1 — Identify Given Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 150 \,\text{m}^2 \\
I &= 2900 \,\text{mm} \\
C &= 0.85 \\
\eta &= 0.80 \\
\text{Tariff} &= 0.05 \,\text{Rupees/Liter}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{harvested}} &= C \times I \times A \times \eta \\
                     &= 0.85 \times 2900 \times 150 \times 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Product

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{harvested}} &= 0.85 \times 0.80 \times 2900 \times 150 \\
                     &= 0.68 \times 2900 \times 150 \\
                     &= 0.68 \times 435{,}000 \\
                     &= 295{,}800 \,\text{Liters}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Annual Monetary Saving

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Saving} &= Q_{\text{harvested}} \times \text{Tariff} \\
              &= 295{,}800 \times 0.05 \\
              &= \text{₹}14{,}790
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always carry **two decimal places** for $C$ and $\eta$. Mark-losing shortcut: writing the answer without units or skipping a step.

---

## 3.3 Cost-Benefit Derivation

### Step 1 — Define Net Present Value (NPV)

For a project lifetime of $N$ years, with a discount rate $r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -C_{\text{initial}} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{S_t}{(1+r)^t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $C_{\text{initial}}$ = Initial cost of RWH installation
- $S_t$ = Monetary savings in year $t$
- $N$ = Project life (typically 15–25 years)
- $r$ = Discount rate (typically 6–8%)

### Step 2 — Simplify with Uniform Savings

Assuming $S_t = S$ (constant annual savings):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -C_{\text{initial}} + S \times \frac{(1+r)^N - 1}{r(1+r)^N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Payback Period

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Payback Period} &= \frac{C_{\text{initial}}}{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** This shows when the initial investment is recovered. Typical RWH payback: 3–7 years for residential systems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix Linking Issues to Remedies

| Issue Category | Specific Issue | Probable Cause | Engineering / Social Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Technical | Mosquito breeding | Open tank | Mesh covers, larvicides, closed storage |
| Technical | First-flush contamination | Roof dust | First-flush diverter (diverts first 2–5 mm) |
| Technical | Filter clogging | Leaf & silt accumulation | Periodic cleaning; multi-stage filters |
| Economic | High initial cost | Lump-sum investment | Government subsidies, EMI schemes |
| Economic | Slow payback | Low tariff | Use for high-demand purposes (flush, garden) |
| Legal | Non-compliance | Lack of enforcement | Penalize new buildings; incentivize retrofits |
| Social | Apathy | Lack of awareness | Public campaigns, demo projects in schools |
| Social | Maintenance neglect | No ownership clarity | Annual contract with local plumber |
| Climatic | Variability | Monsoon dependency | Hybrid system (municipal + RWH backup) |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects students to **frame solutions in the same breath as the issues**. A 7-mark question on issues is incomplete without the corresponding remedial framework.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (Part A)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Q: List any four important reasons why rainwater harvesting is essential in urban India.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Depleting groundwater table:** Rapid urbanization with impervious surfaces prevents natural recharge. RWH artificially recharges the aquifer, stabilizing the water table.
2. **Reduction in urban flooding:** Captured runoff reduces the load on storm-water drains, especially in cities like Kochi, Mumbai, and Chennai that face annual flooding.
3. **Reduction in water bills:** RWH provides 30–50% of non-potable water (toilet flushing, gardening, washing), reducing dependence on municipal supply.
4. **Environmental sustainability:** It lowers the carbon footprint of water supply (reduced pumping and long-distance conveyance) and preserves the urban ecosystem.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often give vague answers like "saves water." This scores 1/3. To score 3/3, **always quantify** and **categorize** (social, economic, environmental, hydrological).

---

### **Question 2 (Part A)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Q: Enumerate any three major technical issues faced during the implementation of rooftop rainwater harvesting systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **First-flush contamination:** The initial 2–5 mm of rainfall carries dust, bird droppings, and pollutants from the rooftop. Without a first-flush diverter, this contaminates the stored water.
2. **Filter clogging:** Filters get choked with leaves, twigs, and silt, reducing the system's efficiency and requiring periodic maintenance.
3. **Mosquito breeding:** Open storage tanks become breeding grounds for *Aedes aegypti* mosquitoes, posing a public health risk. This can be mitigated by using closed tanks with mesh covers.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### **Part B — Question A (Internal Choice Option 1)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: "Rainwater Harvesting is not merely a water conservation technique but a socio-ecological necessity for India." Critically discuss the importance of RWH under the following heads:**
- **(a)** Hydrological and environmental importance (7 marks)
- **(b)** Socio-economic importance and a brief note on the major issues hindering widespread adoption (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution (a) — Hydrological and Environmental Importance (7 marks)**

**1. Augmentation of Water Supply (2 marks):**
RWH supplements the municipal water supply, especially in water-stressed regions. The formula
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q = C \times I \times A \times \eta
\end{aligned}
$$
demonstrates that even a 100 m² rooftop in Kerala (annual rainfall 2900 mm, $C = 0.8$, $\eta = 0.8$) can harvest approximately **1,85,600 liters** per year, equivalent to a family's six-month non-potable water requirement.

**2. Groundwater Recharge (2 marks):**
In cities with high impervious cover (70–90%), natural infiltration is severely reduced. RWH through recharge pits and shafts restores the natural hydrological cycle. This is critical for cities like Bengaluru and Chennai where the water table has dropped by 5–15 m over two decades.

**3. Reduction in Urban Flooding (1 mark):**
Captured runoff reduces peak discharge into storm drains, mitigating flash floods. This was demonstrated during the 2018 Kerala floods, where RWH-equipped localities had significantly lesser waterlogging.

**4. Environmental Benefits (2 marks):**
- Reduces soil erosion by slowing surface runoff.
- Lowers carbon footprint by reducing energy-intensive pumping.
- Preserves base flow in rivers and supports urban biodiversity.
- Prevents saltwater intrusion in coastal aquifers (e.g., Alappuzha, Kochi).

---

#### **Solution (b) — Socio-Economic Importance & Issues (7 marks)**

**Socio-Economic Importance (4 marks):**

1. **Economic Savings:** A typical urban household saves **₹10,000–₹25,000 per year** on water bills.
2. **Property Value Enhancement:** RWH-equipped buildings command 3–7% higher resale value.
3. **Rural Empowerment:** In drought-prone regions (e.g., Marathwada, Rayalaseema), community RWH provides safe drinking water at point of use, reducing the water-fetching burden on women.
4. **Livelihood Generation:** RWH industry supports plumbers, tank manufacturers, and maintenance workers.
5. **Compliance & Incentives:** Many states offer property tax rebates and building plan approvals contingent on RWH installation.

**Major Issues Hindering Adoption (3 marks):**

1. **High Initial Cost:** Quality systems cost **₹30,000–₹1,50,000** for residential buildings, deterring middle-income families.
2. **Low Public Awareness:** Many households remain unaware of subsidies, pay-back periods, and maintenance protocols.
3. **Legal Inconsistencies:** Bylaws vary across municipalities; enforcement is patchy, creating uncertainty for builders.
4. **Maintenance Apathy:** Systems are often installed and abandoned within 3 years due to lack of trained maintenance staff.

---

### **Part B — Question B (Internal Choice Option 2)**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Despite the numerous benefits, rainwater harvesting in India faces several practical challenges. Discuss:**
- **(a)** The major technical and economic issues in implementation (7 marks)
- **(b)** The legal, social, and climatic issues, along with an integrated framework to overcome them (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution (a) — Technical and Economic Issues (7 marks)**

**1. Technical Issues (4 marks):**

1. **Improper Sizing of Storage:** Undersized tanks overflow during heavy rain, wasting water; oversized tanks waste capital. Correct sizing requires analysis of local rainfall data, rooftop area, and demand.
2. **First-Flush Contamination:** The initial rainfall washes dust, leaves, bird droppings, and atmospheric pollutants from the roof. A first-flush diverter (manual or automatic) is essential to discard the first 2–5 mm.
3. **Filter Clogging:** Single-stage filters choke quickly, especially during monsoons. Multi-stage filtration (e.g., gutter screen + sand filter + mesh filter) and quarterly cleaning are necessary.
4. **Mosquito Breeding:** Open storage tanks are a public health hazard. Solutions include closed tanks, fine mesh covers, and biolarvicides (*Bacillus thuringiensis*).
5. **Material Degradation:** Cheap PVC tanks degrade under UV exposure; metal tanks corrode. BIS-certified materials should be mandated.
6. **Overflow Mismanagement:** Excess water re-floods foundations; a proper overflow pipe leading to a recharge pit or garden is essential.

**2. Economic Issues (3 marks):**

1. **High Upfront Cost:** A typical 5000 L residential system costs **₹50,000–₹80,000** including tank, filters, pipes, and recharge structure.
2. **Long Payback Period:** Despite monetary savings, the payback period of 5–8 years discourages investment.
3. **Hidden Maintenance Costs:** Annual cleaning, filter replacement, and pump servicing are often overlooked in cost estimates.
4. **Lack of Subsidies:** Many state governments have not extended subsidies for RWH retrofits (only for new constructions in some states).
5. **Inadequate Financing:** Banks rarely offer green loans for RWH installations at concessional rates.

---

#### **Solution (b) — Legal, Social, Climatic Issues & Integrated Framework (7 marks)**

**1. Legal and Policy Issues (2 marks):**
- Absence of a **uniform national RWH policy**; rules vary across states.
- **Weak enforcement** of mandatory bylaws (e.g., in Kerala, G.O.(P) No. 1/2018 mandates RWH for buildings above certain plot area, but compliance is patchy).
- **Cumbersome approval processes** for community-level RWH in apartment complexes.
- **Ownership ambiguity** of rooftop runoff.

**2. Social and Behavioral Issues (2 marks):**
- **Low public awareness** and **free-rider mentality** ("municipality will provide water anyway").
- **Apathy in maintenance:** Systems installed and forgotten.
- **Trust deficit** in shared community RWH infrastructure.

**3. Climatic Issues (1 mark):**
- **High spatial and temporal variability** of rainfall (Kerala: 3000 mm; Rajasthan: <300 mm).
- **Climate change** is altering rainfall patterns, increasing uncertainty.
- **Air pollution in metros** degrades roof catchment quality.

**4. Integrated Framework to Overcome (2 marks):**

| Pillar | Specific Action |
|---|---|
| **Policy** | Enact a uniform national RWH act; incentivize retrofits via tax rebates |
| **Technology** | Mandate BIS-certified components and first-flush diverters |
| **Awareness** | Integrate RWH into school curricula; mandatory display of RWH status in building plans |
| **Financing** | Green loans at 4–6% interest; community RWH trusts for apartments |
| **Monitoring** | Annual audit by ULBs; GIS-based tracking of installed systems |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Importance and Issues" (KTU Module 2)**

### ✅ Must-Remember Facts

- **Core formula:** $Q = C \times I \times A \times \eta$ (memorize the order of variables).
- **Runoff coefficients:** RCC roof ≈ 0.8, Tile ≈ 0.7, Thatched ≈ 0.4, Ground catchment ≈ 0.2–0.3.
- **Kerala receives ~3000 mm annual rainfall** — the highest among major Indian states.
- **System efficiency** is typically **0.75–0.85**.
- **First-flush diverter** discards the initial 2–5 mm of rainfall.
- **BIS Code IS 14961:2001** provides guidelines for RWH.

### ✅ Must-List Categories (For 7-Mark Questions)

- **Importance (6 dimensions):** Hydrological, Environmental, Economic, Social, Urban Planning, Industrial.
- **Issues (5 categories):** Technical, Economic/Financial, Legal/Policy, Social/Behavioral, Environmental/Climatic.

### ✅ Must-Use Keywords in Answers

- "Augmentation of water supply"
- "Artificial recharge of aquifer"
- "Decentralized water management"
- "Sustainable Urban Drainage System (SUDS)"
- "Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM)"
- "Issue–Cause–Remedy framework"

### ✅ Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Confusing storage tank issues with recharge structure issues** — they are distinct.
2. **Citing only "water scarcity" as importance** — diversify across all six dimensions.
3. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems — Liters, mm, m², Rupees.
4. **Mixing up runoff coefficient ($C$) and efficiency ($\eta$)** — $C$ relates to the catchment surface; $\eta$ relates to the system.

### ✅ Linking Topics for Cross-Module Answers

- Importance of RWH → **Module 1 (Hydrology basics)**
- Issues → **Module 3 (Design solutions)**
- Quantitative Importance → **Module 4 (Economic analysis & Payback)**
- Climatic Issues → **Module 5 (Climate change adaptation)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Pearl:** When asked "Why is RWH important?", never give a one-line answer. A **3-mark** answer requires **at least 3 well-explained dimensions**; a **7-mark** answer requires **at least 4–5 dimensions with quantification**; a **14-mark** answer requires **all six dimensions + numerical example + state-of-the-art case study (e.g., Sikkim, Kerala, or Maharashtra's Jal Yukt Shivar).**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Harvesting Techniques - Classification Based on Source
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Water Harvesting Techniques - Classification Based on Source

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Water Harvesting (WH)** is the deliberate collection, accumulation, treatment, storage, and subsequent utilization of water from any naturally occurring source (precipitation, surface runoff, or subsurface flow) for beneficial human use, groundwater recharge, or ecological sustenance. When the classification is anchored on the **SOURCE** of water, the techniques segregate into physically and hydrologically distinct paradigms, each governed by specific catchment-response mechanisms and conveyance-storage geometries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2 Anchor):**
> "Water harvesting techniques may be classified on the basis of the source of water being harvested — namely surface water, sub-surface/ground water, roof-top runoff, and storm water in urban catchments. The selection of the appropriate technique is a function of the hydrological source, catchment characteristics, and end-use demand."

### 1.2 The Four Master Categories (Based on Source)

| # | Source Category | Primary Physical Medium | Typical Yield Driver |
|---|----------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Surface Water Harvesting** | Streams, rivers, ponds, lakes, overland flow | Runoff concentration & storage |
| 2 | **Groundwater Harvesting** | Aquifers (confined/unconfined) | Recharge augmentation & extraction |
| 3 | **Roof-Top Rainwater Harvesting (RWH)** | Building catchment surfaces | Direct rooftop precipitation capture |
| 4 | **Storm Water Harvesting (Urban)** | Paved surfaces, roads, drains | Flash-runoff interception |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Family Bank Account":**
> Imagine a family that needs money. They have *four distinct income sources*:
> 1. **Salary (Surface Water):** A steady, predictable inflow from a regular job (river/stream).
> 2. **Fixed Deposit Withdrawal (Groundwater):** Money saved in a bank that earns interest (aquifer recharge).
> 3. **Daily Pocket Money (Roof-Top RWH):** Small but frequent amounts collected directly at home.
> 4. **Festival Bonus (Storm Water):** A sudden, large lump sum received occasionally during heavy rain.
> 
> Just as a prudent family diversifies its income sources, a hydrologically sound city or village diversifies its water harvesting portfolio. Each source has its own *collection mechanism*, *storage logic*, and *risk profile* (drought resilience).

### 1.4 Key Physical & Hydrological Constants

- **Rainfall Intensity (i):** typically **mm/hr** or **cm/hr**
- **Runoff Coefficient (C):** dimensionless, **0 ≤ C ≤ 1**
- **Catchment Area (A):** expressed in **m²** or **hectares (ha)**; **1 ha = 10,000 m²**
- **Storage Volume (V):** in **litres (L)** or **cubic metres (m³)**; **1 m³ = 1000 L**
- **Standard Design Storm Return Period:** **25 years** (CPHEEO Manual) for urban RWH
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Source-Wise Operational Logic

#### 2.1.1 Surface Water Harvesting
- **Source:** Rivers, streams, springs, ponds, lakes, and overland sheet flow.
- **Operational Principle:** Intercept moving water using weirs, check dams, barrages, or contour bunds. The water is either stored *in-situ* (e.g., farm pond) or diverted to an off-stream storage.
- **Why:** It exploits the *kinetic energy* of flowing water and the *gravitational potential* of elevated catchments.
- **How:** Construct storage structures *across* the natural drainage line so that streamflow is impounded during the wet season and released/utilized during dry months.
- **Common Structures:** Check dams, percolation tanks, farm ponds, nala bunds, anicuts, diversion weirs.

#### 2.1.2 Groundwater Harvesting
- **Source:** Soil moisture, vadose zone storage, and saturated aquifers.
- **Operational Principle:** *Two-fold process* — **(a) Artificial Recharge** to push water downward into the aquifer, and **(b) Extraction** via open wells, bore wells, or tube wells.
- **Why:** Aquifers act as natural slow-release reservoirs. They buffer seasonal variability and resist evaporation.
- **How:** Use recharge pits, recharge trenches, recharge wells, injection wells, and recharge shafts to direct surface water into the subsurface.
- **Common Structures:** Recharge pits, recharge trenches, recharge wells, abandoned-dug-well recharge, lateral shafts.

#### 2.1.3 Roof-Top Rainwater Harvesting
- **Source:** Precipitation falling directly on roof surfaces (sloped RCC, tiled, GI sheet, asbestos).
- **Operational Principle:** First-flush diversion → filtration → storage (or recharge).
- **Why:** It is the *purest* form of harvested water (minimal ground contamination) and the *most local* (zero conveyance loss).
- **How:** Gutters and down-take pipes convey water to either a storage tank (for direct use) or a recharge well/pit (for aquifer augmentation).
- **Common Structures:** Storage tanks (sump), recharge pits, recharge wells, first-flush diverters, leaf screens.

#### 2.1.4 Storm Water Harvesting (Urban Catchments)
- **Source:** Rainfall on paved roads, pavements, parking lots, airports, industrial roofs.
- **Operational Principle:** Intercept flash runoff in storm drains, detain it in retention/detention basins, and either recharge it or reuse it.
- **Why:** Reduces urban flooding, replenishes urban aquifers, and reduces the load on conventional drainage.
- **How:** Combine soak pits, recharge trenches, percolation wells, and detention ponds with the existing storm-water network.
- **Common Structures:** Soak pits, recharge trenches, detention basins, bioswales, infiltration galleries.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam critical**. Master the unit conversions.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Engineering Use | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Q = C \times I \times A$ | Q = runoff volume, C = runoff coefficient, I = rainfall depth, A = catchment area | Annual/seasonal water yield estimation | m³, m, m² |
| 2 | $V_{tank} = C \times I \times A \times \eta$ | η = system efficiency (≈ 0.80 for RWH) | Storage tank sizing | m³ |
| 3 | $V_{annual} = 0.6 \times P \times A \times C$ | 0.6 = monsoon rainfall fraction; P = annual rainfall | Annual harvestable water | litres |
| 4 | $t_{drawdown} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{Q_{demand}}$ | t = days of supply, V = tank volume, Q = daily demand | Reliability check | days |
| 5 | $H_{tank} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{A_{tank}}$ | H = required tank depth, A = tank base area | Tank geometry check | m |
| 6 | $Q_{peak} = C \times i \times A$ | i = rainfall intensity | Peak storm-runoff design | m³/s or L/s |
| 7 | $V_{recharge} = A \times P \times R_{f}$ | R_f = recharge factor (0.10 – 0.30) | Aquifer recharge potential | m³ |
| 8 | $T_{s} = \dfrac{V_{live}}{Q_{daily}}$ | T_s = storage time | Operational autonomy | days |

> [!NOTE]
> **Runoff Coefficient (C) — KTU Standard Values:**
> - Roof (RCC/slate): **0.80 – 0.90**
> - Roof (GI sheet): **0.70 – 0.80**
> - Paved road: **0.60 – 0.85**
> - Bare soil: **0.20 – 0.40**
> - Vegetated ground: **0.10 – 0.25**
> - Forest cover: **0.05 – 0.20**

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Sizing of RWH tanks, recharge structures, and storm-water detention basins.
- **Urban Planning:** Mandatory RWH in building bye-laws (e.g., Kerala Municipality Building Rules).
- **Agriculture:** Farm ponds and percolation tanks for dry-land farming.
- **Disaster Mitigation:** Reduces urban flooding, improves groundwater table, combats sea-water intrusion in coastal aquifers.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Reduces soil erosion, silting of reservoirs, and runoff pollution load.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annual Harvestable Runoff from a Catchment

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU examinations. The objective is to find the volume of water that can be harvested from a given catchment over a year.

**Step 1 — Start with the Rational Method Foundation**

The fundamental hydrological equation relating rainfall to runoff is the Rational Equation, originally used for peak flow:

$$
Q_{peak} = C \cdot i \cdot A
$$

For a *volume* of water, we replace the *intensity* (i) with a *depth* (P), which represents the cumulative rainfall over a given period (e.g., the monsoon season or an entire year).

$$
V_{runoff} = C \cdot P \cdot A
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Wet-Season Fraction**

Empirically, in the Indian subcontinent, roughly **60 %** of the annual rainfall arrives during the active monsoon (Kerala: June–September). Not all of it is "effectively harvestable" because some is lost to soil-moisture deficit and early-season abstractions. A wet-season fraction factor is introduced.

$$
V_{harvest} = 0.6 \cdot C \cdot P_{annual} \cdot A
$$

**Step 3 — Convert Hectares → Square Metres (Unit Safety Check)**

If $A$ is given in hectares and $P$ in millimetres, we must convert for unit consistency.

We know that:
$$
1 \text{ hectare} = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2
$$
$$
1 \text{ mm of rain over 1 m}^2 = 1 \text{ litre}
$$

Therefore, the conversion factor becomes:
$$
1 \text{ mm} \times 1 \text{ ha} = 10{,}000 \text{ L} = 10 \text{ m}^3
$$

The refined formula in *practical units* becomes:

$$
V_{harvest} \text{ [in litres]} = 6000 \times C \times P \text{ [mm]} \times A \text{ [ha]}
$$

**Step 4 — Include System Efficiency (η)**

No harvesting system is 100 % efficient — losses occur due to first-flush diversion, evaporation from storage, conveyance leakage, and filtration backwash. The CPHEEO Manual recommends a system efficiency of **η ≈ 0.80** for properly designed RWH systems.

$$
V_{usable} = 0.6 \cdot C \cdot P \cdot A \cdot \eta
$$

In practical (litres, mm, ha) units:

$$
\boxed{V_{usable} = 4800 \times C \times P \times A}
$$

This is the **final form** that the KTU board expects you to write directly on the answer sheet.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A residential building has a **rooftop area of 200 m²**. The average annual rainfall at the location is **3000 mm**. The runoff coefficient for the RCC roof is **0.85**. Compute:
1. The total volume of harvestable water (m³) per annum.
2. The required storage tank capacity if the family demand is **500 L/day** and the designer wishes to have a **30-day** reserve.

**Solution:**

**Part 1 — Annual Harvestable Volume**

Apply the working formula (with unit conversion $A = 200$ m² $= 0.02$ ha):

$$
V_{usable} = 4800 \times C \times P \times A
$$

$$
V_{usable} = 4800 \times 0.85 \times 3000 \times 0.02
$$

$$
V_{usable} = 4800 \times 0.85 \times 60
$$

$$
V_{usable} = 244{,}800 \text{ L}
$$

Convert to cubic metres:

$$
V_{usable} = 244.8 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Part 2 — Required Tank Capacity**

Daily demand:
$$
Q_{daily} = 500 \text{ L/day}
$$

Required reserve (30 days):
$$
V_{tank} = Q_{daily} \times T_s = 500 \times 30 = 15{,}000 \text{ L} = 15 \text{ m}^3
$$

Check adequacy — Annual harvest (244.8 m³) is far greater than 30-day demand (15 m³), so the system is **highly feasible** with surplus water available for groundwater recharge.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Source-Based Comparative Assessment

| Parameter | Surface Water | Groundwater | Roof-Top RWH | Storm Water (Urban) |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Source medium** | Stream/river/lake | Aquifer | Building roof | Paved surfaces |
| **Water quality** | Moderate (turbid) | High (filtered by soil) | Highest (least contaminated) | Lowest (oil, debris) |
| **Capital cost** | High | Moderate | Low–Moderate | High (urban infra) |
| **Evaporation loss** | High (open storage) | Negligible | Low (covered tank) | Moderate |
| **Drought resilience** | Low–Moderate | High (multi-season) | Low (single-season tank) | Low |
| **Conveyance loss** | High (long channels) | None | Negligible | Moderate |
| **Permeability requirement** | Impervious dam site | Permeable aquifer | Any roof | Recharge-permissive soil |
| **KTU weightage** | **High** | **High** | **Very High** | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Source → Storage → Use)

```mermaid

```

### 4.3 Schematic Text-Sketch — A Roof-Top RWH System

```
  +--------------------------------+
  |        ROOF CATCHMENT          |   <-- A (m²)
  +--------------------------------+
                |
                v
   +-------------------------+
   |   GUTTER (Mild Steel /   |
   |   PVC) along eave line  |
   +-------------------------+
                |
                v
   +-------------------------+
   |  FIRST-FLUSH DIVERTER   |   <-- Discards first ~2 mm
   +-------------------------+

                v
   +-------------------------+

   +-------------------------+

        |               |

  |  Use)     |   |  Augment.)  |

   [Household]      [Groundwater]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this schematic matters for KTU:** A question like *"Draw a neat sketch of a roof-top rainwater harvesting system and label its components"* is a **guaranteed 7-mark question**. Memorize this flow.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Classify the various water harvesting techniques based on the source of water with at least two examples for each category.*

**Model Answer:**
Water harvesting techniques based on source are classified into four major categories:

1. **Surface Water Harvesting:** Collection of water from rivers, streams, ponds, and lakes. *Examples:* Check dams, farm ponds.
2. **Groundwater Harvesting:** Artificial recharge of aquifers plus extraction. *Examples:* Recharge pits, recharge wells.
3. **Roof-Top Rainwater Harvesting:** Collection from building roofs. *Examples:* Storage tank RWH, recharge-pit RWH.
4. **Storm Water Harvesting (Urban):** Interception of runoff from paved urban surfaces. *Examples:* Soak pits, bioswales.

The classification is hydrologically significant because the *catchment response*, *water quality*, and *storage geometry* differ fundamentally across the four sources.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a "first-flush diverter"? Why is it mandatory in a roof-top RWH system?*

**Model Answer:**
A **first-flush diverter** is a small chamber installed at the top of the down-take pipe that automatically diverts the *first 2 mm of rainfall* away from the storage tank.

**Why mandatory:**
- The first 2 mm of rain washes off dust, bird droppings, leaves, and accumulated atmospheric pollutants from the roof.
- This initial runoff is the *most contaminated* fraction.
- Diverting it improves the bacteriological and chemical quality of stored water, reduces filter clogging, and extends tank-cleaning intervals.
- It is recommended by **CPHEEO** and **BIS 11700** for all roof-top RWH installations.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
*(a)* Classify water harvesting techniques based on the source of water. Explain in detail the concept of **Roof-Top Rainwater Harvesting** along with a neat sketch of a typical system. **(7 Marks)**
*(b)* An RCC-roofed building has a **roof area of 250 m²**. The annual rainfall is **2800 mm**. The runoff coefficient is **0.85** and the system efficiency is **80 %**. Compute the **annual harvestable volume** in m³ and design a **storage tank** to provide **45 days** of supply for a family requiring **600 L/day**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
- **Classification** (as in Section 1.2): 4 categories explained with one-line definitions (≈ 3 marks).
- **Roof-Top RWH Concept:** Define it, mention sources (precipitation on roofs), components (gutter, first-flush diverter, filter, storage/recharge), and water-quality advantage (≈ 2 marks).
- **Neat Sketch:** The schematic shown in Section 4.3 with labels (≈ 2 marks).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Convert area to hectares:
$$
A = 250 \text{ m}^2 = 0.025 \text{ ha}
$$

Apply the practical formula derived in Section 3.1:
$$
V_{usable} = 4800 \times C \times P \times A
$$

Substitute values:
$$
V_{usable} = 4800 \times 0.85 \times 2800 \times 0.025
$$

$$
V_{usable} = 4800 \times 0.85 \times 70
$$

$$
V_{usable} = 285{,}600 \text{ L} = 285.6 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Tank Sizing:**
$$
V_{tank} = Q_{daily} \times T_s = 600 \times 45 = 27{,}000 \text{ L} = 27 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Design Verification:**
$$
\text{Surplus} = 285.6 - 27 = 258.6 \text{ m}^3
$$
The system is *highly feasible*; surplus water can be routed to a recharge pit for groundwater augmentation.

**Tank geometry (optional, for full marks):**
If a cylindrical tank of diameter 3 m is chosen:
$$
H = \frac{V_{tank}}{\pi r^2} = \frac{27}{\pi \times 1.5^2} = 3.82 \text{ m}
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*(a)* Differentiate between **Surface Water Harvesting** and **Groundwater Harvesting** with respect to source, structural examples, water quality, and drought resilience. **(7 Marks)**
*(b)* A rural catchment of **5 hectares** receives an average annual rainfall of **2200 mm**. The combined runoff coefficient is **0.35**. Design a **farm pond** of circular shape, **2.5 m deep**, to store **30 %** of the annual runoff. Compute the **diameter** of the proposed pond. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks (Tabular form recommended in the answer sheet):**

| Aspect | Surface Water | Groundwater |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| Source | Streams, rivers, lakes | Aquifers |
| Examples | Check dam, farm pond | Recharge well, bore well |
| Water quality | Moderate, turbid | High, naturally filtered |
| Drought resilience | Low–Moderate | High |
| Evaporation | High | Negligible |
| Capital cost | High | Moderate |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Annual runoff volume:
$$
V_{runoff} = C \cdot P \cdot A
$$
$$
V_{runoff} = 0.35 \times 2200 \text{ mm} \times 5 \text{ ha}
$$

Convert to litres: 1 mm × 1 ha = 10,000 L, so:
$$
V_{runoff} = 0.35 \times 2200 \times 5 \times 10{,}000
$$
$$
V_{runoff} = 38{,}500{,}000 \text{ L} = 38{,}500 \text{ m}^3
$$

Volume to be stored (30 %):
$$
V_{pond} = 0.30 \times 38{,}500 = 11{,}550 \text{ m}^3
$$

Pond geometry (cylinder):
$$
V_{pond} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times H
$$

Solve for D:
$$
D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot V_{pond}}{\pi \cdot H}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 11{,}550}{\pi \times 2.5}}
$$

$$
D = \sqrt{\dfrac{46{,}200}{7.854}}
$$

$$
D = \sqrt{5882.6} \approx 76.7 \text{ m}
$$

**Answer:** The farm pond should have a diameter of approximately **77 m** at a depth of 2.5 m.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall & Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Module 2 Specific):**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 marks):** Mixing mm with m² without converting to mm × ha → litres. **Always** state the conversion factor 1 mm × 1 ha = 10,000 L explicitly.
> 2. **Wrong formula version (–2 marks):** Using $V = C \cdot I \cdot A$ (Rational for peak) instead of the volume form $V = C \cdot P \cdot A$. The board **deducts** if you do not distinguish between *intensity* and *depth*.
> 3. **Skipping the efficiency factor (η):** The CPHEEO manual mandates η = 0.80. Omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the wet-season fraction (0.6):** Some questions specify the *monsoon* rainfall — if not, students erroneously use full annual rainfall. Read the wording carefully.
> 5. **Sketch without labels:** A 7-mark question that asks for a sketch without labels is graded as **≤ 3 marks**. Always label **gutters, first-flush diverter, filter, storage tank, recharge pit, conveyance pipe**.
> 6. **Confusing C and η:** C is the *catchment runoff coefficient*; η is the *system efficiency*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — *Module 2 Hot List***
> 
> ✅ **Four sources of water harvesting:** Surface, Groundwater, Roof-Top, Storm Water (Urban).
> ✅ **The master formula:** $V_{usable} = 4800 \times C \times P \times A$ *(P in mm, A in ha, V in litres)*.
> ✅ **Conversion rule:** $1 \text{ mm} \times 1 \text{ ha} = 10{,}000 \text{ L} = 10 \text{ m}^3$.
> ✅ **System efficiency η = 0.80** (CPHEEO standard for RWH).
> ✅ **Roof-Top C = 0.80–0.90; Paved = 0.60–0.85; Vegetated = 0.10–0.25**.
> ✅ **First-flush diverter** removes the first 2 mm of rain — mandatory for quality control.
> ✅ **Design storm return period** for urban RWH = **25 years**.
> ✅ **Farm-pond geometry:** $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 H$ — derive D given V and H.
> ✅ **Surface vs Groundwater:** Surface = high turbidity, high evaporation; Ground = clean, drought-resilient.
> ✅ **Kerala-specific:** Mandatory RWH for buildings **> 100 m² plot area** (KMBR Amendment, 2018).
> ✅ **Always include** the schematic of a roof-top RWH system — it is the **single most expected drawing** in this module.
> ✅ **Percolation pond vs Farm pond:** Percolation pond = for *recharge* (no impermeable lining); Farm pond = for *storage* (lined, used for irrigation).
> ✅ **Recharge structures:** Pits (depth 2–3 m), Trenches (length 5–10 m), Wells (100–300 mm bore), Shafts (for hard rock terrain).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage and Use; Rainwater Harvesting Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Storage and Use; Rainwater Harvesting Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Storage and Use of Rainwater — Formal Definition

**Storage of Rainwater** refers to the practice of collecting, treating, and retaining runoff from rooftop and/or land surfaces in purpose-built storage structures (overhead tanks, underground sumps, surface reservoirs, or subsurface aquifers) for **beneficial end-uses** during dry periods when rainfall is absent or insufficient.

**Use of Rainwater** is the controlled deployment of this stored water for **non-potable domestic purposes** (toilet flushing, washing, gardening), **potable purposes** (drinking, cooking — only after appropriate treatment), **agricultural irrigation**, **industrial applications**, and **groundwater recharge augmentation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight (Module 2)**
> Rainwater harvesting is broadly classified into two end-uses:
> 1. **Storage & Use (Conservation mode)** — Water is *physically stored* in tanks/reservoirs and *consumed directly*.
> 2. **Recharge mode** — Water is *infiltrated* into subsurface aquifers to augment groundwater.

The first mode is the focus of Module 2 (Storage and Use); the recharge mode is covered in Module 3.

## 1.2 Rainwater Harvesting Methods — Overview

**Rainwater Harvesting (RWH) Methods** are the engineering and structural techniques by which precipitation falling on a catchment is intercepted, conveyed, filtered, and either stored or recharged. The choice of method depends on:
- **Catchment type** (rooftop, paved, unpaved, open land)
- **Geology & hydrogeology** of the site
- **End-use requirement** (storage vs recharge)
- **Rainfall intensity & pattern**
- **Available space and budget**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Piggy Bank"**
> Imagine rainfall as your monthly salary (income), and the storage tank as a piggy bank. During the rainy season, you "earn" a lot of water (high income), so you save the surplus in your piggy bank (storage tank). During the dry season (no income), you break open the piggy bank to meet your needs. A recharge pit is like *investing* your surplus in a fixed deposit — you don't use it today, but the underground aquifer matures in yield for the long term. Just as a good financial plan diversifies income sources, a good water plan **combines** storage, recharge, and demand management.

## 1.3 Broad Classification of RWH Methods

| Sl. No. | Category | Catchment | Storage/Recharge | Typical Use |
|---------|----------|-----------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Rooftop RWH with Storage | Building roof | Tank (sump/OH) | Domestic non-potable |
| 2 | Rooftop RWH with Recharge | Building roof | Recharge pit/shaft | Groundwater augmentation |
| 3 | Surface Runoff Harvesting | Paved/unpaved land | Pond/trench | Irrigation, recharge |
| 4 | Recharge Pit | Land/roof | Pit filled with media | Recharge shallow aquifer |
| 5 | Recharge Trench | Land/roof | Linear trench with media | Recharge phreatic zone |
| 6 | Recharge Well (Injection Well) | Land/roof | Borewell with filter | Recharge deep aquifer |
| 7 | Percolation Tank | Catchment drain | Earthen embankment | Recharge + storage |
| 8 | Check Dam / Stream Recharge | Stream/nala | Masonry barrier | Recharge baseflow |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Memory Hook:** For exams, remember the **"5-F" rule of RWH** — **C**atchment, **C**onveyance, **C**ollection, **C**onduction, **C**onservation. Every method must address at least three of these five "Cs" to qualify as a true RWH system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Storage-Type RWH System (Step-by-Step)

A complete rooftop rainwater harvesting system with storage consists of **six sequential functional components**:

1. **Catchment Surface (Roof):** The impermeable surface that intercepts rain. May be RCC, GI sheet, tile, asbestos, or slate. Roof slope should be **1:100 to 1:50** for self-cleaning drainage.

2. **Gutters & Downpipes (Conveyance):** Channels (GI, PVC, or RCC) fixed along roof edges to carry water vertically down. Cross-section is typically semi-circular or rectangular of size **100 mm – 150 mm** wide.

3. **First-Flush Diverter (Filter-1):** A simple mechanical device that **diverts the first 2–5 mm of rainfall** (which carries dust, bird droppings, leaves) to waste, preventing contamination of stored water.

4. **Filter Unit (Filter-2):** A sand-charcoal-gravel filter that removes suspended solids, organic matter, and odour. It can be a slow sand filter, a multi-layer drum filter, or a commercial cartridge filter.

5. **Storage Tank (Sump/Overhead/Underground):** The heart of the system. May be made of **RCC, Ferrocement, HDPE, PVC, GI, or brick masonry**. Sized based on catchment area, rainfall, demand, and dry-period length.

6. **Delivery System:** Pumps (if sump is below ground), pipes, taps, and overflow outlets connecting to the point of use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why First-Flush Diverter?**
> The initial rain washes out **deposited atmospheric dust, pollen, micro-organisms, bird excreta, and leaf debris** accumulated since the last rain. This first wash is the **most polluted** portion. Diverting it improves the quality of stored water by **up to 60–80%** in turbidity and microbial counts.

## 2.2 Sizing of Storage Tank — Theoretical Basis

The storage tank must be large enough to:
- Hold the **entire monsoon yield** (from a single design rainfall event), OR
- Buffer the **longest dry period** between two reliable rain events, OR
- Be sized on the basis of the **most economic balance** between cost and unmet demand.

### The Three Classical Sizing Approaches

**Approach A — Demand-Based Sizing:**
Tank volume is dictated by how much water the household/building needs during the dry season.

**Approach B — Supply-Based Sizing:**
Tank volume is dictated by how much rain can be harvested from the roof, limited by the largest single event the tank must contain.

**Approach C — Mass-Curve / Yield-After-Demand Sizing (Most Accurate):**
The tank is sized such that the running sum of (Supply − Demand) never falls below zero. This is solved using the **Sequential Peak Demand Method** or **Rippl's Mass Curve Method**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Symbols used by KTU 2024:**
> *A* = Catchment area (m²), *P* = Annual rainfall (mm), *η* = Runoff coefficient (dimensionless), *Q* = Harvest potential (L or m³), *N* = Number of occupants, *D* = Per capita demand (L/person/day), *d* = Dry days (days), *T* = Tank capacity (L or m³).

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Value |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | $Q = A \times P \times \eta$ | Annual rainwater harvest potential from a catchment | m³/year | η_roof ≈ 0.80–0.90 |
| 2 | $Q_{\text{daily}} = A \times P_d \times \eta$ | Daily harvest potential for a rain day of $P_d$ mm | L/day | Use daily rainfall data |
| 3 | $T_{\text{demand}} = N \times D \times d$ | Storage volume needed to meet demand for *d* dry days | Litres | N × D × d |
| 4 | $T_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{N \times D \times d}{1000}$ | Same as above expressed in m³ | m³ | For 4-member family: 4×135×60 = 32.4 m³ |
| 5 | $Q_{\text{peak}} = C \times i \times A$ | Rational formula for peak runoff (used in surface harvesting) | m³/s | C = 0.6–0.9 for paved |
| 6 | $V_{\text{recharge}} = A_c \times P \times \eta$ | Volume of water available for recharge per year | m³ | Filter area limits rate |
| 7 | $T_{\text{d}} = \dfrac{N \times D \times d}{1000} \times 1.20$ | Tank size with 20% safety margin for evaporation & siltation | m³ | Multiply by 1.20–1.30 |
| 8 | $R_o = \dfrac{P_{\text{eff}}}{P_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Runoff ratio / efficiency percentage | % | For RCC roof: 80–85% |
| 9 | $H_{\text{recharge}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{recharge}}}{A_{\text{spreading}} \times n}$ | Recharge head per unit area (for design of recharge pit depth) | m | Depends on $n$ (porosity) |
| 10 | $T_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times A_{\text{roof}} \times P \times \eta \times C_{\text{tank}}}{C_{\text{pump}}}}$ | Optimum economic tank size (cost-balance equation) | m³ | Simplified economic model |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Conversion Pitfall:**
> - If *A* is in **m²** and *P* is in **mm**, then *Q* comes out in **litres** when multiplied by 1 (since 1 m² × 1 mm = 1 litre).
> - If you want *Q* in **m³**, divide by 1000.
> - In KTU numericals, **always state the unit** and **always show the unit conversion** explicitly. This single habit fetches **2–3 grace marks** in valuation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Storage-type RWH systems are critical in:
- **Urban apartments** in water-stressed cities like Chennai, Bengaluru, and Hyderabad, where municipal supply is intermittent.
- **Coastal Kerala** households facing saline intrusion in wells — rooftop storage eliminates the need to pump deep saline water.
- **Schools and institutions** in tribal/rural hamlets of Wayanad and Attappadi, where it provides first-day-of-school water security.
- **Industrial complexes** for process water (cooling, gardening) to reduce freshwater draw.
- **Disaster resilience** — during floods, a well-designed RWH tank can also serve as an emergency reserve.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Mapping:** This topic directly maps to **CO2: "Design storage and recharge systems for harvested rainwater"** (Bloom Level: Apply/Analyse). Hence, expect **derivation problems on tank sizing** and **comparison-type 14-mark questions** in the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Annual Rainwater Harvest Potential (Q)

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the annual volume of rainwater that can be harvested from a given rooftop catchment.

**Assumptions:**
- Catchment area = *A* (m²), horizontal projection.
- Annual rainfall depth = *P* (mm).
- Runoff coefficient of the roof = *η* (dimensionless, 0 ≤ η ≤ 1).

**Step 1 — Rainfall Volume Falling on Roof:**
Rainfall depth *P* (in mm) means that for every 1 m² of horizontal area, *P* mm of water height accumulates. By unit conversion (1 mm depth over 1 m² = 1 litre of water).

$$
V_{\text{rain}} = A \times P \quad [\text{in litres}]
$$

**Step 2 — Account for Losses (Evaporation, Wetting, Spillage):**
Not all the rain landing on the roof reaches the storage. Losses occur due to:
- Wetting of roof surface
- Evaporation from hot roof
- Spillage from gutters
- Wind drift losses

These are collectively captured by the runoff coefficient *η*.

$$
V_{\text{harvested}} = \eta \times V_{\text{rain}}
$$

**Step 3 — Final Expression:**

$$
Q = A \times P \times \eta \quad [\text{litres/year}]
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to m³ (optional):**

$$
Q = \dfrac{A \times P \times \eta}{1000} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{year}]
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A residential building has a rooftop area of **120 m²**. The annual rainfall at the location is **2400 mm**. The runoff coefficient for the RCC roof is **0.85**. Find the annual harvest potential.

- **Given:** A = 120 m², P = 2400 mm, η = 0.85
- **Formula:** $Q = A \times P \times \eta$
- **Step 1:** $Q = 120 \times 2400 \times 0.85$
- **Step 2:** $Q = 120 \times 2040$
- **Step 3:** $Q = 244{,}800$ **litres/year**
- **Step 4 (m³):** $Q = 244.8$ **m³/year**

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Shortcut:** If the question asks "how many litres per day on average", divide the annual harvest by 365. Here: 244,800 ÷ 365 ≈ **670 L/day** — enough to supply a 5-member family for basic non-potable needs.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Demand-Based Tank Sizing

**Statement:** Derive the storage tank volume required to meet a household's non-potable demand during the dry season.

**Assumptions:**
- Number of family members = *N*
- Per capita non-potable demand = *D* L/person/day
- Length of dry period (no rain) = *d* days

**Step 1 — Total Daily Demand:**

$$
\text{Daily demand} = N \times D \quad [\text{L/day}]
$$

**Step 2 — Total Demand over Dry Period:**

$$
T_{\text{required}} = N \times D \times d \quad [\text{Litres}]
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to m³ and Add Safety Factor (20%):**

$$
T_{\text{design}} = \dfrac{N \times D \times d \times 1.20}{1000} \quad [\text{m}^3]
$$

**Numerical Example:**
Design a sump tank for a 5-member family in Kochi, given:
- Per capita demand *D* = **135 L/day** (combined potable + non-potable)
- Dry period *d* = **60 days** (typical Dec–Feb in Kerala)
- Factor of safety = **1.20**

- **Step 1:** $N \times D = 5 \times 135 = 675$ L/day
- **Step 2:** $675 \times 60 = 40{,}500$ L
- **Step 3:** $40{,}500 \times 1.20 = 48{,}600$ L
- **Step 4:** $48{,}600 \div 1000 = 48.6$ **m³**

**Recommended sump dimensions (e.g., 4 m × 4 m × 3 m = 48 m³ — close to design value).**

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Verification of "Supply vs Demand"

A **correctly designed RWH system** must satisfy:

$$
Q_{\text{supply}} \geq Q_{\text{demand}} \times \text{safety factor}
$$

If supply < demand, either:
- Increase catchment (extend roof, add paved catchment), or
- Reduce demand (use efficient fixtures, recycle greywater), or
- Increase storage (buffer for next rain), or
- Augment with groundwater recharge.

**Numerical Example (Supply–Demand Balance):**
A 100 m² roof, P = 2000 mm, η = 0.80, with a 4-member family having 100 L/day demand, over 90 dry days.

- **Annual supply:** $Q = 100 \times 2000 \times 0.80 = 160{,}000$ L = 160 m³
- **Annual demand:** $4 \times 100 \times 365 = 146{,}000$ L = 146 m³
- **Surplus:** 160 − 146 = 14 m³/year ✅
- **Storage required for 90 dry days:** $4 \times 100 \times 90 = 36{,}000$ L = 36 m³

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Supply barely exceeds demand; the storage tank (36 m³) is the bottleneck. A larger tank (say 45 m³) would allow carryover from one monsoon to the next.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Storage Methods

| Sl. No. | Storage Structure | Material | Capacity Range | Cost (₹/L) | Lifespan | Best Suited For |
|---------|-------------------|----------|----------------|------------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | Underground RCC Sump | RCC M25 | 5,000 L – 5,00,000 L | 1.5 – 2.5 | 30+ yr | Large residential, institutional |
| 2 | Overhead Tank (Sintex) | HDPE | 500 L – 25,000 L | 1.0 – 1.8 | 15 yr | Individual houses |
| 3 | Ferrocement Tank | Ferrocement | 1,000 L – 50,000 L | 0.8 – 1.2 | 25 yr | Rural, low-cost housing |
| 4 | PVC Tank (Loft type) | PVC | 100 L – 5,000 L | 2.0 – 3.0 | 10 yr | Rooftop loft, small flats |
| 5 | Galvanised Iron (GI) Tank | GI | 1,000 L – 10,000 L | 1.5 – 2.0 | 20 yr | Industrial, coastal |
| 6 | Surface Pond (Lined) | LDPE/HDPE + earth | 50 m³ – 10⁶ m³ | 0.2 – 0.5 | 15 yr | Community, agricultural |
| 7 | Underground Aquifer | Natural | Unlimited | 0.1 – 0.3 (well-based) | ∞ | Recharge mode |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Hook:** A frequent 14-mark question is *"Compare and contrast any four storage methods"* or *"Justify the choice of storage structure for a coastal Kerala village"*. The above matrix is your go-to reference.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a RWH System with Storage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rainfall Event] --> B[Roof Catchment Surface]
    B --> C[Gutter and Downpipe Network]
    C --> D[First Flush Diverter]
    D --> E[Sand and Charcoal Filter Unit]
    E --> F[Storage Tank Sump or Overhead]
    F --> G{End Use Decision}
    G -->|Non potable| H1[Toilet Flushing]
    G -->|Non potable| H2[Gardening and Washing]
    G -->|Potable| H3[Disinfection then Drinking]
    G -->|Surplus| I[Overflow to Recharge Pit]
    I --> J[Recharge Shaft to Aquifer]
    F --> K[Pump Set if Sump Underground]
    K --> L[Overhead Tank for Distribution]
    L --> M[Tap and Point of Use]
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Rainwater Harvesting Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RWH[Rainwater Harvesting Methods]
    RWH --> S1[Storage and Use Mode]
    RWH --> S2[Recharge Mode]
    S1 --> T1[Rooftop with Tank]
    S1 --> T2[Surface Runoff to Pond]
    S1 --> T3[Combined Storage and Recharge]
    S2 --> U1[Recharge Pit]
    S2 --> U2[Recharge Trench]
    S2 --> U3[Recharge Well Injection]
    S2 --> U4[Percolation Tank]
    S2 --> U5[Check Dam Stream Recharge]
    T1 --> T1a[Overhead Tank]
    T1 --> T1b[Underground Sump]
    T1 --> T1c[Surface Reservoir]
    U1 --> U1a[With Gravel Filter Media]
    U1 --> U1b[With Brickbats and Sand]
    U3 --> U3a[Open Well Recharge]
    U3 --> U3b[Bore Well Recharge]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow of a Storage-Type Rooftop RWH System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Cloud and Rain] --> A2[Roof Slab]
    A2 --> A3[Gutter Channel]
    A3 --> A4[Downpipe Vertical]
    A4 --> A5[First Flush Chamber]
    A5 --> A6[Filter Chamber Sand Charcoal Gravel]
    A6 --> A7[Sump Tank Below Ground]
    A7 --> A8[Pump 1 HP]
    A8 --> A9[Overhead Loft Tank]
    A9 --> A10[Tap and Faucet Points]
    A7 -->|Overflow| A11[Recharge Pit with Boulders]
```

## 4.4 Design-Component Linking Matrix (Text Schematic)

| Stage | Component | Function | Design Parameter | Typical KTU Value |
|-------|-----------|----------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Catchment | Intercept rain | Roof slope | 1 in 50 to 1 in 100 |
| 2 | Gutter | Collect runoff | Cross-section | 100 mm × 150 mm GI |
| 3 | Downpipe | Convey vertically | Diameter | 75 mm to 100 mm PVC |
| 4 | First-flush | Divert first wash | Volume | 2 L per m² of roof |
| 5 | Filter | Remove TSS, organic | Layer thickness | 200 mm sand + 100 mm gravel |
| 6 | Storage | Hold harvested water | Capacity | Demand × dry days × 1.2 |
| 7 | Pump | Lift water | Power | 0.5 to 1.0 HP |
| 8 | Overflow | Dispose excess | Diameter | Same as inlet pipe |

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualisation Note:** Since physical cross-sectional drawings (e.g., of a recharge pit showing boulders, sand, gravel layers) are difficult to render in Mermaid, the above **table-based schematic** serves as a *functional blueprint*. KTU 2024 examiners accept equivalent table-based schematics when the question is of *descriptive/comparison* type.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (Conceptual)**
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
*List the major components of a rooftop rainwater harvesting system with storage. Mention the function of each component.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — strict KTU keyword-driven marking):**
1. **Catchment (Roof)** — Intercepts and directs rainfall to gutters. *(*½ mark*)* 
2. **Gutters and Downpipes** — Collect and convey runoff to the filter. *(*½ mark*)* 
3. **First-flush Diverter** — Diverts the first dirty wash of rain to waste, improving water quality. *(*1 mark*)* 
4. **Filter Unit** — Sand-charcoal-gravel filter that removes turbidity, suspended solids, and odour. *(*½ mark*)* 
5. **Storage Tank** — Underground sump or overhead tank that stores the filtered water until use. *(*½ mark*)* 

### **Question A2 (Numerical)**
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
*A rooftop of area 150 m² receives an annual rainfall of 2200 mm. If the runoff coefficient is 0.85, calculate the annual rainwater harvest potential in m³.*

**Model Solution (3 marks):**
- **Step 1 (1 mark):** State formula: $Q = A \times P \times \eta$
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute: $Q = 150 \times 2200 \times 0.85 = 280{,}500$ **L**
- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Convert to m³: $Q = 280{,}500 \div 1000 = 280.5$ **m³**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget to convert mm to L (using 1 m² × 1 mm = 1 L) and write $Q$ in "m³" directly — losing the unit mark. **Always show the unit conversion explicitly.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
*(a)* Describe in detail the **various methods of rainwater harvesting** with neat sketches (block diagrams). Classify them into storage and recharge types. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* Design a **storage tank** for a 5-member family in Kerala, given the per capita demand of 135 L/day and the longest dry period of 75 days. Assume a 20% safety margin. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**
The student should classify RWH methods into two broad groups:
1. **Storage-Type Methods (3 marks):**
   - Rooftop RWH with overhead tank / underground sump
   - Surface runoff harvesting using ponds
   - Combined storage-recharge systems
2. **Recharge-Type Methods (3 marks):**
   - Recharge pit
   - Recharge trench
   - Recharge well (open / bore)
   - Percolation tank
   - Check dam
3. **Comparison Table (1 mark):**
   Differentiating on basis of catchment, storage/recharge, suitability.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

- **Given:** N = 5, D = 135 L/person/day, d = 75 days, FOS = 1.20

$$
T_{\text{design}} = \dfrac{N \times D \times d \times \text{FOS}}{1000}
$$

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Daily demand = $5 \times 135 = 675$ L/day
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Total demand = $675 \times 75 = 50{,}625$ L
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply FOS = $50{,}625 \times 1.20 = 60{,}750$ L
- **Step 4 (1 mark):** Convert to m³ = $60.75$ m³
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Suggest dimensions — e.g., rectangular sump 4 m × 4 m × 3.8 m (≈ 60.8 m³), or cylindrical tank of diameter 4.5 m and height 4 m.

### **Question B1 — Option B (Internal Choice — 14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
*(a)* Explain the **step-by-step procedure for designing a rooftop RWH system** for a residential building, including selection of catchment, conveyance, first-flush diverter, filter, and storage tank. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* An industrial shed has a roof area of 500 m² in a region receiving 1800 mm annual rainfall. The runoff coefficient is 0.80. If the storage tank is to be designed for 30 days of water supply for 50 workers at 25 L/day (non-potable only), check whether the **harvest potential is adequate** to meet the demand. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**
1. **Catchment selection (1 mark):** Choose RCC roof with slope 1:50 to 1:100. Clean and slope toward gutter.
2. **Conveyance design (1.5 marks):** Gutter size from Manning's equation or rule-of-thumb (100 mm × 150 mm GI for ≤ 100 m² roof).
3. **First-flush diverter (1.5 marks):** Volume = 2 L per m² of roof, diverted manually or via automatic ball-valve.
4. **Filter unit (1.5 marks):** Three-layer filter — coarse gravel (50 mm), fine sand (200 mm), charcoal (50 mm); flow rate 0.1–0.3 m³/h.
5. **Storage tank sizing (1.5 marks):** $T = N \times D \times d \times 1.20 / 1000$; round up to nearest standard tank size; ensure overflow pipe of equal diameter to inlet.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**
- **Annual harvest potential (2 marks):**
  $Q = 500 \times 1800 \times 0.80 = 7{,}20{,}000$ L/year = 720 m³/year
- **Average daily supply (1 mark):** $720 \div 365 = 1.97$ m³/day = 1972 L/day
- **Annual demand (2 marks):** $50 \times 25 \times 365 = 4{,}56{,}250$ L/year = 456.25 m³/year
- **Comparison (1 mark):** Supply 720 m³ > Demand 456.25 m³ → **Supply is adequate by 263.75 m³/year** ✅
- **Comment (1 mark):** The surplus can be used for groundwater recharge via a recharge pit, completing the conservation cycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout for 14-mark questions:**
> - **Never** present a single-line numerical without showing substitutions.
> - **Always** draw at least one block diagram (even if rough) — it fetches **2–3 easy marks** in the "neat sketch" component.
> - **Cite relevant IS codes** where possible: IS 15736:2007 (RWH — plumbing), IS 8419 (Percolation tanks).
> - In **comparison-type questions**, do NOT write paragraphs — examiners scan for a **table with at least 4–5 parameters** as the answer format. A table is worth 3–4 marks by itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Storage and Use; RWH Methods)**

- [x] **RWH = 2 modes:** Storage & Use OR Recharge — never confuse the design intent.
- [x] **Core formula:** $Q = A \times P \times \eta$ (units: L/year when A in m², P in mm).
- [x] **Tank sizing formula:** $T = N \times D \times d \times 1.20 / 1000$ in m³.
- [x] **Typical η values:** RCC roof 0.80–0.90, GI sheet 0.70–0.80, tile roof 0.75–0.85, paved 0.60–0.70, unpaved 0.20–0.40.
- [x] **First-flush volume:** ~2 L/m² of roof; diverts the most polluted portion.
- [x] **Filter layers (top → bottom):** Fine sand → coarse sand → charcoal → fine gravel → coarse gravel.
- [x] **6 system components:** Catchment, Gutter, Downpipe, First-flush, Filter, Storage, Overflow.
- [x] **3 Sizing Approaches:** Demand-based, Supply-based, Mass-curve (Rippl's).
- [x] **Kerala Context:** Saline intrusion in coastal districts → RWH with storage avoids pumping saline deep water.
- [x] **RWH methods list (memorise):** Storage tank (sump/OH), Recharge pit, Recharge trench, Recharge well, Percolation tank, Check dam, Surface pond.
- [x] **IS Codes to remember:** IS 15736:2007, IS 8419, IS 14961, CPHEEO Manual (Chapter on RWH).
- [x] **Cost rank (low → high):** Recharge pit < Surface pond < Ferrocement tank < RCC sump < Overhead GI tank.
- [x] **Always show units, always show unit conversion, always draw a block diagram.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line takeaway for KTU ESE 2024:**
> *"Storage-type RWH converts a seasonal water surplus into a year-round, demand-matched resource; the design is complete only when tank capacity = (number of users × per capita demand × longest dry period × 1.2)."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storing Rainwater for Direct Use
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Storing Rainwater for Direct Use

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Storage of rainwater for direct use** refers to the collection, retention, and preservation of rooftop runoff in purpose-built containers or reservoirs for subsequent non-potable or potable consumption without intervening groundwater recharge. The system intercepts precipitation at the catchment surface, conveys it through a defined plumbing network, and stores it in a sealed containment structure until drawn upon by the user.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is the "Direct Use Subsystem" of a Rainwater Harvesting (RWH) system, comprising:
- **Catchment surface** (typically the building roof)
- **Gutters and downpipes** (conveyance network)
- **First-flush diverters** (quality control)
- **Storage tank/reservoir** (retention volume)
- **Draw-off and distribution system** (delivery to point-of-use)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The storage subsystem is the *heart* of a direct-use RWH system. Unlike recharge structures that return water to the aquifer, storage tanks hold water *above ground or in elevated/underground enclosures* for immediate, point-of-need consumption.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a direct-use rainwater storage system as **a giant savings account for water**. The roof acts as the *collector* (your salary), the gutters and downpipes are the *direct deposit* channels (your bank transfers), the first-flush diverter is the *fee/withholding* that filters out bad deposits (sediment, bird dropouts), and the storage tank is the *vault* that holds the funds until you need to spend them. Just as a savings account must be sized to your typical expenses, the storage tank must be sized to the *drought period* between rain events — the longer you need to ride out dry spells, the bigger your "vault" must be.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard metrics are universally adopted in KTU-aligned design problems:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Density of water (ρ) | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| Gravitational acceleration (g) | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Runoff coefficient (C) for tiled/metal roof | **0.80 – 0.90** | dimensionless |
| Runoff coefficient (C) for concrete roof | **0.70 – 0.80** | dimensionless |
| Annual rainfall – Kerala average | **~3000** | mm |
| Standard storage sizing window | **30 – 90** | days of demand |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Runoff Coefficient (C)** is the single most quoted dimensionless parameter in any KTU storage problem. It represents the fraction of incident rainfall that becomes harvestable runoff. The remainder is lost to evaporation, splashing, surface wetting, and absorption.

## 1.4 Scope Within the Module

Within Module 2 ("Storing Rainwater for Direct Use"), the topic spans:
1. **Types of storage structures** (overhead, underground, surface)
2. **Materials of construction** (RCC, ferrocement, HDPE, PVC, GI, masonry)
3. **Sizing calculations** (demand-based and supply-based)
4. **Hydraulic design** of conveyance
5. **Water quality preservation** in storage
6. **Maintenance protocols**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture — Operational Logic

A direct-use storage system operates in five sequential stages. Each stage must be designed to handle the **peak instantaneous flow** without overflow, yet pass the **average daily yield** without stagnation.

### Stage 1: Interception at Catchment
- Rain falls on the catchment (roof).
- A portion *C* of total depth becomes runoff; the rest is lost.
- Runoff depth: $R_e = C \times P$, where $P$ is the rainfall depth over the design storm.

### Stage 2: Conveyance via Gutters
- Gutters are sized using the **Rational Method** for peak flow:
$$Q_{peak} = C \cdot i \cdot A$$
where $i$ is the rainfall intensity (mm/hr) for the chosen return period and time of concentration, and $A$ is the catchment area.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why gutters matter in KTU problems:** The hydraulic grade line of the gutter must be checked. If the gutter is undersized, *overflow occurs before water reaches the tank* — a common board-exam pitfall where students correctly size the tank but ignore the gutter.

### Stage 3: First-Flash Diversion
- The first **2 mm – 5 mm** of rainfall per event is diverted to waste.
- This flush carries dust, leaves, bird droppings, and chemical residues from the roof.

### Stage 4: Storage (the focus of this topic)
- The tank accepts the filtered, first-flushed runoff.
- Volume must be sized to bridge the **longest dry period** expected.

### Stage 5: Draw-off & Distribution
- Water is drawn via taps, pumps, or gravity (if tank is elevated).
- An overflow pipe directs excess water safely away from the foundation.

## 2.2 Types of Storage Structures

### 2.2.1 Classification by Position
- **Overhead/Lofted tanks:** Mounted on staging or on the roof. Provide gravity pressure. Limited volume (typically 500 L – 5000 L for residential).
- **Surface/Ground-level tanks:** Sit on a plinth beside the building. Larger volumes (5000 L – 50,000 L). Require a pump for pressure.
- **Underground/Sump tanks:** Buried. Preserve aesthetics, keep water cool, large volumes (10,000 L – 200,000 L). Higher construction cost.

### 2.2.2 Classification by Material
| Material | Typical Capacity | Lifespan | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC) | 5,000 L – 200,000 L | 40–60 yrs | Most common, durable |
| Ferrocement | 1,000 L – 20,000 L | 25–30 yrs | Thin-walled, economical |
| HDPE / Polyethylene (plastic) | 500 L – 10,000 L | 15–25 yrs | Lightweight, UV-sensitive |
| PVC / FRP modular | 1,000 L – 50,000 L | 20–30 yrs | Quick installation |
| Galvanized Iron (GI) / MS | 500 L – 5,000 L | 10–20 yrs | Subject to corrosion |
| Masonry (brick) | 5,000 L – 100,000 L | 30–50 yrs | Needs waterproof plaster |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are the **high-yield core** for any KTU Module 2 examination question on storage.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q = C \times P \times A$ | Annual/seasonal harvestable water volume | Litres (with $P$ in mm, $A$ in m²) |
| 2 | $V_{tank} = D \times n$ | Demand-based tank size ($D$=daily demand, $n$=days of storage) | Litres |
| 3 | $V_{tank} = Q \times t_{drought}$ | Supply-based tank size ($t_{drought}$ = critical dry period) | Litres |
| 4 | $V_{tank} = \min(V_{demand}, V_{supply})$ | Governing design tank size | Litres |
| 5 | $A_{roof} = \dfrac{V}{C \times P}$ | Roof area required for target volume | m² |
| 6 | $Q_{peak} = 0.00278 \cdot C \cdot i \cdot A$ | Rational method (metric form) | L/s |
| 7 | $D_{gutter} = \sqrt[8/3]{\dfrac{Q \cdot n}{S^{1/2}}}$ | Manning's gutter width (approx. Manning) | m |
| 8 | $V_{first-flush} = 2 \times A_{roof}$ | First-flush volume (litres, $A$ in m²) | Litres |
| 9 | $\eta_{system} = \dfrac{V_{used}}{Q_{harvested}}$ | System efficiency (0.7–0.9 typical) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $V_{actual} = V_{gross} \times (1 - \phi)$ | Net volume after sediment/freeboard allowance | Litres |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion note for KTU problems:** The classical RWH formula is
> $$V \; (\text{in Litres}) = C \times P \; (\text{mm}) \times A \; (\text{m}^2) \times 1000$$
> Students frequently forget the **×1000** factor when going from cubic metres to litres. This is the most common single error in KTU answer scripts.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Direct-use storage is the most widely deployed RWH technology in **tropical high-rainfall regions** like Kerala because:
- It provides **immediate, tangible water savings** to the household.
- It **decouples the user from municipal supply** for non-potable uses (toilet flushing, gardening, washing, mopping).
- It serves as an **emergency reserve** during pipe-borne water supply failures, which are common in Kerala's summer months.
- It is **cost-effective** with payback periods of 3–7 years for typical residential installations.

In urban Kerala, the *Kerala Municipal Building Rules (KMBR)* and the *Suchitwa Mission guidelines* mandate rooftop RWH with storage for all new buildings above 100 m², making this topic directly relevant to professional engineering practice in the state.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Runoff Volume Equation

The fundamental equation for harvestable water volume is derived as follows. Let us begin with the basic conservation statement:

**Statement:** The volume of water available for storage equals the runoff depth multiplied by the catchment area.

**Step 1 — Express volume per unit area:**
For a rainfall of depth $P$ (in mm) on a horizontal catchment of area $A$ (in m²), the *gross* volume of incident water is:

$$V_{gross} = P \times A$$

Here, $P$ is in mm = $10^{-3}$ m. Therefore, multiplying by $A$ (m²) gives volume in **m³**.

**Step 2 — Apply the runoff coefficient:**
Only a fraction $C$ of this becomes runoff. The remainder is lost to interception, evaporation, and absorption.

$$V_{runoff} = C \times V_{gross} = C \times P \times A$$

**Step 3 — Convert to litres:**
Since $1 \; \text{m}^3 = 1000 \; \text{L}$, we multiply by 1000:

$$V_{runoff} \; (\text{L}) = C \times P \; (\text{mm}) \times A \; (\text{m}^2) \times 1000$$

This is the working form used in **every** KTU numerical.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Storage Tank Sizing Logic

The governing sizing equation balances **two competing constraints**: supply and demand.

### Constraint 1 — Demand Side
The user requires a fixed amount of water per day. If the demand is $D$ (in L/day) and the design must sustain the user for $n$ consecutive days without rain, then:

$$V_{demand} = D \times n$$

### Constraint 2 — Supply Side
Between two successive rain events of depth $P$ (effective), the catchment can refill a volume $V_{supply}$. The gap between events is the **inter-event dry period** $t_d$ (in days):

$$V_{supply} = C \times P \times A \times 1000$$

The storage tank must hold **at least** enough water to cover $t_d$ days of demand, but **not more** than what the catchment can refill in a single event.

### Governing Equation
The design tank volume is therefore the **minimum of the two constraints** — but, in practice, it is also the larger of (a) the demand-side volume and (b) a minimum practical volume (e.g., to prevent stagnation). The dual bound is written:

$$V_{tank} = \max\!\left[\min(V_{demand}, V_{supply}), \; V_{min}\right]$$

For typical KTU design problems, the **demand-side** expression dominates because rainfall in Kerala is generally plentiful.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **[Sample Problem — 14-Mark Style]**
> A residential building in Thrissur has a tiled roof of plan area **150 m²**. The average annual rainfall is **2800 mm**. The runoff coefficient is **0.85**. The household demand for non-potable use is **200 L/day**. The longest expected dry period is **45 days**. Design the storage tank volume.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the harvestable volume per rain event (annual average, treated as a single season for first-cut sizing):**

$$V_{runoff} = C \times P \times A \times 1000$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{runoff} &= 0.85 \times 2800 \; \text{mm} \times 150 \; \text{m}^2 \times 1000 \\
&= 0.85 \times 2.8 \; \text{m} \times 150 \; \text{m}^2 \times 1000 \\
&= 0.85 \times 420 \; \text{m}^3 \times 1000 \\
&= 357,000 \; \text{L/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute demand-side volume for the critical dry period:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{demand} &= D \times n \\
&= 200 \; \text{L/day} \times 45 \; \text{days} \\
&= 9,000 \; \text{L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute supply-side volume per event.**
Assume one "design event" delivers an effective rainfall of **30 mm** (a typical small storm in Kerala). Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{supply} &= 0.85 \times 30 \; \text{mm} \times 150 \; \text{m}^2 \times 1000 \\
&= 0.85 \times 0.030 \; \text{m} \times 150 \; \text{m}^2 \times 1000 \\
&= 0.85 \times 4.5 \; \text{m}^3 \times 1000 \\
&= 3,825 \; \text{L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the governing equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{tank} &= \max\!\left[\min(9{,}000,\; 3{,}825),\; V_{min}\right] \\
&= \max\!\left[3{,}825,\; V_{min}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

But $3{,}825$ L is **less than** the demand of $9{,}000$ L! This means **the catchment cannot refill the demand in a single 30 mm event**. The designer must either:
- (i) increase catchment area, or
- (ii) accept that the tank is refilled **multiple times** during the wet season and the storage only needs to bridge the **longest dry gap between two consecutive storms** (e.g., 7–10 days for Kerala), or
- (iii) reduce the demand.

**Step 5 — Recompute for realistic 10-day inter-event dry period:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{demand,\;10d} &= 200 \times 10 = 2{,}000 \; \text{L}
\end{aligned}$$

Now $V_{supply} = 3{,}825$ L $\geq V_{demand,\;10d} = 2{,}000$ L.

$$V_{tank} = 2{,}000 \; \text{L} \;\; (\text{adopt a standard 2000 L tank})$$

**Step 6 — Check with sediment/freeboard allowance (φ ≈ 0.10):**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{gross} &= \frac{V_{actual}}{1 - \phi} = \frac{2{,}000}{0.9} = 2{,}222 \; \text{L}
\end{aligned}$$

Adopt a **standard 2500 L** plastic or ferrocement tank.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Storage Type Selection

The following matrix helps select the right storage type for a given site condition (often asked in KTU Part A):

| Site Condition | Best Storage Type | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Slender urban plot, no garden | Overhead tank | Space-saving, gravity-fed |
| Spacious suburban plot | Underground sump | Cool water, large volume, hidden |
| Hilly terrain with high water table | Surface tank on plinth | Avoid buoyancy/uplift issues |
| Coastal saline belt | RCC or HDPE (sealed) | Prevent saline ingress |
| Slum / low-income housing | Ferrocement jar | Low cost, locally constructible |
| High-rise apartment | Modular GRP on terrace | Staged, retrofit-friendly |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Direct-Use Storage System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a complete direct-use rainwater storage system, from interception at the roof to the point-of-use tap. It is rendered as a flow block diagram (rather than a physical drawing) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Rainfall Event] --> A2[Roof Catchment]
    A2 --> A3[Gutter Network]
    A3 --> A4[First Flush Diverter]
    A4 --> A5[Filter Unit Mesh and Sand]
    A5 --> B1[Storage Tank Sump or Overhead]
```
