```mermaid
    B1 --> B2[Overflow Pipe to Recharge Pit]
    B1 --> B3[Sediment Drain at Base]
    B1 --> C1[Water Drawing Tap Non Potable]
    C1 --> C2[Toilet Flush]
    C1 --> C3[Garden Tap]
    C1 --> C4[Wash and Mop]
    B1 --> D1[Optional Disinfection Module]
    D1 --> E1[Potable Use Point]
    B1 --> F1[Level Indicator]
    F1 --> G1[User Monitoring]
```

## 4.2 Component-Level Storage Sub-System Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram zooms into the **storage tank** itself, showing all internal and external fittings that examiners expect students to label.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Inlet Pipe from Filter] --> S2[Tank Inlet Elbow]
    S2 --> S3[Inlet Calming Pipe Vertical Drop]
    S3 --> S4[Main Storage Volume]
    S4 --> S5[Sediment Zone Bottom 100 mm]
    S5 --> S6[Drain Valve with Plug]
    S4 --> S7[Overflow Weir at Top]
    S7 --> S8[Overflow Pipe to Recharge]
    S4 --> S9[Draw Off Pipe with Strainer]
    S9 --> S10[Delivery to Tap]
    S4 --> S11[Inspection Hatch with Lid]
    S4 --> S12[Vent Screen SS Mesh]
    S4 --> S13[Water Level Gauge]
```

## 4.3 Sizing Logic Flow Chart (for Board-Exam Schematic Questions)

The following Mermaid flow chart represents the **decision logic** for sizing a storage tank. Examiners often award marks for a correct logic block.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Define Demand D and Dry Period n] --> P2[Compute V demand = D times n]
    P1 --> P3[Compute Effective Rainfall P for Design Event]
    P3 --> P4[Compute V supply = C times P times A times 1000]
    P2 --> P5{V demand greater than V supply}
    P4 --> P5
    P5 -->|Yes| P6[Tank is supply limited, use V supply as min]
    P5 -->|No| P7[Tank is demand limited, use V demand]
    P6 --> P8[Apply Freeboard Factor phi]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Adopt next standard size up]
    P9 --> P10[End: Specify Type Material and Position]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters in KTU valuation:** Examiners frequently award 2–3 marks for a *correctly labelled schematic*. The above flow chart, when redrawn by hand in the answer script, fetches full marks provided the arrows and decision diamonds are unambiguous.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
**Differentiate between underground and overhead storage tanks for rooftop rainwater harvesting, citing two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Underground Tank | Overhead Tank |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Position | Below ground level | Above ground / on roof staging |
| Pressure at tap | Requires pump | Gravity-fed |
| Temperature | Cool (insulated by earth) | Warmer (exposed to sun) |
| Aesthetics | Hidden | Visible (may clutter façade) |

*Two advantages of underground tank:* (i) large storage volume is feasible in a small footprint, (ii) water stays cool, reducing bacterial growth.

*Two advantages of overhead tank:* (i) gravity-fed distribution eliminates pumping cost, (ii) easier inspection and maintenance.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]**
**State the role of a first-flush diverter in a direct-use rainwater storage system. Quantify the typical volume diverted per square metre of roof area.**

**Model Answer:**

A first-flush diverter routes the *initial, most contaminated* portion of each rainfall event to waste, preventing dust, bird droppings, leaf litter, and chemical residues from entering the storage tank. This significantly improves the potability and aesthetic quality of stored water.

**Typical diverted volume:**
$$V_{ff} = 2 \; \text{L/m}^2 \;\; (\text{common rule of thumb; range } 2 \text{ – } 5 \; \text{L/m}^2)$$

For a 100 m² roof, the diverted volume per event is approximately **200 litres** (using 2 L/m²).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Design-Oriented**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** A residential building in Kottayam has a sloped tiled roof with a horizontal projected plan area of **120 m²**. The mean annual rainfall is **3050 mm**. Assuming a runoff coefficient of **0.85** and accounting for a first-flush loss of **2 L/m²** per event occurring **100 times per year**, compute the *net annual harvestable volume* in litres. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the household demand is **150 L/day** for non-potable uses (toilet, garden, washing) and the longest expected continuous dry period is **30 days**, design the storage tank capacity. Comment on the suitability of an underground RCC tank versus an overhead HDPE tank for this site. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Gross runoff volume (annual):**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{gross} &= C \times P \times A \times 1000 \\
&= 0.85 \times 3050 \; \text{mm} \times 120 \; \text{m}^2 \times 1000 \\
&= 0.85 \times 3.050 \; \text{m} \times 120 \; \text{m}^2 \times 1000 \\
&= 0.85 \times 366 \; \text{m}^3 \times 1000 \\
&= 311{,}100 \; \text{L/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Annual first-flush loss:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{ff} &= 2 \; \text{L/m}^2 \times 120 \; \text{m}^2 \times 100 \; \text{events} \\
&= 24{,}000 \; \text{L/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Net harvestable volume:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{net} &= V_{gross} - V_{ff} \\
&= 311{,}100 - 24{,}000 \\
&= 287{,}100 \; \text{L/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** **Net annual harvestable volume ≈ 2,87,100 L/yr (≈ 287.1 m³/yr).**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Demand-side storage volume:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{demand} &= D \times n = 150 \; \text{L/day} \times 30 \; \text{days} = 4{,}500 \; \text{L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Check supply-side volume per average event.**
Treat 3050 mm/year over 100 events ≈ 30.5 mm/event. Effective per-event volume:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{supply} &= 0.85 \times 30.5 \; \text{mm} \times 120 \; \text{m}^2 \times 1000 \\
&\approx 3{,}111 \; \text{L/event}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply governing equation:**
Since $V_{demand} (4{,}500) > V_{supply} (3{,}111)$, the tank is **demand-limited**; adopt the larger value, but also consider the inter-event dry period more realistically. For a Kerala climate, the realistic inter-event dry period is 7–10 days:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{tank} &= 150 \times 10 = 1{,}500 \; \text{L} \;\; \text{(base demand)} \\
V_{gross} &= \frac{1{,}500}{1 - 0.10} = 1{,}667 \; \text{L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Adopt a standard 2000 L tank.**

**Step 4 — Comparative comment:**

| Criterion | Underground RCC | Overhead HDPE |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Capacity flexibility | Up to 50,000 L | Max ~5,000 L (structural limit) |
| Cost for 2000 L | ₹25,000 – 35,000 | ₹8,000 – 12,000 |
| Water temperature | Cool (~25°C) | Warm (35–40°C in summer) |
| Lifespan | 40+ years | 15–20 years |
| Construction effort | High (excavation, waterproofing) | Low (placement on staging) |

*Conclusion:* For a **2,000 L** requirement, an **overhead HDPE tank** is more economical and easier to retrofit. For larger demands (>5,000 L), the underground RCC option is preferable.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Conceptual/Analytical Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **components of a rooftop rainwater storage system** for direct use in a typical Kerala household. Label each component clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **four key design considerations** for selecting the material of a rainwater storage tank. A homeowner in Palakkad has a budget constraint of ₹20,000 for a 5,000 L tank. Recommend the most suitable material with justification. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

A neat sketch should show the following components (in vertical sequence from top to bottom):
1. **Roof catchment** (sloped roof with tiles or metal sheets)
2. **Gutter** (fixed at eaves, slight slope 1:100)
3. **Downpipe** (vertical conveyance, ≥75 mm dia for residential)
4. **First-flush diverter** (chamber with ball-valve or tipping-bucket)
5. **Filter unit** (sand-gravel-charcoal or mesh filter)
6. **Storage tank** (overhead or underground)
7. **Overflow pipe** (with mosquito-proof mesh screen)
8. **Draw-off tap** (with strainer)
9. **Vent** (insect-proof mesh)
10. **Inspection hatch / manhole**

(Marks awarded for: correct sequence, labels, arrows, indication of flow direction.)

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Four key design considerations:**

1. **Water-tightness / non-reactivity:** Tank must not leach chemicals or rust into stored water. RCC, HDPE, ferrocement, and FRP are non-toxic; GI/MS may corrode if not lined.

2. **Structural strength & durability:** Tank must withstand hydrostatic pressure, soil loads (for underground), and seismic forces. RCC excels; HDPE is good for above-ground but can bulge in extreme heat.

3. **Cost & constructability:** Initial cost vs. lifespan. Ferrocement and RCC are economical for large volumes. HDPE has low cost but limited lifespan.

4. **Maintenance & cleaning access:** Must have a manhole/inspection hatch, drain plug, and ventilation.

**Recommendation for Palakkad (5,000 L, ₹20,000 budget):**

| Material | Cost for 5,000 L | Verdict |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| RCC (underground) | ₹40,000 – 60,000 | Exceeds budget |
| HDPE (above-ground) | ₹12,000 – 18,000 | Within budget |
| Ferrocement (above-ground) | ₹15,000 – 22,000 | Within budget |
| GI / MS | ₹18,000 – 25,000 | Marginal |
| FRP | ₹25,000 – 35,000 | Exceeds budget |

**Best choice: Ferrocement tank.** Justification: low cost (~₹18,000 for 5,000 L), 25–30 year life, locally constructable (employment to local mason), good thermal insulation, and within the ₹20,000 budget. HDPE is a close second but has a shorter lifespan (15–20 yr) and is UV-sensitive.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Numerical errors:** Forgetting the **×1000** factor when converting m³ → L is the #1 mistake. Always state units explicitly.
> - **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m for rainfall depth. Standardise before substitution.
> - **Ignoring first-flush loss:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if the first-flush diversion is not accounted for in the net harvestable volume.
> - **Sketch omissions:** A "neat sketch" in Q.B(a) without *labelled arrows* and *flow direction* loses 2 marks. Always show flow arrows.
> - **Material selection:** Don't recommend RCC for a small budget or HDPE for a 50,000 L demand. Match the recommendation to the *constraint*.
> - **Demand-based vs. supply-based confusion:** Clearly state *which constraint governs* before writing the final tank size.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Master equation (must memorise):** $V = C \times P \times A \times 1000$ — this single line is worth up to 5 marks in any KTU numerical.
- ✅ **Runoff coefficient (C):** 0.80–0.90 for tiled/metal roofs; 0.70–0.80 for concrete; 0.50–0.60 for unpaved surfaces.
- ✅ **First-flush rule:** 2 L/m² of roof is the most commonly quoted value in KTU answers.
- ✅ **Storage sizing logic:** Tank = max(demand, supply) with freeboard factor (1 − φ); typical φ = 0.10.
- ✅ **Material ranking by lifespan:** RCC > Masonry > Ferrocement > FRP > HDPE > GI/MS.
- ✅ **Positioning:** Overhead = gravity-fed, small volume; Underground = large volume, pump-fed.
- ✅ **Kerala-specific:** Average annual rainfall ~3000 mm; longest dry period in practice is 7–15 days, not 30+ days. Adjust the design dry period to realistic local data.
- ✅ **Conversion factor:** 1 m³ = 1000 L (this trips up half the cohort).
- ✅ **Components to label:** Catchment → Gutter → First-flush → Filter → Tank → Overflow → Tap → Vent → Inspection hatch.
- ✅ **Quality tip:** Always state *assumptions* in the solution (C value, freeboard %, design event depth) — examiners award method marks for clearly stated assumptions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board-Exam Strategy:** For a 14-mark design question, allocate your time as — *Schematic (3 min, 2 marks) → Assumptions (1 min, 1 mark) → Numerical (8 min, 8 marks) → Discussion/Commentary (3 min, 3 marks)*. This pacing is the difference between a 10/14 and a 14/14 answer script.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recharging Groundwater Aquifers from Roof Top Runoff
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recharging Groundwater Aquifers from Roof Top Runoff

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rooftop Rainwater Harvesting (RTRWH) for Groundwater Recharge** is a hydrological engineering technique wherein the precipitation falling on the catchment surface of a building's roof is collected, conveyed through gutters and downpipes, subjected to preliminary filtration, and subsequently diverted into the subsurface geological formations (aquifers) through specially engineered recharge structures such as recharge pits, recharge trenches, recharge shafts, or abandoned borewells.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> "Rooftop rainwater harvesting is the technique of collecting rainwater from roof catchments, storing it in surface storage tanks, or recharging it into the groundwater aquifer by constructing appropriate recharge structures."

In the context of this KTU module, the emphasis is on **artificial recharge of aquifers** rather than surface storage. The harvested runoff is not held in a tank for direct use; instead, it is transferred to the sub-soil zone to replenish the natural groundwater reservoir.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your rooftop as a giant **funnel** and the aquifer as a **sponge** buried deep in the ground. When it rains, the water slides off the roof (like a slanted tray). Normally, this water rushes to the drains, mixes with sewage, and is lost. RTRWH inserts a "controlled spout" at the edge of this funnel — the water is filtered (cleaned of dust, leaves, and debris) and then poured directly onto the sponge. Over time, the sponge soaks up the water and the groundwater table rises, just like a well that never runs dry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy (KTU Board Examiner Perspective):**
> Think of the **aquifer as a bank account** and rainfall as your **salary credit**. If you keep withdrawing (over-extraction through borewells) without depositing (recharge), the account goes into overdraft. RTRWH acts as a **direct deposit** mechanism into this groundwater bank.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Runoff Coefficient (C) for RCC roofs:** **0.80 to 0.90**
- **Runoff Coefficient (C) for GI/Asbestos roofs:** **0.70 to 0.85**
- **Runoff Coefficient (C) for Thatched roofs:** **0.50 to 0.60**
- **Standard annual rainfall of Kerala:** **≈ 3000 mm** (varies from 2000 mm in the south to 3600 mm in the north)
- **Porosity of loose sand strata:** **0.30 to 0.40**
- **Hydraulic conductivity of coarse sand:** **10⁻³ to 10⁻² m/s**
- **Specific yield of unconfined aquifers:** **0.10 to 0.30**

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always state the runoff coefficient range when solving KTU numericals. Using a single value like 0.85 without justification attracts a half-mark deduction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The rooftop rainwater harvesting system for groundwater recharge operates through a **sequential cascade** of six engineering stages:

1. **Catchment Surface (Roof)**
   - The sloped roof acts as the primary collection plane.
   - Material selection is critical: RCC (Reinforced Cement Concrete) is preferred due to smooth surface texture, low microbial contamination, and minimal leaching.
   - **Why?** A rough surface traps silt and reduces the runoff coefficient.

2. **Gutter System (Conveyance at Eaves)**
   - Semi-circular or rectangular channels fixed at the roof edge.
   - Sized using the Rational Formula: $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ (where $I$ is rainfall intensity).
   - **How?** Graded at a minimum slope of **1 in 120** toward the downpipe.

3. **Downpipe / Vertical Conduit**
   - Carries water from gutter to ground level.
   - Standard diameters: **75 mm to 100 mm** for residential, **150 mm** for institutional buildings.
   - **How?** Vertical alignment with mild offsets at junctions.

4. **First-Flush Diverter**
   - Discharges the initial dirty runoff (first 1–2 mm of rainfall) which contains dust, bird droppings, and atmospheric pollutants.
   - **Why?** The roof accumulates contaminants during dry periods; the first wash carries the maximum pollutant load.

5. **Filtration Chamber**
   - Sand-gravel-charcoal media filter (typically layered: coarse gravel at bottom, fine sand at top, with an intermediate charcoal layer).
   - Removes suspended solids, organic matter, and improves aesthetic clarity (though not for potable use).
   - **How?** Water percolates downward through stratified media.

6. **Recharge Structure (Final Discharge)**
   - The cleaned water is delivered to the subsurface aquifer.
   - Common structures: Recharge Pit, Recharge Trench, Recharge Well, Abandoned Borewell, or Soak Pit.
   - **Why?** Direct percolation recharges the water table and prevents surface stagnation.

### 2.2 Types of Recharge Structures (Classification Matrix)

| Structure | Best Suited For | Typical Dimensions | Cost Index |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Recharge Pit** | Small residential plots, sandy-loam soils | 1.5 m deep × 1 m wide | Low |
| **Recharge Trench** | Large open spaces, apartment complexes | 0.5–1 m wide × 1–1.5 m deep × variable length | Medium |
| **Recharge Shaft / Dry Well** | Areas with impervious clay top layer | 5–10 m deep × 1.5–3 m wide | Medium-High |
| **Abandoned Borewell** | Restoring defunct deep borewells | Matches borewell diameter | Low (Retrofit) |
| **Percolation Tank** | Large community catchments | 1000–5000 m³ storage | High |
| **Recharge Well with Injection Pipe** | Confined aquifers below clay layer | 50–100 m deep | High |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Standard Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C \times I \times A$ | Q = Peak runoff rate, C = Runoff coefficient, I = Rainfall intensity, A = Catchment area | m³/s (SI) | Sizing of gutters and drains |
| 2 | $V = P \times A \times C$ | V = Volume of harvestable water, P = Precipitation depth, A = Roof area, C = Runoff coefficient | m³ or litres | Annual water yield estimation |
| 3 | $V_{litres} = P_{mm} \times A_{m^2} \times C \times 1000$ | Conversion of mm·m² to litres | litres | Direct numerical problems |
| 4 | $V_{pit} = L \times B \times D$ | L = Length, B = Breadth, D = Depth of recharge pit | m³ | Pit dimensioning |
| 5 | $Q_{recharge} = K \times A_{cross} \times i$ | Darcy's Law — K = Hydraulic conductivity, $A_{cross}$ = Cross-sectional area, i = Hydraulic gradient | m³/s | Aquifer recharge rate |
| 6 | $t_{drain} = \frac{V_{pit}}{Q_{recharge}}$ | Drain time of pit after rain event | seconds/hours | Filter design check |
| 7 | $S = \frac{\pi}{4} \times d^2 \times H \times n$ | S = Storage in shaft, d = Diameter, H = Height, n = Porosity of fill | m³ | Shaft design |
| 8 | $N = \frac{P \times A \times C}{V_{single\ pit}}$ | Number of recharge pits required | dimensionless | Multi-pit design |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** A typical 14-mark problem asks you to compute the volume of harvestable rooftop water given rainfall (mm), roof area (m²), and runoff coefficient, followed by designing a recharge pit of specified dimensions.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Urban Water Security:** In cities like Kochi and Thiruvananthapuram, where over-extraction has lowered water tables by 5–10 m, RTRWH offers decentralized recharge.
- **Flood Mitigation:** Reduces peak stormwater runoff by 30–50% in urban catchments, easing load on municipal drains.
- **Climate Resilience:** Buffers against monsoon variability by sustaining baseflow in dry seasons.
- **Coastal Saline Intrusion Barrier:** Maintains freshwater head in coastal aquifers of Kerala, preventing saltwater ingress.
- **Compliance:** Mandatory under the Kerala Building Rules and the Central Ground Water Authority (CGWA) notification for buildings above 300 m² plot area.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Annual Harvestable Rooftop Runoff Volume

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the total volume of rainwater that can be harvested annually from a rooftop catchment of area $A$, subjected to annual rainfall $P$, with a runoff coefficient $C$.

**Step 1: Define the rainfall depth in consistent units**

Let the annual precipitation depth be $P$ measured in millimetres (mm). Converting to metres:

$$P_{m} = \frac{P_{mm}}{1000}$$

**Step 2: Compute the volume of water striking the roof**

The roof is a horizontal projection of area $A$ (in m²). The volume of rain that physically lands on this area in one year is:

$$V_{incident} = A \times P_{m} = A \times \frac{P}{1000}$$

This gives $V_{incident}$ in cubic metres (m³).

**Step 3: Apply the runoff coefficient**

Not all incident water becomes runoff. Evaporation, wetting of the roof surface, and absorption losses are accounted for by the dimensionless runoff coefficient $C$:

$$V_{runoff} = C \times V_{incident}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the expression for $V_{incident}$**

$$V_{runoff} = C \times A \times \frac{P}{1000}$$

**Step 5: Final compact form**

$$\boxed{V_{runoff} = \frac{C \cdot A \cdot P}{1000} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{)}}$$

**Conversion to litres:** Since 1 m³ = 1000 litres, multiply by 1000:

$$V_{litres} = C \times A \times P \quad \text{(litres)}$$

This is the most frequently used form in KTU numerical problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Sizing of a Cuboidal Recharge Pit

**Statement:** Derive the dimensions of a recharge pit to be constructed in sandy-loam soil with permeability $k$ such that the pit drains completely within 24 hours after a single monsoon pulse of volume $V_{roof}$.

**Step 1: Define the pit volume**

For a rectangular pit of length $L$, breadth $B$, and depth $D$:

$$V_{pit} = L \times B \times D$$

**Step 2: Apply Darcy's Law for infiltration through the pit bottom and sides**

The pit loses water through three surfaces: the **bottom** and the **four vertical side walls**. Assuming the water level inside the pit is at the ground surface (worst case):

$$Q_{infiltration} = k \times i \times A_{wetted}$$

For vertical percolation, the hydraulic gradient $i \approx 1$ (gravity-driven flow). The wetted area is the bottom + side walls:

$$A_{wetted} = (L \times B) + 2(L \times D) + 2(B \times D)$$

**Step 3: Express the discharge**

$$Q_{infiltration} = k \times \left[ (L \cdot B) + 2D(L + B) \right]$$

Units: m³/s if $k$ is in m/s and dimensions in metres.

**Step 4: Set up the drain time equation**

The time $t$ for the pit to drain from full to empty is:

$$t = \frac{V_{pit}}{Q_{infiltration}}$$

**Step 5: Apply the design constraint $t \leq 24$ hours**

Convert 24 hours to seconds: $t = 24 \times 3600 = 86400$ s.

Setting $t = 86400$ s:

$$L \cdot B \cdot D = 86400 \times k \times \left[ (L \cdot B) + 2D(L + B) \right]$$

**Step 6: Simplification for square pit ($L = B = a$)**

Let $L = B = a$. Then:

$$a^2 \cdot D = 86400 \cdot k \cdot \left[ a^2 + 4aD \right]$$

Dividing both sides by $a$:

$$a \cdot D = 86400 \cdot k \cdot (a + 4D)$$

Solving for $a$ in terms of $D$ and $k$:

$$a \cdot D - 345600 \cdot k \cdot D = 86400 \cdot k \cdot a$$

$$D(a - 345600 k) = 86400 \cdot k \cdot a$$

$$\boxed{a = \frac{345600 \cdot k \cdot D}{D - 86400 \cdot k}}$$

This closed-form solution enables the designer to pick a feasible depth $D$ and back-calculate the side dimension $a$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style)

**Problem:** A residential building in Thrissur has a rooftop area of **150 m²**. The annual rainfall is **2800 mm**. The runoff coefficient for the RCC roof is **0.85**. Design a **single square recharge pit** of depth **1.5 m** in sandy soil with permeability $k = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. Check whether the pit drains within 24 hours.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Annual harvestable volume**

$$V_{litres} = 0.85 \times 150 \times 2800$$

$$V_{litres} = 357{,}000 \text{ litres} = 357 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Single-event design volume (assume 50 mm pulse)**

For a single storm event contributing 50 mm of effective rain:

$$V_{event} = 0.85 \times 150 \times 0.050 = 6.375 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Square pit side dimension**

Using the derived formula with $D = 1.5$ m, $k = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s:

$$a = \frac{345600 \times (5 \times 10^{-5}) \times 1.5}{1.5 - 86400 \times (5 \times 10^{-5})}$$

Compute numerator: $345600 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.5 = 345600 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-5} = 25.92$

Compute denominator: $1.5 - 86400 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} = 1.5 - 4.32 = -2.82$

The negative denominator indicates that with $k = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s, the soil is too impermeable relative to the chosen depth. The pit cannot drain in 24 hours.

**Step 4: Recalculate minimum permeability needed**

Setting denominator positive: $D > 86400 \cdot k$, hence:

$$k < \frac{D}{86400} = \frac{1.5}{86400} = 1.74 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}$$

This means the soil must be **coarser** than fine sand. A **gravel-pack** is needed, or the pit depth should be reduced.

**Step 5: Revised design with $D = 1.0$ m**

$$k_{req} = \frac{1.0}{86400} = 1.16 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}$$

With a gravel pack providing effective $k = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s, and depth $D = 1.0$ m:

$$a = \frac{345600 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.0}{1.0 - 86400 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{34.56}{1.0 - 8.64} = \frac{34.56}{-7.64}$$

This is still infeasible. Conclusion: **A single pit of these dimensions is inadequate**; multiple pits or a **recharge trench** is required.

**Step 6: Revised design using 4 pits**

Each pit receives $V = 6.375 / 4 = 1.594$ m³. For a pit of $1 \text{ m} \times 1 \text{ m} \times 1.5 \text{ m}$:

$$V_{pit} = 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.5 = 1.5 \text{ m}^3$$

This is close to the design load. Verified as feasible.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Error:** Students often forget to convert 24 hours into seconds (86400 s) when applying Darcy's Law. This single omission causes a 4–6 mark deduction.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Recharge Structure Selection

| Parameter | Recharge Pit | Recharge Trench | Recharge Shaft | Borewell Recharge |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capital Cost | Low | Medium | High | Lowest (uses existing) |
| Land Footprint | Small | Linear, can be long | Small point | Negligible |
| Construction Depth | 1.5–3 m | 1–1.5 m | 5–15 m | 50–200 m |
| Soil Suitability | Permeable topsoil | Permeable + confined recharge | Reaches deep permeable strata | Deep aquifers only |
| Maintenance Frequency | Annual cleaning | Annual desilting | 2–5 yearly | Annual flushing |
| Recharge Efficiency | 60–75% | 70–85% | 80–90% | 85–95% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Rooftop RWH Recharge System

The diagram below maps the sequential interaction of components from roof to aquifer, suitable for both conceptual understanding and KTU diagram-based questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RAINFALL EVENT] --> B[ROOFTOP CATCHMENT SURFACE]
    B --> C[GUTTER COLLECTION NETWORK]
    C --> D[FIRST FLUSH DIVERTER]
    D --> E[FILTER CHAMBER SAND GRAVEL CHARCOAL]
    E --> F[RECHARGE STRUCTURE SELECTION NODE]
    F --> G[OPTION A RECHARGE PIT]
    F --> H[OPTION B RECHARGE TRENCH]
    F --> I[OPTION C RECHARGE SHAFT]
    F --> J[OPTION D ABANDONED BOREWELL]
    G --> K[UNCONFINED AQUIFER ZONE]
    H --> K
    I --> L[DEEP PERMEABLE STRATUM]
    J --> L
    K --> M[RISING WATER TABLE]
    L --> M
    M --> N[RECOVERY VIA EXTRACTION WELL]
    N --> O[DOMESTIC AND IRRIGATION USE]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Sectional View of a Recharge Pit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[TOP SURFACE GRASS OR CONCRETE COVER] --> P2[COARSE GRAVEL LAYER 50 MM]
    P2 --> P3[MEDIUM SAND LAYER 300 MM]
    P3 --> P4[FINE SAND LAYER 200 MM]
    P4 --> P5[PEBBLE LAYER 50 MM]
    P5 --> P6[PERFORATED CONCRETE RING BOTTOM]
    P6 --> P7[NATIVE SOIL PERMEABLE STRATUM]
    P7 --> P8[GROUNDWATER AQUIFER BELOW]
    P9[INLET PIPE FROM FILTER] --> P2
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic Diagram for Choosing a Recharge Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[START: SOIL TYPE AT SITE] --> Q2{PERMEABLE TOP LAYER EXISTS}
    Q2 -->|YES ABOVE 2 M| Q3[USE RECHARGE PIT OR TRENCH]
    Q2 -->|NO CLAYEY TOP| Q4[USE RECHARGE SHAFT]
    Q3 --> Q5{AVAILABLE LAND AREA}
    Q5 -->|LESS THAN 5 M SQUARE| Q6[CHOOSE PIT]
    Q5 -->|MORE THAN 5 M SQUARE| Q7[CHOOSE TRENCH]
    Q4 --> Q8{EXISTING BOREWELL AVAILABLE}
    Q8 -->|YES| Q9[RETROFIT ABANDONED BOREWELL]
    Q8 -->|NO| Q10[DRILL NEW RECHARGE BOREWELL]
```

### 4.4 Subsurface Hydrological Process (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[RAINFALL INPUT] --> R2[INFILTRATION ZONE]
    R2 --> R3[UNSATURATED VADOSE ZONE]
    R3 --> R4[CAPILLAR FRINGE]
    R4 --> R5[PHREATIC SURFACE WATER TABLE]
    R5 --> R6[CONFINED OR UNCONFINED AQUIFER]
    R6 --> R7[BASE FLOW TO RIVER SPRING]
    R6 --> R8[EXTRACTION BOREWELL]
    R8 --> R9[END USE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Marking Note:** A well-labeled block diagram of the system flow (Section 4.1) carries **3 to 4 marks** in 14-mark descriptive questions. Always include labeled arrows indicating water flow direction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define the term "Rooftop Rainwater Harvesting" and list any **three** essential components of a rooftop recharge system.

**Model Answer:**

**Rooftop Rainwater Harvesting (RTRWH)** is the technique of collecting precipitation from the rooftop catchment of a building, filtering it, and directing it into an aquifer or storage tank for groundwater recharge or future use.

**Three essential components:**

1. **Catchment Surface (Roof):** RCC, GI sheet, or tiled roof that intercepts rainfall.
2. **Conveyance System:** Gutters, downpipes, and first-flush diverters that transport water from the roof to the recharge structure.
3. **Recharge Structure:** Recharge pit / trench / shaft / borewell that delivers filtered water into the subsurface aquifer.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners award the full 3 marks only when the definition is followed by a properly classified list. Avoid mixing storage tank components with recharge components.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

What is a **first-flush diverter**? Why is it essential in a rooftop recharge system?

**Model Answer:**

A **first-flush diverter** is a device installed at the junction of the downpipe and the filter chamber that automatically diverts the initial **2 mm to 5 mm** of rainfall runoff away from the recharge system. It operates on the principle of a manual or automatic ball-valve that closes once the contaminated first wash has been flushed out.

**Essentiality:**

- The roof remains dry for the inter-monsoon period (3–6 months), during which **dust, bird droppings, leaves, and atmospheric pollutants** accumulate.
- This accumulated contamination is washed off in the first few minutes of rain, producing highly polluted water.
- Diverting this load prevents **clogging of the filter media**, reduces **bacterial contamination of the aquifer**, and extends the **operational life** of the recharge structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** A common student error is to describe the first-flush diverter as a "filter." It is **not a filter** — it is a **diversion device**. This distinction is worth 1 mark.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

---

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **rooftop rainwater harvesting system** designed for groundwater recharge. Label all major components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A residential building in Palakkad has a rooftop area of **200 m²**. The average annual rainfall is **2200 mm**, and the runoff coefficient for the RCC roof is **0.85**. Design a **square recharge pit** of depth **2.0 m**, and determine the **number of pits required** to store the annual harvestable volume if the pits are to be recharged only during the four monsoon months. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Working of a Rooftop RWH System for Groundwater Recharge:**

The system operates as a **five-stage cascade**:

1. **Catchment:** Rainwater strikes the sloped RCC roof. The smooth surface ensures high runoff coefficient (≈ 0.85).
2. **Collection (Gutters):** Water flows along the roof slope into semi-circular **GI/PVC gutters** fixed along the eaves, graded at 1 in 120 toward the downpipe.
3. **Conveyance (Downpipes):** Vertical 75–100 mm diameter pipes carry water to ground level. A **first-flush diverter** is fitted at the top of the downpipe to discard the initial 2 mm of dirty runoff.
4. **Filtration:** Water enters a **chamber containing layers of gravel (bottom), coarse sand, fine sand, and activated charcoal (top)**. Suspended solids and organic matter are removed as water percolates downward.
5. **Recharge:** The filtered water is delivered to a **recharge pit** (typically 1.5 m × 1.5 m × 2 m) filled with graded gravel, where it percolates into the native soil and recharges the **unconfined aquifer** below.

**Sketch (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid Diagram):** The block diagram should show Roof → Gutter → First-Flush Diverter → Filter → Recharge Pit → Aquifer, with labeled arrows for water flow.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1: Compute annual harvestable volume**

$$V_{annual} = C \times A \times P = 0.85 \times 200 \times 2200$$

$$V_{annual} = 374{,}000 \text{ litres} = 374 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Compute volume of a single square pit**

$$V_{pit} = a^2 \times D = a^2 \times 2.0$$

**Step 3: Estimate number of pits required (conservative design)**

If each pit is designed as **1.5 m × 1.5 m × 2.0 m**:

$$V_{pit} = 1.5 \times 1.5 \times 2.0 = 4.5 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Account for soil permeability and refill rate**

Assuming the soil accepts 25% of pit volume per recharge cycle (typical for sandy-loam), effective storage per cycle per pit:

$$V_{effective} = 0.25 \times 4.5 = 1.125 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5: Number of cycles in 4 monsoon months**

Assume **20 major rain events** (≈ one every 6 days on average). Total cycles per pit:

$$V_{season} = 20 \times 1.125 = 22.5 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6: Number of pits required**

$$N = \frac{V_{annual}}{V_{season}} = \frac{374}{22.5} \approx 16.6$$

Rounding up, **N = 17 recharge pits** are required.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often divide 374 m³ by 4.5 m³ and report 83 pits. This is wrong because it ignores the **cyclical recharging capacity** of each pit. Always multiply by the number of refills per season.

---

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — May 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Compare **recharge pits, recharge trenches, and recharge wells** as methods of recharging groundwater from rooftop runoff. State suitable conditions for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of Darcy's law, derive the **expression for the drain time** of a square recharge pit of side $a$ and depth $D$ in soil of permeability $k$. What pit dimensions would you recommend for a site with $k = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s and a desired drain time of 12 hours? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

| Feature | Recharge Pit | Recharge Trench | Recharge Well |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | Cuboidal (1–3 m deep) | Long linear channel | Vertical shaft/borewell |
| **Best Soil** | Sandy-loam with permeable top layer | Stratified soil, moderate depth | Deep confined aquifer below clay |
| **Land Requirement** | Small | Larger (length-wise) | Minimal point footprint |
| **Recharge Rate** | 60–75% efficient | 70–85% efficient | 85–95% efficient |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Suitable Condition** | Individual houses, sandy soils | Apartment complexes, large open areas | Urban dense areas, deep aquifers, abandoned borewells |
| **Maintenance** | Annual silt removal | Annual desilting | Flushing and rehabilitation |

**Selection Logic:**

- If topsoil is permeable (>10⁻⁵ m/s) → **Pit**.
- If horizontal space is available but vertical depth is limited → **Trench**.
- If topsoil is clayey/impervious → **Well/Shaft** to bypass the impervious layer.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1: Volume of water in the pit**

$$V_{pit} = a^2 \times D$$

**Step 2: Infiltration discharge through bottom and side walls (Darcy's Law)**

$$Q = k \cdot i \cdot A_{wetted}$$

With $i = 1$ (gravity flow) and $A_{wetted} = a^2 + 4aD$:

$$Q = k \left( a^2 + 4aD \right)$$

**Step 3: Derive drain time**

$$t = \frac{V_{pit}}{Q} = \frac{a^2 D}{k(a^2 + 4aD)} = \frac{aD}{k(a + 4D)}$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values**

Given $k = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s and $t = 12 \text{ h} = 43200$ s:

$$\frac{aD}{10^{-4}(a + 4D)} = 43200$$

$$aD = 4.32 \times 10^{-3} (a + 4D)$$

$$aD = 4.32 \times 10^{-3} a + 1.728 \times 10^{-2} D$$

Rearranging:

$$a(D - 4.32 \times 10^{-3}) = 1.728 \times 10^{-2} D$$

$$a = \frac{1.728 \times 10^{-2} D}{D - 4.32 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 5: Trial with $D = 1.5$ m**

$$a = \frac{0.02592}{1.5 - 0.00432} = \frac{0.02592}{1.49568} = 0.0173 \text{ m}$$

This is impractically small.

**Step 6: Trial with $D = 0.05$ m (shallow pit)**

$$a = \frac{0.02592 \times 0.05}{0.05 - 0.00432} = \frac{0.001296}{0.04568} = 0.0284 \text{ m}$$

Still impractically small — the soil is too permeable for these dimensions.

**Step 7: Redesign for larger storm volume**

For a 1 m³ storm event with desired drain time of 12 h and $D = 1.0$ m:

$$a^2 \times 1.0 = 1.0 \implies a = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

Check drain time:

$$t = \frac{1.0 \times 1.0}{10^{-4}(1.0 + 4.0)} = \frac{1.0}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2000 \text{ s} \approx 33.3 \text{ min}$$

This drains in 33 minutes — far faster than 12 hours. **Implication:** The pit is over-designed; a smaller pit (e.g., 0.5 m × 0.5 m × 1.0 m, $V = 0.25$ m³) would drain in:

$$t = \frac{0.5 \times 1.0}{10^{-4}(0.5 + 4.0)} = \frac{0.5}{4.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 1111 \text{ s} \approx 18.5 \text{ min}$$

**Recommended Design:** Use a **0.5 m × 0.5 m × 1.0 m pit** for each 0.25 m³ of inflow, or install a **perforated concrete ring** at the bottom to slow infiltration and extend the drain time to 12 hours as required.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** In Q.B(b), many students incorrectly equate the wetted area to the bottom area alone ($a^2$), forgetting the **four vertical side walls**. This omission underestimates infiltration by a factor of 5 and leads to a 3-mark penalty.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- **Definition:** RTRWH = collection + filtration + subsurface recharge (not just storage).
- **Master Formula:** $V_{litres} = C \times A \times P$ — memorize in this exact form for KTU numericals.
- **Runoff Coefficients:** RCC = 0.85, GI = 0.80, Thatched = 0.55 (intervals matter, not just one value).
- **Pit Volume:** $V_{pit} = L \times B \times D$ for cuboidal; $V_{shaft} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 H$ for cylindrical.
- **Drain Time Formula:** $t = \frac{aD}{k(a + 4D)}$ — derived from Darcy's Law with $i = 1$.
- **Unit Conversion:** 24 h = 86400 s; 1 m³ = 1000 litres; 1 mm × 1 m² = 1 litre.
- **First-Flush Diverter:** It is a **diversion device**, NOT a filter — definitional precision matters.
- **Kerala Building Rules:** Mandatory for plots > 300 m²; recharge structures must be inspected by LSGD.
- **Soil Permeability Threshold:** For a 1.5 m deep pit to drain in 24 h, $k \geq 1.74 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s (fine sand minimum).
- **Component Sequence:** Roof → Gutter → First-Flush → Filter → Recharge Structure → Aquifer.
- **Recharge Structure Selection:** Permeable top → Pit; Impermeable top → Shaft/Borewell.
- **Annual Kerala Rainfall:** 2000–3600 mm; use location-specific value, not the national average of 1200 mm.
- **Darcy's Law** must include **side wall infiltration** in pit drain-time derivations.
- **Examiner Pet Peeves:** Missing unit conversions, omitting soil permeability check, and confusing storage with recharge.
- **Diagram Value:** A properly labeled flow diagram fetches **3–4 marks** in 14-mark descriptive questions — never skip it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recharging Groundwater Aquifers with Runoff from Ground Areas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recharging Groundwater Aquifers with Runoff from Ground Areas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Groundwater Recharge from Ground Areas** refers to the engineered process of collecting, conveying, and infiltrating surface runoff generated from open grounds, paved surfaces, parks, roads, agricultural fields, and other non-rooftop catchment areas into the subsurface aquifer system through scientifically designed recharge structures such as recharge pits, recharge trenches, spreading basins, percolation tanks, and injection wells.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OECET832 – Rainwater Harvesting, Module 2), the technique is formally described as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Artificial Recharge by Ground Area Runoff"** — The deliberate transfer of excess surface runoff from the ground catchments into suitable hydrogeological formations (aquifers) to augment the natural groundwater storage, restore declining water tables, and improve the quality of the sub-surface water by natural soil filtration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large natural sponge buried under your backyard** that holds fresh water. The sponge slowly dries up because no water is being poured into it. Now, every time it rains, water flows off the ground, into the drains, and straight into the sea — wasted! 

What if we **channeled that runoff water down into the sponge** through a small hole? The sponge would refill, and the surrounding trees would have water all year round. That "sponge" is the **aquifer**, and the "hole" is the **recharge structure**.

**Plain English Summary:**
- Catchment = the ground area where rain falls (parking lot, playground, open field).
- Runoff = the rainwater that does *not* soak in but flows away.
- Recharge structure = the engineered "hole" that lets this runoff soak deep down.
- Aquifer = the underground "sponge" of water-bearing rock/sand.

### 1.3 Why Ground Areas Need Separate Treatment

Rooftop runoff is usually clean and confined, so a simple pit or shaft works. **Ground area runoff** is different because:

| Property | Rooftop Runoff | Ground Area Runoff |
|---|---|---|
| Quantity per unit area | High (almost 90% of rainfall) | Low (depends on soil type & slope) |
| Quality | Relatively clean | Carries silt, debris, organic matter |
| Catchment size | Small (per house) | Large (parks, layouts) |
| Pollutant load | Low | High (oil, hydrocarbons from roads) |
| Best recharge method | Recharge pit / shaft | Recharge trench, basin, percolation tank |

> [!NOTE]
> The Runoff Coefficient (**C**) for different ground surfaces is a **mandatory** KTU 2024 syllabus value. The most important ones are: paved/concrete surface **C ≈ 0.85–0.90**, unpaved/open ground **C ≈ 0.20–0.40**, and sandy/gravelly soil **C ≈ 0.10–0.20**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Ground Area Recharge System

A complete engineering scheme for recharging groundwater with ground-area runoff consists of four functional sub-units:

1. **Catchment Surface** — the actual ground (impervious or pervious) generating runoff.
2. **Collection & Conveyance System** — open drains, gutters, grassed swales, or pipes that carry the runoff to the recharge point.
3. **Pre-Treatment Unit** — desilting chamber, silt trap, screen, or vegetated buffer to remove suspended solids, debris, oil, and grease.
4. **Recharge Structure** — pit / trench / basin / well / percolation tank where the cleaned water finally infiltrates the aquifer.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

- **Step 1 — Runoff Generation:** When the rainfall intensity (i) exceeds the soil's infiltration capacity (f), the excess water starts flowing over the surface. The volume generated depends on the **rainfall depth, area, and runoff coefficient**.
- **Step 2 — Surface Flow & Conveyance:** Runoff moves along the natural gradient. It is intercepted by drains or swales that direct it to the recharge point.
- **Step 3 — Pre-Treatment:** Runoff from ground areas carries **silt, organic matter, hydrocarbons, and pathogens**. A silt trap or vegetated filter strip is mandatory to prevent the recharge structure from silting up and losing its hydraulic efficiency.
- **Step 4 — Recharge / Infiltration:** Cleaned water is spread over a large area (basin/trench) or pushed down a shaft (well) so that it percolates into the aquifer at a rate faster than natural.
- **Step 5 — Storage in Aquifer:** The water joins the saturated zone and is recovered later through wells, bore-wells, or natural springs.

### 2.3 Classification of Ground Area Recharge Structures

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **five principal structures**. Each is chosen based on the soil type, depth of water table, and aquifer characteristics.

#### A. Recharge Pit
A small, shallow, square/circular pit filled with boulders, gravel, and sand. Used where the aquifer is shallow and the soil is moderately permeable.

#### B. Recharge Trench
A long, narrow excavation (typically 0.5 m to 1.5 m wide and 1.0 m to 3.0 m deep) filled with graded aggregate. Constructed along a contour line. Excellent for sloping terrain and small ground catchments.

#### C. Spreading Basin (Recharge Basin)
A large, shallow, levelled depression in open ground, often **1 m to 2 m deep** and covering hundreds of square metres. The water is simply spread and allowed to infiltrate.

#### D. Percolation Tank
A small masonry/earthen dam built across a stream or nala. Water is stored temporarily (during monsoon) and allowed to percolate. Suitable for **unconfined aquifers** and rural catchments.

#### E. Injection Well (Recharge Well)
A deep bore-well, often 50 m to 250 m deep, with a casing pipe and gravel pack. Used when the **aquifer is deep, confined, or overlain by impervious clay**. It directly forces water into the deep aquifer.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important** reference for solving KTU 2024 university exam questions on this topic. Memorize it thoroughly.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation / Symbol | Units (SI) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Runoff Volume from Ground Area** | $Q = C \times P \times A$ | $m^3$ | $P$ = rainfall depth (m), $A$ = area (m²), $C$ = runoff coefficient |
| 2 | Runoff coefficient (concrete / paved) | $C \approx 0.85$ | dimensionless | Roads, pavements, terraces |
| 3 | Runoff coefficient (open ground) | $C \approx 0.20$ to $0.40$ | dimensionless | Parks, playgrounds |
| 4 | Runoff coefficient (sandy soil) | $C \approx 0.10$ to $0.20$ | dimensionless | Infiltration-friendly soils |
| 5 | **Storage Volume of Recharge Pit** | $V_{pit} = L \times B \times H$ | $m^3$ | Cuboidal pit |
| 6 | **Storage Volume of Recharge Trench** | $V_{trench} = L \times B \times D$ | $m^3$ | $L$ = length, $B$ = width, $D$ = depth |
| 7 | Volume of Spreading Basin | $V_{basin} = A \times h$ | $m^3$ | $A$ = basin area, $h$ = water depth |
| 8 | **Number of Recharge Pits Required** | $N = \dfrac{Q_{runoff}}{V_{pit} \times \eta}$ | dimensionless | $\eta$ = infiltration efficiency ≈ 0.7–0.9 |
| 9 | Time of Concentration (Kirpich) | $t_c = 0.01947 \times L^{0.77} \times S^{-0.385}$ | minutes | $L$ = length (m), $S$ = slope |
| 10 | Peak Runoff Rate (Rational Method) | $q_p = C \times i \times A$ | $m^3/s$ | $i$ = rainfall intensity (m/s) |
| 11 | Recharge Well Discharge Capacity | $Q_w = \dfrac{2.72 \times k \times b \times s}{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r}\right)}$ | $m^3/day$ | $k$ = permeability, $b$ = aquifer thickness |
| 12 | Drawdown in Recharge Well | $s = \dfrac{Q_w}{2.72 \times k \times b} \times \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r}\right)$ | m | Used in design checks |
| 13 | Effective Runoff (after losses) | $Q_{eff} = Q_{runoff} - L_{seep} - L_{evap}$ | $m^3$ | Seepage + evaporation losses |
| 14 | **Volume of Desilting Chamber** | $V_{desilt} = Q_{peak} \times t_{detention}$ | $m^3$ | Usually detention time 2–5 min |

**Note on use of the vertical bar symbol:** In KTU 2024 answer sheets, if you need to express a modulus, always write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — never use the literal `|` character in a markdown table.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

This system is critically applied in:

- **Urban Layouts & Townships** — to dispose of stormwater legally and prevent flooding.
- **Industrial Estates** — to comply with the **CGWA (Central Ground Water Authority)** notification mandating rainwater harvesting in all establishments drawing groundwater.
- **Highways & Roads** — roadside recharge trenches double as storm drains and recharge structures.
- **Agricultural Fields** — percolation tanks revive failing bore-wells in drought-prone districts.
- **Coastal Areas** — recharge wells help arrest **salt-water intrusion** by maintaining the freshwater hydraulic head.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Runoff Volume from a Ground Catchment

The runoff volume from any catchment, including ground areas, is derived from the basic hydrologic equation:

**Statement:** The runoff from a catchment is equal to the product of the rainfall depth, the catchment area, and the runoff coefficient.

**Step 1 — Start with total rainfall volume striking the ground.**

$$
V_{rain} = P \times A
$$

Here, $P$ is the rainfall depth in metres and $A$ is the area in square metres. The product is in cubic metres.

**Step 2 — Introduce the runoff coefficient C.**

Not all rainfall becomes runoff. Some infiltrates into the soil, some evaporates, and some is stored in surface depressions. The fraction that actually runs off is captured by $C$, where $0 < C < 1$.

$$
Q_{runoff} = C \times V_{rain}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2.**

$$
Q_{runoff} = C \times P \times A
$$

This is the **Rational Runoff Equation** adapted for ground-area harvesting. The result $Q_{runoff}$ is in $m^3$ if $P$ is in m and $A$ is in $m^2$.

**Step 4 — Convert to litres for quick verification.**

$$
Q_{litres} = Q_{runoff} \times 1000
$$

Since $1\,m^3 = 1000$ litres, this is a useful check for small urban plots.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sizing of a Recharge Trench

A recharge trench is sized to hold the entire monsoon runoff while it infiltrates into the soil.

**Step 1 — Estimate the design runoff volume for the monsoon.**

$$
Q_{runoff} = C \times P_{season} \times A
$$

For a single design storm, $P_{season}$ is the cumulative seasonal rainfall. For a single-event design, $P$ is the design storm depth (typically the 2-year, 24-hour storm).

**Step 2 — Choose trench dimensions to be evaluated.**

Assume a trench of length $L$, width $B$, and depth $D$. The volume held is:

$$
V_{trench} = L \times B \times D
$$

**Step 3 — Account for the void ratio of the fill material.**

The trench is filled with boulders, gravel, and sand. About **30 % to 40 %** of the volume is solid material; the rest is void space available to store water.

$$
V_{available} = V_{trench} \times n
$$

where $n$ is the porosity of the fill (≈ 0.35 for mixed aggregate).

**Step 4 — Equate the runoff to the available void volume.**

$$
C \times P \times A = L \times B \times D \times n
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the required trench length** (taking $B$ and $D$ as design choices based on site).

$$
L = \dfrac{C \times P \times A}{B \times D \times n}
$$

This is the **final design formula** for a recharge trench — a high-yield KTU question.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Recharge Pits Required for a Layout

**Step 1 — Total runoff from the ground area.**

$$
Q_{total} = C \times P \times A
$$

**Step 2 — Storage volume of one pit.**

$$
V_{pit} = L \times B \times H
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the efficiency factor $\eta$.**

Not all the void volume participates in recharge; some is lost to clogging, evaporation, and bypass flow. A factor of $\eta = 0.75$ is typical.

$$
V_{effective} = V_{pit} \times \eta
$$

**Step 4 — Divide total runoff by effective pit volume.**

$$
N = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{V_{pit} \times \eta}
$$

Round $N$ **up** to the next whole number.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Peak Runoff Rate (Rational Method)

For the design of inlet drains and silt traps, the *peak* flow is required.

**Step 1 — Identify the design rainfall intensity $i$ (in m/s) for a duration equal to the time of concentration $t_c$.**

**Step 2 — Apply the Rational Formula.**

$$
q_p = C \times i \times A
$$

where $q_p$ is in $m^3/s$, $A$ is in $m^2$, and $i$ is in $m/s$.

**Step 3 — Unit conversion to common KTU exam units.**

If $i$ is given in mm/hr and $A$ in hectares, then:

$$
q_p = \dfrac{C \times i \times A}{360}
$$

The constant **360** converts (mm/hr × hectares) to $m^3/s$. This is a frequently tested conversion.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Recharge Structure

| Soil Type | Water Table Depth | Best Structure | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sandy, highly permeable | Deep (> 10 m) | Injection well / spreading basin | Recharge reaches deep aquifer |
| Sandy, highly permeable | Shallow (< 5 m) | Recharge pit / trench | Easy and low cost |
| Loam / clay-loam | Deep | Injection well | Slow surface infiltration |
| Loam / clay-loam | Shallow | Recharge trench with filter media | Surface infiltration acceptable |
| Impervious clay | Any depth | Percolation tank / injection well | Surface storage needed |
| Rocky / fissured | Shallow | Spreading basin | Water flows through fissures |
| Rocky / fissured | Deep | Injection well in fractures | Targets deep fractured zones |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Ground Area Recharge System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RAINFALL EVENT] --> B[GROUND CATCHMENT AREA]
    B --> C[RUNOFF GENERATION]
    C --> D[SURFACE DRAIN / SWALE]
    D --> E[PRE-TREATMENT UNIT]
    E --> F[DESILTING CHAMBER]
    F --> G[SCREEN / GRIT TRAP]
    G --> H[RECHARGE STRUCTURE]
    H --> H1[RECHARGE PIT]
    H --> H2[RECHARGE TRENCH]
    H --> H3[SPREADING BASIN]
    H --> H4[PERCOLATION TANK]
    H --> H5[INJECTION WELL]
    H1 --> I[SOIL INFILTRATION ZONE]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> J[DEEP AQUIFER DIRECT]
    I --> K[SHALLOW AQUIFER STORAGE]
    K --> L[RECOVERY WELL / BOREWELL]
    J --> L
    L --> M[DOMESTIC / IRRIGATION USE]
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Block Schematic of a Typical Recharge Trench

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[SURFACE GROUND LEVEL] --> L2[TOP LAYER - GRASS / TOPSOIL 100 MM]
    L2 --> L3[FILTER LAYER - SAND 200 MM]
    L3 --> L4[INTERMEDIATE LAYER - FINE GRAVEL 300 MM]
    L4 --> L5[COARSE LAYER - COBBLES AND BOULDERS 600 MM]
    L5 --> L6[GEOTEXTILE MEMBRANE LINER]
    L6 --> L7[NATURAL SUBSOIL FORMATION]
    L7 --> L8[AQUIFER WATER TABLE]
    R1[INLET PIPE FROM GROUND DRAIN] --> L3
    V1[VENT PIPE FOR AIR RELEASE] --> L1
    O1[OVERFLOW WEIR TO STORM DRAIN] --> L1
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Selection of Recharge Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[START - GROUND AREA RUNOFF AVAILABLE] --> Q1{AQUIFER TYPE?}
    Q1 -->|CONFINED DEEP| W[INJECTION WELL]
    Q1 -->|UNCONFINED SHALLOW| Q2{SOIL PERMEABILITY?}
    Q2 -->|HIGH SANDY| P[RECHARGE PIT OR TRENCH]
    Q2 -->|LOW CLAYEY| Q3{CATCHMENT SIZE?}
    Q3 -->|LESS THAN 1 HA| T[RECHARGE TRENCH WITH FILTER]
    Q3 -->|MORE THAN 1 HA| B[SPREADING BASIN OR PERCOLATION TANK]
    W --> DONE[STRUCTURE SELECTED]
    P --> DONE
    T --> DONE
    B --> DONE
```

### 4.4 Recharge Well Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP[GROUND LEVEL] --> A1[CONCRETE PLATFORM 1.5 M X 1.5 M]
    A1 --> A2[CASING PIPE - MS OR PVC 150 MM DIA]
    A2 --> A3[SLOTTED PIPE SECTION - PERFORATED CASING]
    A3 --> A4[GRAVEL PACK - 50 TO 100 MM THICK]
    A4 --> A5[DEEP AQUIFER ZONE]
    IN1[INLET FROM GROUND DRAIN] --> A1
    V1[AIR VENT VALVE] --> TOP
    SP1[SAMPLING PORT FOR WATER QUALITY] --> A2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Runoff Coefficient" and state its typical values for three different ground surfaces used in rainwater harvesting design.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Runoff Coefficient (C)** is the dimensionless ratio of the depth of water that runs off a surface to the depth of rainfall received on it. It accounts for losses due to infiltration, evaporation, surface storage, and surface texture.

Typical values for ground surfaces are:

- Concrete / paved roads: **C ≈ 0.85 to 0.90**
- Open ground / parks (compacted soil): **C ≈ 0.20 to 0.40**
- Sandy / gravelly soil (loose, vegetated): **C ≈ 0.10 to 0.20**

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for listing three values with reasoning.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
*List any three types of recharge structures used for ground area runoff and give one specific site condition where each is preferred.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Recharge Trench** — preferred on **sloping terrain with shallow soil**, where the trench can be aligned along a contour.
2. **Percolation Tank** — preferred in **rural catchments over unconfined aquifers**, often built across a small stream to store monsoon water.
3. **Injection Well (Recharge Well)** — preferred when the **aquifer is deep or confined by an impervious clay layer**, because surface infiltration cannot reach the deep zone.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark per structure with correct site condition.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style A]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various methods of recharging groundwater aquifers using runoff from ground areas. Describe with neat sketches the constructional features of a recharge trench and a percolation tank.

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Introduction** (1 mark) — Need for artificial recharge, role of ground area runoff.
2. **List of methods** (2 marks) — Recharge pit, recharge trench, spreading basin, percolation tank, injection well. Brief one-line description of each.
3. **Recharge Trench — Constructional Features** (2 marks):
   - Typical dimensions: 0.5 m to 1.5 m wide × 1.0 m to 3.0 m deep × variable length.
   - Filled with graded media: boulders at the base, gravel in the middle, sand on top, with a geotextile separator.
   - Inlet from drain, overflow to nearest storm drain, vent for air release.
4. **Percolation Tank — Constructional Features** (2 marks):
   - Small earthen/masonry dam built across a stream.
   - Compacted impervious core wall or masonry weir.
   - Spillway section for surplus flow, sluice gate for silt flushing.
   - Water stored temporarily (mostly during monsoon), percolates through the bed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A residential layout of 1.5 hectares has 60 % paved area and 40 % open ground. The seasonal rainfall is 1200 mm. Using the weighted runoff coefficient method, estimate (i) the total seasonal runoff volume, and (ii) the size of a cuboidal recharge pit (1.5 m × 1.5 m × 2.0 m) required, assuming the fill porosity is 0.35 and 75 % effective.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Weighted Runoff Coefficient**

For paved area: $C_1 = 0.85$; for open ground: $C_2 = 0.30$ (assume for compacted soil).

$$
C_{weighted} = 0.60 \times 0.85 + 0.40 \times 0.30 = 0.51 + 0.12 = 0.63
$$

**Step 2 — Total Catchment Area**

$$
A = 1.5 \, ha = 1.5 \times 10^4 \, m^2 = 15000 \, m^2
$$

**Step 3 — Rainfall Depth in Metres**

$$
P = 1200 \, mm = 1.2 \, m
$$

**Step 4 — Total Seasonal Runoff Volume**

$$
Q_{runoff} = C \times P \times A = 0.63 \times 1.2 \times 15000
$$

$$
Q_{runoff} = 11340 \, m^3
$$

**(i) Answer: Total seasonal runoff = 11,340 m³ (≈ 11.34 million litres).**

**Step 5 — Volume of One Recharge Pit**

$$
V_{pit} = L \times B \times H = 1.5 \times 1.5 \times 2.0 = 4.5 \, m^3
$$

**Step 6 — Effective Storage Per Pit**

$$
V_{eff} = V_{pit} \times n \times \eta = 4.5 \times 0.35 \times 0.75 = 1.18125 \, m^3
$$

**Step 7 — Number of Pits Required**

$$
N = \dfrac{Q_{runoff}}{V_{eff}} = \dfrac{11340}{1.18125} = 9599.36
$$

**(ii) Answer: Approximately 9600 recharge pits are required** — a clearly impractical number, indicating that for this catchment, a **recharge trench or spreading basin** is the appropriate choice instead of a pit.

**Conclusion:** This comparative calculation is a classic KTU 14-mark answer, demonstrating both numerical skill and engineering judgement.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style B]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a flow diagram, describe the functional components of a typical groundwater recharge system for ground area runoff. Discuss the design criteria for selecting a recharge structure.

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Flow Diagram** (2 marks) — Use a labelled block diagram with the following functional stages: *Catchment → Runoff Generation → Conveyance (Drains) → Pre-Treatment (Silt Trap) → Recharge Structure → Aquifer → Recovery Well*.
2. **Catchment description** (1 mark) — open ground, paved areas, slopes, soil type.
3. **Conveyance** (1 mark) — open drains, swales, pipes; non-erosive velocity considerations.
4. **Pre-treatment** (1 mark) — desilting chamber with 2–5 min detention time.
5. **Design Criteria for Recharge Structure** (2 marks):
   - Depth to water table (must be > 2 m to prevent water-logging).
   - Soil permeability (k > 10⁻⁵ m/s for surface structures; for k < 10⁻⁶ m/s, use injection well).
   - Aquifer type and thickness.
   - Quantity and quality of runoff available.
   - Land availability and topography.
   - Cost and maintenance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a 2.0 hectare park, the average annual rainfall is 950 mm. The weighted runoff coefficient is 0.35. Design a **rectangular recharge trench** of width 1.2 m and depth 1.5 m with a porosity of 0.40. Calculate the length of the trench required and comment on whether one trench is sufficient or multiple trenches are needed.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Catchment Area**

$$
A = 2.0 \, ha = 2 \times 10^4 \, m^2 = 20000 \, m^2
$$

**Step 2 — Rainfall in Metres**

$$
P = 950 \, mm = 0.95 \, m
$$

**Step 3 — Runoff Volume**

$$
Q_{runoff} = C \times P \times A = 0.35 \times 0.95 \times 20000
$$

$$
Q_{runoff} = 6650 \, m^3
$$

**Step 4 — Trench Storage Per Metre Run**

The trench is 1.2 m wide and 1.5 m deep. The unit storage per metre length is:

$$
V_{per\,m} = B \times D \times n = 1.2 \times 1.5 \times 0.40
$$

$$
V_{per\,m} = 0.72 \, m^3 \text{ per metre of length}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Required Trench Length**

$$
L = \dfrac{Q_{runoff}}{V_{per\,m}} = \dfrac{6650}{0.72}
$$

$$
L = 9236.11 \, m \approx 9236 \, m
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation**

A single trench of length **9.24 km** is clearly impractical for a 2-hectare park. The realistic design solution is:

- **Multiple parallel trenches** along the contours. If we adopt trenches of, say, **20 m length** spaced 5 m apart, the number required is:

$$
N_{trenches} = \dfrac{9236}{20} = 461.8 \approx 462 \, \text{trenches}
$$

- **Alternative:** Replace the trench with a **single spreading basin of depth 1.5 m**, whose required area is:

$$
A_{basin} = \dfrac{Q_{runoff}}{D} = \dfrac{6650}{1.5} = 4433.33 \, m^2
$$

This occupies only **22 %** of the 2-hectare park — far more practical.

**Conclusion:** The numerical answer (length of trench) is required by the KTU question, but the design **comment** carries the **higher-order thinking marks** and is what separates a KTU distinction answer from a mere pass-grade answer.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the porosity factor (n)** in recharge pit / trench calculations. Without it, your storage volume is grossly over-estimated.
> 2. **Mixing up units**: mm vs m, hectares vs m². Always show the unit conversion step explicitly on the answer sheet — the KTU examiner awards a **partial-mark step** for this.
> 3. **Omitting the efficiency factor $\eta$**: KTU 2024 answers should always include the 0.7–0.9 efficiency factor; its absence is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Picking the wrong structure**: The KTU paper often asks "which structure is suitable?" — students who blindly write "recharge pit" for every question lose 2 marks. Always justify the choice with at least one site condition.
> 5. **Forgetting to round UP the number of pits/trenches** — partial pits are physically impossible; $N$ must be the next whole integer.
> 6. **Drawing diagrams without labels** — KTU 2024 valuation key requires **all components labelled**, including the depths and the fill material layers.
> 7. **Skipping the pre-treatment discussion** — the syllabus explicitly emphasises that ground area runoff carries silt and pollutants; not mentioning a silt trap is a 1-mark penalty.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ground area runoff** is collected from open grounds, paved areas, and parks, *not* rooftops.
- The **Rational Equation $Q = C \times P \times A$** is the foundation of every design calculation.
- **C** is highest (≈ 0.85) for paved surfaces and lowest (≈ 0.10) for sandy soil.
- The **five recharge structures** are: Recharge Pit, Recharge Trench, Spreading Basin, Percolation Tank, and Injection Well.
- **Recharge Trench** formula: $L = \dfrac{C \times P \times A}{B \times D \times n}$
- **Recharge Pit count** formula: $N = \dfrac{C \times P \times A}{L \times B \times H \times n \times \eta}$
- **Peak runoff** (for design of inlet drains): $q_p = C \times i \times A$ with proper unit conversion.
- **Pre-treatment is mandatory** — desilting chamber with 2–5 min detention time.
- **Selection criteria** depend on: depth to water table, soil permeability, aquifer type, land availability, and runoff quality.
- **Injection wells** are essential when the **aquifer is deep or confined**; surface structures cannot reach the deep zone.
- **Percolation tanks** are ideal in rural catchments over unconfined aquifers, built across small streams.
- **Hectare–m² conversion** is $1\,ha = 10^4\,m^2$ — show this conversion on the answer sheet.
- **mm–m conversion** is $P(m) = P(mm)/1000$ — always do this step.
- **Porosity (n) of graded aggregate** ≈ 0.30–0.40; **Efficiency (η)** ≈ 0.70–0.90.
- **Always round UP** the number of recharge structures.
- **CGWA compliance**: As per the KTU syllabus highlight, all groundwater-abstracting establishments in India must implement such structures.
- **A recharge well reaching a confined aquifer** also helps to arrest **saltwater intrusion** in coastal regions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modular Rain Water Harvesting System - Coarse Mesh/Leaf Screen, Gutter, Downspout/Conduit, First Flushing Device, Filter, Sand Filter, Charcoal Water Filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modular Rain Water Harvesting System - Coarse Mesh/Leaf Screen, Gutter, Downspout/Conduit, First Flushing Device, Filter, Sand Filter, Charcoal Water Filter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Modular Rainwater Harvesting System (MRWHS) — Formal Definition

A **Modular Rain Water Harvesting System (MRWHS)** is an engineered, component-wise assembly of standardized units that intercepts, conveys, treats, stores, and distributes rainwater collected from a catchment surface (typically a rooftop). The term *modular* emphasizes that each functional stage — collection, coarse filtration, conveyance, first-flush diversion, fine filtration, disinfection/storage — is provided by an **independent, replaceable, and standard-sized element**. This design philosophy, strongly endorsed in KTU 2024 syllabus and in **IS 15797:2008** (Code of Practice for Rainwater Harvesting), enables **plug-and-play scalability**, easy maintenance, and **decentralized deployment** in both urban and rural Kerala settings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – OECET832):**
> Students are expected to identify, sketch, and justify the *function* of each modular component: **Coarse Mesh / Leaf Screen → Gutter → Downspout/Conduit → First Flushing Device → Filter → Sand Filter → Charcoal Water Filter.** The flow is *unidirectional*, and the *order* of components is non-negotiable.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Human Digestive System"

Think of a Modular RWH system as the **human digestive tract**:

- The **roof (catchment)** is the *mouth* — it accepts raw input (rain + debris).
- The **coarse mesh/leaf screen** is the *teeth* — physically breaks up and rejects large solids.
- The **gutter** is the *esophagus* — a horizontal channel that moves the input along.
- The **downspout/conduit** is the *throat* — directs the flow vertically.
- The **first-flush device** is the *stomach's acid purge* — ejects the contaminated first portion.
- The **sand filter** is the *small intestine* — fine mechanical and biological filtration.
- The **charcoal (activated carbon) filter** is the *liver* — chemical and odour purification.

Just as a missing organ disrupts digestion, omitting any one of these modules **breaks the water-quality chain** and may lead to contaminated storage, mosquito breeding, or system failure.

## 1.3 Sequence of Flow & Cumulative Function

The system follows a **strict top-down hierarchy**:

$$
\text{Roof} \rightarrow \text{Leaf Screen} \rightarrow \text{Gutter} \rightarrow \text{Downspout} \rightarrow \text{First-Flush Diverter} \rightarrow \text{Filter} \rightarrow \text{Sand Filter} \rightarrow \text{Charcoal Filter} \rightarrow \text{Storage Tank}
$$

Each stage progressively **increases water purity** and **decreases turbidity, BOD, and pathogen load**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Standard:** Per **CPHEEO Manual on Water Supply & Treatment (1999)** and **IS 15797:2008**, every domestic RWH system in Kerala **must** incorporate at least the first four modules; the sand and charcoal filters are *recommended* for potable-grade reuse.

| # | Module | Primary Function | Typical Material |
|---|--------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Leaf Screen | Reject leaves, twigs, bird droppings | GI wire / PVC mesh (5–10 mm) |
| 2 | Gutter | Horizontal conveyance | PVC / GI / Aluminium |
| 3 | Downspout | Vertical conveyance | PVC / Cast iron |
| 4 | First-Flush Diverter | Discard first 2–5 mm of rainfall | PVC ball-valve type |
| 5 | Filter | Sediment & debris removal | Mesh + gravel bed |
| 6 | Sand Filter | Fine particulate, microbial | Layered sand & gravel |
| 7 | Charcoal Filter | Odour, chlorine, organics | Activated carbon |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Module 1 — Coarse Mesh / Leaf Screen

**Operational Logic:**
1. Mounted at the **mouth of the gutter** or at roof-level rain-heads.
2. Made of **galvanized iron (GI) wire, PVC, or stainless-steel** mesh.
3. Aperture size: **5 mm to 10 mm** (Kerala rain often carries mango/coconut leaves).
4. Inclined at **45°–60°** to allow self-cleaning by wind and water.

**Why it matters:** Removes up to **70–80%** of gross solids, dramatically extending the life of downstream components.

## 2.2 Module 2 — Gutter

The gutter is the **horizontal channel** fixed along the eaves line.

- **Shape:** Rectangular (PVC) or semi-circular (GI).
- **Size:** Designed using the Rational Formula for roof runoff.
- **Slope:** Minimum **1 in 120** (≈ 0.83%) toward the downspout.

**Design criteria** (CPHEEO + IS 15797):
- Carrying capacity: 1.25 × peak design flow.
- Velocity range: **0.6 m/s to 0.9 m/s** (self-cleansing yet non-erosive).

## 2.3 Module 3 — Downspout / Conduit

A **vertical pipe** that conveys gutter water to the first-flush device or filter chamber.

- Diameter: typically **75 mm to 110 mm** for residential roofs in Kerala.
- Anchored with brackets at ≤ **1.5 m spacing**.
- Should discharge into a **rainwater head** with a leaf trap before the first-flush unit.

## 2.4 Module 4 — First Flushing Device (First-Flush Diverter)

A device that **diverts and discards the first 2 to 5 mm of rainfall**, which contains the **highest concentration of contaminants** (bird droppings, dust, hydrocarbons, heavy metals washed from the roof).

- **Ball-valve type:** A floating ball seals the outlet once the chamber is full.
- **Manual type:** A valve is opened by the user after the first rain.
- **Sizing:** Volume of first-flush chamber ($V_{ff}$) is given by:

$$
V_{ff} = A_c \times d_{ff}
$$

where $A_c$ = catchment (roof) area in m², and $d_{ff}$ = first-flush depth (typically **2 mm = 0.002 m**).

## 2.5 Module 5 — Filter (Sediment / Pre-Filter)

A **two-stage coarse filter** placed after the first-flush device:
- **Stage 1:** Fine wire mesh (1–2 mm).
- **Stage 2:** Pebble/gravel bed (10–20 mm) for sediment interception.

Removes suspended solids up to **100–150 microns**.

## 2.6 Module 6 — Sand Filter

A **slow sand filter** operating by **physical straining, sedimentation, and biological schmutzdecke action**.

Layered cross-section (top → bottom):
1. **Supernatant water layer** (0.5 m).
2. **Fine sand** ($D_{10} \approx 0.15$–$0.30$ mm, depth 0.5–0.8 m).
3. **Coarse sand** (depth 0.05 m).
4. **Gravel** (graded 2–20 mm, depth 0.2–0.3 m).
5. **Under-drain system** (perforated PVC laterals).

> [!NOTE]
> **Schmutzdecke** — the biologically active layer (algae, bacteria, protozoa) that forms on top of fine sand within 2–3 weeks of operation. It is the **principal pathogen-removal mechanism** of a slow sand filter.

**Filtration rate:** Typically **100 to 200 L/m²·h** for domestic RWH.

## 2.7 Module 7 — Charcoal (Activated Carbon) Water Filter

The **final polishing unit**, removing:
- Dissolved **organic compounds** (VOC).
- **Chlorine** and chlorination by-products.
- **Odour and colour** (especially from decaying leaves).
- Trace **heavy metals** (Pb, Hg) via adsorption.

**Key parameter:** **Iodine Number ≥ 900 mg/g** indicates high adsorption capacity (per **IS 2752**).

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Application |
|---|-----------|---------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Roof runoff (Rational) | $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A_c$ | L/s | Peak gutter/downspout flow |
| 2 | First-flush volume | $V_{ff} = A_c \cdot d_{ff}$ | L | Sizing diverter chamber |
| 3 | Gutter cross-section | $A_g = \dfrac{Q}{v}$ | m² | Manning/continuity check |
| 4 | Manning velocity | $v = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | m/s | Gutter capacity |
| 5 | Downspout diameter | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi v}}$ | m | Sizing vertical conduit |
| 6 | Sand filter area | $A_{sf} = \dfrac{Q_{design}}{v_f}$ | m² | Slow sand filter footprint |
| 7 | Filter rate | $v_f = \dfrac{Q}{A_{sf}}$ | L/m²·h | Hydraulic loading check |
| 8 | Annual yield | $Y = P \cdot A_c \cdot C \cdot \eta$ | L/year | System sizing |
| 9 | Charcoal contact time | $EBCT = \dfrac{V_{carbon}}{Q}$ | min | Adsorber sizing |
| 10 | Headloss in filter | $h_f = k \cdot \dfrac{L}{D_{10}} \cdot v_f$ | m | Carmen-Kozeny approximation |

> **Where:** $C$ = runoff coefficient (0.8 for tiled roof), $I$ = rainfall intensity (mm/h), $A_c$ = catchment area (m²), $P$ = annual rainfall (mm), $\eta$ = system efficiency (**0.7–0.85**), $n$ = Manning's roughness, $R$ = hydraulic radius, $S$ = slope, $D_{10}$ = effective sand size, $k$ = empirical constant.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

In Kerala's **KSDMA (Kerala State Disaster Management Authority)** programmes, modular RWH units are deployed in schools and anganwadis. The **CHARCOAL filter** module has shown **>90% reduction in faecal coliforms** when combined with upstream sand filtration — a result published in the *Journal of Water & Health* and adopted by the **Suchitwa Mission**, Government of Kerala.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Sizing the First-Flush Chamber

The first-flush volume is directly proportional to the roof area and the depth of rainfall to be discarded. We will derive the working formula and apply it to a typical Kerala residential building.

**Step 1 — Conceptual basis:**
The first $d_{ff}$ millimetres of rain falling on area $A_c$ produces a *sheet* of water of volume:

$$
V = \text{Area} \times \text{Depth}
$$

**Step 2 — Unit harmonization:**
Convert depth from mm to m by dividing by 1000.

$$
V_{ff} \; (\text{m}^3) = A_c \; (\text{m}^2) \times \dfrac{d_{ff} \; (\text{mm})}{1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Conversion to litres:**
Since $1 \; \text{m}^3 = 1000 \; \text{L}$,

$$
V_{ff} \; (\text{L}) = A_c \times d_{ff}
$$

where both $A_c$ and $d_{ff}$ are used in m² and mm respectively (a convenient form for KTU numericals).

**Step 4 — Worked Example:**
Consider a tiled-roof house in **Thiruvananthapuram** with $A_c = 100 \; \text{m}^2$. Adopt $d_{ff} = 2 \; \text{mm}$ (conservative Kerala norm).

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ff} &= 100 \times 2 \\
       &= 200 \; \text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
A **200-litre** first-flush chamber (e.g., a 200 L PVC drum plumbed with a ball valve) must be installed. This is a standard 14-mark derivation often asked in KTU examinations.

## 3.2 Derivation — Sizing the Slow Sand Filter Area

**Step 1 — Given:**
Design flow $Q = 500 \; \text{L/h}$ (peak domestic demand), permissible filtration velocity $v_f = 150 \; \text{L/m}^2 \cdot \text{h}$ (per IS 8419).

**Step 2 — Continuity equation:**

$$
Q = A_{sf} \times v_f
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for area:**

$$
A_{sf} = \dfrac{Q}{v_f}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{sf} &= \dfrac{500}{150} \\
       &= 3.33 \; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Choose practical dimensions:**
A square filter with side = $\sqrt{3.33} \approx 1.83$ m, or two units of $1.2 \times 1.4$ m each.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance of Each Module

| Module | Removes | Removal Efficiency (%) | Frequency of Cleaning | Bypass Risk if Fails |
|--------|---------|------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Leaf Screen | Leaves, twigs, large debris | 70–85 | Weekly (monsoon: 2x) | Clogged gutter overflow |
