| Gutter | Conveys flow | N/A (conduit) | Quarterly | Roof stagnation, mosquito breeding |
| Downspout | Conveys flow | N/A (conduit) | Quarterly | Leakage, water seepage in walls |
| First-Flush Diverter | Dust, bird droppings, hydrocarbons | 60–80 of initial load | After every dry spell | High microbial load in tank |
| Sediment Filter | Sand, silt | 50–70 | Monthly | Clogged sand filter |
| Sand Filter | Turbidity, bacteria, protozoa | 95–99 (coliforms) | Annually (top scraping) | Turbid water in storage |
| Charcoal Filter | Odour, chlorine, organics, Pb | 70–90 (chlorine) | 6–12 months (carbon change) | Foul smell, taste issues |

## 3.4 KTU-Style Numerical Template

**Problem:** A school in **Kottayam** has a roof area of 250 m². Annual rainfall = 2700 mm. Runoff coefficient $C = 0.85$, system efficiency $\eta = 0.80$. Design (a) the first-flush chamber for $d_{ff} = 2$ mm, (b) the storage tank for a 30-day dry period assuming daily demand of 500 L, and (c) the slow sand filter area for a peak filtration rate of 200 L/m²·h.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ff} &= A_c \times d_{ff} \\
       &= 250 \times 2 \\
       &= 500 \; \text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Annual yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= P \times A_c \times C \times \eta \\
  &= 2700 \times 250 \times 0.85 \times 0.80 \\
  &= 459,000 \; \text{L/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Required storage for 30 dry days:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{30} &= 30 \times 500 = 15,000 \; \text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) — Sand filter area:**
Take peak hourly flow = 1000 L/h.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{sf} &= \dfrac{Q}{v_f} = \dfrac{1000}{200} = 5 \; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget the unit conversion of $d_{ff}$ from mm to m in the runoff formula. Also, the runoff coefficient $C$ for **tiled roofs in Kerala is 0.80–0.85**, NOT 1.0. Mis-reading this gives an inflated yield and costs 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Modular RWH System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the modular rainwater harvesting system, including the data-flow / process-flow between each module.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Rainfall Event] --> B1[Roof Catchment]
    B1 --> C1[Coarse Mesh Leaf Screen]
    C1 --> D1[Gutter Horizontal Channel]
    D1 --> E1[Downspout Conduit]
    E1 --> F1[First Flushing Device]
    F1 --> G1[Sediment Pre Filter]
    G1 --> H1[Slow Sand Filter]
    H1 --> I1[Activated Charcoal Filter]
    I1 --> J1[Storage Tank]
    J1 --> K1[Pump and Distribution]
    K1 --> L1[End Use Domestic]

    F1 --> F2[Reject Pipe to Recharge Pit]
    H1 --> H2[Drain for Backwash]
    J1 --> J2[Overflow to Recharge Well]
    J1 --> J3[Vented Mosquito Proof Screen]

    C1 -. Clean Weekly .-> M1[Maintenance Schedule]
    D1 -. Clean Quarterly .-> M1
    E1 -. Clean Quarterly .-> M1
    F1 -. Reset After Dry Spell .-> M1
    G1 -. Clean Monthly .-> M1
    H1 -. Scrape Top Layer Annually .-> M1
    I1 -. Replace Carbon 6 to 12 Months .-> M1
    J1 -. Inspect Every 3 Months .-> M1
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Slow Sand Filter (Mermaid Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SAND_FILTER_UNIT
        SF1[Inlet from Pre Filter] --> SF2[Supernatant Water Layer 0.5 m]
        SF2 --> SF3[Schmutzdecke Biologically Active Layer]
        SF3 --> SF4[Fine Sand D10 0.15 to 0.30 mm Depth 0.5 to 0.8 m]
        SF4 --> SF5[Coarse Sand Supporting Layer 0.05 m]
        SF5 --> SF6[Graded Gravel 2 to 20 mm Depth 0.3 m]
        SF6 --> SF7[Perforated Under Drain Laterals]
        SF7 --> SF8[Filtered Water Outlet to Charcoal Filter]
    end

    SF9[Waste Washout Pipe] -.-> SF3
```

## 4.3 Modular Sequence & Hydraulic Grade Line

The diagram below shows the **relative elevation** (hydraulic grade line, HGL) of each module — a typical KTU Board Exam sketch question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Roof 5.0 m] --> R2[Leaf Screen 4.9 m]
    R2 --> R3[Gutter 4.5 m Slope 1 in 120]
    R3 --> R4[Downspout 4.5 m to 1.5 m]
    R4 --> R5[First Flush 1.5 m Ground]
    R5 --> R6[Sand Filter 1.5 m]
    R6 --> R7[Charcoal Filter 1.5 m]
    R7 --> R8[Storage Tank 0.0 m Below GL]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Sketch Tip:** When drawing a sectional view in the answer script, always show the **hydraulic grade line as a continuous dashed line** descending from the roof to the storage tank, indicating the **gravity-driven** nature of the system. No pumps are needed between modules — only at the final delivery point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **List and explain the function of any three modular components of a rainwater harvesting system.**

**Model Answer:**
The three modular components and their functions are:
1. **Coarse Mesh / Leaf Screen:** Mounted at the gutter inlet, it physically excludes leaves, twigs, and large debris. Aperture 5–10 mm; cleans weekly during monsoon.
2. **First-Flush Diverter:** Diverts the first 2–5 mm of rainfall (which carries the highest concentration of dust, bird droppings, and roof-deposited hydrocarbons) to a waste/recharge pit. It improves the microbiological and chemical quality of stored water.
3. **Charcoal (Activated Carbon) Filter:** The final polishing unit; removes odour, colour, residual chlorine, dissolved organic compounds, and trace heavy metals by adsorption. Carbon is replaced every 6–12 months.

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **What is the significance of the *schmutzdecke* layer in a slow sand filter used in rainwater harvesting?**

**Model Answer:**
The *schmutzdecke* is a thin, biologically active layer (2–5 mm thick) that forms on the top surface of a slow sand filter within 2–3 weeks of operation. It consists of **algae, bacteria, protozoa, and fine organic matter** held within a gelatinous matrix. Its significance is threefold:
1. It provides **biological purification** — bacteria and protozoa prey on pathogens (e.g., *E. coli*, *Vibrio*), achieving >95% coliform removal.
2. It performs **fine mechanical straining** of suspended particles.
3. It generates **oxygen**, maintaining aerobic conditions that prevent anaerobic foul-smelling by-products.

The layer is removed (scraped) once it causes excessive head loss — typically every 12–18 months — and is allowed to re-form naturally.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (a) & (b).** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
> **Module 2 — Modular Rainwater Harvesting System.**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **sequence of components** in a modular rainwater harvesting system from the roof catchment to the storage tank. State the function and a suitable material for **each component**.
> **(b)** A residential building in **Ernakulam** has a tiled-roof catchment of 150 m². Annual rainfall = 2800 mm; runoff coefficient = 0.85; first-flush depth = 2 mm. Design (i) the first-flush chamber volume, (ii) the annual rainwater yield, and (iii) the slow sand filter area for a peak flow of 800 L/h at a filtration rate of 200 L/m²·h.

### Question 3A — Alternative Choice 1

> **Q3A (a) & (b).** `[KTU University Exam – Past Year 2022]`
> **(a)** Describe in detail the **principle, design, and maintenance** of a **slow sand filter** as used in domestic rainwater harvesting. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the role of **activated charcoal (carbon) filtration** in a modular RWH system, including the parameters that govern its design and the indicators for replacement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Slow Sand Filter — Principle, Design, Maintenance:**

- **Principle:** Combines *physical straining*, *sedimentation*, and *biological metabolism* in a thin schmutzdecke layer.
- **Design parameters:**
  - Filtration rate: **100–200 L/m²·h**.
  - Effective sand size ($D_{10}$): **0.15–0.30 mm**.
  - Uniformity coefficient ($C_u$): **< 3**.
  - Sand depth: **0.5–0.8 m**.
  - Gravel support: graded 2–20 mm, depth **0.2–0.3 m**.
  - Supernatant water depth: 0.5–1.0 m.
- **Maintenance:** Top 1–2 cm of sand is scraped when head loss reaches 0.5–0.8 m or filtration rate drops below acceptable limits. Scraping is done **annually** in Kerala's climate. Re-sanding required every 3–5 years.

**(b) Activated Charcoal Filter — Role, Design, Replacement:**

- **Role:** Adsorbs dissolved organic matter, chlorine, odour, colour, and trace heavy metals (Pb, Hg). It is the *final polishing stage* before storage or use.
- **Design parameters:**
  - **Iodine Number ≥ 900 mg/g** (adsorption capacity indicator).
  - **EBCT (Empty Bed Contact Time):** 5–10 minutes for domestic use.
  - **Bed depth:** 0.5–1.0 m; **backwash capability** required.
- **Replacement indicators:**
  1. Treated water regains a chlorine/odour smell.
  2. Δ-P across the bed increases sharply.
  3. EBCT breakthrough test fails.
  4. **Every 6–12 months** as a routine schedule.
- The spent carbon can be reactivated thermally (1100 °C) or disposed of safely.

### Question 3B — Alternative Choice 2

> **Q3B (a) & (b).** `[KTU University Exam – Past Year 2023]`
> **(a)** Explain with a sketch the working of a **first-flush diverter** and derive the expression for its chamber volume. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare the **coarse mesh**, **sand filter**, and **charcoal filter** in terms of the type of impurities removed, removal efficiency, and maintenance frequency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) First-Flush Diverter — Sketch & Derivation:**

- A vertical chamber (PVC drum or masonry tank) is plumbed *in line* before the storage tank.
- During the first 2–5 mm of rain, water flows into the chamber and a **floating PVC ball** rises with the water level.
- Once the chamber is full, the ball **seats itself against the outlet**, blocking the waste path and diverting subsequent, cleaner water to the storage tank.
- The first-flush water is held until **manual draining** or slowly discharged to a recharge pit.

**Derivation:**

$$
V_{ff} = A_c \times d_{ff}
$$

- $V_{ff}$ = first-flush volume (litres),
- $A_c$ = roof catchment area (m²),
- $d_{ff}$ = first-flush depth (mm).

For a **120 m² tiled roof in Thrissur** with $d_{ff} = 2$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ff} &= 120 \times 2 = 240 \; \text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence a **240 L PVC drum diverter** is required.

**(b) Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | Coarse Mesh | Sand Filter | Charcoal Filter |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|-----------------|
| Impurity type | Leaves, twigs | Turbidity, bacteria, protozoa | Odour, chlorine, organics |
| Mechanism | Physical screening | Straining + biological | Adsorption |
| Removal efficiency | 70–85% (gross solids) | 95–99% (coliforms) | 70–90% (chlorine, VOC) |
| Maintenance | Weekly | Annual (scraping) | 6–12 months (carbon change) |
| Failure impact | Gutter clogging | Turbid water | Foul-smelling water |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing *first-flush* with *filter*:** Many students wrongly treat the first-flush device as a filter. It is a *diverter* that *discards* water, not one that treats it.
> 2. **Ignoring the *biological* role of the sand filter:** Marks are awarded for *schmutzdecke* — omitting this is a 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Wrong units in numericals:** Always write the unit of $V_{ff}$ as litres, not m³, in the final answer (unless explicitly asked).
> 4. **Skipping the sketch in 7-mark sub-parts:** A 14-mark Part B question without a labelled diagram loses **at least 2 marks** in valuation.
> 5. **Mislabelling activated carbon as *charcoal coal*:** Use the precise KTU/IS terminology — *activated carbon* or *activated charcoal*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Strict sequence:** Leaf Screen → Gutter → Downspout → First-Flush → Sediment Filter → Sand Filter → Charcoal Filter → Storage.
- **Modular design** means *independent, replaceable, scalable* units — adopted from **IS 15797:2008**.
- **Leaf screen aperture** = 5–10 mm; cleaning is **weekly** during the Kerala monsoon (June–September).
- **Gutter slope** ≥ **1 in 120**; velocity range 0.6–0.9 m/s.
- **First-flush depth** = 2–5 mm; volume formula $V_{ff} = A_c \times d_{ff}$.
- **Sand filter** uses a *biological schmutzdecke* — slow filtration, 100–200 L/m²·h.
- **Charcoal filter** is the *final polishing* stage; Iodine Number ≥ 900 mg/g; replace every 6–12 months.
- **Annual yield** formula: $Y = P \times A_c \times C \times \eta$ with $C = 0.8$–$0.85$ for tiled roofs and $\eta = 0.7$–$0.85$.
- **Maintenance** is *top-down* — coarser modules cleaned more frequently than finer ones.
- **Standards to memorize:** **IS 15797:2008**, **IS 8419** (slow sand filter), **CPHEEO Manual 1999**, **KSDMA Guidelines**.
- **Kerala-specific:** The KSDMA / Suchitwa Mission recommends *all seven modules* for schools and public buildings, with **mandatory mosquito-proof screening** on the storage tank vent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Recharging Subsurface Aquifers - Through Recharge Pit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Methods of Recharging Subsurface Aquifers - Through Recharge Pit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Recharge Pit** is a shallow, engineered surface excavation (generally **2 m to 5 m** deep) that is subsequently backfilled with a graded filter media consisting of boulders, gravels, and coarse sand, designed to receive, store, filter, and transmit surface runoff vertically downward into the underlying subsurface aquifer. It is classified as a **direct (in-situ) artificial groundwater recharge** structure and is the most widely adopted method for augmenting groundwater in areas with significant thickness of unsaturated (vadose) zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** A recharge pit is "an artificially created excavation, usually rectangular or circular, refilled with porous media like brickbats, stones, gravels, and sand, that intercepts surface runoff and permits its vertical percolation to recharge the shallow aquifer below."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **giant funnel inverted into the ground**. When rain falls on your rooftop or land, instead of letting the water run off into drains and be lost as surface flow, you pour it into a hole filled with rocks. The rocks act like a **natural sieve and a slow-release valve** — they trap dirt, slowly release the water, and gently push it deep into the earth to refill the underground water "tank" (aquifer) that wells draw from.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** A recharge pit is essentially a **"sponge pit"** in the ground. You pour dirty rainwater in, and clean, filtered water slowly trickles out the bottom to recharge the water table below.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Depth of Pit | **2 m – 5 m** | Must terminate above the post-monsoon water table |
| Top Width | **1.0 m – 3.0 m** | Sized based on catchment and recharge volume |
| Side Slope | **1:1 to 1.5:1 (V:H)** | Ensures slope stability in cohesionless soils |
| Runoff Coefficient (C) | **0.6 – 0.9** | For impervious catchments like rooftops, pavements |
| Filter Porosity (n) | **0.30 – 0.40** | Determines effective storage volume |
| Hydraulic Conductivity (K) | **10⁻⁴ m/s – 10⁻⁶ m/s** | Governs percolation rate through bottom |
| Emptying Time | **24 – 72 hours** | Critical to allow re-oxygenation and drying |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A recharge pit is specifically recommended when the **unsaturated zone is moderately thick (5 m – 15 m)** and the underlying aquifer is **unconfined or semi-confined** with adequate specific yield.

### 1.4 Position Within Recharge Method Taxonomy

```
Artificial Recharge Methods
├── Surface Spreading Methods
│   ├── Flooding
│   ├── Basin Method
│   ├── Furrow / Ditch Method
│   └── Recharge Trench
│
├── Sub-Surface (Vertical) Methods   ◄── RECHARGE PIT BELONGS HERE
│   ├── Recharge Pit
│   ├── Recharge Shaft / Bore Pit
│   ├── Recharge Well
│   └── Injection Well
│
└── Combined / Modified Methods
    └── Recharge Pit with Recharge Well
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanism — The "Why" and "How"

The recharge pit operates on a four-stage vertical hydraulic process:

1. **Interception (Collection Stage):** Surface runoff from a defined catchment (rooftop, paved courtyard, open ground) is conveyed through a pipe, channel, or gutter into the pit. The water is generally passed through a **desilting chamber** upstream to remove coarse suspended solids.

2. **Filtration (Purification Stage):** As water enters the pit, it encounters a graded filter bed arranged in **coarse-to-fine layers** from bottom to top (boulders → gravels → coarse sand → fine sand). This **reverse gradation** ensures that:
   - The finer media are protected from clogging at the interface.
   - Particulate matter is trapped within the upper layers (which can be easily cleaned).
   - Microbial and turbidity load is reduced.

3. **Storage (Buffering Stage):** The void spaces between the boulders and gravels temporarily store water, acting as a **buffer reservoir**. This dampens peak inflows and ensures continuous recharge even after rainfall ceases.

4. **Percolation (Recharge Stage):** Water moves downward through the **bottom sand/gravel base** by gravity, into the vadose zone, and finally reaches the water table. The rate is governed by **Darcy's Law** through unsaturated flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Question Answer:** Why use graded filter media instead of just an open hole? — Because graded media (i) prevents collapse of pit walls, (ii) filters suspended impurities to protect the aquifer from contamination, and (iii) maintains sustained high infiltration rates by preventing surface sealing.

### 2.2 Types of Recharge Pits

**Type 1 — Conventional Dry Recharge Pit:**
- Simple excavation filled with filter media.
- Suitable where the **post-monsoon water table is more than 4 m – 5 m** below ground.
- No standing water normally; pit dries out between rain events.

**Type 2 — Wet Recharge Pit (with Recharge Well):**
- A borehole or recharge well is drilled at the center or one end of the pit.
- The well extends from the pit bottom to a deeper, confined/perched aquifer.
- Useful when the **shallow aquifer is poor or already full**, but a deeper transmissive layer exists.

**Type 3 — Bore Pit (Recharge Shaft):**
- A deeper, narrower shaft (typically **0.5 m – 1.0 m diameter**, **5 m – 15 m deep**) filled with boulders.
- Used where surface space is limited or vadose zone is thick.
- Essentially a "miniature recharge pit" of vertical geometry.

**Type 4 — Recharge Pit with Recharge Trench (Hybrid):**
- Combines a pit with a horizontal trench extending laterally.
- Increases the contact area with the aquifer for higher recharge rates.

### 2.3 Site Selection Criteria

| Criterion | Requirement |
|---|---|
| Aquifer Type | Unconfined / semi-confined, high specific yield |
| Depth to Water Table | **> 4 m – 5 m** (post-monsoon) |
| Soil Strata | Sandy / gravelly / lateritic (high K) preferred |
| Land Slope | Gentle (< 5%); avoid steep or flood-prone zones |
| Catchment Type | Impervious (rooftop, paved area) preferred |
| Distance from Well | **Minimum 10 m – 15 m** (to avoid contamination) |
| Distance from Septic Tank | **Minimum 15 m – 30 m** (per CGWB norms) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are the "must-know" set for solving any KTU exam problem on recharge pit sizing.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | Rational formula for peak runoff (ROR) | $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $I$ in $\text{m}/\text{s}$, $A$ in $\text{m}^2$ |
| 2 | $V_{\text{runoff}} = C \cdot P \cdot A$ | Annual (or event) runoff volume | $V$ in $\text{m}^3$, $P$ in $\text{m}$, $A$ in $\text{m}^2$ |
| 3 | $V_{\text{recharge}} = \eta \cdot V_{\text{runoff}}$ | Effective recharge volume (η = recharge efficiency) | $V$ in $\text{m}^3$ |
| 4 | $V_{\text{pit}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{recharge}}}{N}$ | Volume per pit (N = number of pits) | $V$ in $\text{m}^3$ |
| 5 | $L = \dfrac{V_{\text{pit}}}{B \cdot D \cdot n}$ | Pit length (with B = width, D = depth, n = porosity) | $L$ in $\text{m}$ |
| 6 | $t_{\text{empty}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{pit}}}{K \cdot A_{\text{base}}}$ | Time to drain pit through bottom | $t$ in $\text{s}$, $K$ in $\text{m}/\text{s}$ |
| 7 | $Q_{\text{Darcy}} = K \cdot i \cdot A$ | Darcy's Law for flow through porous media | $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 8 | $K_{\text{combined}} = \dfrac{\sum K_i \cdot d_i}{\sum d_i}$ | Effective vertical K of layered strata | $K$ in $\text{m}/\text{s}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In the rational formula, $I$ (rainfall intensity) is used for peak flow design, while $P$ (rainfall depth) is used for seasonal/annual volume design. **Do not interchange them** — this is a common valuation pitfall.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Recharge pits are deployed extensively in:
- **Urban rooftop harvesting schemes** (e.g., CGWB model projects in Delhi, Chennai, Bengaluru)
- **Industrial complexes** to comply with mandatory recharge norms (e.g., 100 m² recharge area per hectare built-up)
- **Coastal aquifers** to form a hydraulic barrier against saltwater intrusion
- **Drought-prone rural watersheds** under MGNREGA / Jal Jeevan Mission
- **Institutional campuses** (KTU campuses, IITs, NITs) to meet green-building and sustainability metrics

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Sizing a Recharge Pit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the exact problem type KTU examiners set in ESE Part B (14 marks).** Practice the entire numerical flow.

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**

A residential building has a **rooftop catchment area of 500 m²** in a region receiving an **annual rainfall of 1200 mm** with a **runoff coefficient of 0.85**. It is proposed to construct a **rectangular recharge pit** of depth **3.0 m** and width **2.0 m**, filled with a graded filter media of **effective porosity 0.35**. The **recharge efficiency** is **80%**, and the **hydraulic conductivity of the bottom strata** is **5 × 10⁻⁵ m/s**.

**Determine:**
**(a)** The annual volume of runoff available and the effective volume available for recharge.
**(b)** The required length of the recharge pit, and the time taken to empty the pit.

---

### 3.2 Part (a) — Runoff Volume & Effective Recharge

**Step 1 — Identify the given data and convert to SI units:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 500 \text{ m}^2 \\
P &= 1200 \text{ mm} = 1.200 \text{ m} \\
C &= 0.85 \quad \text{(dimensionless)} \\
\eta &= 0.80 \quad \text{(recharge efficiency, dimensionless)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total annual runoff volume using the rational volume formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{runoff}} &= C \cdot P \cdot A \\
&= 0.85 \times 1.200 \text{ m} \times 500 \text{ m}^2 \\
&= 510.0 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Out of 1.200 m of rainfall, 85% becomes runoff = 1.020 m equivalent depth of runoff over the rooftop. Total volume is the product of this depth and the catchment area.

**Step 3 — Compute the effective volume available for recharge:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{recharge}} &= \eta \cdot V_{\text{runoff}} \\
&= 0.80 \times 510.0 \text{ m}^3 \\
&= 408.0 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** 80% of the runoff is considered practically rechargable. The remaining 20% is lost to evaporation, overflow, and inefficiencies in the conveyance system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer for Part (a):**
> - Annual runoff volume = **510.0 m³**
> - Effective recharge volume = **408.0 m³**

---

### 3.3 Part (b) — Pit Length & Emptying Time

**Step 1 — Identify the additional data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 3.0 \text{ m} \quad \text{(pit depth)} \\
B &= 2.0 \text{ m} \quad \text{(pit width)} \\
n &= 0.35 \quad \text{(filter media porosity)} \\
K &= 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Decide design assumption:**

Assume the entire effective recharge volume of 408.0 m³ is to be infiltrated in a single annual cycle through **one** recharge pit. This is a conservative design assumption; in practice, multiple pits or a single pit operated over multiple filling cycles could be used.

$$
N = 1 \text{ (number of pits)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the pit volume (accounting for filter porosity):**

The geometric volume of the filter media filled inside the pit is $V_{\text{geom}} = L \cdot B \cdot D$. However, the **water storage capacity** of this volume is only $n \cdot V_{\text{geom}}$ (because solids occupy $(1-n)$ fraction of space).

**Equate water storage capacity to effective recharge volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{recharge}} &= n \cdot V_{\text{geom}} = n \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D \\
L &= \dfrac{V_{\text{recharge}}}{n \cdot B \cdot D}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \dfrac{408.0}{0.35 \times 2.0 \times 3.0} \\
&= \dfrac{408.0}{2.10} \\
&= 194.286 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Round up to a practical value:**

$$
L \approx 195 \text{ m} \quad \text{(rounded up for safety)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A single pit of length 195 m is impractical. **The KTU-accepted real-world interpretation** is to construct **multiple pits in parallel** (e.g., 4 pits of ~50 m each) or to operate the single pit over **multiple filling cycles** per year. In exam solutions, state this caveat explicitly to fetch full marks.

**Step 6 — Compute the bottom percolation area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{base}} &= L \cdot B \\
&= 195 \text{ m} \times 2.0 \text{ m} \\
&= 390 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the emptying time using the bottom percolation formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{empty}} &= \dfrac{V_{\text{pit}}}{K \cdot A_{\text{base}}} \\
&= \dfrac{408.0 \text{ m}^3}{(5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}) \times 390 \text{ m}^2} \\
&= \dfrac{408.0}{0.0195} \text{ s} \\
&= 20923.08 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to hours:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{empty}} &= \dfrac{20923.08}{3600} \\
&= 5.812 \text{ h} \\
&\approx 5 \text{ h } 49 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer for Part (b):**
> - Required pit length = **≈ 195 m** (or split into 4 × 50 m parallel pits)
> - Time to empty the pit = **≈ 5.81 hours (5 h 49 min)**

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Recharge Pit vs. Other Methods

| Parameter | Recharge Pit | Recharge Trench | Recharge Well | Recharge Basin |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Land Area Required** | Moderate | Moderate-Large | Very Small | Very Large |
| **Depth of Influence** | Shallow (≤ 5 m) | Shallow (≤ 3 m) | Deep (any depth) | Surface only |
| **Construction Cost** | Low | Low | High | Moderate |
| **Clogging Risk** | Moderate | Moderate | High (well screen) | High (silt) |
| **Suitable Aquifer** | Unconfined, shallow | Unconfined, shallow | Any, including confined | Unconfined, deep WT |
| **Maintenance** | Easy (top layer replace) | Easy | Difficult (drilling) | Moderate (de-silting) |
| **Best For** | Urban rooftops | Linear catchment | Space-constrained sites | Open countryside |

---

### 3.5 Hydraulics of Infiltration — Darcy's Law Application

The downward flow through the bottom of the pit follows **Darcy's Law** for vertical unsaturated flow:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= K \cdot i \cdot A_{\text{base}} \\
\text{where } i &= \dfrac{h + D}{D} \quad \text{(total head loss gradient)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For purely gravity-driven vertical flow, the gradient $i \approx 1$ (since suction head $h \ll D$). Thus:

$$
Q \approx K \cdot A_{\text{base}}
$$

This is why **hydraulic conductivity of the bottom strata is the single most critical parameter** controlling pit performance.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**"Define a recharge pit. List any four conditions under which it is the most suitable artificial recharge method."**

**Model Answer:**

A recharge pit is a shallow, surface excavation (typically **2 m to 5 m** deep) backfilled with graded filter media such as boulders, gravels, and sand, designed to receive surface runoff and allow its vertical percolation into the subsurface aquifer.

**Suitable Conditions (any four):**
1. The **post-monsoon water table is more than 4 m – 5 m** below ground level (sufficient vadose zone).
2. The **aquifer is unconfined or semi-confined** with moderate to high specific yield.
3. **Soil strata are sandy, gravelly, or lateritic** with hydraulic conductivity $\geq 10^{-5}$ m/s.
4. A **small impervious catchment** (rooftop, paved courtyard) is available within a limited footprint.
5. **Land area is restricted**, and horizontal methods (basins/trenches) are not feasible.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**"Differentiate between a dry recharge pit and a recharge shaft (bore pit). Mention two advantages of each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Dry Recharge Pit | Recharge Shaft (Bore Pit) |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Wide, shallow (rectangular/square) | Narrow, deep (circular) |
| Typical Dimensions | 1 m – 3 m wide, 2 m – 5 m deep | 0.5 m – 1.0 m diameter, 5 m – 15 m deep |
| Construction | Open excavation | Drilled/bored shaft |
| Aquifer Depth | Shallow (< 10 m) | Moderate to deep (10 m – 30 m) |
| Land Footprint | Larger | Compact |

**Advantages of Recharge Pit:** (i) Easy to construct with manual labour; (ii) Simple maintenance by replacing the top filter layer.

**Advantages of Recharge Shaft:** (i) Suitable where surface space is severely constrained; (ii) Can reach deeper, more transmissive aquifers that pits cannot.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **constructional features of a recharge pit**, including the typical filter media gradation from top to bottom. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rooftop of area **800 m²** in a residential building receives an **average annual rainfall of 900 mm**. The runoff coefficient is **0.80** and the proposed recharge efficiency is **75%**. Design the dimensions of a rectangular recharge pit of depth **2.5 m** and width **2.0 m**, filled with filter media of porosity **0.32**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

A recharge pit is constructed through the following sequential steps:

1. **Excavation:** A rectangular pit is dug to the design depth (**2 m – 5 m**) maintaining side slopes of **1:1 to 1.5:1 (V:H)** to prevent collapse. The pit bottom is levelled and lightly compacted.

2. **Geotextile Lining (Optional):** In cohesionless sandy soils, a non-woven geotextile is placed on the side walls to prevent migration of fines into the filter media, while still permitting water passage.

3. **Filter Media Placement (Reverse Gradation — Coarse to Fine from Bottom to Top):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Layer 1 (Bottom):} & \quad \text{Boulders } 75 \text{ to } 150 \text{ mm, thickness } \approx 1.0 \text{ to } 1.5 \text{ m} \\
\text{Layer 2:} & \quad \text{Coarse gravel } 20 \text{ to } 40 \text{ mm, thickness } \approx 0.6 \text{ to } 0.9 \text{ m} \\
\text{Layer 3:} & \quad \text{Medium gravel } 5 \text{ to } 20 \text{ mm, thickness } \approx 0.3 \text{ to } 0.5 \text{ m} \\
\text{Layer 4 (Top):} & \quad \text{Coarse sand } 1 \text{ to } 5 \text{ mm, thickness } \approx 0.2 \text{ to } 0.3 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This **reverse gradation** ensures that the finer media are protected from being washed into the coarser layers below, thereby maintaining sustained permeability and preventing clogging of the bottom interface.

4. **Inlet Arrangement:** A screened inlet pipe (mesh size 2 mm – 5 mm) delivers water from the rooftop gutter into the top of the pit. A **first-flush diverter** upstream divertes the first 5 mm – 10 mm of rainfall (which contains the highest pollutant load).

5. **Overflow Provision:** An overflow weir is provided at the pit rim to safely discharge excess inflow to a storm drain, preventing backflow into the catchment.

6. **Inspection Chamber:** A 0.6 m × 0.6 m concrete or masonry chamber with a removable cover is built over the pit for periodic inspection and top-layer replacement.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 800 \text{ m}^2, \quad P = 900 \text{ mm} = 0.900 \text{ m}, \quad C = 0.80 \\
\eta &= 0.75, \quad D = 2.5 \text{ m}, \quad B = 2.0 \text{ m}, \quad n = 0.32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Annual Runoff Volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{runoff}} &= C \cdot P \cdot A \\
&= 0.80 \times 0.900 \times 800 \\
&= 576.0 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective Recharge Volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{recharge}} &= \eta \cdot V_{\text{runoff}} \\
&= 0.75 \times 576.0 \\
&= 432.0 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Required Length of Pit (using $L = \dfrac{V_{\text{recharge}}}{n \cdot B \cdot D}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \dfrac{432.0}{0.32 \times 2.0 \times 2.5} \\
&= \dfrac{432.0}{1.60} \\
&= 270.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical Realisation:**

A single 270 m long pit is impractical. The recommended design is to construct **3 pits in parallel**, each of length:

$$
L_{\text{each}} = \dfrac{270}{3} = 90 \text{ m}
$$

> **Final Design:** Three rectangular recharge pits, each **90 m × 2.0 m × 2.5 m**, operating in parallel.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting the design and performance** of a recharge pit. Explain the role of the **desilting chamber** and **first-flush diverter** with sketches. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A recharge pit of size **5 m × 3 m × 3 m (L × B × D)** is filled with a filter of effective porosity **0.40**. The bottom strata has hydraulic conductivity **$K = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s**. Calculate **(i)** the water storage capacity of the pit, and **(ii)** the time required for the pit to empty completely through bottom percolation (assume unit hydraulic gradient). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Factors Affecting Design and Performance:**

1. **Catchment Characteristics:** Type (rooftop, paved, open ground), area, and slope determine the volume of runoff.
2. **Rainfall Pattern:** Intensity, duration, and seasonal distribution govern peak inflow rates and the number of filling cycles per year.
3. **Hydrogeology:** Depth to water table, aquifer type (unconfined/confined), specific yield, and transmissivity control the rate of recharge.
4. **Soil Permeability:** Hydraulic conductivity ($K$) of the bottom strata is the single most important parameter governing percolation rate. $K < 10^{-6}$ m/s renders the pit ineffective.
5. **Filter Media Gradation:** Improper gradation causes clogging, siltation, and reduced recharge efficiency.
6. **Water Quality:** High turbidity, oil/grease, or chemical contamination from catchment leads to choking of the filter and contamination of the aquifer.
7. **Maintenance Frequency:** Without periodic top-layer replacement, performance degrades by 40% – 60% within 2 – 3 years.

**Desilting Chamber:**

A desilting chamber is a **small settling tank** (typically 1 m × 1 m × 1 m) constructed upstream of the recharge pit. Its functions are:
- Reduces inflow velocity, allowing suspended solids to settle by gravity.
- Prevents rapid clogging of the filter media.
- Sized using **Stokes' Law** for the design particle size (usually 0.05 mm – 0.1 mm).

**First-Flush Diverter (FFD):**

A first-flush diverter is a **simple mechanical device** that automatically diverts the first **2 mm – 10 mm** of rainfall runoff (which carries the highest concentration of dust, bird droppings, leaf litter, and atmospheric pollutants) away from the recharge system. It typically uses:
- A **ball-float** that seals the inlet once the chamber is full.
- A **manual or automatic** drain valve to release the captured first-flush water to a non-potable outlet.

This protects the pit filter from severe fouling and safeguards the aquifer from contamination.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 5 \text{ m}, \quad B = 3 \text{ m}, \quad D = 3 \text{ m}, \quad n = 0.40 \\
K &= 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}, \quad i = 1.0 \quad \text{(unit gradient, gravity flow)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Water Storage Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{geom}} &= L \times B \times D = 5 \times 3 \times 3 = 45.0 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{\text{storage}} &= n \times V_{\text{geom}} = 0.40 \times 45.0 = 18.0 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Time to Empty the Pit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{base}} &= L \times B = 5 \times 3 = 15.0 \text{ m}^2 \\
Q_{\text{out}} &= K \cdot i \cdot A_{\text{base}} = (2 \times 10^{-4}) \times 1.0 \times 15.0 = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
t_{\text{empty}} &= \dfrac{V_{\text{storage}}}{Q_{\text{out}}} = \dfrac{18.0}{3.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 6000 \text{ s} \\
t_{\text{empty}} &= \dfrac{6000}{3600} = 1.667 \text{ h} \quad \text{(i.e., } 1 \text{ h } 40 \text{ min)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answers:**
> (i) Storage capacity = **18.0 m³**
> (ii) Emptying time = **1.667 hours (1 h 40 min)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by porosity (n) in storage calculations** — students often report geometric volume as storage capacity, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up the rational formula** — using $P$ (rainfall depth) for $I$ (intensity) or vice versa. Peak flow design uses intensity; volume design uses depth.
> 3. **Ignoring the recharge efficiency factor (η)** — directly equating runoff volume to recharge volume costs 1 – 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting unit conversion** — rainfall given in mm must be converted to m (divide by 1000) before multiplying with catchment in m².
> 5. **Not interpreting the practical design** — a calculated pit length of 195 m or 270 m is academically correct but practically absurd. Examiners reward students who suggest **splitting into multiple pits** or **multi-cycle operation**.
> 6. **Hydraulic gradient assumption** — for vertical gravity drainage, $i = 1.0$. If you incorrectly use $i = (D + h)/D$ with $h \neq 0$, you lose the conceptual mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Recharge Pit (KTU 2024 Module 3):**

- A **recharge pit** is a shallow excavation (2 m – 5 m deep) refilled with graded boulders, gravel, and sand to recharge shallow unconfined aquifers.
- Best suited when the **post-monsoon water table is more than 4 m – 5 m deep** and the **unsaturated zone is permeable** ($K \geq 10^{-5}$ m/s).
- **Filter gradation is reverse** — coarse boulders at the bottom, fine sand at the top — to prevent clogging and protect finer layers.
- The **annual runoff volume** is $V_{\text{runoff}} = C \cdot P \cdot A$; the **effective recharge volume** is $V_{\text{recharge}} = \eta \cdot V_{\text{runoff}}$.
- The **pit length** is computed from $L = \dfrac{V_{\text{recharge}}}{n \cdot B \cdot D}$, where $n$ is the effective porosity of the filter media.
- The **emptying time** is $t_{\text{empty}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{storage}}}{K \cdot A_{\text{base}}}$, and is ideally **24 – 72 hours** to allow re-oxygenation.
- The pit should be **at least 10 m – 15 m away from any drinking water well** and **15 m – 30 m from any septic tank/sewage line**.
- A **desilting chamber** and **first-flush diverter** are mandatory pre-treatment units to extend the functional life of the filter.
- A **recharge shaft (bore pit)** is preferred when land is limited or the desired recharge depth is greater than what a conventional pit can achieve.
- Hydraulically, recharge pit performance is governed by **Darcy's Law** with $i \approx 1$ for vertical gravity drainage.
- A calculated single-pit length that is impractically large should be reported with a **practical design note** (multiple parallel pits or multi-cycle operation) to secure full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recharge Through Abandoned Hand Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recharge Through Abandoned Hand Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Recharge through abandoned hand pump** is a *decentralized, low-cost artificial groundwater recharge technique* in which a non-functional or abandoned India Mark-II / hand pump borehole is retrofitted to function as a vertical recharge shaft. The existing borehole — originally drilled to lift water upward using a reciprocating piston mechanism — is converted to allow surface water (rooftop runoff, harvested rainwater, or pond water) to flow *downward* into the shallow aquifer, thereby reversing the hydraulic gradient and augmenting the groundwater storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This technique falls under **"Methods of Artificial Recharge"** (Module 3) and is classified as a **gravity-based recharge structure** (as opposed to pumping-based recharge like injection wells). It is specifically recommended by the **Central Ground Water Board (CGWB)** and the **Ministry of Jal Shakti** for rural water-scarcity zones.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine a **battered old drinking straw** stuck in a soft drink glass. Originally, you sucked the drink *up* through the straw. Now the straw is broken and the glass is empty. 

Instead of throwing the straw away, you pour fresh drink *into* the top of the straw. The liquid travels down the same path it once climbed, refilling the glass from below.

**The abandoned hand pump works exactly like this:**

- The **borehole** = the straw (a pre-existing vertical conduit into the ground)
- The **aquifer** = the glass (the underground water storage)
- The **hand pump mechanism** = removed or bypassed
- The **rainwater/rooftop runoff** = the fresh drink being poured in

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics to Remember for KTU Exams:**
> - **Typical borehole depth:** $6$ m to $15$ m (deeper in hard rock)
> - **Diameter of bore:** $100$ mm to $150$ mm
> - **Standard type:** India Mark-II / Mark-III hand pump (commonly installed under Jal Jeevan Mission)
> - **Recharge capacity:** Approximately $0.5$ m³/hr to $1.5$ m³/hr (depending on aquifer permeability)
> - **Cost:** **₹ 1,500 – ₹ 4,000** per structure (lowest among all artificial recharge methods)

---

### Why This Method Exists (The Problem It Solves)

In thousands of Indian villages, hand pumps were installed in the 1980s-2000s at depths of $8$ m–$12$ m. Over the decades, due to:

1. **Over-extraction** of groundwater
2. **Droughts** and reduced recharge
3. **Geological hard-rock aquifers** (low storage capacity)

…the water table dropped below the suction lift ($7.5$ m for hand pumps). The pumps became *abandoned* — structurally sound but functionally dead. Rather than drill new, expensive borewells ($>$ ₹ 50,000), engineers cleverly **repurpose** these dead pumps as **recharge injection points**.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy Shortcut for Exam:** "Old pipe that once *drank* from the earth, now *feeds* it."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Concept — Broken Down Step-by-Step

The recharge system is engineered through a precise, layered construction. Each layer serves a specific hydrological function.

---

#### **Component 1: Recharge Well (The Hand Pump Casing)**

The original **GI (Galvanized Iron) pipe casing** of the hand pump is retained. This casing:

- Prevents the borehole walls from collapsing
- Provides a clean, smooth vertical conduit
- Extends from the surface to the bottom of the borehole

**Why retained?** Drilling a new borehole costs **₹ 30,000 – ₹ 60,000**, while reusing the existing casing reduces the cost to under **₹ 4,000** — a **$90$% cost reduction**.

---

#### **Component 2: Removal of Pump Assembly**

The complete pump assembly (piston, cylinder, handle, connecting rod) is extracted. A **sleeve/cap arrangement** is fitted at the top to:

- Allow inflow of recharge water
- Prevent entry of debris, animals, and mosquitoes
- Enable periodic inspection and cleaning

> [!NOTE]
> The cylinder assembly, if left inside, becomes a barrier to downward flow. **It MUST be removed.**

---

#### **Component 3: Recharge Water Collection Pit**

A small **desilting cum collection pit** (typically $1$ m $\times$ $1$ m $\times$ $1$ m) is constructed adjacent to the hand pump. This pit:

- Receives rooftop rainwater through a gutter/downpipe
- Allows silt and suspended solids to settle
- Connects to the hand pump casing via a **perforated PVC pipe** or a **funnel arrangement**

---

#### **Component 4: Filter Media Stack (Inside the Borehole)**

This is the **most critical engineering component** of the entire system. After the pump cylinder is removed, the open borehole is filled with a graded filter media from bottom to top:

| Layer (from bottom to top) | Material | Particle Size | Thickness | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Layer 5 (top) | Coarse sand | $2$ mm – $0.5$ mm | $0.3$ m | Final filtration |
| Layer 4 | Fine gravel | $5$ mm – $2$ mm | $0.3$ m | Intermediate filtration |
| Layer 3 | Pebbles | $20$ mm – $5$ mm | $0.3$ m | Reduces flow velocity |
| Layer 2 | Bricks / broken stone | $40$ mm – $20$ mm | $0.3$ m | Structural support |
| Layer 1 (bottom) | Boulders / cobbles | $100$ mm – $40$ mm | $0.5$ m | Prevents filter washout |

**Total filter media height** inside bore: $1.7$ m – $2.0$ m

---

#### **Component 5: The Hydraulic Flow Logic**

Water follows this pathway:

$$
\text{Rooftop} \rightarrow \text{Gutter} \rightarrow \text{Desilting Pit} \rightarrow \text{Inlet Pipe} \rightarrow \text{Hand Pump Casing} \rightarrow \text{Filter Media} \rightarrow \text{Aquifer}
$$

The **hydraulic gradient** is reversed:

$$
i_{recharge} = \frac{H_{surface} - H_{groundwater}}{L} \;\; (\text{positive downward})
$$

where $H_{surface}$ is the water level in the desilting pit, $H_{groundwater}$ is the static water table, and $L$ is the depth of the water column.

---

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear almost every year in KTU ESE / Module tests.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = K \cdot A \cdot i$ | **Darcy's Law** — discharge through filter media | m³/s |
| 2 | $i = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L}$ | Hydraulic gradient | dimensionless |
| 3 | $V = K \cdot i$ | Darcy velocity (discharge velocity) | m/s |
| 4 | $v = \dfrac{V}{n}$ | Actual seepage velocity (where $n$ = porosity) | m/s |
| 5 | $t = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{Q}$ | Recharge time for a given roof-collected volume | seconds (s) |
| 6 | $V_{tank} = P \cdot A_{roof} \cdot C$ | Volume of rainfall harvested from rooftop | m³ |
| 7 | $A_{catch} = \dfrac{V_t}{P \cdot C}$ | Catchment area required for target volume | m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Glossary (use these in KTU answers):**
> - $K$ = Hydraulic conductivity of filter media ($\approx 10^{-3}$ m/s for coarse sand)
> - $A$ = Cross-sectional area of bore (m²)
> - $i$ = Hydraulic gradient (dimensionless)
> - $P$ = Precipitation / rainfall depth (m)
> - $C$ = Runoff coefficient ($0.8$ for RCC roof, $0.6$ for tiled roof)
> - $n$ = Effective porosity of filter media ($\approx 0.30$ for sand)

---

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Sector | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Rural water supply** | Recharge of shallow aquifers serving hand pumps in villages |
| **Disaster management** | Quick recharge in drought-affected districts (e.g., Marathwada, Bundelkhand) |
| **Urban retrofitting** | Colony-level recharge in semi-urban layouts with existing boreholes |
| **Government schemes** | Implemented under **Mission Bhagirathi**, **Jal Jeevan Mission**, and **Atal Bhujal Yojana** |
| **Climate adaptation** | Building resilience against monsoon variability in hard-rock terrain |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's favourite line in answers:** *"This method is particularly suited to regions with deep water tables, low-percolation soils, and pre-existing hand pump infrastructure, offering a sustainable, low-cost, and community-managed groundwater augmentation solution."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Estimating Annual Recharge Volume from a Single Abandoned Hand Pump

**Given Data (KTU Standard Problem Format):**

- Rooftop catchment area: $A_{roof} = 50$ m²
- Annual rainfall: $P = 1.0$ m ($1000$ mm)
- Runoff coefficient: $C = 0.8$ (RCC roof)
- Filter media hydraulic conductivity: $K = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s
- Bore diameter: $d = 0.10$ m
- Filter media length: $L = 2.0$ m
- Porosity of filter media: $n = 0.30$
- Average hydraulic head: $\Delta h = 1.5$ m (water level in pit above aquifer)

---

#### **Step 1: Annual Volume of Harvested Water Available**

$$
V_{available} = P \cdot A_{roof} \cdot C
$$

$$
V_{available} = 1.0 \;\text{m} \times 50 \;\text{m}^2 \times 0.8
$$

$$
V_{available} = 40 \;\text{m}^3/\text{year}
$$

**Interpretation:** A $50$ m² roof in a region receiving $1$ m of rainfall yields $40$ m³ of recharge water annually — enough to refill approximately **160 drinking-water barrels** ($250$ L each).

---

#### **Step 2: Cross-Sectional Area of the Bore**

$$
A_{bore} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2
$$

$$
A_{bore} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.10)^2
$$

$$
A_{bore} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2
$$

---

#### **Step 3: Hydraulic Gradient Across the Filter**

$$
i = \frac{\Delta h}{L}
$$

$$
i = \frac{1.5}{2.0}
$$

$$
i = 0.75
$$

---

#### **Step 4: Apply Darcy's Law to Find Instantaneous Recharge Discharge**

$$
Q = K \cdot A_{bore} \cdot i
$$

$$
Q = 5 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m/s} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2 \times 0.75
$$

$$
Q = 2.945 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

---

#### **Step 5: Convert to Litres per Hour (for KTU board-style final answer)**

$$
Q = 2.945 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s} \times 10^3 \;\text{L/m}^3 \times 3600 \;\text{s/hr}
$$

$$
Q = 10.6 \;\text{L/hr}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This figure is the *infiltration capacity* of the structure**, i.e., the maximum rate at which the filter media can pass water. If the roof delivers more than $10.6$ L/hr, water will overflow the pit — a critical design check.

---

#### **Step 6: Time Required to Recharge the Full 40 m³**

$$
t = \frac{V_{available}}{Q}
$$

$$
t = \frac{40 \;\text{m}^3}{2.945 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

$$
t = 1.358 \times 10^7 \;\text{s}
$$

Convert to hours:

$$
t = \frac{1.358 \times 10^7}{3600} \;\text{hrs} \approx 3772 \;\text{hrs}
$$

Convert to monsoon-season days (assume $90$ rainy days, $24$-hr availability not realistic → use $6$ effective hours/day):

$$
t_{effective} = \frac{3772}{6} \approx 629 \;\text{days}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight:** The recharge does *not* happen in a single monsoon — it spreads across multiple seasons. KTU exam answers should always state: *"The 40 m³ is recharged cumulatively over the monsoon and post-monsoon period at the rate limited by filter media conductivity."*

---

### Derivation 2: Reverse Calculation — Catchment Area Required for a Target Recharge

**Problem:** A village panchayat wants to recharge $V_t = 50$ m³/year using abandoned hand pumps. Rainfall is $P = 0.8$ m and runoff coefficient is $C = 0.75$. Find the minimum roof catchment area.

---

#### **Step 1: Starting Equation**

$$
V_t = P \cdot A_{roof} \cdot C
$$

#### **Step 2: Rearrange for $A_{roof}$**

$$
A_{roof} = \frac{V_t}{P \cdot C}
$$

#### **Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$
A_{roof} = \frac{50}{0.8 \times 0.75}
$$

$$
A_{roof} = \frac{50}{0.60}
$$

$$
A_{roof} = 83.33 \;\text{m}^2
$$

#### **Step 4: Engineering Rounding & Practical Note**

A roof area of approximately **$84$ m²** is needed. In a typical $100$ m² village house, this is feasible — confirming the method is **practically scalable at household level**.

---

### Analytical Matrix — When to Use This Method vs. Others

| Parameter | Abandoned Hand Pump | Recharge Pit | Recharge Trench | Injection Well |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Very Low | Low | Moderate | Very High |
| **Depth** | $6$–$15$ m | $2$–$5$ m | $3$–$6$ m | $30$–$200$ m |
| **Aquifer type** | Shallow phreatic | Shallow phreatic | Shallow phreatic | Deep confined |
| **Skill needed** | Low | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Maintenance** | Annual cleaning | Quarterly | Quarterly | Continuous |
| **Best suited for** | Abandoned boreholes | Open spaces | Linear roadside spaces | Urban high-rises |
| **Recharge volume** | $0.5$–$1.5$ m³/hr | $1$–$3$ m³/hr | $2$–$5$ m³/hr | $5$–$50$ m³/hr |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU comparative questions**, remember: *"Abandoned hand pump recharge is preferred when (a) deep water table exists, (b) boreholes are pre-existing, (c) budget is constrained, and (d) community ownership model is feasible."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Cross-Section of a Recharge-Converted Abandoned Hand Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[RAINWATER FROM ROOFTOP] --> B[GUTTER AND DOWNPIPE]
    B --> C[DESILTING CUM COLLECTION PIT 1m x 1m x 1m]
    C --> D[PERFORATED INLET PIPE 50mm DIA]
    D --> E[GI CASING FORMER HAND PUMP PIPE 100mm DIA]
    E --> F[GRADED FILTER MEDIA STACK INSIDE BOREHOLE]
    F --> G[COARSE SAND LAYER 0.3m]
    F --> H[FINE GRAVEL LAYER 0.3m]
    F --> I[PEBBLES LAYER 0.3m]
    F --> J[BRICKS BROKEN STONE LAYER 0.3m]
    F --> K[BOULDERS COBBLES LAYER 0.5m]
    K --> L[SHALLOW AQUIFER ZONE 6m TO 15m BELOW GL]
    L --> M[RECHARGE TO GROUNDWATER STORAGE]
    C --> N[OVERFLOW WEIR TO NEARBY DRAIN]
    E --> O[REMOVABLE TOP CAP FOR INSPECTION]
```

---

### Diagram 2: Functional Block Architecture — Hydraulic Flow of the System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT ZONE]
        A1[Rooftop Catchment]
        A2[Rainfall Intensity P]
    end
    INPUT --> B1[Conveyance: Gutter Pipe]
    B1 --> B2[Desilting Pit Treatment]
    B2 --> B3[Filter Media Stack]
    B3 --> C1[Aquifer Storage]
    C1 --> D1[Recharge Volume Q m3 per year]
    C1 --> D2[Recovery via Neighbouring Wells]
    style INPUT fill:#e0f7fa
    style B1 fill:#fff9c4
    style B2 fill:#fff9c4
    style B3 fill:#fff9c4
    style C1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style D1 fill:#a5d6a7
    style D2 fill:#a5d6a7
```

---

### Diagram 3: Construction Sequence (Step-by-Step Visual Roadmap)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[STEP 1: IDENTIFY ABANDONED HAND PUMP] --> S2[STEP 2: REMOVE PUMP ASSEMBLY PISTON CYLINDER HANDLE]
    S2 --> S3[STEP 3: INSPECT BOREHOLE FOR STABILITY]
    S3 --> S4[STEP 4: CLEAN CASING WITH WATER FLUSH]
    S4 --> S5[STEP 5: INSTALL FILTER MEDIA BOTTOM TO TOP BOULDERS PEBBLES GRAVEL SAND]
    S5 --> S6[STEP 6: CONSTRUCT DESILTING PIT ADJACENT TO BORE]
    S6 --> S7[STEP 7: CONNECT PIT TO BORE VIA INLET PIPE]
    S7 --> S8[STEP 8: FIT REMOVABLE CAP ON BORE TOP]
    S8 --> S9[STEP 9: CONNECT GUTTER FROM ROOF TO PIT]
    S9 --> S10[STEP 10: TEST FLOW AND MONITOR FOR 1 MONTH]
    style S1 fill:#ffcdd2
    style S5 fill:#c8e6c9
    style S10 fill:#bbdefb
```

---

### Diagram 4: Failure Mode & Maintenance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P{Recharge Rate Dropped?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Inspect Top Cap for Blockage]
    P -->|No| R[System Operating Normally]
    Q --> S[Remove Cap and Check Inlet]
    S --> T[Filter Media Clogged?]
    T -->|Yes| U[Flush with Pressurized Water or Replace Media]
    T -->|No| V[Check Desilting Pit for Silt Accumulation]
    V --> W[Desilt the Pit Every Quarter]
    W --> X[Re-test and Resume Operation]
    U --> X
    R --> Y[Annual Inspection Recommended]
    style P fill:#fff9c4
    style U fill:#ffcdd2
    style X fill:#c8e6c9
    style Y fill:#bbdefb
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define artificial recharge of groundwater. List any three methods, with one line on "recharge through abandoned hand pump."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Artificial recharge is the engineered process of *deliberately augmenting* the natural infiltration of surface water into underground aquifers to restore depleted groundwater storage.

**Three methods (1.5 marks):**
1. Recharge pits
2. Recharge trenches / soak pits
3. Injection wells / tube wells

**Abandoned hand pump (0.5 mark):** It is a method where a defunct hand pump borehole is converted into a vertical recharge shaft by removing the pump assembly and filling the casing with graded filter media to allow rooftop runoff to enter the shallow aquifer.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Why is the pump assembly removed before using a borehole for recharge?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The cylinder and piston of a hand pump are designed to **resist downward flow** (they lift water up). If left in place, they act as a **flow barrier**, reducing recharge efficiency by $60$–$80$%. **(1 mark)**

2. The metallic components **corrode rapidly** in stagnant recharge water, releasing iron oxides that **clog the filter media** and the aquifer pores, reducing transmissivity. **(1 mark)**

3. The removed assembly allows the borehole to function as an **open vertical conduit**, with full diameter available for the graded filter media stack and unobstructed flow into the aquifer. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the concept, site suitability, and step-by-step construction procedure of "recharge through abandoned hand pump" with a neat sketch. (7 marks)**

**(b) A $60$ m² rooftop in a region receiving $900$ mm annual rainfall is to be used to recharge a shallow aquifer through an abandoned hand pump. Runoff coefficient is $0.8$, filter hydraulic conductivity is $4 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s, bore diameter is $100$ mm, filter length is $1.5$ m, and head difference is $1.0$ m. Calculate: (i) Annual recharge volume, (ii) Instantaneous discharge through the filter using Darcy's law, and (iii) Comment on whether the filter can handle the inflow. (7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution to A(a) — Concept, Suitability, and Construction (7 marks)**

**Concept (2 marks):**
Recharge through abandoned hand pump is a **gravity-based artificial recharge technique** that converts a non-functional hand pump borehole into a recharge shaft. Rooftop rainwater is routed through a desilting pit into the bore, where graded filter media removes fine particulates before the water enters the shallow aquifer.

**Site Suitability (2 marks):**
- Abandoned or dry hand pumps with bore depth $6$–$15$ m
- Shallow aquifers in **alluvial, lateritic, or weathered hard-rock** formations
- Areas with **declining water tables** below the suction limit ($7.5$ m)
- Locations with **impervious rooftops** (RCC, asbestos, tiled) to maximize runoff

**Construction Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Identify abandoned hand pump and verify borehole integrity
2. Remove the complete pump assembly (handle, rod, cylinder, piston)
3. Clean the GI casing using water jetting
4. Fill the bore with graded filter media (boulders → pebbles → gravel → sand) from bottom to top
5. Construct a desilting cum collection pit ($1 \times 1 \times 1$ m) adjacent to the bore
6. Connect pit to bore via a $50$ mm perforated PVC inlet pipe
7. Fit a removable MS cap on top of the bore for inspection
8. Connect rooftop gutter/downpipe to the desilting pit
9. Test the system during the first monsoon and monitor weekly

---

##### **Solution to A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $A_{roof} = 60$ m²
- $P = 900$ mm $= 0.9$ m
- $C = 0.8$
- $K = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s
- $d = 100$ mm $= 0.10$ m
- $L = 1.5$ m
- $\Delta h = 1.0$ m

**(i) Annual Recharge Volume (2 marks):**

$$
V = P \cdot A_{roof} \cdot C
$$

$$
V = 0.9 \times 60 \times 0.8
$$

$$
V = 43.2 \;\text{m}^3/\text{year}
$$

**(ii) Instantaneous Discharge via Darcy's Law (3 marks):**

$$
A_{bore} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2
$$

$$
i = \frac{\Delta h}{L} = \frac{1.0}{1.5} = 0.667
$$

$$
Q = K \cdot A \cdot i
$$

$$
Q = 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.667
$$

$$
Q = 2.095 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
Q = 7.54 \;\text{L/hr}
$$

**(iii) Comment (2 marks):**

The instantaneous recharge capacity of the filter is $7.54$ L/hr. Assuming an effective rainfall duration of $6$ hrs/day during the monsoon, the filter can recharge:

$$
V_{day} = 7.54 \times 6 = 45.25 \;\text{L/day}
$$

Over $90$ monsoon days: $V_{monsoon} = 45.25 \times 90 = 4072$ L $= 4.07$ m³ — which is **far less** than the available $43.2$ m³/year.

> **Conclusion:** The filter media is the **bottleneck**. To recharge the full volume, either **(a)** the filter length should be increased, **(b)** coarser media should be used, or **(c)** multiple hand pumps in the same catchment should be retrofitted. A practical design recommendation is to limit the rooftop area to **$8$–$10$ m² per hand pump** in such soils.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Discuss the advantages, limitations, and design considerations of recharge through abandoned hand pumps. Compare it with recharge pits and injection wells. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the role of filter media in recharge boreholes. With reference to a 5-layer filter media stack, justify the particle size selection and thickness for each layer. (7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution to B(a) — Comparison and Design (7 marks)**

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Lowest cost** recharge method (₹ 1,500–₹ 4,000 per structure)
2. **No new drilling** — leverages existing borehole infrastructure
3. **Easy to construct** — semi-skilled labour sufficient
4. **Community ownership** — manageable at household/ward level
5. **Reaches deeper aquifers** (up to $15$ m) than surface recharge methods

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Low recharge volume** ($0.5$–$1.5$ m³/hr) due to small bore diameter
2. **Clogging risk** — filter media requires annual replacement
3. **Cannot be used in fissured/rocky terrain** without pre-casing
4. **Limited catchment** — only suitable for rooftop runoff, not storm water

**Design Considerations (1.5 marks):**
- Aquifer permeability must be verified (transmissivity $> 10$ m²/day)
- Water table must be at least $1$ m below filter stack
- Desilting pit must be cleaned quarterly
- Top cap must be mosquito-proof and tamper-proof

**Comparison Table (1.5 marks):**

| Feature | Abandoned Hand Pump | Recharge Pit | Injection Well |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | ₹ 1,500–₹ 4,000 | ₹ 3,000–₹ 8,000 | ₹ 50,000–₹ 2,00,000 |
| Depth | $6$–$15$ m | $2$–$5$ m | $30$–$200$ m |
| Recharge rate | Low | Medium | High |
| Maintenance skill | Low | Low | High |

---

##### **Solution to B(b) — Filter Media Justification (7 marks)**

**Role of Filter Media (2 marks):**
The filter media in a recharge borehole serves **three critical functions:**
1. **Prevents silt and clay particles** from entering the aquifer and clogging pore spaces
2. **Reduces flow velocity** to prevent scouring of the borehole walls
3. **Provides biological treatment** through the schmutzdecke layer that develops on the sand surface

**5-Layer Filter Media Stack (5 marks):**

| Layer | Material | Size | Thickness | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (bottom) | Boulders / cobbles | $100$ mm – $40$ mm | $0.5$ m | Provides a **stable base** at the bore bottom; prevents finer layers from washing down into the aquifer; allows high vertical flow |
| 2 | Bricks / broken stone | $40$ mm – $20$ mm | $0.3$ m | Acts as **transitional support**; further breaks flow turbulence; the angular edges trap coarse debris |
| 3 | Pebbles | $20$ mm – $5$ mm | $0.3$ m | **Energy dissipator** — converts turbulent inflow into laminar flow, protecting the finer layers above |
| 4 | Fine gravel | $5$ mm – $2$ mm | $0.3$ m | **Intermediate filtration** — captures medium silt and organic matter that escapes the upper layers during high flow |
| 5 (top) | Coarse sand | $2$ mm – $0.5$ mm | $0.3$ m | **Final polishing filter** — removes fine suspended solids; the **schmutzdecke** (biological film) on top of this layer provides pathogen removal |

> **Design Principle:** The grading follows the **filter rule** — the $D_{15}$ of a coarser layer must be at least $4$ times the $D_{15}$ of the finer layer below it. This prevents the finer media from migrating into the coarser layer.

> **Total filter media height** $= 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.3 = 1.7$ m, fitted inside a standard $1.8$–$2.0$ m section of the borehole above the aquifer entry point.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Marks are deducted when students write "$Q = 2 \times 10^{-6}$" without converting to **L/hr** or **L/day** for a practical answer.
> 
> 2. **Writing $i = \Delta h / L$ without explaining the sign convention:** KTU expects "downward gradient" or "positive value" explicitly mentioned.
> 
> 3. **Skipping the desilting pit in diagrams:** Examiners specifically look for the pit in cross-section sketches. A bare borehole with arrows loses $1$–$2$ marks.
> 
> 4. **Confusing "recharge through hand pump" with "injection well":** They are NOT the same. Hand pump recharge is **gravity-based, low-cost, rural, shallow**; injection wells are **pumping-based, expensive, urban, deep**.
> 
> 5. **Not mentioning maintenance:** Answers without "annual filter media replacement" or "quarterly desilting" are considered incomplete.
> 
> 6. **Using $|$ in tables:** Mathematical absolute value notation using vertical pipes **breaks the markdown table** in KTU's online evaluation portal. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\text{mod}(x)$ instead.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Read this 5 times before the exam.**

- [ ] **Definition:** A low-cost artificial recharge method that converts defunct hand pump boreholes into vertical recharge shafts.
- [ ] **Typical bore depth:** $6$ m – $15$ m
- [ ] **Bore diameter:** $100$ mm – $150$ mm
- [ ] **Cost:** ₹ 1,500 – ₹ 4,000 (lowest among recharge methods)
- [ ] **Recharge rate:** $0.5$ – $1.5$ m³/hr
- [ ] **Filter media stack:** 5 layers, total height $1.7$ m, **boulders at bottom, sand at top**
- [ ] **The pump assembly MUST be removed** before recharge use
- [ ] **Desilting pit dimensions:** $1 \times 1 \times 1$ m, cleaned every quarter
- [ ] **Darcy's Law:** $Q = K \cdot A \cdot i$ — must be memorised in symbol form
- [ ] **Hydraulic gradient:** $i = \Delta h / L$ (always positive for downward flow)
- [ ] **Runoff coefficient:** $C = 0.8$ for RCC roof, $0.6$ for tiled roof
- [ ] **Volume formula:** $V = P \cdot A_{roof} \cdot C$
- [ ] **Filter rule:** $D_{15}(\text{coarse}) \geq 4 \times D_{15}(\text{fine})$
- [ ] **Schmutzdecke layer** forms on the topmost sand — provides biological purification
- [ ] **Schemes using this method:** Mission Bhagirathi, Atal Bhujal Yojana, Jal Jeevan Mission
- [ ] **Best suited for:** Hard-rock terrain with deep water tables and existing hand pump infrastructure
- [ ] **NOT suited for:** Heavy storm-water recharge, deep confined aquifers (use injection wells instead)
- [ ] **Annual maintenance:** Replace top $0.3$ m of sand; flush gravel layers
- [ ] **Watch the units:** mm vs m, s vs hr, m³ vs L — conversions are mandatory
- [ ] **Always draw:** Rooftop → Gutter → Desilting Pit → Filter → Aquifer in any diagram
- [ ] **Key advantage for KTU answer:** "Cost-effective, community-managed, retrofits existing infrastructure"
- [ ] **Key limitation for KTU answer:** "Low recharge rate; frequent maintenance; small catchment handling"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recharge Through Abandoned Dug Well/Open Well
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Recharge Through Abandoned Dug Well/Open Well # MODULE 3 — RECHARGE THROUGH ABANDONED DUG WELL / OPEN WELL ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Artificial Recharge through Abandoned Dug Well / Open Well** is a direct, point-source groundwater recharge technique in which a defunct, dry, or under-utilised existing dug well (or open well) is retrofitted with a filtration assembly, desilting chamber, and conveyance pipe so that harvested rainwater (from rooftop or surface runoff) is delivered into the well shaft and allowed to percolate vertically through the existing bottom filter media and side wall openings directly into the underlying **water-table aquifer**. An "abandoned well" in the KTU context is defined as any dug well that has been decommissioned by the owner or the local panchayat/municipality due to one or more of the following reasons: - **Drastic decline in yield** (discharge reduced below **1.0 m³/hr** for a domestic well). - **Deterioration of water quality** (excess fluoride, nitrate, salinity, bacteriological contamination). - **Dewatering of the well** (water table dropped below well bottom due to over-pumping in the locality). - **Structural unserviceability** (lining damaged, siltation > **50%** of well depth). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Kitchen Funnel & the Sponge:** > Imagine your kitchen sink has a sponge sitting in a bucket beneath the drain-hole. The sponge is the **aquifer**, the drain-hole is the **well opening**, and the funnel is the **recharge pipe**. When you pour water through the funnel, instead of letting it spill over the counter (runoff), you push it through the existing hole into the dry sponge so the sponge re-soaks from within. An abandoned dug well works exactly like that pre-existing drain-hole — you simply redirect rooftop runoff through it, the way nature once used it. The well shaft, lined in masonry, becomes a **vertical recharge shaft**, and the bottom gravel pack and side openings become the **percolation interface** with the aquifer. The key intuition for the KTU student is that this method *re-uses* an existing civil asset (the well) rather than drilling a new recharge bore. It is the **most cost-effective** artificial recharge structure in rural and peri-urban Kerala because the well already penetrates the phreatic (unconfined) aquifer. ### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Board Numerical Values) - **Typical diameter of dug well:** **1.5 m to 3.0 m** - **Typical depth:** **5 m to 15 m** (below ground level) - **Recommended recharge rate per well:** **0.5 m³/hr to 5.0 m³/hr** (depends on aquifer permeability) - **Minimum water table depth below well bottom for recharge viability:** **≥ 0.5 m** - **Specific yield (Sy) of laterite/sandy aquifers in Kerala:** **0.08 to 0.20** (dimensionless) - **Filter media sand specification:** Effective size (d₁₀) = **0.20 mm to 0.35 mm**, Uniformity Coefficient (Cu) ≤ **3.0** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Why an Abandoned Well Works as a Recharge Shaft A dug well, when originally constructed, was sunk to a depth that intersected the **phreatic surface (water table)** of the local aquifer. Over the years, if the water table declines, the well goes dry. However, the well's **bottom and side walls are still in direct hydraulic contact with the aquifer matrix** through: - The **unlined bottom portion** (if any). - The **weep-holes** or porous sections of the masonry lining. - The **gravel pack / boulder pack** at the well base. When rainwater is introduced into the well shaft: 1. It first settles in the **desilting chamber** (removing suspended silt and organic load). 2. It passes through a **filter media bed** (sand + gravel) that traps fine particles. 3. It enters the **well shaft**, where the standing water column creates a positive head differential. 4. It **percolates downward and laterally** through the gravel pack and into the aquifer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Principle:** The recharge is driven by the **hydraulic gradient (i)** between the water level inside the well and the natural water table outside. The well acts as a **hydraulic potentiometric surface** higher than the surrounding aquifer, forcing infiltration. This is governed by **Darcy's Law** in the vertical direction. ### 2.2 Site Selection Criteria (KTU Board Favourite) | S.No. | Criterion | Recommended Value | |------|-----------|-------------------| | 1 | Water table depth below well bottom | ≥ **0.5 m** (preferably 1–3 m) | | 2 | Aquifer transmissivity (T) | ≥ **50 m²/day** | | 3 | Specific yield (Sy) of aquifer | ≥ **0.08** | | 4 | Roof catchment area | ≥ **50 m²** (for sustainable yield) | | 5 | Distance from sanitary sources | ≥ **15 m** (septic tank), ≥ **30 m** (sewer line) | | 6 | Soil type overlying aquifer | Sandy loam, laterite with high permeability | | 7 | Slope of ground | Gentle slope preferred for gravity flow | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The following table contains the **complete formula set** that a KTU 2024 examiner expects for any numerical on abandoned dug well recharge. Master these for the 14-mark Part B questions. | # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use | |---|---------|-------------------------------| | 1 | $Q = A \times v$ | Basic recharge rate; A = cross-section of well bottom (m²), v = percolation velocity (m/hr) | | 2 | $Q = K \times i \times A$ | **Darcy's Law** (vertical) for flow through aquifer; K = hydraulic conductivity (m/day), i = hydraulic gradient | | 3 | $v = K \times i$ | Darcy velocity (flux) used for vertical infiltration | | 4 | $i = \dfrac{h_{well} - h_{gw}}{L}$ | Hydraulic gradient between well water level and natural water table; L = vertical distance | | 5 | $A_{well} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times D^2$ | Cross-sectional area of circular dug well; D = diameter of well (m) | | 6 | $V_{runoff} = C \times I \times A_{roof}$ | **Rational Method** for runoff available from rooftop; C = runoff coefficient (**0.80–0.90** for RCC roof), I = rainfall intensity (m/hr), A_roof = roof area (m²) | | 7 | $V_{tank} = \dfrac{Q_{runoff} \times t}{1000}$ | Volume stored in recharge well for time t (m³) | | 8 | $Q_{recharge} = S_y \times A_{influence} \times \dfrac{\Delta h}{\Delta t}$ | Groundwater recharge using **Specific Yield method**; Sy = specific yield, Δh = rise in water table, A_influence = area of influence (m²) | | 9 | $A_{influence} = \pi \times R^2$ | Circular area of influence around recharge well; R = radius of influence (m) | | 10 | $R = \sqrt{\dfrac{Q \times t}{\pi \times S_y \times \Delta h}}$ | Radius of influence (mound-spread) equation | | 11 | $H_{filter} = \dfrac{q \times L_{media}}{K_{media}}$ | Head loss across filter media (m); q = discharge per unit area, L = media thickness, K = permeability | | 12 | $q = \dfrac{Q}{A_{filter}}$ | Filtration rate (m³/m²/day) — should be ≤ **5 m³/m²/day** for sand filters | | 13 | $D_{gravel} = 2 \text{ to } 5 \times D_{sand}$ | Standard design rule — gravel pack size relative to filter sand | | 14 | $S_{spacing} = 2R$ | Recommended minimum spacing between two recharge wells to avoid interference | | 15 | $Q_{filter} = K_{media} \times \dfrac{H}{L} \times A$ | Filter bed flow capacity — used to size the filter chamber | | 16 | $V_{desilt} = 0.25 \times V_{runoff}$ | Design volume of desilting chamber (typically 25% of seasonal runoff) | > **Units Convention for KTU Board:** > - Discharge Q → **m³/hr** or **litres per minute (LPM)** > - Hydraulic conductivity K → **m/day** > - Specific yield Sy → **dimensionless** > - Hydraulic gradient i → **dimensionless (m/m)** ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility This method is extensively used in: - **Kerala State Groundwater Department's** "Jala Samrudhi" projects. - **Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme (MGNREGS)** recharge structures in Alappuzha, Thrissur, Palakkad districts. - **Smart Village** rooftop harvesting pilots. - **Industrial campuses** where defunct industrial borewells are converted to recharge wells — saving ₹1.5–2.5 lakh per new borehole. The technique directly supports the **Central Ground Water Board (CGWB)** guideline of "recharge at point of use", reducing evaporation losses, transmission losses, and energy cost of pumping. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Recharge Rate Through a Circular Dug Well (Vertical Darcy Flow) **Statement:** Determine the vertical recharge rate through the bottom of a circular abandoned dug well of diameter **D** when the water column inside the well stands at height **h_w** above the well bottom, and the natural groundwater table lies at a depth **h_g** below the well bottom. **Given:** D = 2.0 m, h_w = 2.5 m (water column above well bottom), h_g = 1.5 m (water table below well bottom), K = 4 m/day (hydraulic conductivity of aquifer), L = thickness of aquifer layer accepting recharge = 3.0 m. **Solution:** The driving head for vertical percolation is the difference between the water level inside the well and the natural water table. $$ \Delta h = h_{well-bottom} + h_w -
