(h_{well-bottom} - h_g) = h_w + h_g $$ Substituting the numerical values: $$ \Delta h = 2.5 + 1.5 = 4.0 \text{ m} $$ The hydraulic gradient (i) is the head loss per unit vertical flow length: $$ i = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L} = \dfrac{4.0}{3.0} = 1.333 \text{ (dimensionless, m/m)} $$ The cross-sectional area of the well bottom through which vertical percolation occurs is: $$ A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (2.0)^2 = \pi \text{ m}^2 \approx 3.1416 \text{ m}^2 $$ Applying Darcy's Law for vertical downward flow through the aquifer matrix beneath the well: $$ Q = K \times i \times A $$ $$ Q = 4.0 \times 1.333 \times 3.1416 $$ $$ Q = 16.755 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \approx \textbf{16.76 m}^3\textbf{/day} $$ Converting to litres per hour for practical utility: $$ Q = 16.755 \times \dfrac{1000}{24} = 698.1 \text{ L/hr} \approx \textbf{700 LPH} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** A 2 m diameter abandoned well in a moderately permeable sandy aquifer (K = 4 m/day) can receive about **16,755 litres of rooftop runoff per day** as groundwater recharge — equivalent to replenishing domestic water supply of a small household for ~10 days from a single heavy monsoon shower. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Runoff Volume Available from a Rooftop Catchment **Statement:** A residential building in Thrissur has a rooftop area of **120 m²**. The annual rainfall is **3000 mm**, with a runoff coefficient of **0.85** (terrace tile surface). Compute the total volume of runoff available for recharge, and check if it can fill an abandoned dug well of diameter 2.0 m and depth 8.0 m (assumed empty). **Given:** A_roof = 120 m², I_annual = 3000 mm = 3.0 m, C = 0.85 **Solution:** Annual runoff volume from the rooftop: $$ V_{runoff} = C \times I \times A_{roof} $$ $$ V_{runoff} = 0.85 \times 3.0 \times 120 = 306 \text{ m}^3 $$ The storage capacity of the abandoned dug well (cylindrical shaft): $$ V_{well} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \times H $$ $$ V_{well} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (2.0)^2 \times 8.0 = 8\pi \approx 25.13 \text{ m}^3 $$ Comparing the available runoff with the well's storage capacity: $$ \dfrac{V_{runoff}}{V_{well}} = \dfrac{306}{25.13} \approx 12.18 $$ This means the annual runoff is **12.18 times** the well's storage capacity, so the well will fill completely **more than 12 times** in the monsoon season. Since excess water will overflow through a bye-pass arrangement, the system is well-designed and the well is the bottleneck, not the catchment. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Insight (KTU Board Favourite):** When V_runoff ≫ V_well, an **overflow / bypass pipe** must be provided at the top of the well to safely discharge excess water into a secondary recharge trench or storm drain. This prevents the well from overflowing onto the surroundings and causing mosquito breeding / waterlogging. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Filter Media Head Loss Calculation **Statement:** A recharge filter bed is designed with a sand layer of effective size d₁₀ = 0.25 mm and thickness L = 0.6 m. The hydraulic conductivity of the filter sand is K = 1.5 m/day. If the filtration rate is q = 4 m³/m²/day, calculate the head loss across the filter. **Given:** q = 4 m³/m²/day, L = 0.6 m, K = 1.5 m/day **Solution:** The hydraulic gradient across the filter bed is given by: $$ i_{filter} = \dfrac{q}{K} = \dfrac{4.0}{1.5} = 2.667 \text{ m/m} $$ The head loss across the filter layer using Darcy's Law: $$ H_{filter} = i_{filter} \times L $$ $$ H_{filter} = 2.667 \times 0.6 = 1.6 \text{ m} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse the filter hydraulic conductivity K (small, ~1–5 m/day) with the aquifer K (large, ~5–50 m/day). The filter is the **controlling element** for head loss, not the aquifer. If H_filter is excessive (>2 m), the gravel support layer must be coarser to reduce resistance. ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Radius of Influence and Well Spacing **Statement:** An abandoned well is being recharged at Q = 16.76 m³/day in an aquifer with specific yield Sy = 0.12. If the water table mound rises by Δh = 0.5 m in 30 days, compute the radius of influence (R) and recommend the minimum spacing to an adjacent recharge well. **Given:** Q = 16.76 m³/day, Sy = 0.12, Δh = 0.5 m, t = 30 days **Solution:** Using the standard empirical formula for radius of influence (cone of impression method): $$ R = \sqrt{\dfrac{Q \times t}{\pi \times S_y \times \Delta h}} $$ $$ R = \sqrt{\dfrac{16.76 \times 30}{\pi \times 0.12 \times 0.5}} $$ $$ R = \sqrt{\dfrac{502.8}{0.1885}} = \sqrt{2667.4} \approx 51.6 \text{ m} $$ Therefore, the recommended minimum spacing between two adjacent recharge wells to avoid hydraulic interference (no double-counting of recharge benefit): $$ S_{spacing} = 2R = 2 \times 51.6 = \textbf{103.2 m} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Field Practice:** CGWB guidelines recommend a **practical spacing of 100–150 m** between recharge structures in laterite aquifers of Kerala. The derived value (103.2 m) aligns well with this standard. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Scoring of Recharge Structures | Parameter | Abandoned Dug Well | Recharge Pit | Recharge Trench | Injection Well | |-----------|-------------------|--------------|-----------------|----------------| | Capital cost | **Very Low** | Low | Medium | **High** | | Recharge rate | 0.5–5 m³/hr | 1–3 m³/hr | 0.5–2 m³/hr | 5–20 m³/hr | | Maintenance ease | **Easy** (manual) | Easy | Moderate | Difficult | | Aesthetic | Acceptable (existing) | Moderate | Moderate | Hidden | | Land requirement | **Zero** (existing well) | 4–6 m² | 6–12 m² | 1–2 m² | | Skill for construction | Mason | Mason | Mason + Earthwork | Driller | | KTU CGWB weightage | **High** | Medium | Medium | Low (residential) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Cross-Sectional Block Architecture of an Abandoned Dug Well Recharge System The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete functional architecture** of the system — from rooftop to aquifer — including all critical components and flow direction. This is the schematic that KTU examiners expect in 14-mark answers (7 marks for the diagram itself). ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Rooftop Catchment
RCC Tiled Slab
120 sqm] B[Rainwater
Collection
Gutter] C[First Flush
Diverter
Manual Ball Valve] D[Desilting
Chamber
Concrete Tank
Volume 0.5 cum] E[Filtration
Unit
Sand plus Gravel
Layers] F[Recharge
Shaft
Abandoned Dug Well
D 2m H 8m] G[Bypass
Overflow
Pipe to Storm Drain] H[Filter Media
Sand Bed
d10 0.25mm
Thickness 0.6m] I[Gravel Pack
at Well Bottom
Thickness 0.3m] J[Weep Holes
in Masonry
Lining] K[Water Table
Phreatic
Aquifer Zone] L[Vented Cap
with Mesh
for Mosquito Proof] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G E --> H F --> I F --> J I --> K J --> K F --> L ``` ### 4.2 Process Flow Logic (Sequential Engineering Stages) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Stage 1
Roof Wash
and Splash] --> S2[Stage 2
Gutter
Conveyance] S2 --> S3[Stage 3
First Flush
Removal] S3 --> S4[Stage 4
Desilting
and Settling] S4 --> S5[Stage 5
Filter Media
Polishing] S5 --> S6[Stage 6
Well Shaft
Storage and Percolation] S6 --> S7[Stage 7
Aquifer
Recharge] S7 --> S8[Stage 8
Water Table
Rise and Lateral Spread] ``` ### 4.3 Layered Filter Media Architecture (Top-to-Bottom Stratigraphy) ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Top Layer
Coarse Gravel
Size 20 to 40 mm
Thickness 100 mm] L2[Second Layer
Medium Gravel
Size 10 to 20 mm
Thickness 100 mm] L3[Third Layer
Fine Gravel
Size 5 to 10 mm
Thickness 100 mm] L4[Fourth Layer
Coarse Sand
Size 1 to 2 mm
Thickness 150 mm] L5[Bottom Layer
Filter Sand
d10 equals 0.25 mm
Thickness 600 mm] L6[Base
Well Bottom
Existing Gravel Pack] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L5 --> L6 ``` ### 4.4 Decision Tree — When to Use Abandoned Dug Well Recharge ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Is an Existing
Abandoned Well
Available on Site?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is Water Table
Below Well Bottom
by at least 0.5 m?} Q1 -- No --> ALT1[Consider Recharge
Pit or Trench
Alternative] Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Aquifer Hydraulic
Conductivity
Above 1 m per day?} Q2 -- No --> ALT2[Well is Still
Functional
Use as Pumping Well] Q3 -- Yes --> REC[Proceed with
Abandoned Well
Recharge Design] Q3 -- No --> ALT3[Recharge Rate
will be Low
Consider Bore Well] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define the term "abandoned dug well" in the context of artificial recharge. List any three reasons why a well may be classified as abandoned.** **Model Answer:** An **abandoned dug well** is a manually excavated, large-diameter well that has been decommissioned from active use due to either loss of yield, deteriorated water quality, or a substantial drop in the local water table below the well bottom, but which still retains its **hydraulic connection to the underlying phreatic aquifer** and is therefore suitable for retrofitting as a recharge structure. **Three reasons for abandonment:** 1. **Yield decline** — discharge has dropped below **1 m³/hr** due to falling water table. 2. **Water quality deterioration** — excess fluoride, nitrate, iron, or bacteriological contamination making water unfit for domestic use. 3. **Structural problems** — damaged masonry lining, heavy siltation filling more than **50% of the well depth**, or partial collapse making it unsafe to use. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only "well is dry" — this is **incomplete**. You must specify the **three engineering criteria** (yield, quality, structure) for a 3-mark definition. --- #### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Why is the "first flush diverter" an essential component of an abandoned well recharge system? What is its typical design volume?** **Model Answer:** The **first flush diverter** is essential because the initial 1–2 mm of rainfall washes off dust, bird droppings, leaves, atmospheric pollutants, and microbial contaminants from the rooftop catchment. If this polluted first wash is allowed to enter the recharge well, it will clog the filter media, contaminate the aquifer, and reduce recharge efficiency. The first flush diverter uses a **manual ball-valve or tipping-bucket mechanism** to **divert the first 25–50 litres per 100 m² of roof area** away from the system. For a typical residential roof of 100 m², the first flush volume is approximately **25–50 litres**, calculated as: $$ V_{first-flush} = A_{roof} \times 2.5 \text{ to } 5.0 \text{ mm} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students skip the numerical value. The KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates **1 mark** specifically for the unit (litres per 100 m²) — do not omit it. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question has internal choice — you may attempt **either** Option A **or** Option B. The structure is: **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.** --- #### **OPTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]` **Q: A residential building in Kottayam district has an abandoned dug well of diameter 1.8 m and depth 7.5 m. The rooftop catchment area is 95 m². The local aquifer has hydraulic conductivity K = 3.5 m/day and specific yield Sy = 0.10. The annual rainfall is 2900 mm and the runoff coefficient for the RCC terrace is 0.85.** **(a)** Compute the **annual runoff volume** from the rooftop and the **time** required to fill the abandoned well (assuming no losses and the well is empty). **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Estimate the **steady-state vertical recharge rate** through the well bottom if the standing water column inside the well is 2.0 m above the bottom and the natural water table is 1.2 m below the bottom. Also calculate the **radius of influence** after 30 days of continuous recharge. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Option A** #### **Part (a) — Runoff Volume and Fill Time:** **Step 1:** Annual rainfall depth in metres: $$ I = 2900 \text{ mm} = 2.9 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2:** Annual runoff volume using the Rational Method: $$ V_{runoff} = C \times I \times A_{roof} $$ $$ V_{runoff} = 0.85 \times 2.9 \times 95 $$ $$ V_{runoff} = 234.18 \text{ m}^3 \approx \textbf{234.2 m}^3 $$ **Step 3:** Storage capacity of the abandoned dug well (cylindrical): $$ V_{well} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \times H $$ $$ V_{well} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (1.8)^2 \times 7.5 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 3.24 \times 7.5 $$ $$ V_{well} = 19.10 \text{ m}^3 \approx \textbf{19.1 m}^3 $$ **Step 4:** Number of times the well can be filled per year (theoretical): $$ N = \dfrac{V_{runoff}}{V_{well}} = \dfrac{234.2}{19.1} \approx 12.26 $$ **Step 5:** If runoff is uniformly distributed over the **4-month monsoon (120 days)**, the daily average inflow is: $$ Q_{daily} = \dfrac{234.2}{120} = 1.952 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step 6:** Time to fill the empty well: $$ t = \dfrac{V_{well}}{Q_{daily}} = \dfrac{19.1}{1.952} \approx 9.78 \text{ days} $$ > **Answer (a):** Annual runoff = **234.2 m³**; Time to fill well ≈ **9.78 days** (≈ **10 days**) #### **Part (b) — Recharge Rate and Radius of Influence:** **Step 1:** Driving head (difference between well water level and natural water table): $$ \Delta h = h_w + h_g = 2.0 + 1.2 = 3.2 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2:** Hydraulic gradient (assume vertical percolation length equals the effective aquifer thickness, given L = 2.5 m for the receiving zone): $$ i = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L} = \dfrac{3.2}{2.5} = 1.28 $$ **Step 3:** Cross-sectional area of the well bottom: $$ A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (1.8)^2 = 2.545 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 4:** Apply Darcy's Law for vertical recharge: $$ Q = K \times i \times A $$ $$ Q = 3.5 \times 1.28 \times 2.545 $$ $$ Q = 11.40 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \approx \textbf{475 LPH} $$ **Step 5:** Radius of influence after 30 days (assuming Δh = 0.5 m water table rise): $$ R = \sqrt{\dfrac{Q \times t}{\pi \times S_y \times \Delta h}} $$ $$ R = \sqrt{\dfrac{11.40 \times 30}{\pi \times 0.10 \times 0.5}} $$ $$ R = \sqrt{\dfrac{342.0}{0.1571}} = \sqrt{2177.0} \approx \textbf{46.66 m} $$ > **Answer (b):** Steady-state recharge rate ≈ **11.4 m³/day (475 LPH)**; Radius of influence ≈ **46.66 m**; Recommended spacing to adjacent recharge structure = 2R ≈ **93.3 m** (round to 100 m for design margin). --- #### **OPTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Q: Discuss the components and construction methodology of an artificial recharge system using an abandoned dug well. With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the function of each component. Also discuss the maintenance practices and limitations of this method.** **(a)** Describe in detail the **site selection criteria, components, and construction methodology** of an abandoned dug well recharge system with a neat sketch. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **routine maintenance, common problems, design limitations, and advantages** of this recharge method. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Option B** #### **Part (a) — Site Selection, Components & Construction:** **Step 1 — Site Selection Criteria:** - Water table must be at least **0.5 m** below the well bottom. - Aquifer transmissivity ≥ **50 m²/day**. - Distance from contamination sources (septic tank ≥ **15 m**, sewer line ≥ **30 m**). - Soil cover above aquifer should be permeable (sandy loam, laterite). - Roof catchment area ≥ **50 m²** for sufficient runoff. **Step 2 — Components of the System (in flow order):** 1. **Rooftop catchment** — RCC slab or tiled roof (A = 95 m² in our example). 2. **Gutter and down-pipe** — GI/PVC pipes of 75–100 mm diameter. 3. **First flush diverter** — manual ball-valve type; diverts first 25–50 L/100 m². 4. **Desilting chamber** — brick or RCC tank, volume ≈ 25% of seasonal runoff; provides 30-minute retention for suspended solids to settle. 5. **Filter unit** — vertical RCC chamber containing graded media: coarse gravel (20–40 mm) on top, then 10–20 mm, 5–10 mm, coarse sand (1–2 mm), and filter sand (d₁₀ = 0.20–0.35 mm, Cu ≤ 3) at the bottom. 6. **Recharge well (abandoned dug well)** — circular shaft, 1.5–3.0 m diameter, 5–15 m deep; lined with brick/stone masonry up to the water table. 7. **Gravel pack at well bottom** — 20–40 mm clean boulders, 0.3 m thick. 8. **Weep-holes in masonry lining** — at every 0.5 m vertical interval, to allow lateral seepage. 9. **Overflow/bypass pipe** — at top of well, 100 mm GI pipe to storm drain. 10. **Vented, mosquito-proof cap** — to prevent insect breeding and surface contamination. **Step 3 — Construction Sequence:** 1. Inspect existing well for structural stability; repair masonry cracks. 2. Desilt the well down to the original bottom; remove accumulated debris. 3. Place a 300 mm thick boulder/gravel pack at the well bottom. 4. Construct the desilting chamber and filter unit adjacent to the well (gravity flow preferred). 5. Connect the rooftop down-pipe to the chamber via a first flush diverter. 6. Place graded filter media (coarse to fine) inside the filter unit. 7. Provide overflow pipe and vented cap. 8. Carry out a **test recharge** and measure drawdown recovery to confirm hydraulic continuity. > **Answer (a):** Site selection requires minimum 0.5 m unsaturated zone below the well bottom, permeable soil, and adequate runoff. The system has 10 sequential components with specific design dimensions as detailed above. #### **Part (b) — Maintenance, Problems, Limitations & Advantages:** **Step 1 — Routine Maintenance Schedule:** | Frequency | Activity | |-----------|----------| | **Before monsoon (May)** | Clean gutters, inspect first flush diverter, desilt the chamber | | **During monsoon** | Weekly check for overflow, mosquito breeding, clogging | | **Post-monsoon (October)** | Desilt the chamber, inspect filter media, replace top gravel layer if clogged | | **Annually** | Measure water table in observation well, photograph filter media, test water quality (TDS, nitrate, coliform) | **Step 2 — Common Problems & Remedies:** | Problem | Cause | Remedy | |---------|-------|--------| | Filter clogging | Silt overload | Add pre-filter mesh; replace top sand layer | | Slow recharge | Filter compaction | Loosen media; backwash with clean water | | Water quality deterioration | Septic tank seepage | Re-locate contamination source; add 50 mm charcoal layer | | Mosquito breeding | Stagnant water in chamber | Seal chamber; add biolarvicide; cover with mesh | | Well overflow | Inadequate overflow pipe | Increase bypass pipe diameter; add secondary recharge trench | **Step 3 — Design Limitations:** - Recharge rate is limited by the **existing well diameter** (cannot be enlarged cheaply). - **Infiltration rate reduces over time** as filter media gets clogged; periodic replacement needed. - Not suitable in **clayey aquifers** (K < 0.5 m/day). - **First cost savings are offset** by recurring maintenance cost (~₹2,000–5,000/year per well). - **Cannot recharge deep confined aquifers** — limited to phreatic zone only. **Step 4 — Advantages:** - **Zero land acquisition** cost (well already exists). - **Lowest capital investment** among all recharge structures. - **Re-uses existing civil asset** — sustainable engineering. - **Gravity-driven** — no pumping energy required. - **Aesthetically compatible** in residential areas. - **Improves nearby well yields** within the radius of influence (R ≈ 50–100 m). - **CGWB and KTU-recommended** for Kerala's laterite terrain. > **Answer (b):** Maintenance includes pre-monsoon cleaning, post-monsoon desilting, and annual water quality testing. Major limitations are filter clogging and dependence on well diameter, while the chief advantages are zero land cost and low capital outlay. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Zones:** > 1. **Unit errors in Q = K × i × A:** Many students omit unit conversion of K from m/day to m/hr. Always show the conversion. > 2. **Forgetting the bypass pipe:** In the diagram, the overflow/bypass pipe is **mandatory** for full marks. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks. > 3. **Confusing specific yield (Sy) with specific retention (Sr):** Sy is the drainable porosity; Sr is the water retained against gravity. The formula uses Sy, not Sr. > 4. **Using horizontal Darcy's law for vertical flow:** The equation is the same, but the gradient is computed **vertically** (head difference ÷ vertical flow length), not horizontally. > 5. **Not stating the runoff coefficient (C)** value used: For RCC roof, C = 0.80–0.90; for tiled roof, C = 0.70–0.80. The KTU board requires the assumed value to be **explicitly written**. > 6. **Mixing up d₁₀ and d₆₀:** The Effective Size is **d₁₀** (the sieve size through which 10% of sand passes), NOT d₆₀. Uniformity Coefficient Cu = d₆₀ / d₁₀. --- ### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Summary — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.** - ☐ **Abandoned well** = decommissioned dug/open well with intact aquifer contact, retrofitted for recharge. - ☐ **Standard dimensions:** Diameter 1.5–3.0 m, depth 5–15 m. - ☐ **Recharge rate per well:** 0.5–5.0 m³/hr in typical Kerala laterite. - ☐ **Core equation:** $Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$ (Darcy's Law — vertical form). - ☐ **Runoff equation:** $V_{runoff} = C \cdot I \cdot A_{roof}$, with C = 0.85 for RCC roof. - ☐ **Well storage:** $V_{well} = (\pi/4) \cdot D^2 \cdot H$. - ☐ **Filter sand spec:** d₁₀ = 0.20–0.35 mm, Cu ≤ 3.0. - ☐ **Filtration rate limit:** q ≤ **5 m³/m²/day** for sand filters. - ☐ **First flush volume:** 25–50 L per 100 m² of roof. - ☐ **Desilting chamber volume:** ~25% of seasonal runoff. - ☐ **Specific yield (Sy)** of Kerala laterite: 0.08–0.20. - ☐ **Radius of influence:** $R = \sqrt{(Q \cdot t) / (\pi \cdot S_y \cdot \Delta h)}$. - ☐ **Spacing between two recharge wells:** ≥ 2R (typically 100–150 m). - ☐ **Site distances:** ≥ 15 m from septic tank, ≥ 30 m from sewer. - ☐ **Mandatory components (in order):** Roof → Gutter → First-flush diverter → Desilting chamber → Filter unit → Recharge well → Bypass overflow. - ☐ **Filter media stratigraphy (top → bottom):** Coarse gravel → Medium gravel → Fine gravel → Coarse sand → Filter sand → Gravel pack. - ☐ **Key advantages:** Zero land cost, low capital, re-uses existing asset, gravity-driven, KTU-recommended. - ☐ **Key limitations:** Limited by existing well diameter, filter clogging, not for deep confined aquifers, clay-soil aquifers unsuitable. - ☐ **Standard CGWB spacing norm:** **100–150 m** between recharge structures in laterite terrain. - ☐ **Darcy velocity equation (vertical):** $v = K \cdot i$ — must be in same units (e.g., m/day). - ☐ **Practical runoff coefficient values:** RCC roof 0.80–0.90, Tiled roof 0.70–0.80, Thatched roof 0.50–0.60.

topics.title: Through Recharge Trench
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Through Recharge Trench

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Recharge Trench** (also called an **Infiltration Trench** or **Soak Trench**) is a linear, shallow, and narrow excavation of specified length, width, and depth, typically backfilled with coarse porous media such as **boulders, gravels, pebbles, and coarse sand** (often wrapped in a geotextile filter). Its primary function is to **intercept, store, convey, and infiltrate surface runoff** into the underlying unsaturated/semi-confined aquifer zone for groundwater recharge, simultaneously providing **flood mitigation** and **non-point source pollution control**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A recharge trench is a structural artificial recharge technique that stores runoff in a porous reservoir and discharges it vertically downward through gravitational and capillary forces to augment the groundwater table. It is classified under **"Direct Artificial Recharge Methods"** in Module 3.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** Recharge trenches are the most cost-effective RWH (Rainwater Harvesting) structure for urban areas where space is constrained and borewell recharge shafts are impractical. They are explicitly mentioned in the **Central Ground Water Board (CGWB)** and **CPHEEO manual** as preferred recharge structures for colonies, roads, parks, and institutional campuses.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are watering a **dried-out kitchen sponge** lying flat on a table. If you pour water **directly on top** of the sponge, most of it rolls off. But if you **bury a long, narrow channel** in the sponge and fill the channel with gravel, then pour water into the channel — the sponge will **soak up water from below much more efficiently** because the water now has a *long contact area* and *percolation time* to seep in.

The **recharge trench** is exactly that — a "gravel-filled sponge channel" buried into the ground. It:
1. **Catches** the rain that falls on the catchment (roof, road, open ground).
2. **Stores** it temporarily in the pore spaces of the gravel.
3. **Releases** it slowly downward so the **aquifer** (natural underground water layer) refills.

This is a **passive**, **gravity-driven**, **zero-energy** structure — meaning it needs no pump, no electricity, and almost no maintenance.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics and Design Ranges

| Parameter | Typical Range | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **Depth (D)** | **0.5 m to 1.5 m** | Deeper trenches reach water table faster |
| **Width (W)** | **0.5 m to 1.0 m** | Wider trenches store more volume |
| **Length (L)** | **5 m to 30 m+** | Multiple trenches can be parallel |
| **Longitudinal Slope** | **1 : 100 to 1 : 200** | Ensures gravity flow without scouring |
| **Porosity of media** | **0.25 to 0.40** | Effective storage = 30% of trench volume |
| **Boulder size (bottom)** | **50 mm to 150 mm** | Primary load-bearing layer |
| **Gravel size (middle)** | **20 mm to 40 mm** | Transition layer |
| **Sand size (top)** | **2 mm to 6 mm** | Final filtering layer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the trench filled with stones, not left hollow?** A hollow trench would collapse under soil pressure. The stones give **structural stability** while their **voids** (empty spaces) hold water temporarily — like a built-in underground reservoir.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — The "Why" and "How"

A recharge trench operates on **three coupled hydraulic processes** that work simultaneously:

**Process 1 — Surface Interception (Collection Phase)**
The trench is positioned **perpendicular to the direction of surface runoff**. A shallow feeder channel or graded surface directs stormwater into the trench inlet. Why perpendicular? Because runoff follows the steepest path, and a perpendicular cut intercepts the maximum flow.

**Process 2 — Temporary Sub-Surface Storage (Detention Phase)**
Runoff entering the trench percolates through layered filter media and gets **temporarily held in the voids** between boulders, gravel, and sand. The **effective storage volume (Vₛ)** is governed by the **porosity (n)** of the fill material. This stored water acts as a **buffer**, releasing water slowly — preventing sudden aquifer shock-loading and improving infiltration efficiency.

**Process 3 — Vertical Infiltration to Aquifer (Recharge Phase)**
Water held in voids moves **downward under gravity** through the unsaturated soil column, following **Darcy's Law**. The rate of infiltration is governed by the **hydraulic conductivity (K)** of the underlying native soil. A clayey soil below the trench will give a slow trickle; sandy soil gives a much faster recharge.

### 2.2 Structural Components (Sectional Anatomy)

A standard recharge trench has **6 distinct layers**, each serving a specific function:

1. **Top Cover / Vegetation Layer** — Prevents silt entry, provides aesthetics
2. **Top Sand Filter (300 mm)** — Removes suspended silt and fine organics
3. **Middle Gravel Layer (300 mm)** — Distributes flow uniformly, prevents clogging
4. **Boulder Layer (300–500 mm)** — Provides primary storage volume and structural strength
5. **Geotextile Membrane** — Wraps the trench sides, prevents soil migration into the trench
6. **Inlet Spillway / Inspection Chamber** — Allows controlled entry and periodic maintenance

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table — these formulas carry **direct 14-mark problem-solving potential** in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_t = L \times W \times D$ | Geometric (gross) volume of trench | m³ |
| 2 | $V_s = V_t \times n$ | Effective storage volume (n = porosity) | m³ |
| 3 | $Q = A \times K$ | Darcy's volumetric flow rate (A = cross-section, K = hydraulic conductivity) | m³/s |
| 4 | $v = K \times i$ | Darcy's discharge velocity (i = hydraulic gradient) | m/s |
| 5 | $t_e = \dfrac{V_s}{K \times A_b}$ | Time to empty the trench (Ab = bottom area) | seconds / hours |
| 6 | $A_{req} = \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{i}$ | Required trench cross-section for peak flow | m² |
| 7 | $L_{req} = \dfrac{A_{req}}{W}$ | Required trench length for a fixed width | m |
| 8 | $H = \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{L \times K_{media}}$ | Maximum expected head in the trench | m |
| 9 | $f = \dfrac{C \times I \times A_c}{n \times A_t}$ | Frequency of overflow (rough check) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Q_{peak} = 0.278 \times C \times I \times A_c$ | Rational method peak runoff (used to size the trench inlet) | m³/s |

> **Note on the vertical pipe symbol:** In KTU board exam scripts, you may safely use the actual `|` character in your hand-written answer booklet. The `\vert` and `\mid` restriction applies **only to the markdown tables above** to preserve rendering integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Recharge trenches are used in the following KTU-relevant practical scenarios:
- **Urban road networks** — Recharging sub-base water that would otherwise flood the road.
- **Colony stormwater drains** — Intercepts runoff before it enters municipal drains.
- **Institutional campuses (KTU colleges included)** — Mandatory under **Kerala State Building Rules** for plots > 0.20 hectare.
- **Highway medians and shoulders** — CGWB and MoRTH recommend them in highway drainage design.
- **Agricultural fields** — Recharging shallow aquifers in semi-arid zones.
- **Industrial parks** — Combined with **first-flush diverters**, they recharge while removing oil and grit.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Storage Volume of the Trench

The trench is a rectangular prism backfilled with porous media. We need to compute **how much water the trench can hold** between two successive rain events.

**Step 1:** Define the **gross geometric volume (Vₜ)** of the trench as the product of its length, width, and depth.

$$V_t = L \times W \times D$$

**Step 2:** Since the trench is not solid (it is filled with stones/gravel/sand), only the **void spaces** between particles can hold water. The fraction of void space is called **porosity (n)**. Typical porosity values are:

$$n_{boulder} \approx 0.40, \quad n_{gravel} \approx 0.35, \quad n_{sand} \approx 0.30, \quad n_{clay} \approx 0.45$$

**Step 3:** Multiply gross volume by the effective porosity to get **effective storage volume (Vₛ)**.

$$V_s = V_t \times n$$

**Step 4:** In a multi-layered trench (e.g., 0.3 m sand + 0.3 m gravel + 0.4 m boulder), the **weighted average porosity** is used:

$$n_{eff} = \dfrac{\sum (n_i \times d_i)}{\sum d_i}$$

where $n_i$ and $d_i$ are the porosity and depth of the *i*-th layer.

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):**
A trench is 10 m long, 1 m wide, 1 m deep. The fill consists of:
- Top 0.3 m: Sand (n = 0.30)
- Middle 0.3 m: Gravel (n = 0.35)
- Bottom 0.4 m: Boulder (n = 0.40)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_t &= 10 \times 1 \times 1 = 10 \, \text{m}^3 \\
n_{eff} &= \dfrac{(0.30)(0.3) + (0.35)(0.3) + (0.40)(0.4)}{0.3 + 0.3 + 0.4} \\
n_{eff} &= \dfrac{0.09 + 0.105 + 0.16}{1.0} \\
n_{eff} &= \dfrac{0.355}{1.0} = 0.355 \\
V_s &= 10 \times 0.355 = 3.55 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

The trench can **store 3.55 m³ (3,550 litres)** of stormwater between two rain events.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time to Empty the Trench (tₑ)

The "drawdown time" is critical because if the trench is still full when the next storm arrives, it will overflow.

**Step 1:** Apply **Darcy's Law** for vertical infiltration at the trench base.

$$Q = K \times A_b \times i$$

For purely vertical gravity drainage, the hydraulic gradient **i ≈ 1** (gravity head equals depth of water).

**Step 2:** Substitute **i = 1** and simplify.

$$Q = K \times A_b$$

**Step 3:** Equate the **storage volume** to the **outflow rate × time** to find drawdown time.

$$V_s = Q \times t_e \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_e = \dfrac{V_s}{K \times A_b}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $V_s = V_t \times n$ and $A_b = L \times W$ (assuming a rectangular trench):

$$t_e = \dfrac{L \times W \times D \times n}{K \times L \times W} = \dfrac{D \times n}{K}$$

**Key Insight:** The drawdown time is **independent of length and width**! It depends **only on depth, porosity, and hydraulic conductivity** of the native subsoil.

**Numerical Example:**
Trench depth = 1.0 m, n = 0.355, K of underlying soil = $5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_e &= \dfrac{1.0 \times 0.355}{5 \times 10^{-5}} \\
t_e &= 7100 \, \text{s} \\
t_e &= \dfrac{7100}{3600} \approx 1.97 \, \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

The trench will **completely empty in about 2 hours**, well within the inter-storm dry period in Kerala (typically 6–24 hours).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Trench Length for Peak Runoff

**Step 1:** Estimate the **peak runoff** from the catchment using the **Rational Method** (the CGWB-prescribed method for Kerala).

$$Q_{peak} = 0.278 \times C \times I \times A_c$$

where:
- C = runoff coefficient (0.6–0.9 for paved, 0.3–0.5 for open ground)
- I = rainfall intensity (mm/hr) for the design storm duration
- A_c = catchment area (km²)
- 0.278 is a unit-conversion constant

**Step 2:** The trench cross-section must convey the peak flow at a velocity not exceeding the **filter media's permissible velocity (v_p)** to avoid scouring the top sand layer.

$$A_{req} = \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{v_p}$$

Typical $v_p$ for top sand layer = 0.5 to 1.0 m/min (conservative).

**Step 3:** For a fixed trench width W, the required **length L_req** is:

$$L_{req} = \dfrac{A_{req}}{W} = \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{W \times v_p}$$

**Numerical Example:**
A 0.5-hectare paved campus produces peak runoff. C = 0.85, I = 50 mm/hr, v_p = 0.5 m/min, design W = 1.0 m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_c &= 0.5 \, \text{ha} = 0.005 \, \text{km}^2 \\
Q_{peak} &= 0.278 \times 0.85 \times 50 \times 0.005 \\
Q_{peak} &= 0.0591 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 59.1 \, \text{L/s} \\
A_{req} &= \dfrac{0.0591}{0.5/60} = \dfrac{0.0591}{0.00833} \\
A_{req} &= 7.09 \, \text{m}^2 \\
L_{req} &= \dfrac{7.09}{1.0} = 7.09 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

A trench of **1 m wide × 7.1 m long** (rounded to 7.5 m for safety) will safely handle this peak flow.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Verdict

| Sub-Soil Type | Typical K (m/s) | Recharge Suitability | Drawdown Time (D=1m, n=0.35) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clean gravel | $10^{-2}$ | Excellent | ~35 seconds |
| Coarse sand | $10^{-4}$ | Very Good | ~58 minutes |
| Fine sand | $10^{-5}$ | Good | ~9.7 hours |
| Sandy loam | $10^{-6}$ | Moderate | ~4 days |
| Silty clay | $10^{-7}$ | Poor (need recharge shaft instead) | ~40 days |
| Impervious clay | $\lt 10^{-9}$ | Unsuitable (trench will overflow) | Indefinite |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In Kerala, most subsoils are lateritic (K ~ $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ m/s). For sites with K < $10^{-6}$ m/s, a **recharge shaft (borewell)** must be used **in combination** with a trench — this is a frequent 14-mark KTU question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[RAINFALL EVENT START] --> B1[Catchment Surface: Roof or Pavement]
    B1 --> C1[Graded Surface Runoff]
    C1 --> D1[Inlet Spillway with Mesh Screen]
    D1 --> E1[Top Sand Filter Layer 300mm]
    E1 --> F1[Middle Gravel Layer 300mm]
    F1 --> G1[Boulder Storage Layer 400mm]
    G1 --> H1{Geotextile Membrane Lining}
    H1 --> I1[Native Subsoil Unsaturated Zone]
    I1 --> J1[Aquifer / Water Table Rises]
    G1 --> K1[Overflow Weir to Municipal Drain]
    J1 --> L1[RECHARGE COMPLETE]
    K1 --> L1
    D1 --> M1[Inspection Chamber Optional]
    M1 --> N1[Periodic Desilting Maintenance]
    N1 --> L1
```

### 4.2 Sectional Schematic — Cross-Section of a Standard Recharge Trench

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TopZone [TOP ZONE 0 to 300mm]
        T1[Top Soil and Grass Cover]
        T2[Filter Sand 2mm to 6mm]
    end
    subgraph MidZone [MIDDLE ZONE 300mm to 600mm]
        M1[Gravel 20mm to 40mm]
    end
    subgraph BotZone [BOTTOM ZONE 600mm to 1000mm]
        B1[Boulders 50mm to 150mm]
        B2[Geotextile Wrap]
        B3[Native Lateritic Subsoil]
    end
    subgraph Inlet [INLET STRUCTURE]
        I1[Inlet Pipe from Roof]
        I2[Debris Screen]
    end
    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> M1
    M1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> Z1[Deep Aquifer Recharge]
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic for Selecting Recharge Trench

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Is the site available with length 5m or more?] -->|Yes| Q2
    Q1 -->|No| X1[Use Recharge Pit or Shaft Instead]
    Q2[Is the water table depth greater than 3m?] -->|Yes| Q3
    Q2 -->|No| X2[Use Recharge Well with Care]
    Q3[Is subsoil K greater than 1e-6 m per s?] -->|Yes| Q4
    Q3 -->|No| X3[Combine Trench with Recharge Shaft]
    Q4[Is catchment area less than 1 hectare?] -->|Yes| Z1[PROCEED with Recharge Trench Design]
    Q4 -->|No| X4[Use Multiple Parallel Trenches or Recharge Basin]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**
*Define a recharge trench. List its four major components.*

**Model Answer:**
A recharge trench is a **long, narrow, shallow excavation** backfilled with boulders, gravel, and sand, designed to **intercept, store, and infiltrate surface runoff** to recharge the underlying aquifer.

**Four major components:**
1. **Inlet structure** — receives runoff (pipe, channel, or grate).
2. **Filter media layers** — sand on top, gravel in middle, boulders at bottom.
3. **Geotextile membrane** — prevents soil migration and clogging.
4. **Overflow arrangement** — diverts excess water to a safe drain.

> [!TIP]
> Examiner's tip: Students often forget the **overflow weir** — losing 1 mark. Always include a *safety overflow* in your answer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Expected Pattern)]**
*State Darcy's Law as applied to recharge trench design. Define each term with units.*

**Model Answer:**
Darcy's Law governs the rate of water flow through the porous fill media of the trench:

$$Q = K \times A \times i$$

| Symbol | Term | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Q | Volumetric flow rate | m³/s |
| K | Hydraulic conductivity | m/s |
| A | Cross-sectional area of flow | m² |
| i | Hydraulic gradient (head loss / length) | m/m (dimensionless) |

For vertical gravity drainage in a recharge trench, **i = 1** is assumed.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Explain the **hydraulic design procedure of a recharge trench** for a small urban catchment of 0.4 ha with peak rainfall intensity 60 mm/hr. Assume runoff coefficient C = 0.85, trench width = 1.0 m, permissible filter velocity = 0.5 m/min, and effective porosity of media = 0.35. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **six functional layers** of a recharge trench. Mention the **Darcy's Law-based drawdown time** for the trench and the **conditions under which it overflows**. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Hydraulic Design**

**Step 1: Calculate the peak runoff (Rational Method)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_c &= 0.4 \, \text{ha} = 0.004 \, \text{km}^2 \\
Q_{peak} &= 0.278 \times C \times I \times A_c \\
Q_{peak} &= 0.278 \times 0.85 \times 60 \times 0.004 \\
Q_{peak} &= 0.0567 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate required trench cross-section**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_p &= 0.5 \, \text{m/min} = \dfrac{0.5}{60} = 0.00833 \, \text{m/s} \\
A_{req} &= \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{v_p} = \dfrac{0.0567}{0.00833} \\
A_{req} &= 6.81 \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate required trench length**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{req} &= \dfrac{A_{req}}{W} = \dfrac{6.81}{1.0} = 6.81 \, \text{m} \\
L_{design} &= 7.0 \, \text{m} \, (\text{rounded up for safety})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Provide depth of trench** — Assume D = 1.0 m (standard for Kerala lateritic soils).

**Step 5: Effective storage capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= L \times W \times D \times n \\
V_s &= 7.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.35 = 2.45 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A trench of **7.0 m × 1.0 m × 1.0 m** can store 2.45 m³ and safely handle the 0.4 ha catchment's peak storm.

**Part (b) — Layered Cross-Section & Drawdown**

The recharge trench (top to bottom) consists of:
1. **Top grass/vegetation cover (50 mm)** — Prevents erosion and silting.
2. **Top sand filter (300 mm)** — Removes silt and fine suspended solids.
3. **Middle gravel layer (300 mm)** — Distributes flow uniformly.
4. **Bottom boulder layer (350–500 mm)** — Stores water in voids.
5. **Geotextile wrap** — Prevents side-soil migration.
6. **Native subsoil interface** — Allows vertical infiltration.

**Drawdown Time** (D = 1.0 m, n = 0.35, assuming K = $5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s for lateritic soil):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_e &= \dfrac{D \times n}{K} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 0.35}{5 \times 10^{-5}} \\
t_e &= 7000 \, \text{s} \approx 1.94 \, \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Overflow conditions** (when the trench fails):
- Inflow rate > Infiltration capacity of subsoil.
- Storage volume is full and rain continues.
- Subsoil K < $10^{-7}$ m/s (clayey subsoil).
- Clogging of sand filter due to inadequate maintenance.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Differentiate between a **recharge trench, recharge pit, and recharge shaft**. Under what site conditions would you prefer a trench over the other two? **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A recharge trench of dimensions **15 m × 1.5 m × 1.2 m** is to be constructed with three layers: top sand (n = 0.30, 0.4 m thick), middle gravel (n = 0.35, 0.4 m thick), and bottom boulder (n = 0.40, 0.4 m thick). The subsoil hydraulic conductivity is $K = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. Calculate the **(i)** effective porosity, **(ii)** effective storage volume, and **(iii)** drawdown time. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Comparative Table**

| Feature | Recharge Trench | Recharge Pit | Recharge Shaft |
|---|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Long & narrow (L≫W) | Square/circular, shallow | Deep vertical cylinder |
| Typical depth | 0.5–1.5 m | 1.0–3.0 m | 5–15 m (deeper) |
| Best for | Linear spaces, road shoulders, narrow plots | Open spaces, parks, large plots | Constrained spaces, deep water table |
| Cost | Moderate | Low | High (borewell drilling) |
| Construction | Manual excavation | Manual excavation | Requires rig |
| Prefer trench when | **(i)** Long, narrow strip of land is available. **(ii)** Subsoil K > $10^{-6}$ m/s. **(iii)** Water table > 3 m deep. **(iv)** Catchment area is small (< 1 ha). | | |

**Part (b) — Calculations**

**(i) Effective Porosity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{eff} &= \dfrac{(0.30 \times 0.4) + (0.35 \times 0.4) + (0.40 \times 0.4)}{0.4 + 0.4 + 0.4} \\
n_{eff} &= \dfrac{0.12 + 0.14 + 0.16}{1.2} \\
n_{eff} &= \dfrac{0.42}{1.2} = 0.35
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Effective Storage Volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= L \times W \times D \times n_{eff} \\
V_s &= 15 \times 1.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.35 \\
V_s &= 9.45 \, \text{m}^3 = 9450 \, \text{litres}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Drawdown Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_e &= \dfrac{D \times n_{eff}}{K} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 0.35}{2 \times 10^{-5}} \\
t_e &= \dfrac{0.42}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 21{,}000 \, \text{s} \\
t_e &= \dfrac{21{,}000}{3600} \approx 5.83 \, \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The trench stores **9.45 m³** of stormwater and fully drains in **under 6 hours** — well within the typical Kerala inter-storm dry period.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Forgetting to convert A_c from hectares to km². This gives an answer off by a factor of **100**. Always write the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Porosity vs. void ratio confusion:** In KTU scripts, students often interchange n and e. Remember: $n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$ and **0 < n < 1**.
> 3. **i ≠ 1 in horizontal flow:** The simplification i = 1 is valid **only for vertical gravity drainage**. For horizontal flow in the trench length, use the actual slope gradient.
> 4. **Forgetting the overflow weir:** In design problems, always specify a **safe overflow path** — examiners deduct up to 2 marks if this is missing.
> 5. **Mixing up drawdown and recharge time:** Drawdown = trench emptying. Recharge = water reaching the water table. Both are different; the latter includes subsoil travel time.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **recharge trench** is a long, narrow, backfilled excavation used for **artificial groundwater recharge**.
- [x] It has **6 layers**: grass cover, sand, gravel, boulders, geotextile, and native soil.
- [x] **Trench dimensions** typically range: 0.5–1.5 m deep, 0.5–1.0 m wide, 5–30 m long.
- [x] The **effective storage volume** is $V_s = L \times W \times D \times n_{eff}$ (where $n_{eff}$ = effective porosity).
- [x] The **drawdown time** is $t_e = \dfrac{D \times n_{eff}}{K}$ — independent of length and width.
- [x] **Darcy's Law** $Q = K \times A \times i$ governs both flow in the trench and infiltration into the subsoil.
- [x] The **Rational Method** $Q_{peak} = 0.278 \times C \times I \times A_c$ is used to size the trench inlet.
- [x] Required length: $L_{req} = \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{W \times v_p}$ where $v_p$ = permissible filter velocity.
- [x] Trench works best in subsoils with **K > $10^{-6}$ m/s** (sandy or lateritic soils typical of Kerala).
- [x] For **K < $10^{-6}$ m/s**, combine with a **recharge shaft** (borewell) to bypass the impervious layer.
- [x] Always provide a **safety overflow** weir to a municipal drain.
- [x] The **geotextile membrane** is critical — it prevents siltation and prolongs trench life.
- [x] Standard longitudinal slope: **1:100 to 1:200** to prevent stagnation and scouring.
- [x] Maintenance: **Desilting every 2–3 years** by removing and washing the top sand layer.
- [x] Kerala-specific: Recharge trenches are mandated for **plots > 0.20 ha** under the Kerala Municipality Building Rules and KSPCB guidelines.
- [x] Compared to recharge pits and shafts, trenches are **best for linear, narrow, roadside or median spaces**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recharge Through Shafts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recharge Through Shafts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Recharge Shaft** is a vertical, deep, narrow artificial structure constructed to augment the natural recharge of groundwater aquifers by transmitting surface runoff or stored rainwater directly through low-permeability strata into deeper, water-bearing formations. According to KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, recharge shafts are classified under **artificial recharge techniques** and are specifically recommended where the surface soil has a low infiltration capacity but a highly permeable aquifer exists at a considerable depth below an impervious or semi-pervious layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Recharge shafts are mandatory sub-topic under "Methods of Artificial Recharge" and carry direct 7–14 mark weightage in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

A recharge shaft essentially bypasses the natural infiltration barrier by creating a **hydraulic shortcut** between the ground surface (or a storage sump) and the underlying aquifer.

## 1.2 Classification of Recharge Structures (Vertical Type)

| Structure | Typical Depth | Key Use |
|---|---|---|
| Recharge Pit | 2 – 5 m | Shallow alluvial areas |
| Recharge Trench | 3 – 10 m | Urban runoff, roadside drains |
| Recharge Shaft | 10 – 50 m | Deep water table, clayey terrain |
| Recharge / Injection Well | 50 – 300 m | Confined aquifers, industrial reuse |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of a recharge shaft like a **straw inserted into a tall glass of sand**, where the bottom of the glass holds the water (the aquifer) and the top is sealed by a layer of clay (impervious topsoil). Surface water cannot soak down naturally because the clay cap blocks it. The recharge shaft acts exactly like a straw — it pierces the clay cap and lets water drip straight into the sandy aquifer below. Just as the straw bypasses the seal of a drink can, the shaft bypasses the impervious soil layer.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Metrics

- **Diameter of shaft:** typically **0.5 m to 3 m** (circular, bored or dug)
- **Depth of shaft:** typically **10 m to 50 m** (depending on aquifer depth)
- **Filter pack hydraulic conductivity (K):** desired value **> 10⁻³ m/s** for coarse sand–gravel
- **Minimum submergence of water in shaft:** **0.5 m to 1.0 m**
- **Casing / screen slot opening:** **1.5 mm to 6 mm** depending on aquifer grain size

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Recharge Shaft (Top to Bottom)

A standard recharge shaft consists of the following layers, from top to bottom:

1. **Inlet / Catchment Basin (top)** — diverts runoff or roof-water into the shaft.
2. **Debris / Gravel Guard** — prevents leaves, silt, and floating trash from entering.
3. **Casing Pipe (RCC / PVC / MS)** — supports loose sidewalls of the bore; extends from surface to top of filter pack.
4. **Filter Pack (Coarse Sand + Gravel)** — graded media to disperse water, prevent clogging, and filter fines.
5. **Recharge Screen / Slotted Section** — perforated portion in the aquifer zone that allows water to exit into the formation.
6. **Bottom Plug / Concrete Cap** — seals the shaft at the bottom of the casing to force lateral outflow.
7. **Air Vent** — releases trapped air so the shaft does not become air-locked.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is an air vent needed?** As water rushes down, compressed air below can reduce the recharge rate. A simple 50 mm diameter vent pipe rising above ground level solves this.

## 2.2 Conditions Where Recharge Shafts are Preferred

Recharge shafts are technically superior to recharge pits or trenches in the following field situations:

- **Deep water table** — greater than **8 m to 10 m** below ground level.
- **Low-permeability top layer** — clay or silty-clay of thickness > 3 m.
- **Confined or semi-confined aquifer** — pressurised storage of high yield.
- **Limited land area** — urban colonies, factories, institutions.
- **Roof-top harvesting** in apartments where the storage is on the ground floor and the aquifer is deeper.

## 2.3 Design Logic — Step-by-Step

The design of a recharge shaft is governed by three engineering parameters: **(a) diameter, (b) depth, and (c) filter pack selection**.

### Step 1 — Decide the shaft diameter
The diameter is governed by the volume of water expected per recharge cycle:

$$Q_{design} = V_{runoff} \times C_{runoff\ coefficient}$$

The shaft must be able to swallow the design flow without overflow. A thumb rule used by the **Central Ground Water Board (CGWB)** is:

$$D_{shaft} \ge \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot Q_{design}}{\pi \cdot v_{allowable}}}$$

where $v_{allowable}$ is the entrance velocity into the filter pack, typically **0.3 m/s to 1.0 m/s**.

### Step 2 — Decide the shaft depth
The shaft depth must penetrate the confining layer and reach the upper surface of the aquifer plus a minimum **1.5 m to 3.0 m** of screen length inside the aquifer.

$$H_{shaft} = H_{confining} + H_{screen\ in\ aquifer}$$

### Step 3 — Filter pack design
The filter pack is the heart of the shaft. It must be:

- **Coarser than the aquifer material** by a factor of **2 to 5 times**, to prevent fine migration.
- **Finer than the casing slots**, by a factor of **1.2 to 2.0 times**, to retain the pack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A well-graded filter pack (sand → fine gravel → coarse gravel → boulder) eliminates **90 % of clogging failures** seen in poorly designed shafts.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \cdot v = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v$ | Discharge entering shaft | m³/s |
| 2 | $A_{shaft} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Cross-sectional area of shaft | m² |
| 3 | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi v}}$ | Required diameter from design flow | m |
| 4 | $V_{runoff} = C \cdot I \cdot A_{catchment}$ | Runoff volume (Rational-like) | m³ |
| 5 | $q = K \cdot i$ | Darcy's Law — infiltration into aquifer | m/s |
| 6 | $i = \dfrac{h}{L}$ | Hydraulic gradient | dimensionless |
| 7 | $H_{shaft} = H_{confining} + (1.5 \text{ to } 3.0)$ | Total shaft depth | m |
| 8 | $d_{filter} = (2 \text{ to } 5) \cdot d_{aquifer}$ | Filter pack grain size rule | mm |
| 9 | $V_{tank} = \pi r^2 h$ | Standing water volume in shaft | m³ |
| 10 | $t_{drain} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{Q_{in}}$ | Time to drain shaft | seconds |

> **Mnemonic (for KTU exam):** **"Q = A v"** (Discharge equals Area times velocity) is the single most-tested equation for shaft design.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban drainage mitigation** — prevents road flooding in cities like Kochi, Chennai, Bengaluru.
- **Industrial water-recharge compliance** — mandatory under **CGWA** (Central Ground Water Authority) for industries abstracting > 100 m³/day.
- **Rural drinking-water security** — recharges wells and hand-pumps during dry months.
- **Climate resilience** — buffers against declining groundwater from erratic monsoons, a key issue in Kerala's laterite belt.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Diameter of a Recharge Shaft

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the minimum diameter $D$ of a circular recharge shaft required to swallow a design flow $Q$ at an allowable entrance velocity $v$.

**Given:**
- Design discharge entering shaft = $Q$ (m³/s)
- Allowable entrance velocity through filter pack = $v$ (m/s)
- Shape of shaft = circular
- Diameter = $D$ (m)

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Write the continuity equation** for an incompressible fluid entering the shaft:

$$Q = A_{shaft} \times v$$

**Step 2 — Express the cross-sectional area of a circular shaft** in terms of its diameter:

$$A_{shaft} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the area into the continuity equation:**

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \times v$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to solve for $D$:**

$$D^{2} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi v}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi v}}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\,D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi v}}\,}$$

This equation is the foundation of every recharge-shaft sizing problem in KTU board exams.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-mark style)

**Problem:** A residential complex in Ernakulam wishes to construct a recharge shaft for its roof-top rainwater. The design runoff from the rooftop during a peak 1-hour storm is **50 m³**. The shaft is expected to swallow this volume in **2 hours (7200 seconds)**. The allowable entrance velocity through the filter pack is **0.5 m/s**. Determine:

(a) The minimum diameter of the shaft.
(b) The cross-sectional area of the shaft.
(c) The recommended depth if the confining clay layer is **8 m thick** and the screen must extend **2 m** into the sandy aquifer.
(d) The total volume of filter pack required.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Minimum Diameter**

Design flow rate:

$$Q = \dfrac{V}{t} = \dfrac{50 \text{ m}^3}{7200 \text{ s}}$$

$$Q = 6.944 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Substituting into the diameter formula:

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 6.944 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 0.5}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.7786 \times 10^{-2}}{1.5708}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{1.7688 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$D = 0.1330 \text{ m} \approx 0.133 \text{ m}$$

For practical construction, this is rounded up to a **minimum 0.5 m diameter** shaft (standard CGWB recommendation for residential scale).

**Part (b) — Cross-sectional Area**

$$A_{shaft} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (0.5)^{2}}{4}$$

$$A_{shaft} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.25}{4} = 0.1963 \text{ m}^2$$

**Part (c) — Recommended Depth**

$$H_{shaft} = H_{confining} + H_{screen\ in\ aquifer}$$

$$H_{shaft} = 8 \text{ m} + 2 \text{ m} = 10 \text{ m}$$

A practical design depth of **10 m to 12 m** is recommended to allow for submergence and settlement.

**Part (d) — Filter Pack Volume**

Assuming the filter pack fills the entire depth of the shaft:

$$V_{filter} = A_{shaft} \times H_{shaft} = 0.1963 \times 10$$

$$V_{filter} = 1.963 \text{ m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always state that filter pack volume is computed as **bore volume minus casing volume** for a more accurate result, since the casing displaces a small volume.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Checklist

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shaft diameter | $D$ | 0.5 – 3.0 m | Site, runoff volume |
| Shaft depth | $H$ | 10 – 50 m | Aquifer depth + screen |
| Entrance velocity | $v$ | 0.3 – 1.0 m/s | Filter pack stability |
| Filter pack K | $K$ | > 10⁻³ m/s | Coarse sand/gravel |
| Confining layer penetration | – | full | Mandatory |
| Screen length in aquifer | $L_s$ | 1.5 – 3.0 m | Lateral outflow |
| Air vent diameter | $d_v$ | 50 – 100 mm | Prevents air-lock |
| Standalone water depth | $h_w$ | 0.5 – 1.0 m | Buoyancy check |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Section of a Recharge Shaft (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture and layered flow** of water through a recharge shaft, from the catchment surface down into the aquifer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[ROOF OR CATCHMENT SURFACE] --> A2[RAINFALL OR RUNOFF ENTRY]
    A2 --> B1[INLET CHAMBER AND DEBRIS STRAINER]
    B1 --> C1[AIR VENT PIPE 50 MM DIA]
    B1 --> C2[INSPECTION CHAMBER RCC]
    C2 --> D1[CASING PIPE PVC OR RCC]
    D1 --> E1[FILTER PACK LAYER 1 COARSE SAND 300 MM]
    E1 --> E2[FILTER PACK LAYER 2 FINE GRAVEL 300 MM]
    E2 --> E3[FILTER PACK LAYER 3 PEBBLE 300 MM]
    E3 --> E4[FILTER PACK LAYER 4 BOULDER 300 MM]
    E4 --> F1[PERFORATED SCREEN SECTION 1.5 TO 6 MM SLOTS]
    F1 --> F2[DEEPER SCREEN SECTION 2 M INTO AQUIFER]
    F2 --> G1[CONFINING CLAY LAYER FULLY PENETRATED]
    G1 --> H1[SANDY AQUIFER ZONE HIGH K]
    H1 --> I1[GROUNDWATER TABLE RISES OVER TIME]
    I1 --> J1[RECHARGE COMPLETE TO LOCAL AQUIFER]

    style A1 fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style B1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style D1 fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#4d2a75,color:#000
    style E1 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2a6e2a,color:#000
    style E2 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2a6e2a,color:#000
    style E3 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2a6e2a,color:#000
    style E4 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2a6e2a,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style F2 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style G1 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#5c3a73,color:#000
    style H1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style I1 fill:#a8d8a8,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style J1 fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#7a4f00,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[INPUT STAGE: ROOF OR PAVEMENT RUNOFF] --> P2[PRE-TREATMENT STAGE: DEBRIS STRAINER AND SAND FILTER]
    P2 --> P3[CONVEYANCE STAGE: PIPES OR CHANNELS]
    P3 --> P4[STORAGE STAGE: SUMP OR SHAFT UPPER ZONE]
    P4 --> P5[FILTRATION STAGE: GRADED FILTER PACK]
    P5 --> P6[RECHARGE STAGE: PERFORATED SCREEN INTO AQUIFER]
    P6 --> P7[OUTPUT STAGE: GROUNDWATER TABLE RECOVERY]

    style P1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#5c3a73,color:#000
    style P4 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#9c5a00,color:#000
    style P5 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2a6e2a,color:#000
    style P6 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style P7 fill:#a8d8a8,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Site Selection & Suitability Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[SITE IDENTIFIED] --> S2{DEPTH TO WATER TABLE}
    S2 -- LESS THAN 8 M --> S3[USE RECHARGE PIT OR TRENCH]
    S2 -- GREATER THAN 8 M --> S4{TOP SOIL TYPE}
    S4 -- CLAYEY OR ROCKY --> S5[USE RECHARGE SHAFT]
    S4 -- SANDY --> S6[USE RECHARGE TRENCH]
    S5 --> S7{AQUIFER TYPE}
    S7 -- CONFINED --> S8[USE RECHARGE SHAFT WITH SCREEN]
    S7 -- UNCONFINED --> S9[USE RECHARGE WELL]
    S8 --> S10[PROCEED WITH DETAILED DESIGN]
    S9 --> S10

    style S1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style S5 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2a6e2a,color:#000
    style S6 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style S8 fill:#a8d8a8,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style S9 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#9c5a00,color:#000
    style S10 fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#7a4f00,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**Define a recharge shaft. In which geological condition is it most preferred over a recharge pit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A recharge shaft is a vertical, deep, narrow structure (typically 10–50 m deep, 0.5–3 m diameter) that bypasses low-permeability surface strata to deliver surface water directly into a deeper permeable aquifer. **(1 mark)**

It is preferred over a recharge pit when the **depth to water table exceeds 8–10 m** and the surface soil is dominated by **clay, laterite, or rocky formations** with low infiltration capacity. **(2 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**
**List any four essential components of a recharge shaft.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Casing pipe (RCC / PVC / MS) — supports loose sidewalls. **(0.75 mark)**
2. Filter pack — graded sand–gravel–boulder media for filtration and flow distribution. **(0.75 mark)**
3. Perforated screen — slotted section inside the aquifer to allow lateral outflow. **(0.75 mark)**
4. Air vent — releases trapped air to prevent air-locking. **(0.75 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme awards 14 marks with a typical split of **7 marks per sub-part**. Either **Question A OR Question B** must be answered.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**Design a recharge shaft for a school building in Thiruvananthapuram. The rooftop area is 800 m² and the average annual rainfall is 1800 mm. The runoff coefficient for the concrete rooftop is 0.85. The depth of the impervious clay layer is 9 m, and the aquifer (sandy) is encountered immediately below.**

#### **(a)** Compute the annual runoff volume available for recharge and the design daily flow rate assuming a 90-day recharge season. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** Determine the minimum diameter of the shaft using an allowable entrance velocity of 0.6 m/s, and recommend the total depth of the shaft. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Annual runoff volume and design daily flow rate**

Annual rainfall depth:

$$P = 1800 \text{ mm} = 1.8 \text{ m}$$

Annual runoff volume:

$$V = C \times P \times A_{catchment}$$

$$V = 0.85 \times 1.8 \text{ m} \times 800 \text{ m}^2$$

$$V = 1224 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per year}$$ **— (2 marks)**

**Volume over 90-day recharge season:**

$$V_{season} = 1224 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (entire annual volume used in recharge season)}$$ **— (1 mark)**

**Design daily flow rate:**

$$Q_{daily} = \dfrac{V}{90 \text{ days}} = \dfrac{1224}{90}$$

$$Q_{daily} = 13.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ **— (2 marks)**

**Convert to m³/s:**

$$Q_{daily} = \dfrac{13.6}{86400} = 1.574 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **— (1 mark)**

**Conclusion for (a):** $V = 1224$ m³/year, $Q = 1.574 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s. **— (1 mark)**

---

**(b) Minimum diameter and total depth**

Diameter formula:

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi v}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 1.574 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times 0.6}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{6.296 \times 10^{-4}}{1.885}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{3.341 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$D = 0.0183 \text{ m} \approx 18.3 \text{ mm}$$ **— (3 marks)**

This is impractically small. Therefore, as per **CGWB guidelines**, adopt the **minimum standard diameter of 0.5 m for institutional recharge shafts**. **— (2 marks)**

**Total depth:**

$$H_{shaft} = H_{clay} + H_{screen\ in\ aquifer}$$

$$H_{shaft} = 9 \text{ m} + 2 \text{ m} = 11 \text{ m}$$

Adopt practical design depth = **11 m to 12 m** (including 0.5 m freeboard and 0.5 m sump). **— (2 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**A residential colony in Kottayam has 40 houses, each with a 100 m² rooftop. The average rainfall is 2200 mm/year. The runoff coefficient is 0.80. The top 7 m is laterite clay (low permeability) underlain by a sandy aquifer.**

#### **(a)** Estimate the annual volume of runoff available and the design hourly flow rate assuming the recharge is spread uniformly over 100 days of 6 effective hours each. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** Size the recharge shaft diameter for an entrance velocity of 0.4 m/s. Also calculate the volume of filter pack required for a shaft depth of 10 m. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Annual runoff and design hourly flow rate**

Total catchment area:

$$A = 40 \times 100 = 4000 \text{ m}^2$$

Annual rainfall:

$$P = 2200 \text{ mm} = 2.2 \text{ m}$$

Annual runoff:

$$V = 0.80 \times 2.2 \times 4000$$

$$V = 7040 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$ **— (3 marks)**

Total recharge hours = $100 \times 6 = 600$ hours.

Design hourly flow:

$$Q = \dfrac{7040 \text{ m}^3}{600 \text{ h}} = 11.733 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ **— (2 marks)**

Convert to m³/s:

$$Q = \dfrac{11.733}{3600} = 3.259 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **— (2 marks)**

---

**(b) Diameter and filter pack volume**

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi v}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 3.259 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 0.4}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.01304}{1.2566}} = \sqrt{0.01038}$$

$$D = 0.1019 \text{ m} \approx 102 \text{ mm}$$ **— (3 marks)**

Adopt **standard 0.5 m diameter** (CGWB minimum for colony-scale shafts). **— (1 mark)**

**Filter pack volume:**

$$V_{filter} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times H = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.5^2}{4} \times 10$$

$$V_{filter} = 0.1963 \times 10 = 1.963 \text{ m}^3 \approx 2.0 \text{ m}^3$$ **— (3 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU students lose 2–4 marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion error** — Failing to convert m³/h or m³/day into m³/s before applying $D = \sqrt{4Q / \pi v}$. This is the **#1 cause** of dimensionally wrong answers.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply CGWB minimum diameter** — A theoretically derived diameter of 0.10 m is correct, but examiners expect you to state that **0.5 m is the practical minimum** for residential/institutional scale. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing recharge shaft with recharge well** — A recharge shaft is **open at top and unlined below the filter pack**, while a recharge well has a **fully cased bore with a screen only at the aquifer**. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 4. **Ignoring the air vent** — A recharge shaft without an air vent will air-lock and recharge efficiency drops by 40–60 %. Examiners reward inclusion of the air vent as a design feature.
> 5. **Omitting units in the final answer** — Always write `D = 0.5 m` not just `D = 0.5`. KTU valuation scripts penalise missing units by 0.5 mark per occurrence.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **recharge shaft** is a deep vertical structure (**10–50 m**) used where the water table is deep and the topsoil is impervious. **(High-yield)**
- The governing design formula is $D = \sqrt{4Q / \pi v}$ — **must be remembered verbatim**. **(High-yield)**
- Standard **CGWB minimum diameter = 0.5 m**; standard **entrance velocity = 0.3 to 1.0 m/s**.
- A recharge shaft has **four filter-pack layers** (sand → fine gravel → pebble → boulder), each typically **300 mm thick**. **(Frequent in 7-mark sub-parts)**
- The **screen length inside the aquifer** must be **1.5 m to 3.0 m**, not less. **(Frequent 3-mark question)**
- An **air vent (50–100 mm dia)** is mandatory to prevent air-locking. **(Distinctive KTU point)**
- **Shaft depth** = thickness of confining layer + screen length in aquifer.
- Always convert **runoff volume to design flow rate** in m³/s before substituting into diameter formula.
- Recharge shafts are **not** suitable for shallow unconfined aquifers — use pits or trenches instead.
- **Darcy's Law** ($q = K \cdot i$) governs the rate of infiltration once water reaches the aquifer.
- The most common **CGWB site rule**: recharge shaft location must be at least **3 m away from any building foundation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recharge Trench with Bore
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recharge Trench with Bore

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Recharge Trench with Bore** (also called a **Trench-Cum-Borewell Recharge Structure**) is a hybrid groundwater recharge system in which a shallow, horizontally-oriented surface trench filled with graded filter media is hydraulically connected to one or more vertical borewells that penetrate the deeper water-bearing strata (aquifer). The trench functions as a **sedimentation, filtration, and temporary storage chamber**, while the bore acts as a **vertical conduit** that conveys the pre-treated runoff downward into the aquifer.

According to the **Central Ground Water Board (CGWB) Manual on Artificial Recharge to Ground Water** and the KTU 2024 syllabus (OECET832), this structure is recommended in:
- Areas with limited land availability
- Geological profiles with an upper impervious/semi-pervious layer overlying a deeper permeable aquifer
- Urban and peri-urban settings where horizontal spreading structures are impractical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Recharge Trench with Bore is one of the three principal artificial recharge structures taught in Module 3, alongside Recharge Pits and Recharge Wells. It bridges the gap between surface spreading methods and direct borewell injection.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Straw in a Cup" Analogy**
> Imagine a tall glass of muddy water (representing runoff water mixed with silt). If you simply pour the muddy water onto a sponge (the aquifer), the sponge's surface gets clogged with mud, and water cannot seep in. Now, place a **straw** (the bore) through the sponge to the bottom, and pour the water into a **small bucket** (the trench) on top. The bucket has a layer of sand and gravel (filter media) that strains the mud, and then the clean water flows through the straw directly to the deep sponge layers, bypassing the clogged surface. **This is exactly how a Recharge Trench with Bore works.**

The trench is the "bucket" with multi-layer filtration, and the bore is the "straw" that bypasses the impervious topsoil to deliver clean water to the aquifer.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Design Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Trench Depth | **1.5 to 3.0** | m |
| Trench Width | **1.0 to 2.0** | m |
| Trench Length | **5.0 to 15.0** | m |
| Bore Diameter | **100 to 200** | mm |
| Bore Depth | **15 to 60** | m (depending on aquifer) |
| Runoff Coefficient (C) for urban catchments | **0.6 to 0.9** | dimensionless |
| Filter Media Permeability (K) | **10⁻³ to 10⁻¹** | m/s |
| Specific Yield of Aquifer | **0.05 to 0.25** | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Logic — Step-by-Step

The operation of a Recharge Trench with Bore follows a sequential **6-stage hydrological logic**:

1. **Runoff Collection Stage:** Rainwater from the catchment (rooftop, paved area, or landscaped surface) is conveyed through gutters, pipes, or open channels to the **inlet chamber** of the trench.
2. **Coarse Screening Stage:** The first silt trap / desilting chamber removes large debris (leaves, pebbles, plastic) via a wire-mesh screen.
3. **Sedimentation Stage:** A forebay chamber reduces flow velocity, allowing coarse silt and sand to settle out under gravity (Stokes' law regime).
4. **Filtration Stage:** Water percolates through **graded filter media** (bottom to top: boulders → gravel → coarse sand → fine sand → geotextile) — this is the *Darcy flow* domain.
5. **Storage & Buffer Stage:** The trench temporarily stores water, attenuating peak inflow and providing residence time for infiltration into the sides and bottom of the trench.
6. **Conveyance & Recharge Stage:** Filtered water enters the bore through a **perforated PVC / GI casing** sealed at the top with a **bentonite seal** to prevent short-circuiting of untreated surface water, and is delivered directly into the aquifer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why combine a trench AND a bore?**
> - The trench handles **volume** (large quantities, low-velocity filtration).
> - The bore handles **depth** (bypasses impervious strata).
> - Together, they maximize both **recharge rate** and **water quality**.

## 2.2 Core Design Philosophy — The "Why"

- **Why filter media are graded (boulders at bottom)?** Larger voids prevent clogging of the bore inlet and allow fine particulates to settle in the upper, easily-cleanable layers.
- **Why bentonite seal at top of bore?** Prevents contaminated surface runoff from bypassing the filter and entering the aquifer directly — this is a **mandatory** CGWB stipulation.
- **Why is the trench length-to-width ratio important?** A longer trench provides more **sidewall infiltration area**, increasing recharge rate without deepening the structure.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Recharge Trench with Bore Design**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = C \times P \times A$ | Annual runoff volume | m³ |
| 2 | $Q_{peak} = C \times I \times A$ | Peak runoff rate (Rational Method) | m³/s |
| 3 | $V_{trench} = L \times B \times D$ | Trench storage volume | m³ |
| 4 | $Q_{infiltration} = K \times A_{wetted} \times i$ | Darcy's law for trench walls | m³/s |
| 5 | $Q_{bore} = \dfrac{2.72 \cdot T \cdot (s - s^2/2H)}{\log_{10}(R/r_w)}$ | Modified Dupuit (confined) | m³/day |
| 6 | $Q_{bore} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot K \cdot (H^2 - h^2)}{\ln(R/r_w)}$ | Unconfined aquifer yield | m³/day |
| 7 | $t_{empty} = \dfrac{V_{trench}}{Q_{infiltration}}$ | Time to drain trench | s |
| 8 | $H_{f} = \dfrac{v \cdot L}{K}$ | Head loss through filter (Darcy) | m |
| 9 | $N_{bores} = \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{q_{bore}}$ | Number of bores required | dimensionless |
| 10 | $T_{c} = \dfrac{0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}}$ | Kirpich time of concentration | minutes |

**Where:**
- $C$ = Runoff coefficient (dimensionless)
- $P$ = Annual rainfall (m)
- $A$ = Catchment area (m²)
- $I$ = Rainfall intensity (m/s)
- $K$ = Hydraulic conductivity of filter media (m/s)
- $T$ = Transmissivity of aquifer (m²/day)
- $H$ = Initial saturated thickness (m)
- $h$ = Drawdown at bore (m)
- $R$ = Radius of influence (m)
- $r_w$ = Radius of bore (m)
- $L, B, D$ = Trench length, width, depth (m)
- $v$ = Approach velocity (m/s)
- $N_{bores}$ = Number of bores
- $q_{bore}$ = Recharge capacity of one bore (m³/s)
- $S$ = Slope of catchment (m/m)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Stormwater Management:** Municipal corporations use these structures to reduce flooding and replenish depleting urban aquifers.
- **Industrial Estates:** Coastal industries use them to combat saltwater intrusion by maintaining a freshwater hydraulic mound.
- **Drought-Prone Regions:** In Rajasthan and Tamil Nadu, CGWB has installed over **10,000 such structures** under the Master Plan for Artificial Recharge.
- **Building Bylaws:** Kerala State Building Rules mandate recharge structures of this type for plots > **200 m²** in specified municipal zones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Annual Runoff Volume Available for Recharge

The fundamental hydrological input to the design of a recharge trench with bore is the **volume of runoff** that can be captured from the catchment.

**Step 1 — Identify the rainfall input**
The total volume of rainfall received on the catchment over a year is given by:

$$V_{rain} = P \times A$$

where $P$ is the annual rainfall depth (m) and $A$ is the horizontal projection of the catchment area (m²).

**Step 2 — Apply the runoff coefficient**
Not all rainfall becomes runoff. A fraction is lost to evaporation, interception by foliage, depression storage, and infiltration into the native soil. This fraction is captured by the dimensionless runoff coefficient $C$:

$$V_{runoff} = C \times V_{rain} = C \times P \times A$$

**Step 3 — Adjust for losses within the recharge system**
A portion of the runoff is lost as silt deposition, evaporation from the open trench surface, and overflow during peak storms. Let $\eta$ be the system efficiency, typically **0.75 to 0.85** for well-maintained structures. The *design recharge volume* becomes:

$$V_{design} = \eta \times C \times P \times A$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values (worked example)**
Consider a residential building with the following parameters:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 150 \text{ m}^2 \\
P &= 2.5 \text{ m/year} \quad (\text{Kerala annual average}) \\
C &= 0.80 \quad (\text{impervious rooftop}) \\
\eta &= 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{design} &= 0.80 \times 0.80 \times 2.5 \times 150 \\
&= 0.64 \times 375 \\
&= 240 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The structure must be designed to handle **240 m³/year** of effective recharge volume.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Trench Storage Capacity
