**Step 1 — Define trench geometry**
Let $L$, $B$, $D$ be the length, width, and depth of the trench respectively. The gross storage volume is:

$$V_{trench} = L \times B \times D$$

**Step 2 — Account for void ratio of filter media**
The trench is not filled with water — it is filled with **boulders, gravel, and sand** that have a void ratio $e$ (typically 0.30 to 0.40). The *effective water storage* is:

$$V_{storage} = L \times B \times D \times e$$

**Step 3 — Add sump volume below bore inlet**
A small sump of depth $d_s$ is typically provided below the bore invert to trap residual silt. This adds:

$$V_{sump} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D_{sump}^2 \times d_s$$

**Step 4 — Total effective storage**

$$V_{total} = (L \times B \times D \times e) + \left(\frac{\pi}{4} \times D_{sump}^2 \times d_s\right)$$

**Step 5 — Worked example**
Assume $L = 6$ m, $B = 1.5$ m, $D = 2.0$ m, $e = 0.35$, sump diameter 0.5 m, sump depth 0.3 m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{total} &= (6 \times 1.5 \times 2.0 \times 0.35) + \left(\frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.5^2 \times 0.3\right) \\
&= 6.30 + 0.0589 \\
&\approx 6.36 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Time Required to Empty the Trench (Hydraulic Drainage Time)

This derivation ensures the trench is ready to receive the **next storm event** before it fills up again.

**Step 1 — Identify drainage pathways**
The trench empties through:
- (a) **Vertical infiltration** through the trench bottom into native soil
- (b) **Lateral infiltration** through the trench sidewalls
- (c) **Downward flow** into the bore(s)

**Step 2 — Quantify vertical infiltration (Darcy's Law)**

$$Q_{vert} = K_{soil} \times A_{bottom} \times i$$

where $K_{soil}$ is the native soil hydraulic conductivity, $A_{bottom} = L \times B$ is the trench base area, and $i$ is the hydraulic gradient. For drainage into the water table, $i \approx 1$ (gravity-driven).

**Step 3 — Quantify lateral infiltration from sidewalls**

$$Q_{lateral} = K_{soil} \times (2 \times D \times (L + B)) \times i$$

The wetted perimeter of the sidewalls is $2D(L + B)$.

**Step 4 — Quantify bore inflow (Modified Dupuit Equation for Unconfined Aquifer)**

$$
Q_{bore} = \frac{\pi \cdot K_{aq} \cdot \left(H^2 - h^2\right)}{\ln(R / r_w)}
$$

where $K_{aq}$ is the aquifer hydraulic conductivity, $H$ is the static water table depth, $h$ is the water depth in the bore during recharge, $R$ is the radius of influence, and $r_w$ is the bore radius.

**Step 5 — Total drainage discharge**

$$Q_{drain} = Q_{vert} + Q_{lateral} + Q_{bore}$$

**Step 6 — Time to empty the trench**

$$t_{empty} = \frac{V_{total}}{Q_{drain}}$$

**Step 7 — Worked numerical example**
Assume $K_{soil} = 10^{-5}$ m/s, $L = 6$ m, $B = 1.5$ m, $D = 2.0$ m, $K_{aq} = 10^{-4}$ m/s, $H = 8$ m, $h = 4$ m, $R = 50$ m, $r_w = 0.075$ m.

Calculate $Q_{vert}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{vert} &= 10^{-5} \times (6 \times 1.5) \times 1 \\
&= 9 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Calculate $Q_{lateral}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{lateral} &= 10^{-5} \times (2 \times 2.0 \times (6 + 1.5)) \times 1 \\
&= 10^{-5} \times 30 \\
&= 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Calculate $Q_{bore}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{bore} &= \frac{\pi \times 10^{-4} \times (8^2 - 4^2)}{\ln(50 / 0.075)} \\
&= \frac{\pi \times 10^{-4} \times (64 - 16)}{\ln(666.67)} \\
&= \frac{3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 48}{6.502} \\
&= \frac{0.01508}{6.502} \\
&= 2.32 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total drainage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{drain} &= 9 \times 10^{-5} + 3 \times 10^{-4} + 2.32 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 2.71 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Time to empty:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{empty} &= \frac{6.36}{2.71 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 2346.9 \text{ s} \approx 39.1 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The trench empties in approximately **39 minutes**, which is well within the typical inter-storm interval of Kerala's monsoon climate (2 to 6 hours), confirming the design adequacy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Checkpoints

| Design Check | Criterion | Status |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{design} \leq V_{total}$ | Storage must be adequate | Verified |
| $t_{empty} < T_{inter-storm}$ | Must drain before next rain | Verified (39 min) |
| $Q_{bore} \geq Q_{peak} / 2$ | Bore must be primary conduit | $2.32 \times 10^{-3} \geq Q_{peak}/2$ |
| Filter $K \geq 100 \times K_{soil}$ | Filter must not bottleneck | $10^{-3} \geq 100 \times 10^{-5}$ ✓ |
| Bentonite seal $\geq 1$ m | CGWB compliance | Mandatory |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** and **component interactions** of a Recharge Trench with Bore system. It is rendered as a block-level architecture because the physical drawing (cross-section of a trench + bore) cannot be accurately captured in Mermaid primitives.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rainfall on Catchment] --> B[Roof Gutter and Down Pipe]
    B --> C[Inlet Chamber with Mesh Screen]
    C --> D[Desilting Forebay Chamber]
    D --> E[Recharge Trench with Filter Media]
    E --> F1[Boulder Layer 40 to 80 mm]
    E --> F2[Graded Gravel 20 to 40 mm]
    E --> F3[Coarse Sand Layer]
    E --> F4[Geotextile Membrane Top]
    E --> G[Perforated Bore Casing]
    G --> H[Bentonite Seal 1 m]
    H --> I[Unsaturated Zone]
    I --> J[Aquifer Recharge Zone]
    E --> K[Lateral Infiltration to Native Soil]
    E --> L[Vertical Infiltration through Trench Floor]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style C fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style D fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style E fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style F1 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style F2 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style F3 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style F4 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style G fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
    style H fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
    style I fill:#17becf,color:#fff
    style J fill:#17becf,color:#fff
    style K fill:#bcbd22,color:#000
    style L fill:#bcbd22,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Component Cross-Sectional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1[Recharge Trench Cross Section]
        T1A[Top Geotextile and Sand Cap] --> T1B[Coarse Sand Filter 0.3 m]
        T1B --> T1C[Gravel Layer 0.4 m]
        T1C --> T1D[Boulder Layer 0.5 m]
        T1D --> T1E[Perforated PVC Casing 150 mm]
        T1E --> T1F[Bentonite Annular Seal 1 m]
        T1F --> T1G[Plain Casing to Aquifer Depth]
    end

    subgraph T2[Functional Roles]
        T2A[Filtration and Silt Trap]
        T2B[Temporary Storage]
        T2C[Conveyance to Aquifer]
        T2D[Prevention of Contamination]
        T2E[Long Term Recharge Conduit]
    end

    T1A -.-> T2A
    T1C -.-> T2B
    T1E -.-> T2C
    T1F -.-> T2D
    T1G -.-> T2E

    style T1A fill:#e7ba52,color:#000
    style T1B fill:#e7ba52,color:#000
    style T1C fill:#e7ba52,color:#000
    style T1D fill:#e7ba52,color:#000
    style T1E fill:#c15252,color:#fff
    style T1F fill:#c15252,color:#fff
    style T1G fill:#c15252,color:#fff
    style T2A fill:#7ba87b,color:#fff
    style T2B fill:#7ba87b,color:#fff
    style T2C fill:#7ba87b,color:#fff
    style T2D fill:#7ba87b,color:#fff
    style T2E fill:#7ba87b,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Hydraulic Path Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Primary Flow Vertical into Bore] --> R1[Reaches Aquifer in 5 to 30 minutes]
    P2[Secondary Flow Lateral into Soil] --> R2[Reaches Aquifer in 2 to 10 days]
    P3[Tertiary Flow Percolation through Floor] --> R3[Reaches Aquifer in 5 to 30 days]

    style P1 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style P2 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style R1 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style R2 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style R3 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Hydraulic Path Hierarchy:** The bore delivers **~80%** of the recharge water, sidewall infiltration contributes **~15%**, and bottom percolation **~5%**. This justifies the high cost of the bore — it is the workhorse of the system.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Similar]
**Define a Recharge Trench with Bore. List any four situations where it is preferred over a conventional recharge pit.**

**Model Answer:**

A Recharge Trench with Bore is a hybrid artificial recharge structure consisting of a shallow horizontal trench filled with graded filter media, hydraulically connected to one or more vertical borewells that penetrate the deeper aquifer. The trench performs filtration and temporary storage, while the bore conveys treated water to the aquifer.

**Four situations where it is preferred:**
1. **Limited land availability** in dense urban plots.
2. **Deep water table** with impervious upper strata (e.g., laterite capping).
3. **Low-permeability surface soils** where vertical infiltration alone is insufficient.
4. **High silt load in runoff**, requiring the trench as a pre-treatment chamber.

*(Word limit for KTU Part A: 80 to 100 words. Marks awarded for: Definition 1M, 4 situations 0.5M each = 3M)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar]
**State the role of (a) graded filter media, and (b) bentonite seal, in a recharge trench with bore.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Graded filter media** (boulders, gravel, coarse sand, geotextile) progressively remove suspended solids from runoff as water percolates downward. The grading prevents fine particles from migrating into the bore perforations and causing clogging, while maintaining high hydraulic conductivity.

(b) **Bentonite seal** is an annular impervious layer placed around the upper portion of the bore casing. It prevents untreated surface water and contaminated runoff from bypassing the filter media and directly entering the aquifer, ensuring **hydraulic isolation** of the recharge water.

*(2 marks for role of filter, 1 mark for bentonite = 3M)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]

**(a) Explain with a neat sketch the components and working of a Recharge Trench with Bore. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Recharge Trench with Bore consists of the following components, listed from top to bottom of the system:

**1. Catchment Conveyance System**
The rooftop or paved catchment is connected via gutters and down-pipes to the trench inlet. A first-flush diverter is recommended to discard the initial, most contaminated runoff.

**2. Inlet and Desilting Chamber**
A masonry or precast chamber with a wire-mesh screen removes leaves and large debris. A forebay zone reduces flow velocity, allowing coarse silt to settle (Stokes' settling velocity governs this stage).

**3. Recharge Trench Body**
Dimensions typically 1.5 to 3.0 m deep, 1.0 to 2.0 m wide, and 5 to 15 m long. It is filled with graded filter media:
- **Top layer:** Geotextile membrane (200 g/m²) — prevents fine sand from washing down
- **Second layer:** Fine to coarse sand (0.3 m) — biological / chemical filtration
- **Third layer:** Graded gravel 20 to 40 mm (0.4 m) — primary mechanical filtration
- **Bottom layer:** Boulders 40 to 80 mm (0.5 m) — supports bore and provides high void space

**4. Borewell Assembly**
- **Perforated PVC/MSC casing** (100 to 200 mm diameter) at the aquifer depth
- **Plain casing** above the aquifer, sealed with **bentonite** (1 m thickness) in the annular space
- **Gravel pack** around the perforated section
- **Sanitary cap** at the top to prevent ingress of debris

**5. Overflow / Vent Pipe**
A vertical vent pipe allows air to escape during filling, preventing air-locking of the bore.

**Working:** Runoff → inlet screen → desilting → graded filter media (gravity-fed Darcy flow) → bore inlet → perforated casing → bentonite-sealed passage → gravel pack → aquifer.

*(7M: Components 3M, Working 3M, Sketch label 1M)*

---

**(b) Design a Recharge Trench with Bore for a residential building with the following data: (7 Marks)**

- Catchment area: **200 m²** (RCC rooftop)
- Annual rainfall: **3.0 m**
- Runoff coefficient: **0.85**
- System efficiency: **0.80**
- Void ratio of filter media: **0.35**
- Native soil K: **1 × 10⁻⁵ m/s**
- Aquifer K: **5 × 10⁻⁴ m/s**
- Aquifer saturated thickness (H): **10 m**
- Bore radius: **0.10 m**
- Radius of influence (R): **60 m**
- Permissible drawdown: **5 m** (i.e., h = 5 m)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate annual design recharge volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{design} &= \eta \times C \times P \times A \\
&= 0.80 \times 0.85 \times 3.0 \times 200 \\
&= 408 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Size the trench — assume L = 8 m, B = 1.5 m, D = 2.0 m**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{trench} &= L \times B \times D \times e \\
&= 8 \times 1.5 \times 2.0 \times 0.35 \\
&= 8.4 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **storage capacity per rainfall event** (not annual). The trench must drain between events.

**Step 3: Calculate bore recharge capacity (Unconfined Dupuit equation)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{bore} &= \frac{\pi \cdot K_{aq} \cdot (H^2 - h^2)}{\ln(R/r_w)} \\
&= \frac{\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times (10^2 - 5^2)}{\ln(60/0.10)} \\
&= \frac{3.1416 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 75}{\ln(600)} \\
&= \frac{0.1178}{6.397} \\
&= 1.842 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
&\approx 66.3 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Annual bore capacity (assuming 100 recharge days × 6 hr/day)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bore,annual} &= 66.3 \times 6 \times 100 \\
&= 39{,}780 \text{ m}^3 \gg 408 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A single bore of 200 mm diameter is vastly over-capacity. **One bore** is sufficient. Trench dimensions of **8 m × 1.5 m × 2.0 m** are adequate.

*(7M: Volume 2M, Trench size 2M, Bore capacity 2M, Conclusion 1M)*

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a) Compare Recharge Pit, Recharge Trench, and Recharge Trench with Bore. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Recharge Pit | Recharge Trench | Recharge Trench with Bore |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | Vertical, square/circular (1–2 m dia, 3–5 m deep) | Horizontal, rectangular (1.5–3 m deep, 5–15 m long) | Horizontal trench + vertical bore |
| **Land Area Required** | Small (1–4 m²) | Moderate (10–30 m²) | Moderate (10–30 m²) |
| **Suitability** | Shallow water table (<10 m), permeable topsoil | Shallow water table, high runoff volumes | Deep water table (>15 m), impervious top layer |
| **Recharge Rate** | Low (10–20 m³/hr) | Moderate (20–50 m³/hr) | High (50–200 m³/hr) |
| **Construction Cost** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Filtration Efficiency** | Low (single chamber) | Moderate (graded media) | High (multi-layer + bore gravel pack) |
| **CGWB Recommended Use** | Individual houses, small plots | Apartment complexes, group housing | Commercial buildings, industries, dense urban areas |
| **Risk of Clogging** | High | Moderate | Low (lateral bypass possible) |
| **Maintenance Frequency** | Quarterly cleaning | Biannual cleaning | Annual inspection |

*(7M: 7 distinguishing parameters, 1M each)*

---

**(b) The runoff from a paved courtyard of area 500 m² is to be recharged using a Recharge Trench with Bore. The annual rainfall in the area is 2.0 m. Taking C = 0.75 and η = 0.80, determine: (7 Marks)**
- **(i) Annual design recharge volume**
- **(ii) Required trench dimensions if it is to be drained in 2 hours by a single bore of 150 mm diameter**
- **(iii) Number of bores required if peak rainfall intensity is 50 mm/hr**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Annual design recharge volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{design} &= \eta \times C \times P \times A \\
&= 0.80 \times 0.75 \times 2.0 \times 500 \\
&= 600 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Trench dimensions for 2-hour drainage**

Assume bore capacity $q_{bore} = 1.5 \times 10^{-2}$ m³/s (typical for 150 mm bore in moderate aquifer).

Volume drained by bore in 2 hours:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bore,2h} &= 1.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 7200 \\
&= 108 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Since this is a single-event drainage, assume trench storage $V_{trench} = 30$ m³ (typical for an event).

Choose L = 10 m, B = 1.5 m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{V_{trench}}{L \times B \times e} = \frac{30}{10 \times 1.5 \times 0.35} \\
&= 5.71 \text{ m (impractical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Revised sizing** — choose L = 12 m, B = 1.5 m, D = 2.0 m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{trench} &= 12 \times 1.5 \times 2.0 \times 0.35 = 12.6 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Time to drain: $t = 12.6 / 0.015 = 840$ s = **14 minutes** (well within 2 hours) ✓

**(iii) Number of bores for peak rainfall**

Peak runoff rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{peak} &= C \times I \times A \\
&= 0.75 \times \left(\frac{50}{1000 \times 3600}\right) \times 500 \\
&= 0.75 \times 1.389 \times 10^{-5} \times 500 \\
&= 5.21 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Number of bores:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{bores} &= \frac{Q_{peak}}{q_{bore}} = \frac{5.21 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5 \times 10^{-2}} \\
&= 0.35 \rightarrow \text{rounded up to } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** **One bore of 150 mm diameter** is adequate. Trench of **12 m × 1.5 m × 2.0 m** is recommended.

*(7M: (i) 2M, (ii) 3M, (iii) 2M)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost students 2 to 4 marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the system efficiency factor (η):** Many students compute $V = C \times P \times A$ without accounting for losses. CGWB mandates η = 0.75 to 0.85. Deduct **1 mark** if omitted.
> 2. **Confusing Confined vs Unconfined Dupuit equation:** The choice depends on whether the aquifer has a confining layer. Using the wrong formula yields a 30% to 50% error. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Skipping the bentonite seal in diagrams:** This is a mandatory CGWB compliance item. A neat sketch without it loses **0.5 to 1 mark**.
> 4. **Unit errors in Q calculations:** Mixing m/s, m³/s, m³/hr, and m³/day is the #1 cause of numerical answer rejection. Always write the unit in every step.
> 5. **Not rounding up the number of bores:** $N_{bores}$ must always be rounded **UP** to the next integer (you cannot have 0.35 bores!). Forgetting to round up loses 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting to check $t_{empty} < T_{inter-storm}$:** Examiners reward students who verify design adequacy, not just compute numbers. Always add a concluding sentence.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Recharge Trench with Bore**

- **Definition:** Hybrid structure = horizontal filter trench + vertical borewell to deep aquifer
- **Suitable for:** Limited land, deep water table, impervious topsoil, urban settings
- **Typical dimensions:** Trench 5–15 m × 1–2 m × 1.5–3 m; Bore 100–200 mm dia, 15–60 m deep
- **Filter media (top to bottom):** Geotextile → Sand → Gravel → Boulders
- **Bentonite seal:** Mandatory, minimum **1 m** thickness, around upper bore casing
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $V = \eta \cdot C \cdot P \cdot A$
  - $Q_{peak} = C \cdot I \cdot A$
  - $Q_{bore} = \pi K (H^2 - h^2) / \ln(R/r_w)$ (unconfined)
  - $Q_{bore} = 2.72 T (s - s^2/2H) / \log_{10}(R/r_w)$ (confined)
  - $t_{empty} = V_{trench} / Q_{drain}$
- **Runoff coefficient (C):** 0.6–0.9 for urban; 0.3–0.5 for open ground
- **System efficiency (η):** 0.75–0.85 typical
- **CGWB compliance:** Geotextile + bentonite seal + overflow pipe + first-flush diverter
- **Number of bores rule:** Always round UP to next integer
- **Design verification:** $t_{empty} < $ inter-storm interval (typically 2–6 hours in Kerala)
- **Maintenance:** Annual inspection; silt removal from forebay chamber; jet-cleaning of bore every 3–5 years
- **Cost range:** ₹ 30,000 – ₹ 80,000 for residential installation (2024 Kerala rates)
- **Environmental benefit:** Prevents soil piping, reduces urban flooding, raises water table
- **KTU Module 3 link:** This is the "advanced" recharge structure — knowing its components and the Dupuit equation is a high-weightage topic for both 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Artificial Recharge - Concept of Artificial Recharge of Groundwater
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Artificial Recharge - Concept of Artificial Recharge of Groundwater

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Artificial Recharge of Groundwater** is defined as the deliberate, engineered process of transferring water from the surface (or from another subsurface source) into an aquifer through human-made structures, in order to *supplement* the natural rate of groundwater replenishment, augment aquifer storage, raise water tables, and improve groundwater quality.

In formal KTU textbook phrasing, artificial recharge is the *controlled addition of water to a groundwater reservoir* through recharge structures such as **spreading basins, percolation tanks, recharge trenches, injection wells, and recharge shafts**, when the natural rate of infiltration is inadequate to meet present or future demand.

**Essential Pre-conditions** (as per CGWB and KTU Module-4 norms):
1. The existence of a **permeable aquifer** capable of accepting and transmitting the recharged water.
2. An **available source of surplus water** (stormwater, treated wastewater, canal surplus) at or near the recharge site.
3. A **suitable surface and subsurface profile** (porosity, hydraulic conductivity) that supports infiltration.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Groundwater Bank Account"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Your Groundwater as a Bank Account**
> Imagine your groundwater aquifer is a *savings bank account*. **Natural recharge** (rainfall infiltration, river seepage) is your monthly salary credited automatically. **Groundwater pumping** is your monthly withdrawal. If withdrawals exceed credits, the balance falls and you become overdrawn (water table declines, wells go dry).
> **Artificial recharge** is like doing a *manual, large top-up deposit* during the monsoon surplus — a controlled "transfer" of surface water into the aquifer "account." This increases the stored balance, protects the account from over-drafting, and earns "interest" in the form of safe, cheap, decentralized water storage.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following bolded engineering constants and indicators govern artificial recharge design:

- **Specific Yield (S_y)** — dimensionless ratio (typically **0.10 to 0.30** for sandy aquifers; **0.02 to 0.05** for clayey aquifers).
- **Hydraulic Conductivity (K)** — typically **1 × 10⁻⁵ m/s to 1 × 10⁻³ m/s** for good recharge aquifers.
- **Recharge Rate (R)** — expressed in **MLD (Million Litres per Day)** or **m³/day**.
- **Recommended Percolation Tank Capacity** — **0.1 to 0.5 Mm³** for rural Indian watersheds (per CGWB norms).
- **Minimum Recharge Well Depth** — should penetrate **at least 3 to 5 m below the water table** for adequate submergence.

## 1.4 Why Artificial Recharge is Needed (KTU Module-4 Rationale)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently frame 3-mark questions around the **"necessity of artificial recharge"**. Always list *at least four* drivers:

1. **Rapid urbanization and concretization** → drastic reduction in natural infiltration (pervious area can fall below 10%).
2. **Over-extraction of groundwater** → water table decline of 1 m to 3 m per year in stressed districts of Kerala (e.g., Palakkad, Chittur, parts of Thrissur).
3. **Climate variability / monsoon dependence** → Kerala receives ~3000 mm rainfall in ~120 days; storing only this surface surplus underground buffers dry-season scarcity.
4. **Prevention of seawater intrusion** in coastal aquifers (Trivandrum, Kollam, Kozhikode coastal belts).
5. **Quality dilution** — recharging helps reduce nitrate / fluoride concentration by dilution and dispersion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Principles — The "WHY" Behind Artificial Recharge

Every artificial recharge method ultimately aims to satisfy **two physical conditions** simultaneously:

1. **Hydraulic Condition:** The water must be able to physically pass through the soil/aquifer matrix (governed by **Darcy's Law**).
2. **Storage Condition:** The aquifer must contain **void space** to accept and hold the water (governed by **Porosity (n)** and **Specific Yield (S_y)**).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hydrologic Balance Equation** (fundamental to recharge design):
>
> $$\Delta S = P_{rech} - Q_{pump} - ET_{g} - Q_{base}$$
>
> Where $\Delta S$ is the change in groundwater storage, $P_{rech}$ is the recharge input, $Q_{pump}$ is the withdrawal, $ET_{g}$ is groundwater evapotranspiration, and $Q_{base}$ is the baseflow to streams. Artificial recharge increases $P_{rech}$.

## 2.2 Classification of Artificial Recharge Methods

KTU syllabus prescribes a **two-tier classification**:

### A. Direct (Surface) Methods
- **Spreading Basins** — shallow, levelled basins where water is ponded to infiltrate vertically.
- **Percolation Tanks (Bandharas)** — masonry/earthen dams across small streams to impound runoff for infiltration.
- **Recharge Trenches** — narrow, deep excavations (1.5 m to 3 m) filled with graded gravel, sand, and boulders, located in urban areas.
- **Recharge Pits / Shafts** — vertical dug pits (5 m to 15 m deep) penetrating the unsaturated zone.
- **Stream Channel Modification** — check dams, gabion structures, leaky weirs to enhance streambed infiltration.

### B. Indirect (Subsurface) Methods
- **Injection Wells / Recharge Wells** — bored wells fitted with casing and screens, used when the aquifer is overlain by impervious strata.
- **Induced Recharge** — pumping from wells hydraulically connected to a surface water body (river, lake), creating a gradient that "pulls" surface water downward.
- **Dug Well Recharge** — existing open wells retrofitted with filters and used as recharge points.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Variable | Typical Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Darcy's Law** $Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$ | $K$=hydraulic conductivity, $i$=hydraulic gradient, $A$=cross-sectional area | $Q$ in m³/s | Governs flow into recharge well/basin |
| 2 | **Specific Yield** $S_y = V_w / V_t$ | $V_w$=drained water vol., $V_t$=total aquifer vol. | dimensionless | Determines storage capacity |
| 3 | **Recharge Volume** $V_r = A \cdot S_y \cdot \Delta h$ | $A$=area, $\Delta h$=water-table rise | m³ | Quantifies recoverable water stored |
| 4 | **Percolation Rate** $V_p = K \cdot i \cdot t \cdot A$ | $t$=time duration | m³ | Design of spreading basins |
| 5 | **Infiltration Capacity (Horton's Eq.)** $f_t = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) \cdot e^{-kt}$ | $f_0$=initial, $f_c$=final rate, $k$=decay constant | mm/hr | Predicts basin infiltration behaviour |
| 6 | **Well Recharge (Thiem eq.)** $Q = 2\pi K b (H - h_w) / \ln(R/r_w)$ | $b$=aquifer thickness, $H$=$h_w$=heads, $R$=$r_w$=radii | m³/s | Design of injection wells |
| 7 | **Recharge Ratio (CWC norm)** $= V_{rech} / V_{avail}$ | volumes of water recharged vs available | dimensionless | Project efficiency indicator |
| 8 | **Aquifer Transmissivity** $T = K \cdot b$ | product of conductivity and thickness | m²/day | Indicates aquifer capacity to transmit water |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX TIP for KTU Scripts:** In markdown tables, never use the vertical pipe `\vert` symbol as a delimiter. Always use **$\vert$** or **$\mid$** inside math, e.g., $Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$ where $i = \Delta h \vert L$. This prevents the markdown table parser from breaking.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Urban Flood Mitigation:** Trenches and soak pits reduce peak runoff by **20–40%**, directly addressing Kochi-style urban flooding.
- **Irrigation Buffering:** Artificial recharge structures raise summer water tables, sustaining borewell yields in **Palakkad's paddy belts**.
- **Disaster Resilience:** Acts as a "decentralized reservoir" — a 1 Mm³ percolation tank replaces a costly surface dam with **90% less land footprint**.
- **Coastal Protection:** Maintains the freshwater-saltwater interface inland, preventing salinity ingress.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Recharge Volume in a Spreading Basin (Step-by-Step)

> **Problem Framework (typical KTU 14-mark Part B):** A spreading basin of surface area *A* is operated over a period *t*. The aquifer has a specific yield $S_y$. Compute the volume of water recharged and the rise in water table.

### Step 1 — State Darcy's Law for Vertical Infiltration

The rate of water moving vertically downward through the soil column per unit area equals the saturated hydraulic conductivity when the soil is fully ponded (assuming gravity-dominated flow, $i \approx 1$).

$$
q = K \cdot i = K \cdot 1 = K
$$

Here $q$ is the **flux** (m/s), $K$ is the hydraulic conductivity (m/s), and $i$ is the hydraulic gradient taken as unity for vertical ponded infiltration.

### Step 2 — Compute Total Volume Infiltrated over Time *t*

Multiplying the flux by the basin area *A* and the operating duration *t* gives the cumulative infiltrated volume:

$$
V_{inf} = q \cdot A \cdot t = K \cdot A \cdot t
$$

This is the *gross* water that crosses the soil surface. Not all of it reaches the water table — some is held in the unsaturated zone as **field capacity moisture** and is consumed by evapotranspiration.

### Step 3 — Apply the Specific Yield to Get Net Recharge Volume

Only the water that displaces air from the aquifer's pore space becomes *recoverable* groundwater. This fraction is the specific yield $S_y$ (a dimensionless number between 0 and 1).

$$
V_{rech} = S_y \cdot V_{inf} = S_y \cdot K \cdot A \cdot t
$$

### Step 4 — Convert Recharge Volume into Water-Table Rise $\Delta h$

The water table rises by an amount $\Delta h$ that, multiplied by the aquifer area and specific yield, gives the stored volume. Rearranging:

$$
\Delta h = \frac{V_{rech}}{S_y \cdot A}
$$

Substituting $V_{rech}$ from Step 3 and cancelling $S_y \cdot A$:

$$
\Delta h = \frac{S_y \cdot K \cdot A \cdot t}{S_y \cdot A} = K \cdot t
$$

> **Sanity Check (KTU Examiner Expectation):** The water-table rise equals the *cumulative infiltrated depth* — this is intuitive and is often the basis of follow-up questions.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (Mimicking a KTU 14-mark sub-question)

> **Given:** $K = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m/s, $A = 2000$ m², $t = 30$ days, $S_y = 0.15$.

$$
K \cdot t = (5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (30 \times 24 \times 3600) = (5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (2,592,000) = 12.96 \text{ m}
$$

So $\Delta h = 12.96$ m. Then:

$$
V_{rech} = S_y \cdot K \cdot A \cdot t = 0.15 \cdot 12.96 \cdot 2000 = 3888 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

$$
V_{rech} \approx 3.89 \text{ ML (Million Litres)}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A small 2000 m² basin, operated for 30 days, stores **~3.89 ML** of recoverable groundwater — sufficient for **~50 households @ 70 LPCD for ~110 days**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Recharge Well Discharge Using Steady Radial Flow (Thiem Equation Adapted)

> **Problem Framework:** Design the discharge capacity of an injection well tapping a confined/unconfined aquifer of thickness *b*, with a drawdown $(H - h_w)$ after a recharge time *t*.

### Step 1 — Apply Darcy's Law in Radial Form

For radial flow to/from a well, the velocity at any radius *r* is:

$$
v_r = K \cdot \frac{dh}{dr}
$$

The discharge through a cylindrical surface of radius *r* and height *b* is:

$$
Q = v_r \cdot A_r = K \cdot \frac{dh}{dr} \cdot (2 \pi r b)
$$

### Step 2 — Separate Variables and Integrate

Rearranging:

$$
Q \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = 2 \pi K b \cdot dh
$$

Integrating from the well radius $r_w$ (head $h_w$) to the radius of influence $R$ (head $H$):

$$
Q \int_{r_w}^{R} \frac{dr}{r} = 2 \pi K b \int_{h_w}^{H} dh
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Integrals

$$
Q \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_w}\right) = 2 \pi K b \cdot (H - h_w)
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Q (Thiem Equation for Recharge Wells)

$$
Q = \frac{2 \pi K b (H - h_w)}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r_w}\right)}
$$

> **Note on Notation:** For *recharge* wells, $(H - h_w)$ is positive when the recharge water raises the well head above the ambient aquifer head — the *same formula* applies with a sign change in interpretation.

### Step 5 — Units and Validation

- Numerator: m²/s × m = m³/s ✓
- Denominator: dimensionless logarithm ✓
- For $K = 10^{-4}$ m/s, $b = 20$ m, $(H - h_w) = 5$ m, $R = 200$ m, $r_w = 0.15$ m:

$$
Q = \frac{2 \pi \cdot (10^{-4}) \cdot 20 \cdot 5}{\ln(200/0.15)} = \frac{0.0628}{7.20} \approx 0.0087 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

$$
Q \approx 8.7 \text{ L/s} = 754 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{day} \approx 0.75 \text{ MLD per well}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (KTU Sub-question Type: "Choose the Best Method")

| Site Condition | Recommended Method | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Permeable topsoil, water table > 10 m deep | **Spreading Basin** | Gravity-driven vertical infiltration, low O&M cost |
| Impervious clay layer above aquifer | **Injection Well** | Bypasses impervious stratum, reaches deep aquifer |
| Urban residential area, limited space | **Recharge Trench / Pit** | Compact footprint, treats runoff at source |
| Stream with sandy bed, low flow | **Check Dam / Percolation Tank** | Increases residence time, raises wetted perimeter |
| Coastal sandy aquifer | **Recharge Trench + Injection Well combo** | Combats salinity, raises freshwater mound |
| Rocky terrain with deep fractures | **Borehole / Recharge Shaft** | Targets fracture zones directly |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. (3 Marks)** Define artificial recharge of groundwater. List any **four** objectives of artificial recharge in the Indian context.

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Artificial recharge of groundwater is the engineered process of *deliberately introducing surface water into aquifers* through man-made structures to supplement the natural rate of groundwater replenishment.

**Four Objectives:**
1. To **augment groundwater storage** in over-exploited aquifers.
2. To **raise the declining water table** in stressed watersheds (e.g., Palakkad, Chittur).
3. To **prevent seawater intrusion** in coastal aquifers of Kerala.
4. To **conserve surface runoff** during monsoons for use during the dry season.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **Q2. (3 Marks)** Differentiate between **direct** and **indirect** methods of artificial recharge with **two examples** each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Direct Methods | Indirect Methods |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Water is spread over ground and infiltrates by gravity | Water is injected or induced into the aquifer below ground |
| Aquifer Depth | Suitable for shallow, permeable aquifers | Suitable for deep aquifers or those overlain by impervious layers |
| Example 1 | Spreading basin | Injection well |
| Example 2 | Percolation tank | Induced recharge from riverbed |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Both sub-parts must be answered in sequence. The following provides *TWO* complete alternative questions (A and B); KTU examiners offer one, and the student picks the other.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Q (a) (7 Marks)** Explain with neat sketches the **spreading basin method** of artificial recharge. State **four** design considerations.

**Model Answer:**

**Spreading Basin Method:**
A spreading basin is a shallow, levelled, diked area (typically 0.3 m to 1.0 m deep) into which water is diverted and allowed to pond. The ponded water infiltrates vertically under gravity through the soil and reaches the water table.

**Operational Sequence:**
1. **Diversion** of runoff (or canal surplus) from a stream into the basin via a feeder channel.
2. **Settling** of coarse sediments in a forebay.
3. **Ponding and infiltration** over the basin floor (usually flooded 2 to 4 cycles per year, each lasting 7 to 15 days).
4. **Drying cycle** of 1 to 2 weeks between pondings to prevent clogging and to allow soil aeration.

**Four Design Considerations:**
1. **Soil permeability** — $K \ge 1 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s is preferred; clayey soils require pre-treatment.
2. **Basin floor slope** — kept at < 1% to ensure uniform ponding.
3. **Depth of water table** — at least 1.5 m to 3 m below the basin floor to prevent water-logging.
4. **Sediment load of source water** — high silt content causes *clogging*; pre-treatment via desilting chambers is mandatory.

**Q (b) (7 Marks)** A spreading basin of area **2500 m²** is operated for **45 days**. The hydraulic conductivity of the subsoil is **$7 \times 10^{-6}$ m/s** and the specific yield is **0.18**. Compute the (i) volume of water recharged, and (ii) rise in water table. Assume vertical ponded infiltration.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total infiltration time in seconds:**

$$
t = 45 \text{ days} = 45 \times 24 \times 3600 = 3{,}888{,}000 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Cumulative infiltrated depth:**

$$
\Delta h_{inf} = K \cdot t = 7 \times 10^{-6} \times 3{,}888{,}000 = 27.216 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Volume of water recharged (recoverable):**

$$
V_{rech} = S_y \cdot K \cdot A \cdot t = 0.18 \times 7 \times 10^{-6} \times 2500 \times 3{,}888{,}000
$$

Evaluating step by step:

$$
V_{rech} = 0.18 \times 7 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.72 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
V_{rech} = 0.18 \times 68{,}040 = 12{,}247.2 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

$$
V_{rech} \approx 12{,}247 \text{ m}^{3} \approx 12.25 \text{ ML}
$$

**Step 4 — Water-table rise $\Delta h$:**

$$
\Delta h = K \cdot t = 27.216 \text{ m} \quad \text{(as derived in Step 2)}
$$

> **Note for Examiner:** The water-table rise equals the cumulative infiltrated depth *only* when the rise is computed as $V_{rech}/(S_y \cdot A)$; cancellation gives $\Delta h = Kt$, which is the expected simplification.

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **$V_{rech} \approx 12.25$ ML**
- (ii) **$\Delta h \approx 27.22$ m** (practically capped by the available unsaturated zone thickness).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

**Q (a) (7 Marks)** Discuss the **injection well method** of artificial recharge. State **five factors** governing the design of an injection well.

**Model Answer:**

**Injection Well Method:**
An injection well (also called a recharge well) is a bored, cased well fitted with a screen, used to inject treated water directly into a confined or deep aquifer that is overlain by an impervious stratum. Water is pumped under pressure through the well screen into the aquifer, and then spreads radially under the natural hydraulic gradient.

**Five Design Factors:**
1. **Aquifer transmissivity (T = K·b)** — must be high enough to accept the injected water without excessive back-up.
2. **Quality of injected water** — must be pre-treated to prevent chemical/biological clogging of the screen.
3. **Well screen design** — slot size, length, and open area determine injection capacity; typically 10% to 15% open area.
4. **Depth of water table / piezometric head** — injection well should penetrate well below the confining layer.
5. **Back-pressure and clogging control** — periodic back-flushing and redevelopment are essential to maintain efficiency.

**Q (b) (7 Marks)** An injection well taps a confined aquifer of thickness **25 m** and hydraulic conductivity **$5 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s**. The radius of influence is **250 m**, well radius is **0.20 m**, and the head difference is **6 m**. Estimate the **recharge discharge** using the Thiem equation.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the Thiem Recharge Equation:**

$$
Q = \frac{2 \pi K b (H - h_w)}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r_w}\right)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Given Values:**

- $K = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s
- $b = 25$ m
- $(H - h_w) = 6$ m
- $R = 250$ m
- $r_w = 0.20$ m

**Step 3 — Compute the Logarithm:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_w}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{250}{0.20}\right) = \ln(1250) \approx 7.131
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Numerator:**

$$
2 \pi \cdot K \cdot b \cdot (H - h_w) = 2 \pi \cdot 5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 25 \cdot 6
$$

$$
= 2 \pi \cdot 0.075 = 0.4712 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Discharge:**

$$
Q = \frac{0.4712}{7.131} = 0.0661 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

Converting to m³/day:

$$
Q = 0.0661 \times 86400 \approx 5711 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{day} \approx 5.71 \text{ MLD}
$$

**Final Answer:** **$Q \approx 0.0661$ m³/s $\approx 5.71$ MLD per injection well.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert *days into seconds* in the infiltration formula. Always show the conversion line explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing Specific Yield with Porosity:** $S_y \le n$. Examiners deduct 1 mark for using porosity (n) in the recharge volume formula.
> 3. **Forgetting the Logarithm Base:** The Thiem equation uses the **natural log (ln)**. Using $\log_{10}$ gives a 2.3× error.
> 4. **Ignoring Clogging:** Answers to design questions that omit *sediment/clogging control* lose 1–2 marks. KTU expects the term **"pre-treatment / desilting"** explicitly.
> 5. **Direct vs Indirect confusion:** Spreading basin is *direct*; injection well is *indirect*. Do not interchange them in sub-questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Artificial recharge** = *engineered* addition of water into aquifers; it is *not* equivalent to natural recharge.
- **Necessity drivers:** urbanization, over-extraction, monsoon dependence, seawater intrusion, water-quality dilution.
- **Two main classes:** **Direct (surface)** — spreading basins, percolation tanks, trenches, pits; **Indirect (subsurface)** — injection wells, induced recharge.
- **Key equations (must memorize for KTU):**
  - $Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$ (Darcy)
  - $V_{rech} = S_y \cdot K \cdot A \cdot t$ (Spreading basin)
  - $\Delta h = K \cdot t$ (Water-table rise in simple basin)
  - $Q = 2\pi K b (H - h_w) / \ln(R / r_w)$ (Thiem for recharge well)
  - $T = K \cdot b$ (Transmissivity)
- **Standard specific yield** for design in sandy aquifers: $S_y = 0.15$ to $0.25$.
- **Always state pre-treatment** for water entering any recharge structure to avoid clogging.
- **Decision rule of thumb:** Shallow permeable aquifer + space available → *spreading basin*; deep aquifer with impervious overburden → *injection well*; urban space-crunch → *trench / pit*.
- **Kerala-specific examples (impress the examiner):** Percolation tanks in **Attappady**, recharge shafts in **Thrissur urban wards**, coastal injection wells in **Kollam** to combat salinity.
- **Examiners reward** units, neat sketches, and explicit step-by-step derivations; **deduct** for missing units, skip of intermediate steps, and confusion between *specific yield* and *porosity*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recharge Methods - Basin, Stream, Channel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recharge Methods - Basin, Stream, Channel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Artificial Recharge – The Umbrella Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Artificial Recharge (Definition – KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> Artificial recharge is the *engineered process of augmenting the natural replenishment of groundwater storage in aquifers by diverting surface water into the ground* through specifically designed structures, basins, channels, or sub-surface injection systems, so as to improve yield, quality, and sustainability of the groundwater resource.

Among the several direct methods of artificial recharge, the **three principal surface-spreading techniques** covered in this module are:

| # | Method | Core Idea |
|---|--------|-----------|
| 1 | **Basin Recharge** | Spread water in shallow, broad basins on permeable soil and let it percolate downward. |
| 2 | **Stream Recharge** | Construct barriers (check dams / nala bunds) across natural streams to hold back runoff and force infiltration through the streambed. |
| 3 | **Channel Recharge** | Lengthen the contact time between flowing water and the channel bed by modifying natural channels (e.g., gully plugging, contour trenches, percolation channels). |

---

## 1.2 Basin Recharge – Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Basin Recharge (Definition):**
> A method in which surface water (from a stream, canal, river, or stored runoff) is spread in *artificially created shallow basins* on highly permeable terrain and allowed to infiltrate by gravity into the underlying aquifer.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a **dry bathroom sponge** lying flat on the floor. Now, pour a mug of water onto the centre of the sponge. The water spreads outward and slowly seeps downward through millions of tiny pores. A recharge basin works **exactly the same way** — the **basin floor is the sponge**, and the **water poured into it is the mug of water**. The water does not evaporate (if it rains enough) — it seeps down to refill the underground water table.

**Common sub-types of Basin Recharge:**

- **Percolation Tanks (Moti-bandh)**: Small check dams built across first-order streams in upper catchments to impound runoff and promote percolation through the stream bed and banks.
- **Recharge Basins**: Engineered shallow excavations (0.6 – 1.5 m deep) on permeable alluvium, off-stream.
- **Spreading Basins / Flooding Basins**: Built adjacent to perennial rivers/streams, fed by gravity diversion, and used to flood large areas.
- **Bench Terraces**: Step-like structures cut into slopes to slow runoff and create standing pools.

---

## 1.3 Stream Recharge – Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Stream Recharge (Definition):**
> A technique in which *small masonry or earthen barriers* (check dams, percolation dams, nala bunds) are constructed across seasonal/ephemeral streams to hold back flood water, raise the local water level, and induce vertical percolation through the natural stream bed into the underlying aquifer.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a **kitchen sink drain**. If you pour water very fast, it sloshes over the side. But if you place a **flat board across the drain at a certain height**, water accumulates behind it. The longer the water stays in contact with the sink floor (which is porous to the ground beneath), the more it leaks downward. A check dam across a stream is **that board in the sink** — it gives the water *time and pressure* to seep into the earth.

**Common sub-types of Stream Recharge:**

- **Check Dams / Nala Bunds**: Low-height weirs (1 – 2 m) across gullies/streams; built in series.
- **Percolation Dams**: Slightly higher structures (2 – 5 m) with masonry spillways; suitable for second-order streams.
- **Subsurface Dykes (Underground Bandharas)**: Built beneath the streambed to arrest subsurface flow and raise the water table.

---

## 1.4 Channel Recharge – Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Channel Recharge (Definition):**
> A set of methods in which the natural drainage network (channels, gullies, rivulets) is *modified by in-channel and on-channel structures* so that the *contact time between flowing water and the channel bed* is maximised, thereby increasing the volume of water that infiltrates into the ground.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Picture a long, tilted *chute* down which you pour marbles. The marbles shoot out the bottom in seconds. But if you **fold the chute** by adding ridges, dams, baffles, or zig-zags, the marbles bounce, slow down, and many fall through the floor. A modified stream channel — full of **loose boulders, gully plugs, contour trenches, and percolation channels** — works on the same principle: *slow it down, spread it out, let it soak*.

**Common sub-types of Channel Recharge:**

- **Gully Plugs / Loose Boulder Dams**: Small stone dams built inside gullies at intervals.
- **Contour Trenches / Contour Bunds**: Trenches dug along contours on hill slopes.
- **Percolation Channels**: Lined/unlined channels with check drops to detain water.
- **Stream Development** with *vegetative buffers* and *roughened bed*.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Threshold Values (KTU Board Favourites)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numbers to remember verbatim for the exam:**
> - Optimum depth of a **recharge basin**: **0.6 m to 1.2 m** (deeper basins lose water to evaporation).
> - Typical **size of a percolation tank**: 0.1 – 1.0 hectare (moth) water-spreading area.
> - **Infiltration rate** of alluvial sand: **2 to 25 cm/hr**.
> - **Check dam height**: usually **1.0 m to 2.0 m** above stream bed; max **3 m**.
> - **Spacing** of gully plugs in a channel: every **15 – 30 m** depending on gradient.
> - **Design infiltration rate** for design of recharge structures: **1 cm/hr** to **2 cm/hr** is a conservative value adopted by CGWB.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Design Logic – Applicable to All Three Methods

The design of any surface-spreading recharge structure follows **five logical steps**:

1. **Hydrological Assessment** – estimate the volume of runoff available (R).
2. **Geotechnical Assessment** – test the infiltration rate (I) of the host soil.
3. **Storage Sizing** – size the basin/check-dam such that the water-residence time is ≥ the time needed for design infiltration.
4. **Silt Control Design** – provide for upstream silt trap (desilting chamber or boulder wall).
5. **Spillway & Protection Design** – design a safe spillway for excess floods (usually a 25-year or 50-year return period flood).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this sequence?**
> You cannot "store" water faster than the ground can "absorb" it. Hence, **infiltration capacity** of the soil is the *design bottleneck* — the structure must hold water for a sufficient duration for it to actually soak in.

---

## 2.2 Basin Recharge – Theoretical Analysis

A recharge basin is essentially a **shallow inverted cup on permeable ground**. Its design revolves around three variables:

- **W** = width of basin (m)
- **L** = length of basin (m)
- **d** = depth of water impounded (m)

### 2.2.1 Design Steps

- Step 1: Determine the **post-monsoon water table depth (D)** at the proposed site. If the basin is to function as a *recharge* structure, the water table should be **> 2 m below ground** (CGWB guideline).
- Step 2: Conduct an **infiltration test** (double-ring infiltrometer) to obtain the **field infiltration rate I** (cm/hr). Apply a **clogging correction factor (Cc) of 0.5 to 0.75** to account for silt-induced reduction.
- Step 3: Size the basin so that the volume impounded equals the runoff that can be recharged in a given time.
- Step 4: Provide a **side slope of 1.5 H : 1 V (or 2 H : 1 V)** to prevent sloughing.
- Step 5: Provide an **emergency overflow spillway** to prevent overtopping and breaching.

### 2.2.2 Real-world Utility

Used in **alluvial Indo-Gangetic plains, deltaic regions, and weathered-rock aquifers** (laterite belts of Kerala, basaltic terrain of Maharashtra, granitic aquifers of Telangana/Andhra).

---

## 2.3 Stream Recharge – Theoretical Analysis

A check dam / percolation tank on a stream is a **small water-storage structure with high recharge efficiency**. The design logic differs from a basin because the **stream itself is the source of water** and **the streambed is the infiltration surface**.

### 2.3.1 Design Steps

- Step 1: Identify a **narrow stream cross-section** with a high downstream hydraulic gradient (so that the impounded water drains by gravity).
- Step 2: Conduct an **infiltration test on the streambed** — values are typically **2 to 5 cm/hr** in sandy beds.
- Step 3: Determine **storage volume** from cross-section × length of impoundment.
- Step 4: **Position the dam wall** on a *competent rocky foundation* to prevent under-seepage failure.
- Step 5: Design a **masonry spillway / waste weir** of width sufficient to pass a 25-yr flood.

### 2.3.2 Real-world Utility

Dominant recharge intervention in the **western ghats, sub-Himalayan terrains, and parts of the Deccan plateau** where streams are seasonal.

---

## 2.4 Channel Recharge – Theoretical Analysis

Channel recharge does **not store water in a single pool** — it **intercepts, slows, and spreads** water along a length of channel.

- **Gully Plugs** are placed in a series along a gully, creating a *staircase of small pools*. Each plug:
  - Reduces the longitudinal slope of the gully.
  - Traps silt and builds a fertile terrace over time.
  - Holds water for 12 – 24 hours after every storm, achieving near-100% infiltration in sandy beds.
- **Contour Trenches** are typically **0.5 m wide × 0.5 m deep**, spaced every **5 – 10 m** along the contour on hill slopes.

### 2.4.1 Design Step

Determine the **longest storm duration (T_d)** the structure must detain water. For a gully plug of height *h* and width *w*, the **storage volume V = w × h × L** (where L is the upstream length of the pool).

### 2.4.2 Real-world Utility

Used in **hill-slope erosion control** (Western Ghats, Aravallis) and for **recharging weathered/fractured hard-rock aquifers**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — at least one appears in nearly every Part B question.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning of Terms | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Storage volume of a recharge basin | $V_b = L \cdot B \cdot d$ | $L$ = length, $B$ = width, $d$ = impounded depth | m³ |
| 2 | Recharge rate through basin floor | $Q_b = L \cdot B \cdot I \cdot C_c$ | $I$ = infiltration rate, $C_c$ = clogging factor (0.5 – 0.75) | m³/hr |
| 3 | Time to drain basin | $t_d = \dfrac{d}{I \cdot C_c}$ | $d$ in cm, $I$ in cm/hr → $t_d$ in hours | hr |
| 4 | Storage volume of check dam | $V_{cd} = A_{cs} \cdot L$ | $A_{cs}$ = cross-sectional area of impoundment, $L$ = impoundment length | m³ |
| 5 | Recharge rate from check dam | $Q_{cd} = A_{bed} \cdot I$ | $A_{bed}$ = wetted streambed area beneath impoundment | m³/hr |
| 6 | Volume of water recharged in time *T* | $V_{rec} = A \cdot I \cdot C_c \cdot T$ | $A$ = infiltration area, $T$ = residence time | m³ |
| 7 | Cross-section of trapezoidal check dam pool | $A_{cs} = B \cdot d + s \cdot d^2$ | $B$ = bottom width, $d$ = depth, $s$ = side slope (H:V) | m² |
| 8 | Spillway discharge (ogee / broad-crested) | $Q_{sp} = C \cdot L_{sp} \cdot H^{3/2}$ | $C$ ≈ 1.7 (ogee) or 1.45 (broad-crested), $H$ = head over spillway | m³/s |
| 9 | Sediment storage volume | $V_{sil} = Q \cdot C_s \cdot T \cdot 0.0001$ | $C_s$ = sediment conc. (ppm), $T$ = time (s) | m³ |
| 10 | Runoff depth (rational method) | $R = P - \text{Losses}$ | $P$ = rainfall; Losses = infiltration + depression + ET | mm or cm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit-Trap Alert (KTU Examiner's favourite):** When using formula 2 or 3, keep **$I$ in cm/hr** and **$d$ in cm** consistently, OR convert both to m/hr and m. Mixing cm and m is the most common cause of full-mark loss.

---

## 2.6 Selection Logic – Which Method When?

> [!NOTE]
> This decision-tree logic is asked frequently in KTU papers.

| Site Condition | Preferred Method | Reason |
|----------------|------------------|--------|
| Deep water table (> 5 m), high-permeability soil, surplus runoff | **Basin Recharge** | Maximises vertical infiltration over a large area. |
| Seasonal stream with sandy-gravel bed, narrow gorge available | **Stream Recharge** | Uses stream itself as recharge medium. |
| Steep hill slopes, degraded land, hard-rock terrain | **Channel Recharge** (gully plugs, contour trenches) | Slows runoff, traps silt, recharges fractured rock. |
| Flat terrain, clayey soil, low natural infiltration | **NOT suitable** for any of these — go for **injection wells or recharge shafts** instead. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Design of a Recharge Basin (Storage vs. Infiltration Match)

> **Problem (typical 7-mark KTU sub-part):**
> Design a rectangular recharge basin to be constructed in sandy alluvium with field infiltration rate $I = 10 \text{ cm/hr}$. The expected runoff volume to be recharged in a single storm event is $V_{in} = 1500 \text{ m}^3$. Take clogging correction factor $C_c = 0.5$, allowable water depth in basin $d = 0.6$ m. Adopt a Length:Width ratio of 2:1. Determine basin dimensions and the time required to drain it.

**Step 1 — Identify the design philosophy**

The basin must be sized such that its **storage capacity $V_b$** matches the **incoming runoff volume $V_{in}$** to be recharged.

$$
V_b = V_{in} = 1500 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Write the storage volume equation in terms of unknowns**

$$
V_b = L \cdot B \cdot d
$$

where $L$ = length, $B$ = width, $d$ = depth. Given $L = 2B$ and $d = 0.6$ m:

$$
V_b = (2B) \cdot B \cdot 0.6 = 1.2 \, B^2
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for B**

$$
1.2 \, B^2 = 1500
$$

$$
B^2 = \frac{1500}{1.2} = 1250
$$

$$
B = \sqrt{1250} \approx 35.36 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute L**

$$
L = 2B = 2 \times 35.36 \approx 70.71 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the time to drain the basin**

Apply formula 3 with the clogging correction factor:

$$
t_d = \frac{d}{I \cdot C_c} = \frac{60 \text{ cm}}{10 \text{ cm/hr} \times 0.5} = \frac{60}{5} = 12 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify the recharge rate**

$$
Q_b = L \cdot B \cdot I \cdot C_c = 70.71 \times 35.36 \times 0.10 \times 0.5 \approx 125.0 \text{ m}^3 / \text{hr}
$$

Total volume that can be recharged in 12 hours:

$$
V_{rec} = Q_b \times t_d = 125 \times 12 = 1500 \text{ m}^3 \quad \checkmark
$$

The basin dimensions of **70.7 m × 35.4 m × 0.6 m** are consistent.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Check Dam Sizing and Spillway Design

> **Problem (typical 7-mark KTU sub-part):**
> A nala bund is proposed across a seasonal stream. The impoundment cross-section is trapezoidal with bottom width $B = 8$ m, side slopes $1.5 \, H : 1 \, V$, and design depth $d = 2.0$ m. The impoundment extends $L = 60$ m upstream of the bund wall. Determine (a) the storage volume, and (b) the spillway width required to safely pass a 25-year flood peak of $Q_{25} = 25 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ under a maximum head of $H_{sp} = 0.6$ m. Take spillway coefficient $C = 1.7$ (ogee).

**Step 1 — Compute the trapezoidal cross-section area**

$$
A_{cs} = B \cdot d + s \cdot d^2
$$

where $s = 1.5$ (side slope expressed as ratio H:V).

$$
A_{cs} = (8 \times 2.0) + (1.5 \times 2.0^2) = 16 + 6 = 22 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the storage volume of the check dam**

$$
V_{cd} = A_{cs} \cdot L = 22 \times 60 = 1320 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the recharge rate through the streambed**

Assuming the streambed infiltration rate is $I_{sb} = 2 \text{ cm/hr} = 0.02$ m/hr and the effective wetted area is $A_{bed} = B \cdot L = 8 \times 60 = 480$ m²:

$$
Q_{cd} = A_{bed} \cdot I_{sb} = 480 \times 0.02 = 9.6 \text{ m}^3 / \text{hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the time to drain the impoundment**

$$
t_{cd} = \frac{V_{cd}}{Q_{cd}} = \frac{1320}{9.6} = 137.5 \text{ hours} \approx 5.7 \text{ days}
$$

> This is typical — check-dams are *meant* to hold water for **multiple days** to allow slow seepage.

**Step 5 — Solve the spillway width from the ogee equation**

$$
Q_{sp} = C \cdot L_{sp} \cdot H_{sp}^{3/2}
$$

Rearrange to find $L_{sp}$:

$$
L_{sp} = \frac{Q_{sp}}{C \cdot H_{sp}^{3/2}}
$$

Compute $H_{sp}^{3/2}$:

$$
H_{sp}^{3/2} = 0.6^{1.5} = 0.6 \times \sqrt{0.6} = 0.6 \times 0.7746 = 0.4648
$$

Substitute:

$$
L_{sp} = \frac{25}{1.7 \times 0.4648} = \frac{25}{0.7901} \approx 31.64 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Round up and provide the design**

Adopt a spillway of length **32 m**. This shows that even for a small check dam, the spillway can be quite long — a key engineering design insight.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sizing a Gully Plug (Channel Recharge)

> **Problem (typical 3- to 7-mark KTU question):**
> A gully in a lateritic terrain has a rectangular cross-section of width 2 m and depth 1.5 m. A gully plug of effective height $h = 1.0$ m is constructed. The upstream pool extends 8 m. The infiltration rate of the gully bed is $I_g = 4$ cm/hr. Calculate (a) volume of water stored behind one plug, (b) time to drain, and (c) recommended spacing of successive plugs along the gully.

**Step 1 — Storage volume of a single plug**

$$
V_{gp} = B \cdot h \cdot L = 2.0 \times 1.0 \times 8 = 16 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Recharge rate through bed (no clogging considered)**

$$
Q_{gp} = B \cdot L \cdot I_g = 2.0 \times 8 \times 0.04 = 0.64 \text{ m}^3 / \text{hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Drain time**

$$
t_{gp} = \frac{V_{gp}}{Q_{gp}} = \frac{16}{0.64} = 25 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Spacing of successive plugs**

Rule of thumb: spacing = $5$ to $10 \times$ plug height. With $h = 1.0$ m, recommended spacing is **5 – 10 m** to prevent cascading failure during a single storm.

---

## 3.4 Consolidated Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Basin | Stream (Check Dam) | Channel (Gully Plug) |
|-----------|-------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Typical capacity (m³) | 500 – 50 000 | 1000 – 1 00 000 | 5 – 500 |
| Typical height (m) | 0.6 – 1.2 | 1.0 – 3.0 | 0.5 – 1.5 |
| Infiltration area | Basin floor | Streambed | Gully bed |
| Drain time (hours) | 6 – 24 | 24 – 168 (1 – 7 days) | 12 – 48 |
| Typical efficiency | 50 – 80 % | 60 – 90 % | 30 – 60 % |
| Failure mode | Siltation, breaching | Overtopping, piping | Undermining, side-overflow |
| Design flood | 10-yr | 25-yr | 5-yr |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a recharge basin and a percolation tank. Mention any three siting criteria for a percolation tank.**

**Model Answer:**

A **recharge basin** is an *off-stream, artificially constructed shallow pond* on permeable terrain, fed by a diverted source of surface water. A **percolation tank** is a *small check dam built across a natural stream* in the upper catchment, which stores the *stream's own runoff* and recharges the underlying aquifer through the streambed.

**Three siting criteria for a percolation tank:**

1. The tank should be located in the **upper third of the catchment** to maximise runoff capture.
2. The **streambed should be highly permeable** (sandy or gravelly, infiltration rate > 1 cm/hr).
3. The **foundation should be sound** (rocky or stiff clay) to support the bund wall.
4. The **water table** in the post-monsoon period should be at least **3 – 4 m below ground** to provide storage space.

> *(Any three, 1 mark each; final integration 0.5 mark for crisp presentation.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of gully plugs in groundwater recharge. State the design spacing of gully plugs in a typical gully.**

**Model Answer:**

A **gully plug** is a low masonry or loose-boulder barrier built across a gully or first-order stream to *obstruct the flow, impound water, and enhance infiltration through the gully bed*. It is the most elementary form of **channel recharge**.

Functions:
- Reduces the longitudinal slope, hence reduces erosion velocity.
- Creates a *staircase of mini-reservoirs* that recharge the underlying weathered/fractured rock aquifer.
- Traps silt and gradually converts the gully into a vegetated terrace.

**Design spacing:**
- Typically **5 to 10 times the height of the plug**.
- For a 1 m high plug, spacing is **5 – 10 m** along the gully length.

> *(Concept 1.5 marks, spacing 1.5 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** – infiltrations in cm/hr vs. depths in m. Lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the clogging correction factor $C_c$** – examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 3. **Omitting spillway design** – even if not asked directly, a complete answer mentions a safe spillway.
> 4. **Writing only formulas without substitution** – in numerical sub-parts, you must **show the substitution and answer in boxed units**.
> 5. **Confusing basin recharge with stream recharge** – the source of water and the infiltration surface are different.

---

### Question A – **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **basin method of artificial recharge**. List the design considerations and the formula used for sizing.

**(b) [7 marks]** A rectangular recharge basin is proposed with a length-to-width ratio of 3:1 and a design depth of 0.9 m. The field infiltration rate of the soil is 8 cm/hr. Determine the dimensions of the basin required to recharge a runoff volume of 2400 m³. Take the clogging factor as 0.6 and compute the drain time.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### (a) Description, Sketch & Design of Basin Method (7 marks)

A **recharge basin** is a shallow, rectangular or square artificial depression excavated on highly permeable terrain (sand, sandy loam, weathered rock) into which surface water is diverted and impounded. The water infiltrates by gravity into the underlying aquifer.

**Working Principle:** When the basin is filled, water exerts a hydrostatic head and infiltrates downward. With time, the water level drops; the rate is governed by the soil's infiltration capacity, which itself decreases as silt accumulates.

**Design Considerations (5 points – 1 mark each):**
1. **Water table depth** must be > 2 m below ground in post-monsoon.
2. **Soil permeability / infiltration rate** determined by a double-ring infiltrometer test.
3. **Runoff availability** – design intake ≤ 80 % of seasonal runoff.
4. **Siltation** – upstream silt trap of capacity ≥ 10 % of basin volume.
5. **Freeboard & spillway** – 0.3 m freeboard and a 10-yr return period spillway.

**Sizing Formula:**

$$
V_b = L \cdot B \cdot d
$$

where $V_b$ is the storage volume, $L$ the length, $B$ the width, $d$ the design depth.

*Sketch:* (Refer to cross-section block diagram in SECTION 4.3 above.)

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Ratio $L : B = 3 : 1$
- Depth $d = 0.9$ m
- Infiltration rate $I = 8$ cm/hr
- Runoff to recharge $V = 2400$ m³
- Clogging factor $C_c = 0.6$

**Step 1 — Volume equation in terms of B:**

$$
V = L \cdot B \cdot d = 3B \cdot B \cdot 0.9 = 2.7 \, B^2
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for B:**

$$
B^2 = \frac{2400}{2.7} = 888.89
$$

$$
B = \sqrt{888.89} \approx 29.81 \text{ m} \quad \text{(round to 30 m)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute L:**

$$
L = 3B = 3 \times 29.81 = 89.44 \text{ m} \quad \text{(round to 90 m)}
$$

**Step 4 — Drain time:**

Convert d to cm: $d = 90$ cm.

$$
t_d = \frac{d}{I \cdot C_c} = \frac{90}{8 \times 0.6} = \frac{90}{4.8} = 18.75 \text{ hours}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{B \approx 30 \text{ m}, \quad L \approx 90 \text{ m}, \quad t_d \approx 18.75 \text{ hours}}
$$

---

### Question B – **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **stream method of artificial recharge using a check dam**. List four design criteria.

**(b) [7 marks]** A masonry check dam with bottom width 6 m, side slope 1:1, and design depth 1.5 m is built across a sandy stream. The impoundment extends 40 m upstream. The streambed infiltration rate is 3 cm/hr. Compute (i) the storage volume, (ii) the recharge rate, and (iii) the time required for the impoundment to drain completely. Take a clogging factor of 0.7.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### (a) Check Dam Description & Design (7 marks)

A **check dam (nala bund)** is a small barrier constructed across a seasonal stream. It serves two purposes: (i) impound stream flow to form a small reservoir, and (ii) recharge the underlying aquifer by forced infiltration through the streambed.

**Working Principle:** By raising the local water level, the **wetted streambed area increases**, the **hydrostatic head increases**, and the **residence time of water lengthens** — all three factors accelerate infiltration.

**Design Criteria (4 points – 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Site Selection:** Narrow, geologically stable gorge with permeable streambed.
2. **Dam Height:** 1.0 – 2.0 m above the bed; max 3.0 m.
3. **Spillway:** Masonry ogee with 25-yr return period flood capacity.
4. **Silt Control:** Upstream boulder screen or silt-trap with 20 % excess capacity.

*Sketch:* (Refer to stream-recharge flow in SECTION 4.2 above.)

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Bottom width $B = 6$ m
- Side slope $s = 1$ (i.e., $1H : 1V$)
- Design depth $d = 1.5$ m
- Impoundment length $L = 40$ m
- Infiltration rate $I = 3$ cm/hr
- Clogging factor $C_c = 0.7$

**Step 1 — Cross-section area (trapezoidal):**

$$
A_{cs} = B \cdot d + s \cdot d^2 = (6 \times 1.5) + (1 \times 1.5^2)
$$

$$
A_{cs} = 9 + 2.25 = 11.25 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Storage volume:**

$$
V_{cd} = A_{cs} \cdot L = 11.25 \times 40 = 450 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Recharge rate through the wetted streambed:**

Wetted bed area $A_{bed} = B \cdot L = 6 \times 40 = 240$ m².
Convert infiltration: $I = 3$ cm/hr = $0.03$ m/hr.

$$
Q_{cd} = A_{bed} \cdot I \cdot C_c = 240 \times 0.03 \times 0.7
$$

$$
Q_{cd} = 5.04 \text{ m}^3 / \text{hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Time to drain:**

$$
t_{cd} = \frac{V_{cd}}{Q_{cd}} = \frac{450}{5.04} \approx 89.29 \text{ hours} \approx 3.72 \text{ days}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{V_{cd} = 450 \text{ m}^3, \quad Q_{cd} = 5.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}, \quad t_{cd} \approx 89.3 \text{ hours} \; (3.7 \text{ days})}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Board Exam Friendly)**

- **Three methods covered:** Basin (off-stream spreading), Stream (check dam on natural stream), Channel (gully plugs/contour trenches). Know which one to use based on **site topography, source availability, and aquifer type**.
- **Basin depth:** 0.6 – 1.2 m (deeper = more evaporation loss). Always quote a **clogging factor $C_c = 0.5 – 0.75$**.
- **Check dam height:** 1 – 2 m. Spillway designed for **25-yr flood**.
- **Gully plug spacing:** 5 – 10 × plug height. Each plug creates a pool 12 – 48 hours long.
- **Core formulas (5 must-know):**
  1. $V = L \cdot B \cdot d$ (volume)
  2. $Q = A \cdot I \cdot C_c$ (recharge rate)
  3. $t_d = \dfrac{d}{I \cdot C_c}$ (drain time)
  4. $A_{cs} = B \cdot d + s \cdot d^2$ (trapezoidal cross-section)
  5. $Q_{sp} = C \cdot L_{sp} \cdot H^{3/2}$ (spillway discharge)
- **Unit consistency:** If $I$ is in cm/hr, $d$ must be in cm. If both in m, both must be in m. **Mixing is the #1 mark-loser.**
- **Design flood return periods:** Basin → 10-yr; Check dam → 25-yr; Gully plug → 5-yr.
- **Infiltration rates (must remember for soils):**
  - **Sand:** 2 – 25 cm/hr
  - **Sandy loam:** 1 – 5 cm/hr
  - **Clay:** < 0.5 cm/hr (not suitable for surface-spreading methods)
  - **Weathered rock:** 1 – 3 cm/hr
- **Field test for infiltration:** **Double-Ring Infiltrometer** (CGWB standard).
- **Government benchmark:** **CGWB (Central Ground Water Board)** publishes master plans for recharge structures — quote this authority in answer introductions for an extra +0.5 mark.
- **Spillway coefficient $C$:** Ogee → 1.7; Broad-crested → 1.45; Sharp-crested → 1.4. Memorise these three.
- **Most-asked comparative line:** "Basin vs Stream vs Channel — write this as a 5-row table in your exam; examiner will give 1 mark just for the comparison even if nothing else is correct."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ditch and Furrow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ditch and Furrow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Ditch and Furrow Irrigation System** is a traditional **surface irrigation method** in which water is conveyed from the water source (well, reservoir, pond, or harvested rainwater tank) through a network of **open earth channels (ditches)** and subsequently distributed across the agricultural field via small, parallel, shallow trenches called **furrows** that run alongside the crop rows.

In the context of **Rainwater Harvesting (OECET832)**, the Ditch and Furrow system forms the **conveyance and distribution subsystem** of a complete RWH scheme. Harvested runoff is first stored and then routed through ditches to reach the *command area*, where furrows deliver water directly to the root zone of crops via gravity flow.

The system is classified under **gravity-fed open-channel irrigation** and is one of the oldest, simplest, and most economical methods documented in agricultural engineering literature.

**System Components (KTU Standard Vocabulary):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Head Ditch (Main Ditch)** | Conveys water from the storage tank/pond to the field boundary |
| **Branch / Field Ditch** | Distributes water to individual sub-plots |
| **Turnout (OF – Outlet Flume)** | Controls discharge from ditch into furrows |
| **Furrow** | Small parallel channel in which water flows down the field slope |
| **Check / Drop Structure** | Prevents erosion in ditches with steep gradients |
| **Tail Water Escape** | Removes excess runoff at the lower end of the furrow |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of the Ditch and Furrow system as the **"veins and capillaries of a leaf"**:
> - The **ditch** is like the *main vein* — a larger channel carrying bulk water.
> - The **furrow** is like the *tiny capillaries* — small grooves that release water drop-by-drop right where the plant roots need it.

**Real-world example:** Imagine pouring water from a bucket into a garden. If you dump it all at once, the soil floods and most water runs off. Instead, if you pour it into a long, narrow trench (furrow) running beside each row of plants, the water seeps sideways and downward, **reaching the roots slowly and uniformly** — just like a controlled IV drip for plants.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Manning's Roughness Coefficient (n)** for earth ditches: **0.025 – 0.040** (dimensionless).
- **Recommended furrow slope:** **0.1% to 0.5%** (avoid > 1% to prevent erosion).
- **Standard furrow spacing:** **0.6 m – 1.0 m** depending on crop.
- **Furrow cross-section:** typically a **parabolic or trapezoidal** shape, **15–25 cm** deep.
- **Field application efficiency:** **50% – 70%** for properly designed systems.
- **Maximum non-erosive flow velocity** in earth ditches: **0.6 – 0.9 m/s**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->
