# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Ditch and Furrow system operates on a sequence of **gravity-driven hydraulics** combined with **infiltration physics**. Below is the structured logic flow:

### Step 1: Source Identification & Conveyance Initiation
- Harvested rainwater (from rooftop, farm pond, check dam, percolation tank) is routed to a **head water control structure** (e.g., division box or measuring flume).
- Water enters the **main ditch** at a controlled, measured discharge.

### Step 2: Conveyance Through Ditch Network
- The ditch conveys water with minimal seepage and evaporation loss.
- **Manning's equation** governs the flow velocity and discharge capacity.
- For long ditches, **check structures** are placed at intervals to control water level and prevent scouring.

### Step 3: Diversion into Furrows
- At the head of each furrow, a **turnout** (outlet) regulates the inflow.
- The turnout may be a simple cut, a pipe, or a gated orifice.

### Step 4: Furrow Infiltration (The Core Process)
- As water advances down the furrow, it infiltrates **laterally and vertically** into the soil.
- Infiltration is governed by **Kostiakov's equation** (time-based) or **Philip's equation**.
- The **opportunity time** (duration water sits in a section of furrow) determines the depth infiltrated.

### Step 5: Tail Water Management
- Water reaching the lower end of the furrow (tail water) is either:
  - Reused in a **tail-water recovery ditch**, or
  - Disposed of safely through a **wasteway**.

### Step 6: Cutoff & Advance Phase
- When the desired **advance time** is reached (water reaches the furrow end), inflow is cut off.
- **Recession phase** begins — water continues to infiltrate as it drains.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (The "Why" Behind the "How")

- **Why controlled conveyance?** Uncontrolled flow causes **erosion, waterlogging, and salinity**.
- **Why check structures?** Steep slopes accelerate water → ditch walls erode → siltation of furrows.
- **Why turnout control?** Equal distribution ensures **uniform crop growth**.
- **Why infiltration modeling?** Predicts how long to run water to deliver the required **net irrigation requirement**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical problem on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \cdot V$ | Continuity equation — Discharge = Area × Velocity | m³/s |
| 2 | $V = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$ | **Manning's equation** for open-channel flow | m/s |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{A}{P}$ | Hydraulic radius = Cross-sectional Area / Wetted Perimeter | m |
| 4 | $I = a \cdot t^{b}$ | **Kostiakov infiltration equation** (a, b are soil constants) | cm |
| 5 | $f = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) \cdot e^{-kt}$ | Horton's infiltration equation | cm/hr |
| 6 | $d = \dfrac{q \cdot t}{L}$ | Depth infiltrated = Discharge per unit length × Time / Furrow length | m |
| 7 | $ET_c = K_c \cdot ET_0$ | Crop evapotranspiration | mm/day |
| 8 | $NIR = ET_c - P_{eff}$ | Net Irrigation Requirement | mm |
| 9 | $V_{req} = \dfrac{A_{field} \times NIR}{E_a}$ | Volume of water required (Ea = application efficiency, 0.5–0.7) | m³ |
| 10 | $V = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot b \cdot d \cdot L$ | Volume of trapezoidal ditch (b = top width, d = depth, L = length) | m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize Manning's equation, Kostiakov's equation, and the Net Irrigation Requirement formula — these are KTU exam favourites, appearing in nearly every Module-4 question paper.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agriculture:** Primary irrigation method for row crops (maize, sugarcane, cotton, potato).
- **Rainwater harvesting integration:** Distributes harvested runoff efficiently in farmlands.
- **Watershed management:** Used in conjunction with percolation tanks and contour bunds.
- **Soil & water conservation:** Reduces runoff velocity, increases soil moisture profile.
- **Smart irrigation precursor:** Forms the basis for modern drip and surge-flow systems.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Discharge Capacity of a Ditch (Manning's Equation)

The Manning's equation is empirical, derived from the **Chezy formula** combined with experimental friction factors. Below is the rigorous transition.

### Step A: Start with Chezy's Formula

The Chezy formula for uniform flow in an open channel is:

$$
V = C \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot S}
$$

where:
- $V$ = mean velocity of flow (m/s)
- $C$ = Chezy's roughness coefficient (m$^{1/2}$/s)
- $R$ = hydraulic radius (m)
- $S$ = longitudinal slope of the channel (m/m)

### Step B: Empirical Substitution for Chezy's C

Through extensive experiments (Manning, 1889; later refined by Strickler), it was found that:

$$
C = \dfrac{R^{1/6}}{n}
$$

where $n$ is Manning's roughness coefficient (dimensionless), depending on channel surface.

### Step C: Substitute into Chezy's Formula

$$
V = \dfrac{R^{1/6}}{n} \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot S}
$$

Rewriting $\sqrt{R} = R^{1/2}$:

$$
V = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{1/6} \cdot R^{1/2} \cdot S^{1/2}
$$

### Step D: Combine the Exponents

Add the powers of $R$:

$$
1/6 + 1/2 = 1/6 + 3/6 = 4/6 = 2/3
$$

Therefore:

$$
V = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}
$$

This is the **Manning's equation**, valid for SI units (m, s). 

### Step E: Discharge Form

By continuity $Q = A \cdot V$:

$$
Q = \dfrac{A}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}
$$

This is the **fundamental design equation** for sizing a conveyance ditch.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Net Irrigation Requirement & Volume of Water Required

### Step A: Define Crop Water Need (ETc)

The crop evapotranspiration is the total water lost through evaporation from soil and transpiration from plants:

$$
ET_c = K_c \times ET_0
$$

where:
- $K_c$ = crop coefficient (e.g., 0.3 for initial stage, 1.15 for mid-season, 0.5 for late stage)
- $ET_0$ = reference evapotranspiration (mm/day), from Penman-Monteith or pan-evaporation data

### Step B: Subtract Effective Rainfall

Effective rainfall ($P_{eff}$) is the portion of rainfall that is actually stored in the root zone (typically 60–80% of total rainfall for design purposes):

$$
NIR = ET_c - P_{eff}
$$

where $NIR$ is the **Net Irrigation Requirement** (mm/season or mm/day).

### Step C: Adjust for Application Efficiency

Field application efficiency $E_a$ accounts for conveyance, deep percolation, and runoff losses:

$$
V_{req} = \dfrac{A_{field} \times NIR}{E_a}
$$

where:
- $V_{req}$ = volume of water to be delivered at the field inlet (m³)
- $A_{field}$ = area to be irrigated (m²)
- $NIR$ = net depth of water to be applied (m)
- $E_a$ = application efficiency (decimal, e.g., 0.65 for furrow irrigation)

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A farm of **1 hectare** (10,000 m²) is irrigated by a Ditch and Furrow system. The ditch has a **trapezoidal cross-section** with bottom width 0.6 m, side slope 1:1, depth of flow 0.4 m, longitudinal slope $S = 0.0008$, and Manning's $n = 0.030$. Crop water need: $ET_c = 6$ mm/day; effective rainfall $P_{eff} = 2$ mm/day; application efficiency $E_a = 0.60$. Determine:
> (a) Discharge capacity of the ditch.
> (b) Daily volume of water to be supplied at the field inlet.
> (c) Verify if the ditch can deliver the required discharge.

### Solution:

**Part (a):** Compute the geometric properties of the trapezoidal section.

Top width:

$$
b_{top} = b + 2 \cdot z \cdot d = 0.6 + 2(1)(0.4) = 1.4 \text{ m}
$$

Cross-sectional area of flow:

$$
A = \dfrac{(b + b_{top})}{2} \cdot d = \dfrac{(0.6 + 1.4)}{2} \times 0.4 = 0.4 \text{ m}^2
$$

Wetted perimeter:

$$
P = b + 2 \cdot d \cdot \sqrt{1 + z^2} = 0.6 + 2(0.4)\sqrt{1+1} = 0.6 + 0.8 \times 1.414 = 0.6 + 1.131 = 1.731 \text{ m}
$$

Hydraulic radius:

$$
R = \dfrac{A}{P} = \dfrac{0.4}{1.731} = 0.2311 \text{ m}
$$

Discharge by Manning's:

$$
Q = \dfrac{A}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
R^{2/3} = (0.2311)^{2/3}
$$

Taking logarithms: $\ln(0.2311) = -1.465$, so $\frac{2}{3} \times (-1.465) = -0.977$, hence $R^{2/3} = e^{-0.977} = 0.3764$.

$$
S^{1/2} = \sqrt{0.0008} = 0.02828
$$

Substitute:

$$
Q = \dfrac{0.4}{0.030} \times 0.3764 \times 0.02828
$$

$$
Q = 13.333 \times 0.3764 \times 0.02828 = 13.333 \times 0.01065 = 0.1420 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Part (b):** Compute the net irrigation requirement.

$$
NIR = ET_c - P_{eff} = 6 - 2 = 4 \text{ mm/day} = 0.004 \text{ m/day}
$$

Required volume:

$$
V_{req} = \dfrac{A_{field} \times NIR}{E_a} = \dfrac{10{,}000 \times 0.004}{0.60} = \dfrac{40}{0.60} = 66.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

Convert to discharge rate (assuming 12 hours of irrigation per day = 43,200 s):

$$
Q_{req} = \dfrac{66.67}{43{,}200} = 0.001543 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.543 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Part (c):** Verification.

$$
Q_{ditch} = 0.1420 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \gg Q_{req} = 0.001543 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

✅ **The ditch is heavily overdesigned** — it can carry ~92 times the required discharge. This indicates either the ditch could serve a much larger area, or a smaller ditch could be used for economic efficiency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to **convert units consistently**. Notice we converted mm/day → m/day, and m³/day → m³/s by dividing by irrigation duration in **seconds**, not hours. A 1-mark error here cascades.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Parameters & Selection Criteria

| Design Parameter | Typical Range (Earth Ditch) | Selection Basis |
|---|---|---|
| Longitudinal slope (S) | 0.05% – 0.3% | Steeper → erosion; flatter → silting |
| Manning's n | 0.025 – 0.040 | Vegetation, lining, soil texture |
| Furrow length | 50 m – 200 m | Slope, soil infiltration, field shape |
| Furrow spacing | 0.6 m – 1.0 m | Crop row spacing, soil lateral wicking |
| Furrow depth | 15 cm – 25 cm | Crop type, soil type |
| Flow velocity | 0.3 m/s – 0.9 m/s | Must be < non-erosive velocity |
| Application efficiency (Ea) | 0.50 – 0.70 | Soil texture, slope, management |
| Cutoff time | 70% of advance time | Balances adequacy vs. deep percolation |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Ditch and Furrow system of irrigation. List any four components with their function.**

**Model Answer:**

The Ditch and Furrow system is a surface irrigation method in which water is conveyed from the source through a network of open channels (ditches) and then distributed across the field through small parallel trenches (furrows) running along crop rows, where it infiltrates into the soil under gravity.

**Four key components:**

1. **Head Ditch** — Carries water from the storage/diverting structure to the field.
2. **Branch/Field Ditch** — Distributes water to various sub-plots within the field.
3. **Turnout (Outlet)** — Controls and directs flow from ditch into individual furrows.
4. **Furrow** — A small V- or U-shaped trench in which water flows and infiltrates to irrigate the root zone.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "**furrow**" with "**border**" or "**check basin**". A furrow is a **narrow channel between crop rows**; a border is a **wide strip between raised borders**; a check basin is a **level, surrounded plot**. 1 mark lost per wrong identification.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Manning's equation. Explain the significance of each term with units.**

**Model Answer:**

Manning's equation for uniform open-channel flow is:

$$
V = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}
$$

| Term | Meaning | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| $V$ | Mean velocity of flow | m/s |
| $n$ | Manning's roughness coefficient (depends on channel surface) | dimensionless |
| $R$ | Hydraulic radius (A/P) | m |
| $S$ | Longitudinal energy slope (= bed slope for uniform flow) | m/m |

**Significance:** This equation is the **backbone of ditch design** — it allows engineers to size a channel that will carry the required discharge without eroding or silting up.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**a)** Explain the working of a Ditch and Furrow irrigation system with a neat labelled schematic. Discuss the **design considerations** for ditches and furrows. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A trapezoidal earth ditch has bottom width 0.8 m, side slopes 1.5:1 (H:V), depth of flow 0.5 m, longitudinal slope 0.0005, and Manning's n = 0.035. Compute the **discharge** carried by the ditch. If the same ditch is to be lined with concrete (n = 0.015), what is the **percentage increase** in discharge capacity? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**Working of Ditch and Furrow System (5 points × ~1.4 marks each):**

1. Water from the **source (RWH tank / farm pond / well)** is delivered to a **head regulator / division box** that controls and measures the flow.

2. Water enters the **main ditch**, which conveys it along the **upper boundary** of the field with a gentle longitudinal slope (0.05%–0.3%) to ensure gravity flow without erosion.

3. **Branch/field ditches** divide the flow to individual plots or sub-units of the field.

4. At the head of each furrow, a **turnout** delivers water into the furrow at a controlled, small discharge.

5. Water **advances down the furrow** (advance phase), with simultaneous **infiltration** into the root zone. When the water front reaches the lower end (advance time), the inflow is **cut off** (cutoff phase), and the **recession phase** begins where water continues to infiltrate as it drains.

6. **Tail water** reaching the lower end is either reused in a tail-water recovery ditch or drained via a wasteway.

**Design Considerations:**

- **Longitudinal slope:** 0.1% – 0.5% for furrows; flatter for ditches to balance conveyance vs. erosion.
- **Cross-section:** Trapezoidal preferred for earth ditches (stable side slopes).
- **Non-erosive velocity:** Must be maintained < 0.9 m/s in earth ditches.
- **Freeboard:** Usually 20–30% above design depth to prevent overflow.
- **Furrow length:** Limited by slope, soil infiltration rate, and field size (typically < 200 m).
- **Furrow spacing:** Based on crop row spacing and lateral capillary spread of soil.
- **Lining:** Lined ditches reduce seepage losses from 20–40% to < 5%.

**Schematic:** (Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram — show all components from source to tail water.)

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:**
- $b = 0.8$ m (bottom width)
- $z = 1.5$ (side slope, H:V)
- $d = 0.5$ m (depth of flow)
- $S = 0.0005$
- $n_1 = 0.035$ (earth), $n_2 = 0.015$ (concrete)

**Step 1: Top width**

$$
b_{top} = b + 2 z d = 0.8 + 2(1.5)(0.5) = 0.8 + 1.5 = 2.3 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Cross-sectional area**

$$
A = \dfrac{(b + b_{top})}{2} \times d = \dfrac{(0.8 + 2.3)}{2} \times 0.5 = \dfrac{3.1}{2} \times 0.5 = 0.775 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Wetted perimeter**

$$
P = b + 2 d \sqrt{1 + z^2} = 0.8 + 2(0.5)\sqrt{1 + 2.25} = 0.8 + 1.0 \times \sqrt{3.25}
$$

$$
P = 0.8 + 1.0 \times 1.8028 = 2.6028 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Hydraulic radius**

$$
R = \dfrac{A}{P} = \dfrac{0.775}{2.6028} = 0.2977 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5: Discharge (Earth lining, n = 0.035)**

$$
R^{2/3} = (0.2977)^{2/3}
$$

Using logs: $\ln(0.2977) = -1.2127$; $\frac{2}{3}(-1.2127) = -0.8085$; $e^{-0.8085} = 0.4455$

$$
S^{1/2} = \sqrt{0.0005} = 0.02236
$$

$$
Q_1 = \dfrac{A}{n_1} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2} = \dfrac{0.775}{0.035} \times 0.4455 \times 0.02236
$$

$$
Q_1 = 22.143 \times 0.4455 \times 0.02236 = 22.143 \times 0.00996 = 0.2205 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 6: Discharge (Concrete lining, n = 0.015)**

$$
Q_2 = \dfrac{0.775}{0.015} \times 0.4455 \times 0.02236 = 51.667 \times 0.00996 = 0.5146 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 7: Percentage increase**

$$
\% \text{ increase} = \dfrac{Q_2 - Q_1}{Q_1} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.5146 - 0.2205}{0.2205} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ increase} = \dfrac{0.2941}{0.2205} \times 100 = 133.4\%
$$

**Result:** Concrete lining **increases the discharge capacity by 133.4%** compared to the earth ditch. (A shorter way: since $Q \propto 1/n$, ratio = $n_1/n_2 = 0.035/0.015 = 2.333$, i.e., $133.3\%$ increase — verify with same answer.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly (i) use **wetted perimeter incorrectly for top width** — top width is NOT part of wetted perimeter; only bottom width and slanted sides are. (ii) Forget the **square root of $(1+z^2)$** in $P$ formula. (iii) Confuse $Q \propto 1/n$ (which simplifies the percentage calculation) — this shortcut works only because $A$, $R$, $S$ are unchanged. **1–2 marks lost for each such slip.**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**a)** What are the **advantages and limitations** of the Ditch and Furrow system? Compare it briefly with **border irrigation** and **check basin irrigation**. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A farmer wants to irrigate a 2-hectare field with a Ditch and Furrow system using harvested rainwater. The crop water requirement is $ET_c = 5.5$ mm/day, effective rainfall is 1.5 mm/day, and application efficiency is 65%. The irrigation cycle is 8 hours/day. Calculate the **required discharge (L/s)** that the head ditch must deliver, and **size the ditch** (bottom width and depth) for a trapezoidal section (side slope 1:1) with slope 0.0004 and n = 0.030, assuming the flow depth equals the design depth. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**Advantages of Ditch and Furrow System (any 4 × 1 mark each):**

1. **Low capital cost** — no expensive pipes or sprinkler equipment; earth channels suffice.
2. **Simple technology** — easily understood and maintained by farmers.
3. **No energy requirement** — operates entirely on gravity.
4. **Suitable for most row crops** — maize, sugarcane, cotton, vegetables.
5. **Easy to integrate with RWH** — naturally compatible with farm pond-based harvesting systems.
6. **Flexible layout** — can be modified as per field shape and crop rotation.

**Limitations (any 4 × 1 mark each):**

1. **Low application efficiency (50–70%)** — significant losses to deep percolation and runoff.
2. **High conveyance losses** in unlined earth ditches (20–40% seepage).
3. **Requires precise land leveling and grading.**
4. **Erosion risk** on steeper slopes.
5. **Labour-intensive** — turnout operation, cutoff timing, tail-water management.
6. **Not suitable for sandy soils** with very high infiltration rates.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Ditch & Furrow | Border | Check Basin |
|---|---|---|---|
| Suitable for | Row crops | Close-growing crops | Paddy, orchards |
| Slope | 0.1%–0.5% | 0.2%–0.8% | Nearly level |
| Water application | Slow, in furrows | Sheet flow over strip | Standing water |
| Efficiency | 50–70% | 60–75% | 60–80% |
| Labour | Moderate | Low | High |

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:**
- $A_{field} = 2$ ha $= 20{,}000$ m²
- $ET_c = 5.5$ mm/day, $P_{eff} = 1.5$ mm/day, $E_a = 0.65$
- Irrigation time $t = 8$ hr/day $= 28{,}800$ s
- Ditch: side slope $z = 1$, slope $S = 0.0004$, $n = 0.030$

**Step 1: Net Irrigation Requirement**

$$
NIR = ET_c - P_{eff} = 5.5 - 1.5 = 4.0 \text{ mm/day} = 0.004 \text{ m/day}
$$

**Step 2: Volume required at field inlet**

$$
V_{req} = \dfrac{A_{field} \times NIR}{E_a} = \dfrac{20{,}000 \times 0.004}{0.65} = \dfrac{80}{0.65} = 123.08 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to discharge**

$$
Q_{req} = \dfrac{V_{req}}{t} = \dfrac{123.08}{28{,}800} = 0.00427 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 4.27 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Step 4: Size the ditch (assume bottom width $b = 0.5$ m, depth $d = 0.4$ m as trial)**

Top width:

$$
b_{top} = 0.5 + 2(1)(0.4) = 1.3 \text{ m}
$$

Area:

$$
A = \dfrac{(0.5 + 1.3)}{2} \times 0.4 = 0.36 \text{ m}^2
$$

Wetted perimeter:

$$
P = 0.5 + 2(0.4)\sqrt{2} = 0.5 + 0.8 \times 1.4142 = 0.5 + 1.1314 = 1.6314 \text{ m}
$$

Hydraulic radius:

$$
R = \dfrac{0.36}{1.6314} = 0.2207 \text{ m}
$$

$$
R^{2/3} = (0.2207)^{2/3}; \quad \ln(0.2207) = -1.510; \quad \tfrac{2}{3}(-1.510) = -1.007; \quad e^{-1.007} = 0.3653
$$

$$
S^{1/2} = \sqrt{0.0004} = 0.02
$$

$$
Q_{ditch} = \dfrac{0.36}{0.030} \times 0.3653 \times 0.02 = 12 \times 0.3653 \times 0.02 = 0.0877 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Since $Q_{ditch} = 0.0877 \gg Q_{req} = 0.00427$ m³/s, the chosen ditch is **adequate** (overdesigned, but acceptable for a small farm).

**Final answer:**
- **Required discharge:** $Q_{req} \approx 4.27$ L/s
- **Recommended ditch size:** bottom width **0.5 m**, depth **0.4 m**, trapezoidal with 1:1 side slopes (provides $Q_{capacity} \approx 87.7$ L/s — substantial safety margin for future expansion).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Common error — students compute **$NIR$ in mm but forget to convert to meters** before multiplying by area in m². The result will be off by a factor of 1000. Always write units at every step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Ditch and Furrow (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Surface irrigation using open earth ditches for conveyance and parallel furrows for in-field distribution.
- ✅ **Components:** Head ditch → Branch ditch → Turnout → Furrow → Tail water escape.
- ✅ **Manning's equation:** $V = \frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ — **memorize the exponents and units**.
- ✅ **Hydraulic radius:** $R = A / P$. **Top width is NOT part of wetted perimeter.**
- ✅ **Kostiakov infiltration:** $I = a \cdot t^b$ — used to model furrow water entry into soil.
- ✅ **NIR:** $NIR = ET_c - P_{eff}$ (always in same units, typically mm/day).
- ✅ **Volume required:** $V_{req} = A \times NIR / E_a$, then convert to discharge using irrigation time in **seconds**.
- ✅ **Application efficiency:** 50–70% for furrow systems; lining and proper cutoff improve it.
- ✅ **Recommended slope:** 0.1%–0.5% for furrows, ≤ 0.3% for ditches.
- ✅ **Non-erosive velocity in earth ditches:** 0.6–0.9 m/s.
- ✅ **Lining benefit:** Concrete lining reduces n, increasing $Q \propto 1/n$ by factor $n_{earth}/n_{concrete}$.
- ✅ **Comparison keywords:** Border irrigation = sheet flow on strips; Check basin = standing water; Furrow = narrow trench between rows.
- ✅ **Integration with RWH:** Harvested runoff → storage pond → head ditch → furrows → root zone — close the water cycle on-farm.
- ✅ **Examiner's favourite question:** "Compute the discharge of a trapezoidal ditch using Manning's equation" — practice the **5-step procedure** (Area → Perimeter → R → V → Q).
- ✅ **Common slip-ups:** unit mismatches, top width vs. wetted perimeter, time in seconds vs. hours, $E_a$ as percent vs. decimal.

> **Final Tip for KTU Aspirants:** In Part B numericals, always present your solution in a **numbered step format** with units at every stage. Examiners award step-marks, so even if the final answer is wrong, the logical process earns partial credit. **Draw the schematic first, then solve** — it shows structured thinking and secures the 2–3 marks allotted to the diagram itself.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flooding and Recharge Well Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flooding and Recharge Well Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Artificial Recharge — The Broader Context

**Artificial Recharge** is the engineered process of deliberately transferring surface water into underground aquifers to replenish groundwater, augment groundwater storage, and raise the declining water table. The KTU 2024 scheme classifies artificial recharge techniques broadly into:

- **Direct (Surface) Methods** — Spreading techniques (flooding, basin, ditch-cum-channel, recharge pits, recharge trenches)
- **Indirect (Subsurface) Methods** — Recharge wells, injection wells, recharge shafts
- **Combined Methods** — Integration of surface and subsurface structures

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Module 4 of *OECET832 – Rainwater Harvesting* focuses on the **direct surface spreading method of flooding** and the **subsurface recharge well methods**, including their design, suitability, and limitations.

---

## 1.2 Flooding Method — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Flooding Method):**
> *Flooding* is a surface artificial recharge technique in which excess surface water (river water, canal water, stored stormwater, or treated wastewater) is intentionally allowed to spread and **flood a large, flat, permeable land area**, so that water infiltrates downward under gravity to recharge the underlying aquifer.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Soak-Pit in a Field"

Imagine you are watering a **giant sponge laid flat on a tabletop**. You pour water gently over the entire surface — the water soaks downward through the sponge. This is exactly what flooding does: the **flat permeable land acts as the sponge**, the **surface water acts as the poured water**, and the **aquifer below acts as the storage tank** that captures and holds the infiltrated water.

In contrast, a *recharge well* would be like pushing water into the sponge through a **straw inserted vertically** from the top — bypassing the surface layer.

---

## 1.3 Recharge Well — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Recharge Well):**
> A *recharge well* is a vertical or inclined subsurface structure, drilled or dug through an **impervious or low-permeability overburden** to deliver water directly into a **permeable aquifer** lying beneath, thereby bypassing the low-permeability strata that would otherwise resist vertical infiltration.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Underground Injection Straw"

Think of a recharge well as a **straw pushed through the lid of a thermos bottle**:
- The **thermos lid** = the impervious clay layer near the ground surface
- The **hot water (drink) inside** = the deep permeable aquifer
- The **straw** = the recharge well
- You pour water through the straw directly into the aquifer, **bypassing the lid that would otherwise block it**.

---

## 1.4 Why These Two Methods Together?

The two methods are often taught together because they represent the **two extreme ends** of recharge practice:

| Parameter | Flooding Method | Recharge Well Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Gravity infiltration over a wide surface | Direct injection into a confined aquifer |
| **Strata Suitability** | Permeable topsoil/sandy overburden | Impervious overburden over deep aquifer |
| **Land Requirement** | Very large flat areas | Very small (a single point) |
| **Construction Cost** | Low | High (drilling + casing) |
| **Skill Needed** | Minimal | High (geotechnical + hydrogeological) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric — Units to Remember:**
> - Hydraulic conductivity **K** is in **m/day** or **cm/sec**.
> - Specific yield **S_y** is a **dimensionless** fraction (between **0.05 and 0.30** for most granular aquifers).
> - Recharge well discharge is expressed in **litres per second (lps)** or **m³/hour**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Flooding Method — Operational Theory

The flooding method is governed by the **Horton's Infiltration Equation** and the **Darcy's Law for vertical recharge**.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Source Identification** — A continuous or seasonal source of surface water (river, canal, reservoir, harvested rainwater) is identified upstream of the proposed recharge site.
2. **Site Selection** — A flat, gently sloping area with **highly permeable sandy or sandy-loam soil** is selected. Slope is generally kept below **2%** to prevent runoff and ensure uniform spreading.
3. **Diversion Structure** — A diversion channel or weir diverts water from the source to the flooding area.
4. **Spreading** — Water is released uniformly across the land surface using levees, border strips, or pipeline outlets.
5. **Infiltration** — Water moves vertically downward under gravity, governed by **Horton's equation**.
6. **Storage** — Recharged water joins the aquifer, raising the water table.
7. **Recovery / Drainage** — Excess un-infiltrated water is removed by a downstream drainage channel to prevent **waterlogging**.

### 2.1.2 Horton's Infiltration Equation — The Core Physics

Horton's equation describes how infiltration capacity **f(t)** decays exponentially with time during a flooding event:

$$f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

where:

- $f(t)$ = infiltration capacity at time $t$ (**mm/hr** or **cm/hr**)
- $f_0$ = initial infiltration capacity at $t = 0$ (**mm/hr**)
- $f_c$ = final (steady-state) infiltration capacity (**mm/hr**)
- $k$ = Horton's decay constant (**hr⁻¹**)
- $t$ = elapsed time since flooding began (**hours**)

### 2.1.3 Why Does Infiltration Capacity Decay?

During flooding, fine soil particles and entrapped air fill the surface pores → this **reduces hydraulic conductivity** of the topsoil. As time increases, the infiltration rate approaches a **constant steady-state value $f_c$**, which is the long-term vertical hydraulic conductivity $K_v$ of the unsaturated zone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> Horton's equation is critical for designing the **ponding time** of a flooding site. If $f_c$ is too low (e.g., < 5 mm/hr), flooding will cause waterlogging and the method is unsuitable — designers must then switch to a **recharge well** instead.

---

## 2.2 Recharge Well Method — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Hydrogeological Survey** — Identify a deep aquifer overlain by an impervious (clay/confining) layer using borehole data and resistivity surveys.
2. **Well Drilling** — Drill or bore a vertical hole through the impervious layer into the aquifer using rotary or percussion rigs.
3. **Casing Installation** — Install a **casing pipe** (PVC/MS/HDPE) through the impervious section to prevent collapse and seal off shallow aquifers.
4. **Screen Section** — The lower portion of the well, inside the target aquifer, is fitted with a **slotted/perforated screen** to allow water entry.
5. **Gravel Pack & Filter** — A graded gravel pack and sand filter surround the screen to prevent fines from entering and clogging the well.
6. **Recharge Inlet** — Surface water is delivered to the well head via a pipeline, with a **debris filter** and **air-release valve**.
7. **Injection & Recharge** — Water enters the aquifer through the screen under gravity (gravity-recharge well) or under pressure (pressure-recharge / injection well).
8. **Back-flushing** — Periodic reverse flow is used to clean the well screen.

### 2.2.2 Classification of Recharge Wells

| Type | Construction | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Open / Bored Recharge Well** | Dug or bored without casing; large diameter (1–3 m) | Recharge pits with wells in rural areas |
| **Cased Recharge Well** | MS/PVC casing with screen; small diameter (100–300 mm) | Urban recharge, deep aquifers |
| **Injection Well (Pressure Recharge)** | Fully cased and sealed; water injected under pressure | Confined aquifers, large municipal systems |
| **Recharge Shaft with Well** | Vertical shaft + borewell combination | Two-tier recharge (shallow + deep) |

### 2.2.3 Governing Equation — Darcy's Law for Radial Flow to/from a Well

When water is injected into a well, it flows radially outward through the aquifer, governed by **Darcy's Law**:

$$Q = \frac{2 \pi \cdot K \cdot b \cdot (H - h_w)}{\ln\left(\dfrac{R}{r_w}\right)}$$

where:

- $Q$ = injection / recharge discharge (**m³/sec**)
- $K$ = hydraulic conductivity of aquifer (**m/sec**)
- $b$ = aquifer thickness / saturated depth (**m**)
- $H$ = piezometric head at the well during recharge (**m**)
- $h_w$ = head at the well screen (**m**)
- $R$ = radius of influence (**m**)
- $r_w$ = radius of the well (**m**)

### 2.2.4 Specific Yield and Recharge Volume

The volume of water that an aquifer can store by drainage is given by:

$$V_r = S_y \cdot A \cdot \Delta h$$

where:

- $V_r$ = volume of water recharged (**m³**)
- $S_y$ = specific yield (**dimensionless**)
- $A$ = area of aquifer footprint (**m²**)
- $\Delta h$ = rise in water table (**m**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Field Rule of Thumb (KTU Standard):**
> For a typical alluvial aquifer in Kerala, $S_y \approx 0.12$ and $K \approx 5$–$15$ m/day. These values must be assumed in numerical problems unless otherwise stated.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c)\,e^{-k t}$ | Horton's infiltration — flooding design | mm/hr, hr |
| 2 | $F(t) = f_c \, t + \dfrac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k}\,(1 - e^{-k t})$ | Cumulative infiltration depth $F(t)$ over time $t$ | mm |
| 3 | $Q = \dfrac{2 \pi K b (H - h_w)}{\ln(R / r_w)}$ | Recharge well discharge (Thiem-type) | m³/sec |
| 4 | $V_r = S_y \cdot A \cdot \Delta h$ | Recharge volume from water-table rise | m³ |
| 5 | $R \approx 3000 \cdot s \sqrt{K}$ (Sichardt's empirical) | Approximate radius of influence | m |
| 6 | $Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$ | Darcy's Law (general flow through porous media) | m³/sec |
| 7 | $T = K \cdot b$ | Transmissivity of aquifer | m²/day |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse **Specific Yield $S_y$** (used for unconfined aquifers, water-table storage) with **Storativity $S$** (used for confined aquifers, typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$). Exam questions frequently test this distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cumulative Infiltration Depth $F(t)$ for Flooding

Horton's infiltration capacity function is:

$$f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c)\,e^{-k t}$$

**Step 1 — Recognize that cumulative infiltration is the time integral of infiltration rate.**

By definition, the cumulative depth of water infiltrated from time $0$ to time $T$ is the area under the $f(t)$ curve:

$$F(T) = \int_{0}^{T} f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Horton expression into the integral.**

$$F(T) = \int_{0}^{T} \left[ f_c + (f_0 - f_c)\,e^{-k t} \right] dt$$

**Step 3 — Split the integral into two simpler integrals (linearity of integration).**

$$F(T) = \int_{0}^{T} f_c \, dt \; + \; (f_0 - f_c) \int_{0}^{T} e^{-k t} \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the first integral (a constant).**

$$\int_{0}^{T} f_c \, dt = f_c \cdot T$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the second integral (standard exponential).**

$$\int_{0}^{T} e^{-k t} \, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{-k t}}{-k} \right]_{0}^{T} = \frac{1}{-k}\,(e^{-kT} - 1) = \frac{1 - e^{-kT}}{k}$$

**Step 6 — Combine the two parts.**

$$F(T) = f_c \, T \; + \; (f_0 - f_c) \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-kT}}{k}$$

**Step 7 — Final form (the KTU textbook result).**

$$\boxed{F(T) = f_c \, T + \frac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k}\,\left(1 - e^{-k T}\right)}$$

> **Conversion Logic of Each Step:**
> Step 1 invokes the geometric meaning of integration (area under the curve). Step 2 plugs in the physical law. Step 3 uses linearity — a property that allows splitting integrals. Step 4 is trivial because the integral of a constant is the constant times the interval. Step 5 uses the standard antiderivative of $e^{ax}$ which is $\frac{e^{ax}}{a}$. Step 6 recombines, and Step 7 is the rearranged textbook form.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Yield Recharge Equation

For an unconfined aquifer receiving recharge, the volume of water added per unit rise in water table is governed by **specific yield**.

**Step 1 — Define specific yield $S_y$.**

$S_y$ is the volume of water released from a unit volume of aquifer per unit decline in water table (dimensionless, fraction).

**Step 2 — Express water released per unit horizontal area per unit decline.**

If the water table drops by $\Delta h$ over an area $A$, the volume of water drained out is:

$$V = S_y \cdot A \cdot \Delta h$$

**Step 3 — Reverse the logic for recharge.**

When recharge adds water back, the water table **rises** by $\Delta h$, and the same volume equation applies (water enters pore space):

$$\boxed{V_r = S_y \cdot A \cdot \Delta h}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for water-table rise given recharge volume.**

$$\Delta h = \frac{V_r}{S_y \cdot A}$$

**Step 5 — Convert $V_r$ from litres to m³ for KTU numerical problems.**

$$V_r\,(\text{m}^3) = \frac{V_r\,(\text{Litres})}{1000}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Step 1 anchors the physical definition. Step 2 is a direct application of the definition. Step 3 uses the principle of reversibility — recharge and discharge are inverse processes in unconfined aquifers. Step 4 isolates the desired variable. Step 5 is a unit conversion required because KTU often gives rainfall/recharge volume in litres.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A flooding recharge site has the Horton parameters $f_0 = 80$ mm/hr, $f_c = 10$ mm/hr, and $k = 0.4$ hr⁻¹. The site is flooded continuously for **6 hours**. Determine:
**(a)** The infiltration capacity at $t = 6$ hr.
**(b)** The total cumulative infiltration depth over the 6-hour period.
**(c)** The volume of water infiltrated (in m³) if the flooding area is **5 hectares (50,000 m²)**.

### Solution

#### Part (a) — Infiltration capacity at $t = 6$ hr

Apply the Horton equation:

$$f(6) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c)\,e^{-k t}$$

Substitute $f_0 = 80$, $f_c = 10$, $k = 0.4$, $t = 6$:

$$f(6) = 10 + (80 - 10)\,e^{-0.4 \times 6}$$

Compute the exponent first:

$$-0.4 \times 6 = -2.4$$

Evaluate the exponential:

$$e^{-2.4} \approx 0.0907$$

Multiply by $(f_0 - f_c) = 70$:

$$70 \times 0.0907 \approx 6.35 \text{ mm/hr}$$

Add $f_c$:

$$f(6) = 10 + 6.35 = 16.35 \text{ mm/hr}$$

$$\boxed{f(6) \approx 16.35 \text{ mm/hr}}$$

#### Part (b) — Cumulative infiltration depth $F(6)$

Use the derived Horton cumulative formula:

$$F(T) = f_c \, T + \frac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k}\,\left(1 - e^{-k T}\right)$$

Substitute values:

$$F(6) = 10 \times 6 + \frac{70}{0.4}\,\left(1 - e^{-2.4}\right)$$

Compute term by term:

- First term: $10 \times 6 = 60$ mm
- $\frac{70}{0.4} = 175$ mm
- $1 - e^{-2.4} = 1 - 0.0907 = 0.9093$

Multiply:

$$175 \times 0.9093 \approx 159.13 \text{ mm}$$

Add to first term:

$$F(6) = 60 + 159.13 = 219.13 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{F(6) \approx 219.13 \text{ mm} \approx 0.219 \text{ m}}$$

#### Part (c) — Volume of water infiltrated

$$V = F(6) \times A = 0.219 \text{ m} \times 50{,}000 \text{ m}^2$$

$$V = 10{,}956.5 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 10{,}957 \text{ m}^3 \approx 10.96 \text{ million litres}}$$

> **Conversion Logic of Each Step:**
> Part (a) is a direct substitution into Horton's instantaneous rate. Part (b) uses the time-integrated (cumulative) form derived above — the trick is to split into the steady term and the decay term. Part (c) is a simple volume = depth × area conversion, with the depth converted from mm to m before multiplying by m² to obtain m³.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Flooding Method

The block diagram below shows the operational sequence and component interactions of a flooding recharge system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Surface Water Source River Canal Reservoir] --> B1[Diversion Weir or Head Regulator]
    B1 --> C1[Inlet Channel Carrying Water to Recharge Field]
    C1 --> D1[Spreading Basin or Flooding Field Flat Permeable Land]
    D1 --> E1[Infiltration Through Unsaturated Zone]
    E1 --> F1[Percolation Into Unconfined Aquifer]
    F1 --> G1[Water Table Rise Increase in Storage]
    D1 --> H1[Excess Runoff Returned via Drainage Channel]
    D1 --> I1[Evaporation Losses at Surface]
    G1 --> J1[Recovery via Wells or Springs Downstream]

    classDef source fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000

    class A1,B1,C1 source
    class D1,E1,F1,G1,J1 process
    class H1,I1 loss
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue nodes** = Water source and supply infrastructure.
- **Green nodes** = Infiltration and recharge process (the desired pathway).
- **Red nodes** = Losses (runoff + evaporation) that reduce recharge efficiency.
- The **green pathway** (A1 → J1) is the intended hydrological flow.
- The **red branches** (H1, I1) represent efficiency losses a designer must minimize.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Recharge Well Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Surface Water Source Harvested Rainwater Treated Wastewater] --> F1[Debris Filter and Sand Filter at Wellhead]
    F1 --> V1[Air Release Valve and Flow Meter]
    V1 --> C1[Casing Pipe Through Impervious Stratum]
    C1 --> SC1[Slotted Screen Section Inside Aquifer]
    SC1 --> GP1[Gravel Pack Surrounding Screen]
    SC1 --> AQ1[Radial Injection Into Permeable Aquifer]
    AQ1 --> R1[Groundwater Mounding Around Well Radius of Influence R]
    R1 --> ST1[Long Term Storage in Aquifer Pore Space]
    SC1 --> CL1[Potential Clogging of Screen Fines or Biofilm]
    ST1 --> RE1[Recovery via Pumping Wells or Natural Discharge]

    classDef surface fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef structural fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef hydro fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef issue fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000

    class S1,F1,V1 surface
    class C1,SC1,GP1 structural
    class AQ1,R1,ST1,RE1 hydro
    class CL1 issue
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue nodes** = Surface components (intake + filtration + metering).
- **Purple nodes** = Structural components (casing, screen, gravel pack).
- **Green nodes** = Hydrogeological response (radial flow + storage).
- **Red node CL1** = The principal maintenance issue — periodic **back-flushing** is required to clear clogging.

---

## 4.3 Design Logic Matrix — Choosing Between Flooding and Recharge Well

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{Is Topsoil Permeable K greater than 1e to power minus 4 m per sec?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Land Area Available greater than 5 hectares?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is Impervious Stratum Confining the Aquifer?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Use Flooding Method Low Cost Surface Spreading]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Use Recharge Pits or Trenches Module 3 Alternative]
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Use Recharge Well Method Direct Injection]
    Q3 -->|No| R4[Use Recharge Basin or Ditch Method]

    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef method fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000

    class Q1,Q2,Q3 decision
    class R1,R2,R3,R4 method
```

**Reading the decision tree:**
- Start with the **hydrogeological question**: is the surface permeable?
- If **Yes** + **land is available** → choose **Flooding**.
- If **No** (impervious overburden present) → choose **Recharge Well**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*Define the flooding method of artificial recharge. State any two conditions for its suitability.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Flooding Method:** It is a surface artificial recharge technique in which surface water is intentionally spread over a large, flat, permeable land area so that it infiltrates downward to recharge the underlying aquifer.
>
> **Suitability conditions:**
> 1. The site must have **flat or gently sloping** topography (slope < 2%).
> 2. The **topsoil must be highly permeable** (sandy or sandy-loam with $K > 10^{-4}$ m/sec).
> 3. A **continuous and reliable source of surface water** must be available.
> 4. The **water table must be deep enough** to accommodate the recharged water (typically > 5 m below ground).

**Mark Split (3 M):** Definition (1 M) + Any two conditions (1 M each).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*Differentiate between a recharge well and a recharge pit. Give one situation where a recharge well is preferred over a recharge pit.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Recharge Well | Recharge Pit |
|---|---|---|
| **Depth** | Deep, reaches aquifer (10–200 m) | Shallow, up to 5 m |
| **Strata crossed** | Pierces impervious layer | Stops above the impervious layer |
| **Construction cost** | High (drilling + casing) | Low (manual digging) |
| **Suitable aquifer** | Deep confined / semi-confined | Shallow unconfined |

> **Situation where recharge well is preferred:** When the target aquifer is **deep and overlain by an impervious clay layer** that prevents vertical recharge, a recharge well is the only option as it pierces through the impervious layer to inject water directly into the aquifer.

**Mark Split (3 M):** Any three differences in tabular form (1 M each) + situation (½ M) + reason (½ M).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Recharge Well Numerical Pitfall:**
> 1. Many students **forget to convert $K$** from m/day to m/sec when using the Thiem-type formula — losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Specific yield $S_y$ is dimensionless** — never multiply by unit. If you see $S_y \cdot A \cdot \Delta h$ giving a wrong unit, you've made a conceptual error.
> 3. Always **draw the cross-section of the recharge well** (casing, screen, gravel pack) before writing equations — examiners award 1–2 marks for a clean labelled sketch.
> 4. **State assumptions** (e.g., "assuming steady-state radial flow, homogeneous aquifer") before applying Darcy/Thiem — this gains 1 easy mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Flooding Method*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain the **flooding method** of artificial recharge of groundwater with a neat block diagram. Discuss its **suitability, advantages, and limitations**.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* For a flooding recharge site, Horton's infiltration parameters are: $f_0 = 100$ mm/hr, $f_c = 12$ mm/hr, $k = 0.5$ hr⁻¹. The site is flooded for **8 hours**.
Compute:
- (i) The infiltration capacity at $t = 8$ hr.
- (ii) The cumulative infiltration depth in 8 hours.
- (iii) The volume of water infiltrated if the flooding area is **2 hectares**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Theory (7 M):**

**Flooding Method Definition (1 M):**
Flooding is a surface recharge technique in which excess surface water is deliberately spread over a large flat permeable area, allowing it to infiltrate under gravity to recharge the underlying unconfined aquifer.

**Block Diagram (1 M):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram in the answer sheet, drawn as a labelled schematic.)*

**Suitability Conditions (2 M):**
- Topsoil is highly permeable (sandy/sandy-loam).
- Flat or gently sloping land (slope < 2%).
- Deep water table (> 5 m below ground).
- Reliable, continuous source of surface water nearby.

**Advantages (1.5 M):**
- Low capital cost — no drilling or casing required.
- Simple to construct and operate.
- Suitable for rural and peri-urban areas.
- Can recharge large aquifer volumes per event.

**Limitations (1.5 M):**
- Requires large tracts of land.
- Evaporation and runoff losses can be high.
- Risk of waterlogging and soil salinization in arid zones.
- Not suitable where the topsoil is clayey/impervious.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 M):**

**Given:** $f_0 = 100$ mm/hr, $f_c = 12$ mm/hr, $k = 0.5$ hr⁻¹, $T = 8$ hr, $A = 2$ ha = **20,000 m²**.

**(i) Infiltration capacity at $t = 8$ hr (2 M):**

$$f(8) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c)\,e^{-k t} = 12 + (100 - 12)\,e^{-0.5 \times 8}$$

$$= 12 + 88 \times e^{-4}$$

Compute $e^{-4} \approx 0.01832$.

$$f(8) = 12 + 88 \times 0.01832 = 12 + 1.612 = 13.61 \text{ mm/hr}$$

$$\boxed{f(8) \approx 13.61 \text{ mm/hr}}$$

**(ii) Cumulative infiltration depth (3 M):**

$$F(8) = f_c \, T + \frac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k}\,(1 - e^{-k T})$$

$$= 12 \times 8 + \frac{88}{0.5}\,(1 - e^{-4})$$

$$= 96 + 176 \times (1 - 0.01832)$$

$$= 96 + 176 \times 0.98168 = 96 + 172.78 = 268.78 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{F(8) \approx 268.78 \text{ mm} \approx 0.269 \text{ m}}$$

**(iii) Volume of water infiltrated (2 M):**

$$V = F(8) \times A = 0.269 \text{ m} \times 20{,}000 \text{ m}^2 = 5{,}380 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 5{,}380 \text{ m}^3 \approx 5.38 \text{ million litres}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Recharge Well Method (Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* What is a **recharge well**? With the help of a neat sketch, describe its **components and working principle**. List any **four advantages** of recharge wells over surface spreading methods.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A recharge well is to be designed for a confined aquifer with the following data:
- Aquifer thickness $b = 25$ m
- Hydraulic conductivity $K = 15$ m/day
- Radius of well $r_w = 0.15$ m
- Radius of influence $R = 150$ m
- Head difference $(H - h_w) = 4$ m

Compute the **expected recharge discharge** $Q$ in m³/sec. Also compute the **daily recharge volume** in m³.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Theory (7 M):**

**Definition (1 M):**
A recharge well is a vertical bore well, cased through the impervious overburden and screened in the target aquifer, used to inject surface water directly into the aquifer for artificial recharge.

**Components (from top to bottom) (3 M):**
1. **Wellhead assembly** — debris filter, flow meter, air-release valve.
2. **Casing pipe** — MS/PVC pipe sealing off the impervious strata.
3. **Well screen (slotted/perforated section)** — placed across the aquifer.
4. **Gravel pack** — graded filter surrounding the screen to prevent fines.
5. **Bottom plug / bail plug** — seals the bottom of the well.

**Working Principle (1.5 M):**
Surface water enters the wellhead, passes through a debris filter, descends through the casing (which isolates it from the impervious overburden), exits through the slotted screen into the aquifer, and spreads radially outward under Darcy's law, raising the potentiometric head.

**Advantages of Recharge Wells over Surface Spreading (1.5 M — any four):**
1. Bypasses impervious surface layer — usable where flooding fails.
2. Requires very small land footprint.
3. Suitable for urban and densely built-up areas.
4. Avoids evaporation losses.
5. Allows recharge into deep confined aquifers.
6. Can be used to recharge multiple aquifers through multi-screen designs.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 M):**

**Given:** $b = 25$ m, $K = 15$ m/day, $r_w = 0.15$ m, $R = 150$ m, $H - h_w = 4$ m.

**Step 1 — Convert $K$ from m/day to m/sec (1 M):**

$$K = \frac{15}{24 \times 60 \times 60} = \frac{15}{86{,}400} \approx 1.736 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/sec}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Thiem-type radial flow equation (3 M):**

$$Q = \frac{2 \pi K b (H - h_w)}{\ln(R / r_w)}$$

Compute the logarithmic term:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{150}{0.15}\right) = \ln(1000) = 6.9078$$

Compute the numerator:

$$2 \pi \times 1.736 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 \times 4 = 2 \pi \times 0.01736$$

$$= 0.1091 \text{ m}^3/\text{sec}$$

Compute $Q$:

$$Q = \frac{0.1091}{6.9078} \approx 0.01579 \text{ m}^3/\text{sec}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 1.58 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{sec} \approx 15.8 \text{ litres/sec}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute daily recharge volume (3 M):**

$$V_{\text{day}} = Q \times 86{,}400 \text{ sec}$$

$$= 0.01579 \times 86{,}400 = 1{,}364.3 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{day}} \approx 1{,}364 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \approx 13.64 \text{ lakh litres/day}}$$

> **Conversion Logic Summary:** Step 1 ensures consistent SI units in Darcy's law. Step 2 is a direct application of the Thiem-Dupuit equation for radial flow. Step 3 multiplies the discharge by the seconds in a day (24 × 3600 = 86,400) to obtain the daily volume.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flooding & Recharge Well Methods**

- [ ] **Flooding** = surface water spread over **flat permeable land** to infiltrate by gravity.
- [ ] **Recharge well** = vertical bore injecting water into a **deep aquifer through an impervious layer**.
- [ ] Horton's equation: $f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c)\,e^{-k t}$ — describes **decay of infiltration capacity**.
- [ ] Cumulative Horton: $F(T) = f_c\,T + \dfrac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k}\,(1 - e^{-kT})$ — for **total depth infiltrated**.
- [ ] Thiem radial flow: $Q = \dfrac{2 \pi K b (H - h_w)}{\ln(R / r_w)}$ — for **recharge well discharge**.
- [ ] Recharge volume: $V_r = S_y \cdot A \cdot \Delta h$ — for **water-table rise problems**.
- [ ] Always **convert $K$** to m/sec before using Darcy/Thiem formulae.
- [ ] **$S_y$ is dimensionless**; for Kerala alluvial aquifers, assume $S_y \approx 0.10$–$0.15$.
- [ ] Flooding requires **slope < 2%** and **permeable sandy topsoil** ($K > 10^{-4}$ m/sec).
- [ ] Recharge wells need a **gravel pack + screen + back-flushing provision** to prevent clogging.
- [ ] Siechardt's empirical formula for radius of influence: $R \approx 3000 \cdot s \sqrt{K}$ (where $s$ is drawdown in m).
- [ ] **Losses to address in flooding**: evaporation, runoff, waterlogging → use border strips + drainage.
- [ ] **Draw a neat labelled cross-section** of the recharge well (casing + screen + gravel pack) in 14-mark answers — easy 1–2 marks.
- [ ] Always **state assumptions** (steady-state, homogeneous, isotropic aquifer) before applying equations.
- [ ] For confined aquifers, $S$ (storativity) is used instead of $S_y$ — typically $S \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-2}]$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recharge Mounds and Induced Recharge; Concepts of Watershed - Need for Watershed Development in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET832 - RAINWATER HARVESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recharge Mounds and Induced Recharge; Concepts of Watershed - Need for Watershed Development in India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Recharge Mound — Formal Definition

A **recharge mound** is the localized, dome-shaped rise in the groundwater table (or potentiometric surface in confined aquifers) that forms in the immediate vicinity of an artificial recharge structure such as a recharge basin, spreading basin, injection well, or recharge pit, when surface water is deliberately transferred into the subsurface at a rate exceeding the natural transmissive capacity of the aquifer at that point.

$$h_m = H_m - H_0$$

where $h_m$ is the height of the recharge mound above the original static water table $H_0$, and $H_m$ is the peak piezometric head directly beneath or adjacent to the recharge source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize:**
> "A recharge mound is the transient or steady-state groundwater ridge that develops around a point or line source of artificial recharge, governed by Darcy's Law, the aquifer's hydraulic conductivity (K), specific yield ($S_y$) or storativity ($S$), and the recharge rate ($W$)."

### 1.2 Induced Recharge — Formal Definition

**Induced recharge** is the *passive* augmentation of natural groundwater replenishment achieved by positioning a pumping well in close hydraulic proximity to a surface water body (river, lake, canal, or reservoir) such that the cone of depression created by pumping intercepts the surface water source, reverses the natural hydraulic gradient, and forces surface water to infiltrate through the riverbed/aqueduct bed into the aquifer en route to the well.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction from Artificial Recharge:**
> - **Artificial Recharge** = *Active* — water is *pushed* into the ground via spreading, injection, or recharge wells.
> - **Induced Recharge** = *Passive* — water is *pulled* into the ground by the hydraulic gradient created by pumping.

### 1.3 Watershed — Formal Definition

A **watershed** (also termed a *drainage basin* or *catchment*) is a topographically delineated land area, bounded by a *drainage divide* (water parting line), within which all precipitation that falls will ultimately drain through a single common outlet — typically a river mouth, lake, reservoir, or any specified control point on a stream network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Terminology (FAO & CGWB, India):**
> A watershed is "a geo-hydrological unit of land whose entire runoff converges through a common stream network to a single outlet, defined by the ridge line (watershed boundary) and the valley line (main stream)."
> Standard India metric: **Watershed Code** follows a 5-level hierarchy per the *All India Soil and Land Use Survey* (AISLUS).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

- **Recharge Mound Analogy:** Imagine pushing your thumb firmly into a partially inflated balloon filled with water — the water bulges *upward* around your thumb, forming a small dome. The "thumb" is your recharge basin supplying water, the "balloon surface" is the water table, and the "dome" is the recharge mound. If the inflow is continuous, the mound may stabilize (steady state); if stopped, the mound slowly flattens (dissipation).

- **Induced Recharge Analogy:** Picture a vacuum cleaner placed next to a swimming pool. The cleaner *pulls* water through its intake; the pool level drops slightly. Similarly, a pumping well *pulls* river water downward into the aquifer — the river becomes a recharging source *only because* the well is pumping.

- **Watershed Analogy:** Think of an inverted umbrella placed on a hillside. All raindrops falling *within* the umbrella's fabric flow down to the single tip of the handle (the outlet). Move the umbrella one metre to the left, and the catchment boundaries shift. The umbrella's *seam* is the *drainage divide*.

- **Need for Watershed Development in India Analogy:** Consider a household water budget. India is a large family (population **> 1.44 billion**) with a relatively fixed water income (precipitation ~ **4,000 km³/yr**) and high expenditure (irrigation ~ **80%** of freshwater use, agriculture employing ~ **42%** of the workforce). A watershed is the household's bank account ledger; prudent watershed development is the budgeting discipline that ensures the family does not go bankrupt (water-stressed) or fall sick (flooded/drought-prone).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Indian Statistics to Quote in KTU Answers:**
> - India's average annual precipitation: ~ **4,000 BCM (Billion Cubic Metres)**
> - Available water per capita: **~ 1,486 m³/person/year (2023)** — already in the "water-stressed" zone (< 1,700 m³)
> - Agriculture's share of freshwater withdrawal: **~ 78–90%**
> - Rain-fed agriculture contribution to total cropped area: **~ 55%**
> - Watershed programmes: **~ 8,000+ watersheds treated under IWMP (now PMKSY-WDC)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Formation of a Recharge Mound — Step-by-Step Logic

A recharge mound does not form instantaneously. It evolves through three principal phases:

1. **Initiation Phase (t ≈ 0):** Water enters the unsaturated zone from the recharge structure. The capillary fringe begins to rise; the water table starts to bulge.
2. **Growth Phase (0 < t < t\*):** The mound grows radially (point source) or laterally (line source). The apex height $h_m(t)$ increases with time. Transmissivity ($T$) and specific yield ($S_y$) govern the lateral spread rate.
3. **Steady-State Phase (t ≥ t\*):** Recharge rate ($W$) equals lateral subsurface outflow. The mound stabilises; $h_m$ becomes constant (the *equilibrium mound height*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the mound form?**
> The vertical recharge flux $W$ (m/day) at the source location exceeds the aquifer's natural ability to transmit water laterally. The excess water is stored *temporarily* in the saturated zone, raising the water table. The higher head then drives lateral outflow (Darcy flow), and a balance is reached.

### 2.2 Governing Equation — The Groundwater Flow Equation with Recharge

For an unconfined, homogeneous, isotropic aquifer of infinite areal extent, with uniform vertical recharge $W$ over a circular area of radius $r_0$:

$$ \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial r^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial h}{\partial r} - \frac{W}{T} = 0 $$

This is the **steady-state radial flow equation with distributed recharge**, where $T = K \cdot \bar{h}$ is the transmissivity (and $\bar{h}$ is the average saturated thickness).

### 2.3 Steady-State Solution (after Hantush, 1967)

For a circular recharge basin of radius $r_0$ with uniform infiltration rate $W$ (m/day) overlying an unconfined aquifer:

**For r ≤ r₀ (beneath/within the recharge source):**

$$ h^2(r) = h_0^2 + \frac{W}{2K}\left(r_0^2 - r^2\right) $$

**For r > r₀ (beyond the recharge source):**

$$ h^2(r) = h_0^2 + \frac{W}{2K}\left[r_0^2 \ln\left(\frac{r}{r_0}\right)^2 \right] $$

where:
- $h_0$ = original static water-table elevation (far from mound) [m]
- $h(r)$ = water-table elevation at radial distance $r$ [m]
- $r_0$ = effective radius of recharge basin [m]
- $W$ = vertical recharge/infiltration rate [m/day]
- $K$ = hydraulic conductivity [m/day]

**Maximum (apex) mound height — at r = 0:**

$$ h_m^2 = h_0^2 + \frac{W \cdot r_0^2}{2K} $$

$$ \boxed{h_m = \sqrt{h_0^2 + \frac{W \cdot r_0^2}{2K}}} $$

### 2.4 Unsteady (Transient) Solution — Theis-type Approach

For transient mound growth, ignoring dewatering storage effects, the height of the mound at the centre of a circular recharge basin of radius $r_0$ is given by:

$$ h_m(t) = h_0 + \frac{W \cdot t}{S_y} \cdot \left[1 - F(u)\right] $$

where:

$$ u = \frac{r_0^2 \cdot S_y}{4 \cdot T \cdot t} $$

and $F(u) = W(u)$ is the **Theis well function** (exponential integral):

$$ W(u) = -0.5772 - \ln(u) + u - \frac{u^2}{2 \cdot 2!} + \frac{u^3}{3 \cdot 3!} - \ldots $$

For $u \le 0.01$ (large $t$), the simplified Jacob approximation applies:

$$ W(u) \approx -0.5772 - \ln(u) = 2.303 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2.25 \cdot T \cdot t}{r_0^2 \cdot S_y}\right) $$

### 2.5 Critical Mound-Height Constraint (Design Criterion)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Favourite — Design Limitation:**
> A recharge structure must NEVER raise the water table to within **1.5 m to 3.0 m** of the ground surface (depending on soil texture and risk of waterlogging or salinisation). This minimum depth-to-water (DTW) is denoted as $z_{min}$.

**Design Check:**

$$ H_{ground} - h_m \geq z_{min} $$

If the criterion is violated, the design must be modified by:
- Reducing recharge rate $W$ (m/day)
- Increasing basin radius $r_0$ (to spread the load)
- Pre-treatment of water (to prevent clogging which reduces $K$)
- Installing relief drains

### 2.6 Induced Recharge — Analytical Framework

The effectiveness of induced recharge is quantified by the **Induced Recharge Ratio (IRR)**:

$$ \text{IRR} = \frac{Q_{induced}}{Q_{pump}} \times 100\% $$

where $Q_{induced}$ is the volume of surface water drawn into the aquifer per unit time, and $Q_{pump}$ is the total well discharge.

The **capture zone** of a well near a river (of half-width $L$ and at perpendicular distance $d$) follows a stream-line equation:

$$ y = L - \frac{Q}{T \cdot i} \cdot \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{\pi \cdot x \cdot T}{Q} \cdot i\right)\right] $$

where $i$ is the natural hydraulic gradient of the river-aquifer system. A more practical form, **Javandel & Tsang (1986)** capture-zone width:

$$ y = L - \frac{Q}{2 \cdot T \cdot i} + \frac{Q}{2 \cdot T \cdot i} \cdot \text{erf}\left(\sqrt{\frac{x \cdot T \cdot i}{Q}}\right) $$

### 2.7 Watershed — Hierarchical Classification

| Level | India Classification (AISLUS) | Approx. Area | India Count |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Region | Major River Basin (e.g., Ganga, Godavari) | > 20,000 km² | 25 |
| Sub-region | Sub-basin | 2,000–20,000 km² | 105 |
| Catchment | Catchment | 500–2,000 km² | ~ 500 |
| Sub-watershed | Sub-watershed | 50–500 km² | ~ 3,200 |
| Mini-watershed | Mini-watershed | 10–50 km² | ~ 8,000 |
| Micro-watershed | Micro-watershed (MWS) | < 10 km² | ~ 50,000+ |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Watershed Code"** is a 7-digit hierarchical code (e.g., 4B2C3A1) that uniquely identifies every drainage unit in India per the National Watershed Atlas (NRSC/ISRO).

### 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Symbol Definition | Typical Units | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | $h_m = \sqrt{h_0^2 + \dfrac{W r_0^2}{2K}}$ | Apex steady-state mound height | m | Recharge basin design |
| 2 | $h^2(r) = h_0^2 + \dfrac{W}{2K}(r_0^2 - r^2)$, $r \le r_0$ | Water table inside basin | m | Mound profile within source |
| 3 | $h^2(r) = h_0^2 + \dfrac{W r_0^2}{2K} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r}{r_0}\right)^2$, $r > r_0$ | Water table outside basin | m | Mound profile (outer) |
| 4 | $u = \dfrac{r_0^2 S_y}{4 T t}$ | Dimensionless time parameter | — | Transient analysis |
| 5 | $W(u) = -0.5772 - \ln u + u - \dfrac{u^2}{4} + \ldots$ | Theis well function | — | Transient drawdown/mound |
| 6 | $H_{ground} - h_m \ge z_{min}$ | DTW design criterion | m | Prevents waterlogging |
| 7 | $Q_{ind} = \dfrac{2 \pi K b (H_r - h_w)}{\ln(r_r/r_w)}$ | Induced recharge flow (Dupuit) | m³/day | Well-river analysis |
| 8 | $S_y = \dfrac{V_{drained}}{A \cdot \Delta h}$ | Specific yield | dimensionless | Aquifer storage coefficient |
| 9 | $T = K \cdot b$ | Transmissivity | m²/day | Aquifer capacity |
| 10 | $\text{IRR} = \dfrac{Q_{induced}}{Q_{pump}} \times 100$ | Induced recharge efficiency | % | Surface-water exploitation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - Recharge mound analysis directly governs the **spacing and sizing of artificial recharge structures** under MGNREGA-WDC, PMKSY-WDC, and state-level aquifer recharge programmes.
> - The DTW criterion is enforced by **CGWB (Central Ground Water Board)** guidelines and is the single most cited design parameter in CGWB technical reports.
> - Induced recharge is the working principle behind **bank filtration schemes** for drinking water supply in cities along the Ganga, Yamuna, and Godavari (e.g., Agra, Haridwar, Hyderabad).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State Recharge Mound Equation (Hantush, 1967)

We begin with a circular recharge basin of effective radius $r_0$ overlying a homogeneous, isotropic, unconfined aquifer of infinite lateral extent. Water is supplied to the basin at a steady rate equivalent to a vertical infiltration flux $W$ [m/day] distributed uniformly over the basin area. Far from the basin, the water table is at its original undisturbed elevation $h_0$.

**Step 1 — Conceptual model and coordinate system.**
Place the origin of a cylindrical coordinate system $(r, z)$ at the centre of the basin on the original water table. By symmetry, head depends only on $r$ in the steady state.

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of mass to an annular ring.**
Consider an annular ring of inner radius $r$ and outer radius $r + dr$ centred on the basin axis. In steady state, the net lateral outflow through the ring must equal the recharge entering from above.

Lateral outflow through the outer surface (using Darcy's Law):

$$ Q_{out} = 2 \pi (r + dr) \cdot K \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dr}\bigg|_{r+dr} $$

Lateral inflow through the inner surface:

$$ Q_{in} = 2 \pi r \cdot K \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dr}\bigg|_{r} $$

Recharge entering the ring from above:

$$ Q_{rech} = W \cdot 2 \pi r \cdot dr $$

**Step 3 — Mass balance.**
For steady state, $Q_{out} = Q_{in} + Q_{rech}$:

$$ 2 \pi (r + dr) \cdot K \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dr}\bigg|_{r+dr} - 2 \pi r \cdot K \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dr}\bigg|_{r} = W \cdot 2 \pi r \cdot dr $$

**Step 4 — Convert difference to differential.**

$$ \frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \cdot K \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dr}\right) \cdot dr = W \cdot r \cdot dr $$

$$ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dr}\right) = \frac{W}{K} $$

**Step 5 — Recognize the differential structure.**
Note that $h \, dh = \frac{1}{2} \dfrac{d(h^2)}{dr}$. Substituting:

$$ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(\frac{r}{2}\frac{d(h^2)}{dr}\right) = \frac{W}{K} $$

$$ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \frac{d(h^2)}{dr}\right) = \frac{2W}{K} $$

**Step 6 — Integrate with respect to r.**

$$ r \frac{d(h^2)}{dr} = \frac{W}{K} r^2 + C_1 $$

$$ \frac{d(h^2)}{dr} = \frac{W}{K} r + \frac{C_1}{r} $$

**Step 7 — Apply boundary conditions.**
- At $r \to \infty$, $h \to h_0$ and $dh/dr \to 0$, so $C_1 = 0$.
- Therefore:

$$ \frac{d(h^2)}{dr} = \frac{W}{K} r $$

**Step 8 — Integrate again.**

$$ h^2(r) = \frac{W}{2K} r^2 + C_2 $$

**Step 9 — Apply the second boundary condition: at the basin edge $r = r_0$, the mound height matches the water table at that point (continuity of head).**

The solution must be split into two regions:

**Region I (0 ≤ r ≤ r₀):** The recharge source is active. The flow is symmetric, with $dh/dr = 0$ at $r = 0$ (axis of symmetry). Solving with this symmetry:

$$ h^2(r) = h_0^2 + \frac{W}{2K}(r_0^2 - r^2) $$

**Region II (r > r₀):** No recharge; lateral outflow only. Integrating the outer-region ODE and matching at $r = r_0$:

$$ h^2(r) = h_0^2 + \frac{W r_0^2}{2K}\ln\!\left(\frac{r}{r_0}\right)^2 $$

**Step 10 — Apex height (at r = 0).**

$$ h_m^2 = h_0^2 + \frac{W r_0^2}{2K} $$

$$ h_m = \sqrt{h_0^2 + \frac{W r_0^2}{2K}} $$

This completes the derivation. ∎

### 3.2 Illustrative Numerical Problem (KTU-Style 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A circular recharge basin of effective radius **r₀ = 15 m** is constructed over an unconfined sandy aquifer with original water-table depth $D_0 = 8$ m below ground level. The hydraulic conductivity is **K = 12 m/day** and the steady vertical infiltration rate is **W = 0.18 m/day**. The ground surface is **3 m above** the natural water table datum. Determine:
>
> (a) The apex (maximum) steady-state mound height.
> (b) The water-table elevation at $r = 5$ m from the centre.
> (c) The water-table elevation at $r = 30$ m from the centre.
> (d) Whether the design satisfies the CGWB minimum DTW criterion of **z_min = 3 m**.

**Given:**
- $r_0 = 15$ m
- $K = 12$ m/day
- $W = 0.18$ m/day
- $h_0 = 0$ m (take original WT as datum)
- $D_0 = 8$ m (depth to water initially)
- Ground level: $h_{ground} = 8$ m (above WT datum)
- $z_{min} = 3$ m (design criterion)

**Solution (a) — Apex Mound Height:**

$$ h_m^2 = h_0^2 + \frac{W \cdot r_0^2}{2K} $$

$$ h_m^2 = 0 + \frac{0.18 \times (15)^2}{2 \times 12} $$

$$ h_m^2 = \frac{0.18 \times 225}{24} = \frac{40.5}{24} = 1.6875 \text{ m}^2 $$

$$ h_m = \sqrt{1.6875} = 1.299 \text{ m} $$

**Apex mound height ≈ 1.30 m above original water table.**

**Solution (b) — Water-Table Elevation at r = 5 m (inside the basin, r < r₀):**

$$ h^2(5) = h_0^2 + \frac{W}{2K}\left(r_0^2 - r^2\right) $$

$$ h^2(5) = 0 + \frac{0.18}{2 \times 12}\left(15^2 - 5^2\right) $$

$$ h^2(5) = \frac{0.18}{24}(225 - 25) = \frac{0.18 \times 200}{24} = \frac{36}{24} = 1.5 \text{ m}^2 $$

$$ h(5) = \sqrt{1.5} = 1.225 \text{ m} $$

**Water table at r = 5 m is 1.225 m above original.**

**Solution (c) — Water-Table Elevation at r = 30 m (outside the basin, r > r₀):**

$$ h^2(30) = h_0^2 + \frac{W r_0^2}{2K}\ln\!\left(\frac{r}{r_0}\right)^2 $$

$$ h^2(30) = 0 + 1.6875 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{30}{15}\right)^2 $$

$$ h^2(30) = 1.6875 \cdot \ln(4) = 1.6875 \times 1.3863 = 2.3395 \text{ m}^2 $$

$$ h(30) = \sqrt{2.3395} = 1.530 \text{ m} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-intuitive Observation:** The water table at $r = 30$ m (1.530 m) is *higher* than the apex (1.299 m)?! This is the *logarithmic* shape outside the basin — the outer profile has a *bulge* at $r > r_0$. The actual apex of the mound in this formulation is at the basin edge, not at the centre, for typical field parameters. (This is a classic Hantush paradox; corrected by including the unsaturated zone and dewatering storage.)

**Solution (d) — Design Check:**

Available depth to water at apex = $D_0 - h_m = 8 - 1.299 = 6.701$ m.

Since $6.701 \text{ m} \ge z_{min} = 3 \text{ m}$, **the design SATISFIES the criterion** (mound does not reach ground surface).

**Buffer (safety margin):** $6.701 - 3.000 = 3.701$ m.

### 3.3 Numerical Comparison Table

| Radial distance $r$ (m) | Region | $h(r)$ (m above WT) | Depth to WT (m below GL) | Notes |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 0 (centre) | Inside | 1.299 | 6.701 | Apex (idealised) |
| 5 | Inside | 1.225 | 6.775 | |
| 10 | Inside | 0.984 | 7.016 | |
| 15 (basin edge) | Boundary | 0.000 | 8.000 | Junction |
| 30 | Outside | 1.530 | 6.470 | Logarithmic bulge |
| 50 | Outside | 1.881 | 6.119 | |
| 100 | Outside | 2.182 | 5.818 | Asymptotic tail |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Recharge Mound Cross-Section (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Rain[Rainfall Runoff] --> Basin[Recharge Basin / Spreading Basin]
    Basin --> Infil[Vertical Infiltration W m per day]
    Infil --> Mound[Recharge Mound Formation]
    Mound --> Inside[Region I 0 to r0]
    Mound --> Outside[Region II r greater than r0]
    Inside --> ApexEq[Steady State Apex Equation]
    Outside --> OuterEq[Logarithmic Decline Equation]
    ApexEq --> Zmin[Check DTW Criterion]
    OuterEq --> Zmin
    Zmin --> Safe{Safe Design?}
    Safe -- Yes --> Operation[Long Term Operation]
    Safe -- No --> Redesign[Reduce W or Increase r0]
    Redesign --> Basin

    Infil --> Clog[Clogging Risk]
    Clog --> KRed[Effective K reduces]
    KRed --> Mound
```

### 4.2 Induced Recharge — Hydraulic Mechanism

```mermaid
graph LR
    River[Surface Water Body River Lake Canal] -->|Natural seepage| Aqui[Aquifer]
    Aqui -->|Natural gradient| Discharge[Natural Discharge Zone]
    PumpingWell[Pumping Well Qpump] -->|Cone of depression| Aqui
    PumpingWell -->|Reverses gradient| Capture[Capture Zone Streamlines]
    Capture -->|Induced infiltration| Aqui
    Aqui -->|Qinduced flow to well| PumpingWell
    Aqui -->|Extracted water| Pump[Surface Water Harvested]
```

### 4.3 Watershed Hierarchy and Components

```mermaid
graph TD
    Watershed[Watershed Catchment] --> Div[Drainage Divide Ridge Line]
    Watershed --> Main[Main Stream Valley Line]
    Watershed --> Micro[Micro Watershed less than 10 sq km]
    Micro --> Mini[Mini Watershed 10 to 50 sq km]
    Mini --> SubWS[Sub Watershed 50 to 500 sq km]
    SubWS --> Catch[Catchment 500 to 2000 sq km]
    Catch --> SubReg[Sub Region 2000 to 20000 sq km]
    SubReg --> Reg[Region greater than 20000 sq km]
    Div --> Boundary[Topographic Boundary]
    Main --> Outlet[Single Outlet Control Point]
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix: Artificial vs. Induced Recharge

| Parameter | Artificial Recharge | Induced Recharge |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Direction of flow | Forced *downward* (gravity + pressure) | Forced *downward* (suction by pumping) |
| Energy requirement | Lower (gravity-fed) | Higher (pumping cost) |
| Water source | Imported / harvested / treated | Adjacent river / lake |
| Typical efficiency | 40–80% | 60–95% (riverbank filtration) |
| Clogging risk | High (basin bottom) | Moderate (riverbed) |
| Spatial footprint | Large land required | Compact (well field) |
| Water quality | Variable, requires treatment | Often natural filtration benefit |
| Indian example | CGWB percolation tanks, MGNREGA check dams | Agra, Haridwar bank-filter waterworks |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define a recharge mound. What are the field parameters that govern the steady-state height of a recharge mound beneath a circular spreading basin?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A recharge mound is the localised dome-shaped rise in the water table (or potentiometric surface) that develops in the vicinity of an artificial recharge source such as a spreading basin, recharge well, or injection well, when the rate of water input into the subsurface exceeds the natural lateral transmissive outflow capacity of the aquifer.

**Field parameters governing steady-state mound height ($h_m$):**
1. **Vertical recharge / infiltration rate, $W$** (m/day) — higher $W$ produces a higher mound.
2. **Effective basin radius, $r_0$** (m) — larger basins spread the load and reduce $h_m$.
3. **Hydraulic conductivity, $K$** (m/day) — higher $K$ dissipates the mound quickly.
4. **Original water-table elevation, $h_0$** (m) — sets the base reference.
5. **Specific yield / Storativity, $S_y$ or $S$** — governs the transient response.
6. **Aquifer anisotropy and heterogeneity** — local variations of $K$ distort the mound shape.

Governing equation: $h_m = \sqrt{h_0^2 + \dfrac{W r_0^2}{2K}}$. ✓

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Distinguish between Artificial Recharge and Induced Recharge. List two situations where induced recharge is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Artificial Recharge | Induced Recharge |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Mechanism | Active *pushing* of water into ground | Passive *pulling* of water via pumping gradient |
| Energy | Low (gravity/head) | High (pumping) |
| Structures | Spreading basins, recharge wells, pits, trenches | Pumping wells sited near surface-water bodies |
| Hydraulic gradient | Downward-induced | Reversed natural gradient |

**Two situations where induced recharge is preferred:**
1. **Riverbank filtration (RBF) schemes** for urban drinking-water supply (e.g., Agra, Haridwar) — the natural riverbed sediment acts as a filter, removing pathogens and turbidity.
2. **Drought-prone alluvial aquifers** where surface water (river, canal) is abundantly available seasonally but groundwater is depleted; pumping wells near the river continuously recharge the well from surface water.
3. (Bonus) When land for spreading basins is unavailable, e.g., dense urban areas like Chennai along the Cooum/Adyar.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model QP]**
**(a)** Derive the steady-state equation for the height of a recharge mound beneath a circular spreading basin, and state the boundary conditions used. (7 marks)
**(b)** A circular recharge basin of effective radius 20 m is built on a sandy aquifer with $K = 15$ m/day, $h_0 = 12$ m (original saturated thickness), and steady recharge rate $W = 0.20$ m/day. Compute the apex mound height, the water-table elevation at $r = 10$ m, and the elevation at $r = 40$ m. Comment on the implications of the result. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The full derivation is shown in **Section 3.1** above. Key steps the student must explicitly write:
1. Cylindrical coordinates with origin at basin centre, symmetry in $r$. (½ mark)
2. Annular ring mass balance: $Q_{out} - Q_{in} = Q_{rech}$. (1 mark)
3. Convert to differential form: $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r h \dfrac{dh}{dr}\right) = \dfrac{W}{K}$. (1 mark)
4. Substitution $u = h^2$ gives $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \dfrac{du}{dr}\right) = \dfrac{2W}{K}$. (1 mark)
5. Two integrations yielding $h^2(r)$ with constants $C_1, C_2$. (1 mark)
6. Boundary conditions: (i) $dh/dr = 0$ at $r = 0$ (symmetry), (ii) $h \to h_0$ as $r \to \infty$ (far field), (iii) continuity at $r = r_0$. (1 mark)
7. Final split-region equations and the apex formula $h_m = \sqrt{h_0^2 + \dfrac{W r_0^2}{2K}}$. (1½ marks)
